Livkhi'OOL, February 12. The bark
Eliza Young, for Barreok, is a total
wreck at t,'rookhaven.
Kev 'vrv, February 12. The shin
Hierson, from Antwerp, via Ty bee, for
New Orleans, went ashore during the
last gale ott Hillslmro bar. A wrecking
steamer has gone to her assistance.
London, February 12. Ireland so
far elects forty-one Homo Killers,
twenty-three Conservatives and nine
Liberals.
The heavy galo which prevailed yes
terday on the ilaltlc sea caused it to
overliow along tho eastern coast of
iSchlesswIg-Holstein. The dykes pro
tecting the lowlands were busrt in many
places, and great tracts of country were
Hooded. The damage to property is
immense, and loss of life is feared.
Boston, February 12. The senate
passed a resolution annulling anil re
scinding the resolve of 1872, which
censured Charles Sumner for his action
in relation to battle rings.
Montkkaj,, February 12. Justice
Kamsey to-day in the ease of Uosen
baum, accused of arson in New Jersey,
ordered the prisoner to be extradited.
Key Wkst, February 12. The steam
ship Oulf Stream, from Baltimore for
Hiivana and New Orleans, arrived yes
terday and reports that during the pas
sage a tiro broke out in the hold and
burned two days, und despite the exer
tions of the captain and crew much of
the cargo was destroyed. Tho vessel
was but little injured, the tire being
confined to the hold.
New Yodk, February 12. The mem
bers of the Stock Exchange have
opened a subscription list for the estab
lishment of free soup houses in the
lower part of the city. J. Q. Bennett
has given 10,000 to supply sorp to the
poor from the flro and police stations.
Jules l'illipplti, an actor, wns found
dead in his bed at the Atlantic hotel
yesterday, .v
The western plan of suppressing liq
uor saloons will be tried in about ten
days. Worcester, Mass., will be the
first place operated on. A mass meet
ing of the citizens will be held, when
the plan of operations will be ex
plained, and nu organization for work
formed, which it is proposed to prose
cute vigorously.

K.soxvrM-E, Tenn., February 12.
The Daily Chronicle of this city pub
lishes a long letter from Senator
Brownlow to-morrow on the civil
rights bill. He decidedly opposes the
mixed school feature of the bill and
suys It would destroy the free schools
in the south. He advises the colored
peeple to tell Congress that they do hot
want mixed schools. He also favors
the issue of more currency.
Washington, February 12. Gener
al Sheridan telegraphs (Jencral Sher
man confirming the report of the kill
ing of Lieutenant Bobinson and a pri
vate by the Indians.
Indian Agent Howard, of the White
stone agency, telegraphs to the com
missioner here for military protection,
which will be accorded.
Albany, N. V.,' February 12. The
senate lias agreed to the resolution

appointment of commissioners to the
Centennial exposition.
Jeksey City, February 12. The au

Texas, stating that Hamilton, the de
faulting treasurer of Jersey City, had
len arrested there.
The New York correspondent of a
leading Washington paper gets off the
following good thing:
And so the vexed question of the
chief justiceship is happily settled.
This reminds me of a personal anec
dote. On the very day when Mr.
Williams performed harl-karl und
withdrew from the contest, I met my
learned frlond William M. Kvurls, ul
Downing', here In New York. Indul
lilt; In UMiniill ullowulien nf raw nv
tors. Now 1 ndmlre, imy, I love tills
intellectual gentleman. J like to look
into his scholarly luce. 1 nui fond of
studying that remiukiililo head which
t no combined batters or the l lilted
States and F.unmo have failed to tit. 1
said on tho occasion referred to, and
goodness knows I meant it, too, "ion,
Mr, KviirlH, will be nominated chief
lilhtloe" 'No." Hiild he. "Cushiuu
the iniin.'i Sure enough, dishing was
the limn, But Mr. dishing In his turn
perlormed hurl-Karl, nndyet my sehol
arly friend, W. M, Lvurls, was not
nominated,
Two days ago Tmet him at a mutual
triend's house. Ho reminded mo of
his prophecy that Mr. Cusnlng was to
uo the nominee, and mat no (I'.vorts),
was not thought of. "If you will al
low me," said I, "to relate an anecdote
which was torn mo in normally nearly
thirty yeum ago, yon will llnd it quite
pui io your case."
Mr. Lvarls compelled himself to
listen, and 1 related the following:
Once on a time two Franeistmn
monks were having n rather good time
at a dinner in a tavern In a small
town. A Jew, who was peddling some
small wares came In the room and of-
ferred his articles for sale. One of the
monks conceived tho idea of convert
ing this unbeliever to tho true faith.
"My friend." said the monk, "sit
down; let me talk with you. See what
a miserable trade you are engaged in,
how laborious and degrading.. Now,
If you will allow yourself to be con
verted i win make you my messenger
at the Abby of Croskuwa." "Indeed,"
exclaimed tho wily Jew, "the Idea is
worth considering." "Well," said the
monk, "have u glass of wudku
on the strength of It." "I will,"
said the Israelite, with many thanks.
After drinking his wurikn the Jew
said, "1 suppose 1 can hardly expect to
rise higher than a messenger if I be
come a christian." "My friend," an
swered the monk, "you can rise to be
come a priest." "Ha," exclaimed the
embryo convert, "a priest! My dear
brethren, let us drink to the new priest,
and in fact I whl eat something, too."
"Certainly," exclaimed the monk.
So after the meal was over tho Jew
began "Could 1 expect advancement
alter being a priest?" "My dear friend,"
said the monk, "there is uo reason why
you should not be a bishop." "Lord,
bless me," cried the Jew, a "bishop!
Here, waiter, bring me a bolt le of port,
and let it be good, or else I'll excom
municate you when I become a bishop."
The Franciscan began to get heated
with 'wine himself, and willingly in
dulged tho new bishop with tho expen
sive wine.
When the bottle was empty the dirty
peddlur went to tho charge thus: "I
suppose I must be satisfied to live and
die a bishop." "Not at all," cried the
monk, "a cardinal's hut is in store for
you." "You deprive me of. breath, 'J
said the Jew, "what, shall I have a red
hat with two silk tassels? 01 happi
ness. O! grandeur. Nay, I will never
become a prince of the church without
drinking the health of his holiness
who bestowed it on ine," ami so an
other bottlo of good wine was con
sumed.
"It would be foolish," said the Jew
ouco more, "to expect anything higher
than a cardinal's hat." "My good
friend," saiil the monk, "the chances
to become a l'ope after you are once a
cardinal are by no means very remote;
at utmost it is only one in torty-six or
fifty; Why should not you become a
Pope as well as did that glorious
Pope .who was originally.' only a
swineherd? Yes, you cuu become
a Pope." "Most veuerable brethren,"
said the Jew, "my apostolical blessings
be on you be euro I will not forget
you when I am the head of the church."
"Of course," said the monks, "in
order to raise through these stages it is
first of all necessary for you to be bap
tised in the true l'aithut the earliest
moment." "Ahem! said tho Jew,
"I suppose there is no chance to rise
another step after being a Pope?''
"Thou rascally, villainous, insatiate
vagabond," cried tho now much ir
ritated monk "you have risen from a
dirty pedlar to be the vicar of Christ,
and thou still feclest dissatisfied. What
more can you become? what more can
any human being become?"
"Pardon me," answered tha pedlcr.
"I agree perfectly that if I become a
christian, and, ai a christian, I cannot
get higher tha i to be. tho vicar of
Christ. But if V remain a Jew I have
a chance (a slight one, true,) to become
a Christ. Nov would I be the first Jew
who became a Christ, but as a christian
I never could become one."
"Ha 1 ha laughed Mr. Evarts.
"Stop," said I, "the application is this:
A chief justice is, after all, only the
vicar compared to that highest of olli
ces that is the national disease. Now,
as chief justice you could not have ris
en any higher, whereas, remaining Mr.
William Evarts, you have a chance,
and let me hope not a slight one, to be
come president.
War Import From Great Britain,
From the New York Herald, Janaury iW.l
One of the most important signs of
an improving financial condition in
the country is the decline of imported
luxuries. According to tho report of
the Bureau of Statistics, there was a
considerable falling off in tho importa
tion of tho principal articles of British
manufacture for the year 1873 as com
pared with the previous year, and par
ticularly during the bust months of 1873.
Take cotton niece goods, for example,
and wo find that in 1872 131,817,330
yards were imported, while in 1873 the
number of yards was 100,500,315. So
wc might go through the list of imported
articles and find a corresponding reduc
tion. Iron, however, is specially wor
thy of mention.. In 1872 thcro were
407,304 tons of railroad iron, 04,553
tons of bar, angle, bolt and rod iron,
and 11)5,151 tons of pig iron Imported,
while in 1873 the amount was liail
road iron, 185,702 tons; pig Iron, 102,204
tons; and bar and other kinds of iron
23,000 tons. This Is a difference of
over $20,000,000 in iron alone. If we
can continue to reduce our Imports and
increase our exports,-tho exchanges
will be more in our favor, and this
would do more to bring down the pre
mium on gold and carry us to a speoiel
basis than all the proposed legislation
of Cong! ess.

Statements have recently been

though old in. years, is. fresh In spirits,

(ireat excitement was canned in the

The passage by the legislature of large

ihave rendered it impossible for him to

of taxation which now oppress the

Approval of this act as an act of un
pardonable injustice t all those whose

legialative certificates, which have

he is reluctantly compelled to
may, does not command his confidence
for the work assigned them, nor the

allliir.

The Potter Coumitteft closed its la
bors for the preseot by examining Ex
Govetroor Palmer, of Illinois. His
statement was thak of a thoughtful
and cojisoientious man, and he told his
story in a way that will impress all
who read it. He tells of efforts made
to have a Democrat put on the Re
turning Board, and shows how, on one
flimsy pretext or another, the Republi
can members evaded that duty. He
tells of his efforts to get at the truth
by conversing with citizens of the
State, white and black, and his con
viction was tbat the State was. fairly
oarried for Tildeo. His testimony and
that of Ex-Senator Trumbull, both
oalm and moderate, do not support
the sneer at thera of Mr. Hayes, in
writing to Mr. Sherman, that "new
converts are always violent." By. the
way, Mr. Hayes, not being on good
terms with his party, is he a "convert"
or has bis party experienced a ohango

The history o Maine Republican
Conventions for the last 20 years is a
partial biography of James Of. Blaine.
He usually prepares the resolutions
which are to be adopted nnd tboso to
be voted down, selects the officers of
the Convention, names the candidate
for Governor and makes the principal
speech. Sometimes another Republi
oan having an honest oouviction on
some subject, or desirous of the no
toriety that oomes from death by vio
lence, attempts to do or say somethine
that is not down on Blaine's pro
gramme He either disappears irom
publio life or is immediately found
among Mr. Blaine's most devoted
followers. He is slaughtered or pur
chased. He was the same controlling
person twenty years ago that he was
in the Convention held yesterday.
There is more disturbance in the rank
and file of the Republican party of
MaiDe now than ever before, notwith
standing the harmonious character of
the Convention. It remains to be
seen whether or not the discontented
will be on hand on election dny and
cast their customary vote for Blaine
and Republicanism.
The honestly disposed and indus
trious laborers of other sections than
Washington will do well to ponder
some of the facts ascertained by indi
vidual investigation and by the exami
nation in tho Police Court of the
incendiaries arrested here as the lead
ers of the attempted riot last Friday
and Saturday. Cohen, who aspires to
the distinction of leader, in feeble
imitation of Kearney, is a Jew tramp
from Arkansas. He professes to be a
machinist, but seems to have been
tramping from one Southern city to
another ever since his arrival on these
shores, ten or twelve years ago. He
came here a few months ago and soon
sucoeeded in getting quite a following
among the roughest and most ignorant
of our population. The repectable
labor organizations apqear to have re
pudiated Lira from the first. About
every evening for the past four months
he has delivered harangues at the
street corners to such as he could in
duce to stop anJ hear him. By per
sistent appeals to tha most dejrradiog
and brutal pauinas of human aatar.
ha has exasDeratei a portion of hi
recruits up to the attempt of laft!
week. It aeems that be has almoat
arsr, if sot upon every occasion,
Gin and Brooks'
wound up his pertbrmanoa by, taking
up a "collection to push on labor or
ganization" he professed, but really
to support him in his laziness. One
of his secretaries (he h,d arrived at
at the diBtiuctien of having several
all tramps like himself) has "gone
back" on the "groat agitator", since
they were both arrested, and he testi
fied that though the bargain was for
an equal "divvv,"Cohen always took
the lion's share of the plunder cozen
ed out of his deluded followers.
Cohen yesterday paid his fine and is in
the leoture field again, Any looality
eait have him by making early appli
cation. For the good ot the caust wa
will consent to spare him for the bal
ance of bis natural life. RlX.

.HON. 0. M. ROBERTS.
At was anticipated in' the columns
of the News, a gentleman whose naie
had only been mentioned in a very
quiet way, and then only in caseof a
dead look, was placed before the cod.
ventiou for Governor yesterday after
noon and nominated by acolamatioD.
Judge 0. M. Roberts, of Tyler, having
for the better part of half a century
been oonspicuous in Texas ss a lawyer,
a judge, a soldier, and a most estima
ble gentleman, needs no introduction
to tho constituency to whose suffrages
his Dame is submitted in the present
gubernationalsampaiRn,
Judge Roberts oamo to Texas more
than thirty years ago commenced tbo
practice of hie profession, and was
soon after made district attorney. In
a short time he was mado judge of the
fifth district, which position he held
until the death of the lamented Lips
comb, when ho was elevated to the
vacancy of tile Supremo bench. .On
the secession of Texas from tho union,
Judge Roberts resigned from tho Su
preme court and. took command, as
colonel of the sixteenth Texas regi
ment, in which capacity lie displayed
all the qualities of a soldier and en
deared himself to his men, not only
by gallantly hading thciu wherever
duty called, but by extending over
them the kindly care of a father to
his children. Before the close of the
war be was again placed on the Su
preme bench, front which be was re
moved on the couimoncemcnt of re
construction measures in Texas. In
1866 he was elected Upi'ed States
Senator, but was not permitted to take
his seat. After this he resumed the
practice of his profession, in which
be continued -till tbe inauguration of
Gov. Coke in 1874, when he was ap
pointed Chief Justice of tho Supremo
Court, and to the same position under
tho now constitution he was nominated
by the Democratic Convention that
met in Galveston, January, 187G, and
elected without opposition. He still
holds the office of Chief Justice of
the Supreme Court to the satisfaction
of the peoplo of Texas. Besides na
tive capacity, large experience, uuitcd
with the most sterling integrity, Judge
Roberts is believed to possess in no
ordinary degree tastes and aptitudes
for executive lunctmn. These quali
ties, animated with the patriotism and
publio spirit ot a thorough Texan,
whose heart is as big as the whole
State and pulsates equally for all sec
tions, hold out a well founded assur
anco that he will make not only a
conscientious and faithful, but an
eminently efficient and generally satis
factory Governor.

He is the earliest and most enterpris

serenely pointed to the innocent infant
in his cradle, just as the Mexican gov

sens of its northern border when we
demand reparation. Apollo, noteatiss
lied, forced Hermes to go with bim to
hunt for the oxen, but on the way the

to bo hoped that Diai will not be able

and tbat the person wbo U holding

ia somebody els than the typical

with all purposes and arts of demagogues;
They .talk, plainly and directly of public
needs and of the plainest, boldest means of
attaining ends of public beneficence. Few
resolutions, to be coined into "Constitu
tional" currency, have been proffered for
approval that do not ring of their true pur
pose. Now and then one who has prac
ticed the arts of a popular leader through a
weary life-time forgets the place and occa
sion and speaks to the multitude rather
than' for the country, and members "pudge
one another and smiles are interchanged sig
nificant of the thinness of such harangues
and -resolutions-in such a practical hard
working body. The spirit of the Convention
was illustrated in its refusal to have reports
of speeches published. Members thought
that inducements to talk were strong enough
without that proffered by the publication
of debates, and if literature of this sort be(
in demand, people will read that which
was produced when Madison, Randolph,
Patrick Henry and Giles, and others like
them, were framing a Constitution for the
Old Dominion. We do not pretend to say
that opponents of this publication were
wiser than the rest, but they won and have
lost this day and thus demonstrated the fact
that the Convention means to work and not
ito talk.!-.
TiNone need fear that th? Constitution now
making will not be adopted. The absurdi
ties of the existing Constitution cannot be
exaggerated, and the framers of that which
is to supplant it need have no fears that
their work will not be approved. We have
heard it said, by way of accounting for
ridiculous contradictory clauses in the Davis
Constitution, that the Davis Convention
was divided into two factions one led by
Davis, the other by Hamilton. These men
became bitterly hostile to one another,
Davis seeking to obliterate all laws
and contracts that came oj the
"rebellion" and Hamilton opposing this
revolutionary, ruinous and despotic policy.
The quarrel grew in fierceness till the close
of the session of the costly, extravagant
body. It adjourned with its tak half fin
ished. Davis left in high dudgeon. A
president of the body he forgot or refused
to sign the Constitution. Gen. Canby 'sent
for him and induced him to append his sig
nature to the wretched instrument, which
was then consigned to a. preacher who
patched it together, aa Canby instruct
ed, for fifteen dollars per diem, which Gen.
Canby paid.:. The good parson executed
the unaccustomed task moderately well for
one of his cloth, but the material at his dis
posal came from a mill in which intellects
dull as "fros" did the joiner's work and no
one part is fitted to another, and the whole
is not an unit. This framework of organic
law will be gladly overturned, and no mere
prejudice of the people will defeat the ail op
tion of .the system of government now in
process of creation by as earnest thinkers
and honest patriots as have ever served this
or any American commonwealth.

WHAT THE CONVENTION I1AS DONE.
The first of the standing com-nittees of the
Convention reported yesterday. Another
will soon give us schemes defined for the per
fection of the Judiciary system of Texas.
Very certainly this committee has toiled as
siduously and surely, within the brief period
of its existence, it could hardly have agreed
upon a policy to which even the committee
itself assents with any degree of unanimity.
Upon one single point tbe whole Conven
tion is an unit, that one-third of the coun
ties of Texas are not in a fit condition,
while universal suffrage is the absurd pol
icy of the State, to create for themselves
local courts. .In Northern and Central and
Western Texas where the people, and not
the rabble, are supreme, the masses have
declared that they preferred a judiciary elec
tive by "the people; but since the govern
ment of Texas ia now and must forever re
main a white man' government and there
is no danger of wrong if the judiciary
be creative by its chief magistrate and
legislature, it is commonly asserted that
if no other recourse be presented, the jud
ges slia'd be eligible by the Legislature, the
Governor nominating. But another scheme
has been defined which may accomplish the
purposes of. conservatism, and at the same
time be universally approved. The judges
will be elected by the State at large or by
districts so defined that there will be no
danger that the ermine will be worn by one
unworthy of Us stainless honors. The dis
cussion of this necessity, and the pitiable'
condition to which counties and towns in
southern districts may be reduced, if the
Constitution do not guard them against in
tolerable local taxation imposed by those
who pay no taxes, will lead the Convention
to make the payment of a poll tax a condi
tion precedent to the exercise of the right
of suffrage. It will further be provided
that no indebtedness shall be incurred by a
county or town unless the proposition to
create the debt be first approved, at an elec
tion held for the purpose of determining
the. question by .the .taxpayers and free
holders, who must pay the debt. The De
mocracy of the Belton district and other
like conventions have approved this pur
pose entertained, we believe, by. the great
majority of. the Convention. Practically,
by this provision of organic law, conjoined
with tbe imposition of a poll tax of two or
e7en five dollars, the very aim proposed to
be attained by restricting suffrage in corpo
ations to freeholders is attained. Rings
ruling and ruining towns and counties of
Texas at this hour, constituted of counfy
and town officers co-operating to retain
power in each other's hands and making a
joint stock arrangement by which the reve
nues of both the county and town are made
to serve as a corruption fund and as a basis
of all sorts of speculations all these beau
tiful schemes, now operative in many por
tions of Northern and Southern Texas, will
be suddenly cut off, and that without rem
edy. Thejieople of Texas will not be dis
appointed in these or kindred results. Two
thirds of the members of the Convention
have already shown that they are not dema
gogues. They came to make the best and
most enduring government possible
for Texas, and would make the
people of towns and counties abso
lutely free to do as tbey please; but, as
society is now organized, and as the Fed
eral Constitution subsists, it becomes abso
lutely necessary,' in more than a third of
the counties of Texas and in half of the
towns,' to prevent robbery and wrong and
the desolation of the fairest districts of the
empire by restricting suffrage, to the ex
tent that it affects land and money alone,
to those who have land and money. "TJicre
is and can be," said a hard-fisted black
smith yesterday, "no justice in one man's
voting my money out of my pocket into bis
own and be has. no right to vote awsy my
money unless, at the same time and by the
same act, be votes away his own." No
one can object to J.a scheme of legisla
tion thus wisely approved. With its
perfection and the publication of the
fact everywhere, there will be fewer
person to object to tbe proposition,
already agreed upon in committee, to abol
ish the Immigration Bureau." The best
population of the Southern and .Western
States, when they find that they will be
forever protected, in towns and counties,
against the ravages of the rabble, will seek
homes in Texas. Tbe curse of society and
government at this hour ia wide district of
Tenneaser'-4of Mississippi, Alabama,
Georgia and S iuin Carolia came of the fact
tbat constitutional conventions, wben they
sat in tboe States, were moral coward, and
constituted of demagogue instead of
statesmen. They dared not," a will be done
here in Texas, declare for absolute local
freedom, and to make this desirable, for tbe
restriction of suffrage in corporate elections.

THE CO5STITI.TI0XAI. CO VE?TTIO?f.
It teems tbat the friends cf a constitu
tional conTcntion left the drafting of the
joint resolution altogether lo its enemies.
The resolution provides for an election to
be held on the first Monday in August, at
which election the qualified electors of the
State shall, in each Senatorial district, Tote
for three delegates to a constitutional con
vention. This would, make ninetj mem
bers in all, a body quite large enough, if
composed of the right material, to properly
perform the work assigned them. At the
same election these electors are also re
quired toTote "for convention" ct "against
convention." The presiding justices of the
different counties are to make returns of
the election to the Secretary of State, and
if the majority of votes, when counted,
are "for convention," the Governor is
to issuo bis proclamation calling the
convention to meet on the fir3t Monday
in September. It is strange, indeed, that
legislators should have passed such an im
portant act as this without the proper con
siderauo' Counting the- time from the
hoiarag or the election to that of the as
sembling of the convention,- it will be seen
that only thirty days aro allowed for the
returns to bo made, rotes counted, the
Governor to 'declare the result, issue his
proclamation calling the body together, and
for the delegates thereafter to assemble at
Austin. The time allowed by law for the
counting of votes of general elections and
for members of Congress, under act of
1873, Is limited to not less than forty
days from the date of the election. True
it is, the election for delegates to a con
vention is not embraced under the act re
ferred to, and therefore does not come un
der its provisions. It shows, however,
that while the friends of a constitutional
convention were not vigilant, its ene
mies were actively at work, trying by all
manner of means to defeat one. The joint
resolution calling ,a constitutional conven
tion comes near defeating itself. The
first returns received from the late general
election was lrom the. county of El Paso,
three weeks after the election was held.
The returns of the election for the con
vention will have to be in the hands of the
Secretary of State in fifteen days after it is
held. Such a thing is possible, but at the
same time it will require the gres test activity
to make it a success. Fifteen days more can
be allowed for the votes to be counted, the
result declared, and for the Governor to
call the body together. The limited time
allowed by law is simply absurd, but since
the Governor has lately shown himself pos
sessed of the happy faculty of leading us
out of great difficulties, it is believed ho
can be equal to this emergency. lie must
see that plotters and schemers shall not
succeed in defeating the will of the people
by making laws upon great and leading ques
tions deemed inoperative within themselves.
The convention must be held. J The amend
ments to the Constitution proposed under
the joint resolution passed by the late Leg
islature will not, in all instances, answer
the purposes desired. "We are 1 opposed to
an elective judiciarr. at least. i tita
o -.tr"" Opposed to, an inoperative
school system, and to some other organic
changes as proposed in the amendments.
We regard some of these amendments as
traps set by designing politicians, conceived
for self-aggrandizement, and the evidences
are contained in the provisions of the joint
resolution authorizirig a constitutional con
vention, that these schemers were at work
to defeat the latter, and probably both of
the resolutions.1 It is no time for the peo
ple to be cheated out of a birthright, just
now doubly sacred. They desire a conven
tion; they need one; they are ready, to pay
for one, for they recognize that the only
way to check the inordinate expenses of
the government is to go down to the very
base of it, tear it np, and make a new fab
rication, based upon wisdom, prudence and
integrity a thing not of tyranny and mili
tary dictation, Jbut a guarantee made by
intelligence, freedom and peace,

NEtTSPAPEIt BCSIHESS, FAILCBES
AND SUCCESSES.
None of the New York dailies are mak
ing money, except, perhaps the World and
Herald. The .Tribune and Time$ exhaust
their income in paying for news and for
contributions to their brilliant pages, not
more attractive, however, than those of the
two papers above named. It costs vast
sums to collect news, and the theory of the
New York press is, that anything is worth
telegraphing which is worth printing. Their
correspondents are everywhere, and if the
world were not bo large, and towns and
States so numerous, the New York dailies
would drive all others out of existence.
These newspapers can't bold all that they
would print The Springfield JlepuUican
flourishes, because of the sheer forco of
genius of its editor, under the very shadow
of the great dailies of the great city, and
tells us, very truthfully, that the business
is overdone, and that competition is every
where ruinous. The daily metropolitan
newspaper utilizes the telegraph, to the
amount of thousands of dollars per week.
It has its general and special correspondents
wherever there is news to be found. It em
ploys .specialists for special occasions. It
gathers the daily news not only from its
city and State, but from the United
States, and from every; part of the
world reached by telegraph and mail.
It has already run the monthly magazines
down to a very small circulation, and killed
many of them, and it has' more or less in
fluenced the sale of general literature by in
vading its domain. The scientist, the lec
turer, the clergyman, find it to be their
mouthpiece, even without their knowledge.
There is little use for scientific journals or
for religious newspapers now. Every pro-
yince of thought, knowledge and news is
ransacked by this omnivorous collector,
and subsidized by it. The results of this
gigantic work are furnished to the public
for the pittance of a few cents, and, as
there is no other business which develops
such exciting rivalry and eager, constant
competition, in like manner there is no
other business which furnishes the results
of competition at so small a price. The
result of all this is that only a few of the
larger papers, which have an extensive cir
culation and advertising patronage, can
make any money. Their income is ex
hausted in the outgo, and they are in a state
of chronic poverty all their days, living in
Some .mysterious minnw. HV inrlivirlnaU
similarly afflicted. In the case of the great
daily newspapers, undoubtedly this' fact is
attributable partly to waste of labor grow
ing out of the want of skilled and trained
men, and partly to the expenditure of
money for unimportant matter, growing out
of the necessities of competition.. Just at
present hard times and dull business affect
the business of all papers, but the general
fact remains that the competition for news
between the daily newspapers is exhausting
the receipts of most of them. LTow this
problem will be solved remains tq be seen.
It may Solve itself by the collapse of some
of the superfluous sheets.

RESTniCTJlD Sl'FFUAGE IN TOWNS
AND CITIES. ,T
Grand schemes of "public plunder are
usually concocted in high' places, not in
low; among the full-handed, not among
the poor. Tweed and his knavish confed
erates were ell fellows of substance, as well
as of liberal capacity for acquisition. Jfcvt.
Tweed bought and consolidated the rab
ble as he could not buy the freeholders of
New York City., It was universal suffrage
that robbed the city of millions,' Has made
taxation intolerable and the government of
the American metropolis disgraceful in the
eyes of civilization. If this be true of the
fortunes of New York, how infinitely worse
the condition of Southern municipalities I
"Reconstruction" itself dared not make es
cape from possible ruin by "savage suf
frage" impossible. It was declared, how
ever, that if we secured for ourselves wise
State legislation we must win it by losing
numbers and votes in Congress. Beyond
this, crazed Radicalism dared not go, and
we were left free to protect ourselves at will
in municipalities. State Legislatures need
only declare that none but freeholders 6hall
vote in municipal elections, though non
freeholders may be eligible to office, and
then, those alone imposing, who pay taxes,
will surely make them light enough.1
County and town governments affect prop
erty alone. They neither create nor restrict
personal rights or freedom. The rabble
that reigns in half the towns and cities of
the South have neither interests nor sym
pathies in common with the people they
plunder, and surely none save those who
approve negro suffrage and the delectable
civil rights bill, will hesitate, in the South,
to approve in counties and towns the indis
pensable restriction of suffrage. Sym
pathizers with Radicalism can hardly be ex
pected to co-operate in this reform but a
Radical Congress made. its accomplishment
possible, and to this extent admitted, what
we have all seen, that in local elections,
where ignorance is universal and danger
ous to the last degree, that the only security
left taxpayers is in the restriction of suffrage.
SIGNIFICANCE OF THE ELECTION IN
NEW lTAHIPSIimE.
A' newspaper should tell the unvarnished
truth and not suffer its readers to be misled
by the blinding influence of partisan preju
dices. It begins to appear that the good
people of New Hampshire, however a por
tion of tho Democratic press may seek to
disguise the fact, have significantly
"spanked the party," as the Philadelphia
Timet tells it, and sent it to the foot of the
class, just as they do Republicanism when
things go badly in local or national affairs.
The victory is not sweeping, as administra
tion organs have heralded it, but whether
it is as deep as a well or broad as a barn
door, it matters not. It is enough to change
the political ascendancy in the government.
It was a desperately contested battle, and
exhausted the energies and resources of
both parties, as1 they fought hand to hand
among the bleak hills and snowdrifts of the
little commonwealth. Both parties are
hardshell as their Granite mountains Re
publicans so radical that they believe the
war never, will be over, and Democrats so
Bourbon that they don't believe there ever
was a war, and they both declared their ad.
versaries banditti in geuuine Phil Sheridan
style, and would have drawn the sword of
destruction had not the lingering lessons of
early Puritanism interposed. The recon
structed Confederate generals, Gordon and
Lamar, piped peace and good will to the
frosted huzzas of the Democracy, but the
patriots of the custom house and revenue
districts thundered loyalty at the rebels,
and won. There is no choice for Governor
by the people, but the Legislature being
Republican, will elect the Republican can
didate, and the Congressional delegation
gives one Democratic gain. The Republi
cans cleared the decks for, the fight by. a
square declaration in their platform against
the third term, and they have the substance
of victory m changing the Legislature and
Governor from Democratic to their 'own
faith. The first skirmish of .1875, there
fore, scores one against Grant's renomina
tion, while Grant and Pinchbeck score one
in favor of bayonet rule down South: Be
tween the Republican, belligerents the hon
ors are easy-while between Republicans
and Democrats, the latter have notice that a
State may be against debauched Republican
rule and yet not bo Democratic It is a
very pointed "admonition that the party that
is most wise m 1875 will start with the in
side track in 187C.

to tho Paci3c from Washington or New
York, and three fourths of the whole road
will exist, in a country densely populated,
ana 01 matchless fertility. There is no

which the fruit3 and coffee and sugar of the

TOWNS
We receive every day letters from all the
old States asking for information about
Texas, and especially as to the price, qual
ity and productiveness of land in this vicin
ity. Each letter-writter would know what
schools and churches and factories exist.
We aro also required to tell of the health-
fulness of the city, its population, railways
and resources. There should be answers
given in detail to all these inquiries, and
even the political aspect of our fortunes as
a State and as municipalities should be ac
curately, defined. From beyond the ocean
these inquiries come, and it is quite; impos
sible for this office to respond to all these
questions. We send a copy of the States
man, but this only recounts the incidents of
a single day. or week." Next month a
great number of Texans leave the State to
visit St. Louis,, Louisville, Cincinnati, Chi
cago and other fNorthwestera cities. The
representatives of the State capital should
be men of fine appearance and address, and
while, if occasion requires, they should be
capable of telling, in good English, all they
know. of Texas, they.' should be furnished
with' countless pamphlets, descriptive of
Austin and of rich-, tributary' 'districts'.
These' descriptive pamphlets should., be in
the hands of the people of Austin' and the
adjacent country that they may be widely
distributed, and one should' accompany
every letter that crosses the Mississippi. .If
we had efficient chambers of commerce
or other organizations capable of caring for
the public welfare, such a publication would
be provided at once, by every town and city
in Texas, and distributed as we suggest.

At least we infer this fact from the first ed

It declares its purpose to be "a thoroughly

only to its own convictions, making no hol

questions of the day, or in political con
flicts as they pass. It will ever discard the
blind partisanship that would subordinate
risht to party success, no matter for what
organization or in whose interest such claim
is made, and it will resolutely criticise po
litical errors and the want of public integ
rity wherever found. The a of political
orgaas has passed away never to return.
No journals confessing, either ia tenns cr
by unmanly obedience to partisan dictation,
their devotion to party whether right cr
pwrorg, can now, or heccc forth, command
popular respect and confidence cor can
they nould the ccnTictior.3 cr direct the

cur political progrc?3. TTctj is not a eirgle

HOW TO WRITE FOR NEWSPAPER.,
We would again arge the necessity for
brevity. Correspondents need not begin
letters with any flattering unction laid to
ur souls about "your excellent and popu
lar daily." We know.caoie about that than
most of our readers, and do not forget, that
this is the age of telegraphy and of ste
nography, of postal cards and telegrams.
Newspaper articles and letters have no pre
faces, and to write all around a subject be
fore reaching it, is fatally objectionable.
State facts, and don't stop to philosophize.
We are eminently pious, and can do that
part of the job in our own peculiarly felici
tous manner. Remember the injuaction of
Horace and plunge at once ia mediat res.
Strip yourself of all superfluities and plunge
in, as if the subject were a cold bath. Go
to the bottom at once and come out quickly.
Strike out all particularly fine, flowing sen
tences and consolidate the rest. Ideas and
facts, and not words and rounded periods,
are in demand. And then, when your pleas
ing task is done, review the manuscript ear-
fully and demolish two-thirds of your gush
ing adjectives. Let us udd modestly that
a newspaper is not a school .for tyros in the
art of writing. We -do rot-undertake to
educate writers for the pf ess. 1 They must
learn to write on one side of each, sheet
and employ reasonably good English before
their poetry or prose can adorn these pages.
DEATH OF THE IRISH PATRIOT.
The death Of Tohn .Mitchell,; the Irish
patriot, is announced by telegraph. The
event was anticipated, even at this dis
tance, by the Statesman. He died, as he
lived, in the midst of a tempest excited by
his own restless genius and honorable am
bition. He was a true patriot, but whether
a wise. one is another question. His pres
ence in Parliament would have excluded a
Conservative, who might have served well
the hapless island. Of this John Mitchell,
despite his learning, taste, ability and pa
triotism, was IncapableV His very presence
was a red flag' flaunted in the face of. John
Bull. In history, John Mitchell's name
may be often mentioned "with that of
Emmett, but he, like Emmett, though he
loved his country well and gave his life for
it, served it in nothings Heroism and
genius and honcst'devotion to the noblest
cause that ever evoked the worthiest traits
of human character, giving poetry and
eloquence their richest themes, have been
exhausted in vain in Ireland's behalf.
Freedom's battle is yet to be fought, and,
if won, the task of proper self-government
will be even more difficult of achievement.
r; A New York statute, 'just enacted, de
clares that after January,JL87i),nothingbut
gold and silver coin of the United States
shall be a legal tender in that State,. One
of two consequences jnust ensue t the Fed
eral government must resume specie pay
ment, and all States enact the same law, or
all the gold and silver of the country .will
go to New, York, and Texas, and the rest
of paper-using States, will be cursed with
irredeemable paper. Sinco wo are not to
have money .made more abundant than to-
Jnri rit.r -f Torlr
traction, rather than expansion, and the
East rather than the West ia consulted, it
becomes the policy of Texas to follow, in
the footsteps of New. York, and provide by
law for the retention within our own
borders of the silver -and gold for which
cotton and sucar and cattle are sold. In
no other State than this is silver and gold
the popular and common currency, and it
can only be retained in Texas by excluding
paper, and, to effect this end, the New
York statute would servo an admirable
purpose. If we cannot have fresh vigor
and life and activity infused into ell de
partments of business and of' railway pro
gress, 'by making money abundant, we
should at least retain our share of that cur
rency which is best, and has actual,-' sub
stantial, world-wide value. i:
The jetty system pf improving and deep
ening the water on the bars at the mouth of
the Mississippi havingbceii adopted in a bill
which recently passed the House, attention
has been called by Representative Stanard,
of Missouri, to the various rivers in Europe
wheie this plan.has been used successfully
The River Trave, ia'Prussa, with seven
feet of water at .its mouth before jetties
were resorted to,' was deepened to eighteen
feet. The Oder, by this system was deep
ened from seven feet to twenty-three feet;
the Warno from 1 six to thirteen feet the
Niemen from ten to twenty-three feet, and
the Pugel from twelve to' twenty feet The
improvement of the Pugel is not yet com
pleted. In Russia Jhs.mo'ath of the River
Libau has been deepened .from to six
teen feet, acd the Pernau also jfrom six to-
sixteen fecU .Tee great River Danube is a
stream, with a mouth much similar to that
of the Mississippi. It had but nine feet' of
water when in a natural state,- but the 'ap
plication of jetties deepened it to twenty
one and a half feet. At the mouth of the
Mississippi the work of dredging has been
going on for a long time, and the water on
the bar has varied from fourteen to eighteen
feet. By the jetty system proposed a depth
of twenty feet is to be obtained in thirty
months, and this is to be subsequently in
creased to thirty feet.

LAND. The Unit cntury of Aniericao freedom
clows with the annoaocetnent, not only of
the extinction of slavery, perhaps every

culiar to the genius of steam. It built 'onr

For months past we have been reading
incredible accounts of a discovery made by
toiling mechanician and chemist in Phila
delphia, The story goes that he has learned
by accident to use component elements of
water and air in such a manner that boilers
and fuel are practically dispensed with, and
water and air became the resistless motor
of machinery. The power generated is
simply limitless, and we do not see, if rep
resentations made by capitalists who have
already invested, it is asserted, two millions
of dollars in the discovery, be well
founded, why Eads may not abandon
his task at the Balize; why Galves
ton need indulge further anxiety
because a sandbar walls in the city;
and why Cowdon should dream of croco
diles digging a slack water ship canal even
from New Orleans to Fort Livingston. The
weight of machinery and of fnel, and space
occupied on sea-going vessels, must be infi
nitely reduced, and thus the draft of ves
sels relatively lessened and carrying capac
ity maintained. But if the facts slated be
well founded, there will come of this dis
covery even more startling results. The
power of a bird's wing, as related to' the
weight of the bird, is tiie secret of its ca
pacity for aerial flight, and Buch is the infi
nite power said to be generated by this use
of aqueous gases, and comparative lightness
of machinery, that winged locomotives will
be borne about by balloons, and the cost of
attending Democratic conventions at Lam
pasas and other inaccessible places will be
infinitely lessened. The politicians will
then chant in ecstacy the time-worn ditty:
On wing, of (team I'll flr
From gioceree togrocerryi.

even among Phoenicians, of making mallea
ble glass, is rediscovered, and glass as soft
Ind flexible and irrefragable and transpar
ent as DeMorse's cranium is on exhibition
St Cooper's Institute. Everybody except
ropers-in of parties, distributors of spoils
and habitual placeseekers, will build their
dwelling-places of this indestructible mate
rial, stronger and more durable and beauti
ful than Pentelican marble. Will not Aus
tin become a city of dreamland when these
heights gleam in unutterable splendor be
neath the rays of a tropical sun, and the
scene by night will be of transcend ant
beauty when each lamp that glows along the
streets flashes forth reproduced from a thou
sand reflected images of itself Cleanliness
will invade each kitchen, for each cooking
implement will be of stainless glass. With
this comes necessarily, since filth begets
vice, an immeasurable improvement in pop
ular morals:

every-day life.1 But let ns be patieut.' Letter-writers
are more Inventive than artisans
or mechanics, and even wise men have been

RELIUIOUS TOLKHAACE.
As this is the American centennial year,
it is legitimate to note that among all the
changes for good wrought among men1 dur
ing the last hundred years none has been
greater than that which has so largely
tended to the superinduction of. a spirit of
charity and toleratiou for every religious
belief bearing upon it the stamp of sincer
ity, and no change so surely as this shows
the advance that mankind has made to
wards true religion. The breaking down
of the barriers between sects, or, rather, the
recognition by each of the common pur
pose of all fresh evidence of which we
see every day in acts of mutual concession
-ris the best proof how wide-spreading and
far-reaching the tolerant spirit has become.
Poets, it ii said, are but mirrors in which
are reuected the sout-uie ot tne people
among whom they sing, and Tennyson
truly reflects the spirit of the present age
Ti not the creed that makes the man,
It is the man that justices the creed;
For each mutt sare hi. own sou I aa he can,
Since each ia burdeLed with a ilitti reol net d.
The contrast between the feeling on re
ligious subjects now and that which existed
a century ago is great indeed. Then, in
each State of Europe, one single belief was
regnant as the national religion, and to all
who dared stray beyond its iron bounds was
meted out the harsh penalty of fine, im
prisonment, exile, or death. In Asia still
darker creeds held Bway, and upon their
doubters were visited like penalties, while
in no part of the world could there be
found a nation, State or people in whom
the principle of freedom in things religious
bad made an abiding place. To contem
plate these benighted ages behind us causes
soul-sickness to every true man, and the
fact that this sad record, of intolerance
reaches back almost to the foundation of
the world brings up in deeper, blacker out
line its weary, despairing gloom.
Now," in each civilized land, spiritual lib
erty is not only conceded as a privilege,
but accorded as a right; while even in most
countries that we term heathen "-because
their ways are not our ways there
is practically the same unrestricted freedom
of worshio. The dars; night through which
the world has passed was not without its
uses. As progressive ages have rolled on,
cleansing man .with the fires of tribulation.
a purer; life has been generated, and the
fierce hates of sects and churches have
been forced gradually to fade away and
give place to a letter spirit of fellowship
and love. The world is beginning clearly
to see that the true religious spirit is that
of sympathy, of helpfulness, of active love.
It enters into the lives of other people, re
joices in their jy, sorrows with their sor
row, and forgets itself in them. It cannot
be shut up within the bosom of aoy sect
In a dry-ccttar ft" .iy n itiuaie
the flame once started sweeps over every
thing. Our whole life is traicmg for this
spirit, and if we rightly accept human ex
perience, we constantly rise from its lower
to its higher stages. When all the family
of man shall have fully entered into this
spirit, then will the sublime prayer be ful
filled, that they all may be one."

It has been commonly asserted by carpet
baggers that negroes were victims of intim
idation and often dared "not Vote. But' it
is proven that the rascal Spencer, the negro-
chosen. Senator from Alabama, absolutely
used the army of the United States, in many
districts of Alabama, to repel, .whites and
attract negroes to the voting places. It
was the whites who were intimidated while
Spencer was organizing the Legislature to
lift himself into the Senate of the United
States. But there is a mSre terrible and
all-pervading system of Intimidation by
which d ecent pple of all classes, 'every-
wheje in the Suuth, are driven from the
voting places. There is here an inherent,
indestructible prejudice of race, never .felt
while the two peoples are remote from one
another. When white men are impelled, by
a sense of patriotic duty, to attend at vot
ing places, they ever manifest natural and
violent aversion to thia absolute "chemical
admixture" with blacks. No one objects
to perfect equality before the law., .The
same measure of justice should be meted
out alike to whites and blacks in civil and
criminal courts, but there is a degree of
personal and social equality in this business
of voting, especially as conducted in towns
and cities, against which every prejudice
and instinct of person and race revolts. It
is not intimidation, but self-respect and in:
nate pride affecting the 'conduct of whites,
that gives limitless potency to the African
as a voter. The business j of t voting is
deemed by many' 'disreputable. .We con
stantly hear white people" say theywjll
never vote again. The blessed amendments
have degraded the privilege Of suffrage.! n
the eyes of decency. It Is. contemned and
despised. Stupid negroesj tin the contrary,
have been taught that 'the privilege
of voting is of inestimable value,' and that
the -duratfon'of negro freedom" depends
upon its vigorous exercise. Sambo relig
iously believes this, and votes often and
compactly with his race, and as the leaders
of his party direct. By. this state of facts
the whites, wherever negroes abound, are
ever subjected by the innocent blacks to
the domination of matchless rascals like this
remorseless scoundrel and adventurer,- 8 pen
cer. But we on!y proposed to say that
whites have been often repelled, as shown
by the testimony in Spencer's case from
voting places in the South by force and by
bayonets; the negroes, never. Hardships
endured by blacks, and burdens imposed
upon them by whites, axe mainly fictitious,
and "the heat and burden of the day"

We may talk as. eloquently and write as
charmingly of the, physical attractions of
Texas as if .inspired.; It is easy enough to
tell the world bow redundant are the riches
and boundless the resources and matchless
the grain crops of Texas, each bushel 'of
wheat weighing and worth twenty percent.
more than an equal quantity of the same
product of Eastern' "States."1 Strangers tra
versing boundless plains of living verdure
cannot be mistaken as to the productive
value of 'Texan 1 farms.' There is'nd
country, in all its incidents of soil and cli
mate, under the sun... Ihere u no
country whose very diversity of products
constitutes such perfect unity and begets
all the incidents of s mighty commonwealth.
The breezes that blow perennially from the
sea bear upon their wings ever refreshing
draughts, drunk by vegetation and exhaled
as the perfumes of matchless flowers in the
sweetest breath of spring time. There are
boundless forests of pine in eastern coun
ties, like those of the East, to supply hodses
and fences for the empire, and people of
western districts wilt be blest with every
accessary of comfort and civilization begot
ten of railways. One harbor after another
will be opened snd its channels deepened
and railwavs built, until live oak as well as
pine forests will enrich the people of Texas
as no people have been enriched from such
sources of incalculable riches. The native
home of the bison, succeeded by cattle ef
civilization as the .narrow gaage railway
supplants the mustang of the plains, be
comes the boundless pasture for countless
herds that feed the. nations. In fact, he
who. prophesies most wildly end extrava
gantly of the future magnificence of this
mighty commonwealth is the most rational
of thinkers and capable and accurate of
statesmen.

Pr.orLE.down South, especially Louia
lanians, had learned,' even before the war,
that golden syrups were golden delusions,
and that no description of molasses, except
that" drawn directly from sugar houses, is
cither safe or palatable. Golden syrups,
augar.drips, etc., are delusions and snares.
professor of chemistry has examined a
dozen varieties of syrups sold at the gro
ceries, and says that all of them are ".doc
tored,'' made by the 44 sulphuric acid pro
cess," as follows "A warm (131 degrees
Fah.) mixture of starch and water, of
about the consistency of cream, slowly
poured into a boiling solution of one per
cent sulphuric acid (oil of vitriol), the
whole lxul4 for some time then the acid
is neutralized by chalk, and the mixture set
aside. -When the sediment has settled in
the bottom the liquid is dipped off and
boiled down to a syrup. Thissyrup may be
boiled down ttrsngar-forming-what is
known as'graVieisngar or glucose." Instead
of starch, however, "old rags can be and are

culiar clay, found in Georgia, and shipped

clay is. almost perfectly soluble in water,
and therefore most frequenters of most

like the venerable father of floods."
The partial details of terrible incidents
of the earthquake come from Mar.caibo.
The city of San Jose de Cucata was utter
ly destroyed. The sun rose on the eigh
teenth ult. brightly and the day was beau
tiful. At 11 o'clock the terrific earthquake
shock struck the city. The whole popula
tion rushed into the streets, and the move
ment of the earth was like that of
the waves of the sea and continued
for fifteen seconds. Every house, almost,
Was leveled with the ground and thousands
of people were suffocated by an impalpable
dust that escaped from crevices in the
earth. All was over in two minutes, when
the wind boreaway the dust. The surviv
ors fled to the country and the fearful
day was followed by the Btill more
terrible night. 1 hey had moved to an
open plain where encampments were
established. Rain commenced to fall in
torrents, and amid the impenetrable dark
ness the groans of the wounded filled the
air, varied only by the sharp detona
tions proceeding from explosive materials
amid the ruins. The coming of the next
day showed that not more than 2000 re
mained alive. Beneath the fallen houses
lay from 8000 to 10,000 dead. The letter
also reports the destruction of other places,
as mentioned in the account of the earth
quake by way of Panama some .two weeks
ago.
Perhaps the grandest and most imposing
military and civic-pageant -ever seen in
America ?was witnessed by the countless
thousands of spectators in Boston, who
crowded every balcony, window, tree snd
standing place along a route of eight miles,
The Veteran Division, among the military,
Keras the object of universal attraction.
headed by the Ancient and Honorable Artil
lery, in whose ranks the first gentlemen in
Boston paraded as full privates; judges.
doctors, lawyers, mechanics and manufac
turers "the solid men' of Boston" Gen.
N. P. Banks in command." Old Virginia
and the Palmetto State were honored with
assignments in this trtme de la ereme divis
ion. The Artillery Blues, from Norfolk.
and the Washington Light Infantry, from
Charleston, were specially honored with
continuous rounds of cheers and compli
ments throughout the grand march. The
Eutaw flag, as soon as recognized, brought
tremendous outbursts of patriotic feeling.
Every man in the line wore palmetto cock
ades and bannerets of palmetto leaves in the
muzzles of their rifles. Col. Andrews and
Col. Simmons were with Governor Gaston,
on the grand stand, while the Southern
column passed in review. Their welcome
was hearty, snd was honored by the pres
ence of a large number of the most prom
inent and influential Boston merchants.

and only concession yet demanded at the
bands of Federal power. "No more appro
priations of public lands or of public credit,

credit that the Southern Pacific road may
bje built, the pious Ohio Democracy must
"rise up" and inveigh against "farther ap
propriations to rsilwsys" by Fedeiai power.
This may be very proper stage thunder In
Ohio, but will hardly have the "ring of the
true metal in Texas and the South.

GOVERNOR HUBBARD'S ADMINISTRA
TION. When Oovernor Hubbard took charge
of tha state government of Texan, there
were no startling situations, no brilliant
oaibilitie, or even probabilities, in it.
Nothing but the plain, plodding routine
of everyday, huuiJruui executive life,
with many little unpleasant circum
stances of reconstruction still remain
ing to perplex and annoy. .So far, we
maiuUin, Mr. Hubbard haa done well;
ha gotten aa much out of the situation
a u possible. He haa neglected no
important state interna, and in those
smaller things .upon which he has been
called to act, we 6nd traces of a self.
KMion ami decision of character
which promises good results, should hii
field ever be extended, or he called
uon to handle graver questions of
state policy.
While all this is true of his adminis
tration on general principles, yet in his
frontier policy we deem lover nor Hub
bard peculiarly strong. The clear
headed (talesman sees and. understands
plainly the value of our daily progress
in pushing our line of settlements
farther west, and of rapidly bringing
that rich section under the benign in
duences of law and order, and in this
policy Mr. Hubbard is very strong. He
take hold of the knotty (juration of
handling criminals, their pursuit and
puniohment in a determined and fear
leas way. There is no halting, no inde
cision in his course, and already it has
produced excellent results. II has,
under difficulties of no ordinary charac
ter, maintained and kept in the field.
and op to lU original hih standard of
effectiveness, our devoted baud of fron
tier troojw, which force, if it has but
few Indian seal) hanging in iu lodges
as trophies, has many jailors' certificates
f deposit, and receipts from the gallows
for many a debt svf lawlessness stoned
r and wiped out in the eood old
taic order. (Governor Hubbard is an
East Texas man, but no one ran find a
trace of sectional bias in all his public
acta which looks like favor to his own
Utrict of Texas. If anything that sec
tion complains that west and northwest
Texas has received more attention from
iin than they were entitled to. We
thiiik, however, that in this mat
ter the governor's every act will
bear the closest scrutiny, and
fur the reasons slauoed at above.
We have never been noted for a blind
devotion to Mr. 'Hubbard, but far be it
from us to withhold an honestly earned
plaudit from him or any one else. Gov.
ernor Hubbard has won legitimately
the best devotion of all honest Texans,
and for our part we mean to sny to, and
to urge it We see no service rendered
the frontier of Texas with indifference,
and we permit no man, lie he high or
low, to render her one without acknowl
edging it gratefully, as we bitterly re
sent every injury. We trust Oovernor
II. will hold his line until the end, be
cause in doing so be is rendering the
highest service to a brave, devoted anil

Bath, England., June 6. -While the
Bath and West of England societies
were occupying the Wideombe sus
pension bridge, celebrating Its cen
tenary, It fell, and a hundred persons
were precipitated thirty feet into the
river Avon. Twenty bodies have twen
recovered, but several are still missing.
Nearly two hundred people were on
the bridge when it fell.
Tbe bridge was wooden, of light con
struction, narrow and about three
hundred feet long, between thirty and
forty feet above the river, and resting
on posts morticed into atone work at
either end, and without a centre sup
port. Tbe bridge snapped in the
centre, and the end wrenched clear
from the sides. Tbe whole mass, with
tbe people on it, wo plunged into the
stream, which was about asven feet
deep. Beats from tba shore were im
mediately at work rescuing the drown
ing. Divers are searching for the
dead.

Tho overwhelming defeat of the ad
ministration In the lute Connecticut
election is attributed solely to the third
term question, by the administration
papers, and Is made the subject of a
long article iu the Indianapolis Jour
nal, the accredited organ of Senator
Morton. It allects to believe that but
for this incubus, the result would have
been different, weak us the stato ticket
was and, with profound respect for
the motives, diameter and military
and civic services of General Grant, is
conlideut thutu brilliant victory would
have been achieved had the party de
clared boldly and emphatically against
the third term, and nominated for
governor a man of pronounced opposi
tion thereto. In other words, this
usually astute political journal, lufer
eutiully, is confident that the action of
the administration in Louisiana and
Arkansas allairs, and the known policy
of hate of the leading republicans iu
both branches of congress toward the
south, have been endorsed in Connec
ticut, so fur as they were issues in the
canvass, by a pronounced voteof "si, (MX)
against 4-1,000, the number of votes re
ceived by Greene, Oie republican can
didate and third term partizan.
Such nonsensical twaddle, offered lor
the double purpose of screening the re
buke administered to the policy of
Senator Morton uud cleuriug the presi
dential field for its apostle of hate and
venom, is an insult to the intelligence
of the country. The third term ques
tion was hy no moans an issue hi the
canvass. It was forced in hy the op
position, not as an Issue for it could
be passed upon directly when forced
upon the people of Connecticut by the
radical party buj simply as a tantal
izing question to Mr. lilalue and other
republicans who were suspected of
anti-administration tendencies. The
great pertinent Issue and, outside of
trifling local questions, the only one iu
the canvass was emphasized by the
,-cpublican party in the nomina
tion of Mr. Greene, the r.eaious
the batttle was joined, and upon that
issue alone was it decided, and the
true American, both north and south,
rejoices that the verdict was in favor
of peace, reconciliation and good will.
While unalterably opposed to a third
U-rm, the intelligence of the country
will scout the idea that that question
was the cause of the defeat of the ad
ministration party in Connecticut in
tlie late election,

To the careful readers of the old
testament there is something inex
pressibly sweet in the term "patriarch."
It was applied to the heads of families
and tribes. The offspring of such fath
ers were wont to regard them with
reverence and an nflcctiou peculiar in
itself. Among the people of Mexico
and all Spuiiish America this custom
lias come down to the present day.
The writer, in the course of Prov
idence, spent the Christmas of 1SU5 at
the seaport town of Cluinipoton, in Yu
catan. The grandfather of his land
lord, u venerable man of ninety years,
early came to the house, aud for hours
sat to receive the salutations of his de
scendants and give them his blessing.
Five generations thus assembled, num
bering nearly two hundred souls. The
usual ceremony was substantially in
this wise;
"Jtevercd father, the head of our
house, I come to embrace you and usk
your blessing on this day, commemora
tive of the birth of our Lord and Savior,
Jesus Christ. Bless your child."
The venerable old man, placing his
hand on the head of the applicant,
would respond:
"Holy Father, Savior and Redeemer,
bless this my child make her dutiful
to her parents, loving to her brothers
and sisters, charitable to her neighbors,
fill her heart with holy piety, and
make her a blessing to the human
family in her day and generation."

isthe head of our house a faithful ser

ner of his bosom and the children

his people. Guide his footsteps while

God of HosU
Such scenes, deeply impressed on

sad but blissful emotions.
These reflections were recalled yes
terday by our attention being called to
the fact that there arent least two cases
in Dallas where four generations yet
live in the same family. Mr. John
Browning, Well known to most of our
citizens, is a veritable patriarch, who
has in this town aud vicinity, children,
grand children and great grand chil
dren. Yet he sits his horse like a man

Another is Dr. Paul Venable, recent
ly from Mecklenherg county, Virginia,
who also has children, grand children
and great grand children among us
but who, though feeble in body, is yet
instructive In conversation.
The late Judge John Thomas, of this
couuty, whose death was so recently
announced in these columns, was an
other such patriarch one amoug the
first pioneers to Dallas county hon
ored by his fellow-citizens with the
first county judgeship iu the organiza
tion of the county, twenty-nine years
ago.

not well, now and then, to pause and
give some little reflection to subjects of
this kind, and to recall the words of
holy writ? "Honor thy father and thy

Capt. John Cowdon, who waa born, reared,
nurtured and educated and is coeval with
the SIuEissippi, and whoso life has been
part and parcel cf the tempestuous current
of the majestic river, tells us that the

five cents per bu3hel from Chicago through
New York, to Liverpool the cost is forty
nine cents per bushel; from St. Louis to
NewPrleatvs and thence to Liverpool the

deepen the mouth of the Mississippi or not.
Hirsch's steamers draw only sixteen feet
and these can ascend the river as far as

and spring cast anchor in front of St?
Louis. If this be accomplished the whole
system of trade between the West and Eu

from Pittsburgh and Cincinnati and Louis

Orleans. Therefore the importance, as it

in the course of American trade, of which Mr.

Texas is not a listless spectator of commer

from the very river banks, imperial wealth

miles by countless railways, that Montreal

along the Mississippi rapidly go to decay.
They buy groceries and sell cotton, and

They produce absolutely nothing, and give

They are drained of everything by railways,

of trade in the valley of the Mississippi ag

CONSTITUTIONAL ICEFOB3IS.
It is to be taken for granted that a con
stitutional convention will be the' result of
the election on the first Monday in August.
The present State Constitution is almost
universally condemned, and it is a recog
nized fact that neither retrenchment nor re
form can be thoroughly Instituted until
there is a radical change in the organic law.
The last constitutional convention was held
at a time when the State was 'completely
delivered over into the hands of mercena
ries, and out of it came a creation which
only entangled all subsequent legislation in
labyrinth of extravagance. It is and
always has been, as we believe, the pur
pose of tho present administration to con
duct the government upon the most eco
nomical plans, but there are a thousand
stumbling blocks in the way, and they all
originate in requirements of the present
fundamental law. A system of extrava
gance has been established, 1 and at present
has to be maintained in spite of any dispo
sition to .tne contrary. There are any
amount of men, however,) always to be
found who become, no matter what their
political persuasion, advocates of multitu
dinous official creations. There, are lead
ing men in the Democratic party.
from any and all parts of the State, who
claim that the district judges have not been
increased to an unnecessary cumber, and
yet we have seen two Radical judgesCham-
ocr8 ana cooper, cut down eacn to Holding
courts in one county. The other counties
lately belonging to their districts have been
attached to others, and still no complaint
comes that any of the iudges of tho en
larged districts are unable to attend to the
duties imposed upon theni. Twenty-five
district judges can do the work now done
by thirty-five. This reduction alone would,
in the salaries of the judges and district
attorneys, reduce the expenses of the State
$17,000. The jury system,' too, can be
amended so as to save the people untold
sums of money. Not in the judiciary sys
tem alone could a most healthy retrench
ment bo instituted, but in all the other
branches of government in the departmen
tal service, in the legislative, in the reve
nue service, in the present unwieldy, inope
rative and expensive public school sys
tem, and all the minor departments;-where
State money is consumed, there can be
caused such a reduction in expenditures as
will speedily reduce taxation aad put the
government into a condition to pay as it
goes. It is believed that if the people
will stare to work in earnest on this line,
and elect delegates to the convention who
caabc trusted to secure the needud reforms,
Texas will be speedily redeemed from rav
ages consequent upon its present political
system. And it is to be hoped that the work
towards the consummation of this great du
ty will commence at once with a vigor. The
people, for a time at least, must wake np out
of that lethargy which has become the har
binger of a thousand political and social
evils. They owe present, artivily to them
selves and to those who cccia lifter them,
and unless they are true to this obligation,

CONNECTICUT.
The next State to hold an election is Con
necticut, on the first Monday of ApriL
What is claimed by the administration peo
ple to have been a success in New Hamp
shire has encouraged them to make an ef
fort to retrieve their fortunes In Connecti
cut. The traveling combination of orator
ical performers employed by them ia New
Hampshire, the Picayune says, have been
dispatched to Connecticut, where they are
to repeat their wonderful acts, and speak
their pieces to new audiences. At the com
ing election a full State ticket, a Legisla
ture and four members of Congress are to
be chosen. After these are elected, only
two more States will remain to complete the
Congressional elections California and
Mississippi. Of course there is no expecta
tion that the result in Connecticut can in
fluence the complexion of the House of
Representatives. That is so hopelessly lost
to the Republicans that a dozen States like
Connecticut could not remove its aspect.
Really the only object of the Republicans
in making a serious contest for Connecticut
is to affect the position of the party before
the country with reference to the coming
Presidential canvass. If there is to be any
chance for electing a Republican President
in 1S76, there must be a break in the series
of opposition successes." The advance of
the opposition army must be checked. Its
vanguard must be turned back, If only ia a
skirmish. New Hampshire did cot accom
plish this for them.', At the best for them
and the worst for the Democrats, it was a
drawn fight in a State which has been stead
ily Republican, except in 1871, and 1874,
from 1856 to the present time. i
It is noticeable, also, that ia Connecticut
the issues are much more sharply expressed
than in. New Hampshire. In the latter
State the Republicans took the precaution
to rid themselves, as far as they were able,
of the dead -weight of Grant's unpopu
larity bj pronouncing definitely against a
third term. In Connecticut, whilst noth
ing is said oh this point, j the Republican
convention;, ia plain and' unhesitating
terms,: approved the policy of Grant's ad
ministration, including even the particular
acts which have brought most discredit
upon it. If th'V Republicans carry Connec
ticut under such conditions as these it will
show that they have succeeded in turning
back the adverse tide, at least for a lime.
It must be remarkable, too, that although
Connecticut has not voted for a Democratic
candidate, for the Presidency since 1852,
yet in State elections for the last ten years
it has been quite as often Democratic as Re
publican.' Hence, a Republican victory in
Connecticut will have great significance,
and will lend fresh hopes and. renewed
vigor to the Republican conspirators for a
third term.

Judge Emmons, of the Fedeial circuit
bench, who announced last week that the
civil rights act was unconstitutional, is an
original, straight Republican, and, for all
that, aa honett, upright judge, of greater
ability than a majority of those filling
places upon the woolsack at Washington.
He believes that it is the exclusive province
of States to protect the civil and religious
rights of citizens the punishment of mur
der,' arson, or the obstructions to the right
of attending church, public schools, thea
tres, and forcing the way of being accom
modated in inns and common carriages in
the State, were not only matters not granted
to the general government, but, in the Con
stitution itself, expressly reserved to the
State. In response to aa inquiry made by
the United States grand jury, Judge Em
mons said
"You ask whether it is a crime for which
you have the right to find an indictment
that a negro has been denied the full and
equal enjoyment of accommodations, ad
vantages, facilities and privileges of thea
tres and inns of the State. Such denial is
not an offense over which Congress can give
this court jurisdiction. These are matters
which the State government alone controls.
Parties who think themselves aggrieved
can bring their civil action in this court at
once.. Any decision we may then make can
be reviewed by the Supreme Court,"

If the telegraphic dispatches, received in
this city last Saturday be trustworthy, the
war, at last between Mexico and Texas, has
already begun. Texas has borne quite
enough, and if the United States will cot,
Texas can guard her own confines and at
least repel armed invasion. Several ranches
in the vicinity of Corpus Christi have been
attacked and plundered- within the past
week by armed bands from Mexico. "Stores
have been rifled and houses plundered.
The robbers, in strong force, were within a
few miles of Corpus Christi and the citizens
of the place have been called to arms.
Robber bands also hold the roads deeding
into Brownsville. It is very strange that
Federal power does not intervene. -;If an

week after week there are lawless incursions

to be guarded and under the patronage of a
Governor of a Mexican State and despite
ceaseless robberies and murders of Texan
white men, "the best government the world
ever saw", is calm as a May morningas

At Antolue's Hall may be seen a
cabinet containing a number of rare
curiosities. First a small statue iu
bronze of the Goddess of Liberty of
the French Republio of 1793. It was
stolen from the Tuilleries duriug the
revolution of 18-13 by a soldier, who af
terwurd linked bis fortuues to those of
the gallant but unfortunate Maximil
ian iu Mexico. After the death of his
chief, be succeeded in reaching Texas,

timoou the froutier, engaging iu all

cident to a life on tho border. Then

HReenlli century, which, if they

of tho workmanship of those
days, show that tlio labors of
of their primeval ancestors huve been
but slightly Improved upon by their
progressive and eulighted descendants

thing of interest is the war dress of the
noted Indian chief, Spotted Tail,
which, together with the pipe of peace,
arrows, photographs of Spotted Tail
and other chiefs, go to make up a col
lection of most valuable curiosities.
The cabinet has been sent to Mr. An
toiue to bo rallied oil, who has placed

chances ut oue dollar each. Tnose
who desire to secure a rare curiosity
