Governor Coke has vetoed the In
ternational com promise bill. Tills will,
perhaps, surprise his friends who read
his message wherein he signified his
readiness to co-operate with the legis
'ature iu a fair and equitable adjust
ment of this vexatious question.
here was an evident disposition
hown in that document to settle the
matter honorably. The state looked
to the governor and the legislature to
preserve Its credit and do justice to the
nternatlonal company, his vein is,
to say the least of it, unfortunate for
both parties. That It will mili
tate against the credit of the state is
beyond all question. It leaves tne

state as they were aJt.tlie beginning
of the session, the mostof which lias
been consumed iu an elt'ort to arrive at
satisfactory solution of the contro
versy. The only tiling iuic 10 snow mu
result of the work of the legislature is

and exemption from taxation for
term of years, Is the best that he

His proposition to tlio company is a
miw-kprv in its nresent straits for

barrassments which threatens to pre
cipitate it Into the hands of a receiver
and virtually check railway communi
cation witli western Texas, or at leatt
leave the people of that section at the

Soon as It is licked into shape by a
failure to rr.ake such a compromise

fuuds and thus secure it Irom Its present
perilous condition.
Thr Hekalo does not 'seek to Im

entiously and deliberately, and done

the charter is unconstitutional, it gives
the Company IIO authority, at n liody
corporate, t3 treat with the state, ami
vet the legislature and the cvriir
9
both taeltly iwipiw t"e p.w.-r,
ferred by a charter, eott-i-nl"! si.
by negotiating or tr;li.nf ith lliei

If it is niicnnstiuitimial to loan the

en mt4on six hundred ai.d eighty thou

liiiniiulliitc v sii cab e or uotilil ne

such that the company cannot wait,
and to give It land and exempt It from

the state's credit, is mocking at its on-
lamlty and.givlng It a stone instead of
bread. 'Hie governor has railed to
make out a case, then, so far as the
constitutionality of the matter goes,
Tlui people understand fully the means
bywhlch thochnrtcr wasobtaincd. They
propose simply to wash this piece of
dirty linen at home and to secure rail
wav facilities ami oulet all claims
against tho state, preposterous as they

crtilltof the state, whoso word was

The refusal of the state to pay or settle
with this clamorous company has mili
tated against the credit of Texas, and
will continue, to do so until it Is ar
ranged. Their little bill may be a
fraud or not, but tho spectacle o a
company setting on Texas' doorsteps
every morning, asking, imploring and
threatening about "that little bill" is
scandalous, and Texas will do well to
fix up the matter us soon as possible
and let the importuning company go
about Its business. Thoy have wearied
the patience of the inmates of tho
house, uinl the whole neighborhood is
tired of the dm ami recrimination
which have made the very uir hideous.
The joinpromise bill is effective and
praclical; the governor's proposition
iiiipi acticableaud futile. The legislature
and the governor seem to have fully
realized thnt they had the company iu
a tight place, quite at their mercy.
The legislature drove a hard bargain,
but the governor drives a harder one,
from the fact that there is no imme
diate succor in it to the company with
out which it goes to the wall. It is
iieitheriu 'keeping with the honor or
dignity of a great and powerful state,
simply becimsc.it has the power to use
it by wringing hard terms tram any
individual or con. puny whom may
happen to have at a disadvantage. It

fair play.
Tho most pestiferous, and unfortu
nate feature about this veto is Its po
litical ellect. It will be used us a cam
paign document to bring about a divis
ion of the Kittle. Tlio people of west

ways built, in this way and arc yet
without railroad connection. They
have lost the immigration of years.
Property has depreciated and they
l.ui-o mil. lii-i-n able to keen up with
the rest of tho slate in progress. They
have not been treited J-utly, and this
veto is now I heir especial grievance.
It lias no place,' hbweve'l in the politics
of the state, and should not be used as
a handle tn'any party whip. It is sim
ply a question of public faith and slate
credit, and really should have no more
I bearing upon the question of division
than the defeat of any other bill. It
must be treated and settled upon sim
ply the basis of its merits, it is not
a KtilUcient cause for even sugg sting a
division of the state, which would not
settle the International muddle, nor
irlve west Texas railways ai.y sooner,
Besides it is a bad precede 't to estab
lish. The state would have to be di
vided up into stales about tbe size of
Hhoile Island, if tho cry of division Is
to be ralsetl eveiy time any particular
section does not get railway communi
cation us fast us t would like to have-
it.
There is yet "time fr the legislature
mid the governor -to settle, upon some
plan of adjustment Unit will t e hon
orable to the stntH, bein-llciiil to the
company uml sat isiatiory in toe iteopie,
which will not be.opeitiiuicuuatitu
tioutir! 'objection They have tosettle
It Hiiionir themselves, or else run the
cauiitlct 'of allowinc the state to 4 e

a court for the whole six millions. Of
the two horns of the delemmn; it is

It it needlesg to dear, however little tte
?outh recks of Mr. IkecLer. that he is the
leading representative of Chrifltianity North
of the Ohio. When he has f-llen, tbe
whole Northern Christian charcb is grsr
oualj ah&ken. II ii active enemies are tho
declared enemies of the Christian faithj
and the accusation sow made before tha
courts was first enunciated in a foal news
paper, deroted to freelove and atheism, and
edited by two women, one of these auid to
be the mistress of Til ton. The opinion
obtains that these women forced Tilton to
sacrifice his wife in order t destroy the
greatest living enemy to the progress of
immorality in the East. Whether this as
sumption be well founded remains to be
seen. Very certainly a man cf Beecher's
temper ardent, im passionate nat are, strong
emotions, and forcible violent action and ex
pressions, ever overwrought is not to be
judged by rales that would govern us in
making deductions from the sayings, hur
ried letters aod thoughtful acta of ordinary
aiugitfh men of sluggish nature and
intelligence. IIU modes of expres
sion, even when talking of trifles,
are stronger and infinitely more
significant than those of ordinary people,
and we arc not sure that he has not said in
finitely more, and confessed it with infi
nitely greater freedom, than if his lips had
been restrained by consciousness of that
degree of guilt which Tilton would estab
lish. But we only propose to say that we
cannot presuppose of Beecber that precise
line of conduct, or that tamecess and cool
ness in the face of a capital blunder, crimi
nal as he regarded it, which would have
made others silent or prudent. ITe was
neither one nor the other, and, fearing
nothing for himself, was ready to sacrifice
everything to save the wronged. His is
the noblest of characters, or that of the
sublimcst of mock-heroic villains. The
facta are against Mr: Beecher. Ilia temper
ament, and paosionatc, impulsive nature and
habitually strong modes of expressing him
self, have conspired to convict him.
Tilton'a position is unfortunate. In' destroy
ing those whom he hates, he destroys the
church, his wife and damns himself to ever
lasting hopeless infamy.". Moulton, too," is
immortal as "mutual, friend," and perhaps
infamous as immortal. Ilis aims may be
those of vengeance and his success may sig
nify nothing but the legal bctrajal of con
fidence, and the wordMIoulton" becomes
the sjnoBym of treason in private friend
ships. No conduct of Beecher, however
wrongful to Moulton, could justify acts that
affect Mrs. Tilton as ruinously as the great
preacher. He accompanies Tilton, hope
lessly to measureless depths of fathomless
infamy. Not the less true is it that these
two may accomplish the utter overthrow
f Mr. Beecher. Mcul ton has now given
the solcnTnity of an oath to his many asser
tions and to the authenticity of private let
ters and memoranda never intended to see
light, but produced by him on the trial.
These are declared on the one hand to be
conclusive proof of adultery; on the other
of nothing more than a magisterial inter
ference by a clergyman with a distracted
and warring family, which interference he
afterward keenly repented of, and in the ef
fort to repair tho fortunes of the bouse was
entrapped into a series of admissions which,
arranged together, seem to confess axsin
never dreamed of by Mr. Beecher.
THE BOLE OF TEXAS.
Tho crime of Louisianians is terrible.
No pen can paint, no tongue can tell, no
force or elaquence of genius exnggcrate it.
The people of Louisiana absolutely despise
Kellogg, and Grant i appointed Kellogg.
They hate Kellogg's Legislature, and Grant
elected it. They despise Packard, a doughty
villain, and Grant loves Tackard. They
proved Durell to bo a thief, and Grant hon
ored Durcll. They wept over woes in
flicted by thieves enriched by Durell's court,
and by his receiver in bankruptcy, who
stole a 'million or more, and Grant loved
theso thieves. The people despised Casey
and rejected his claims when Grant sought
to have, this delightful brother-in-law
spewed up from the stomach of Louisiana
into the Federal Senate. Louisiana did
vomit, and a pinchback negro, recking in
filth and moral rottenness, sits worthily be
side Morton, and Grant is grievously mad.
Then, too, theso wicked LouLiianians or
ganized a league to protect themselves as
far as possible against rogues and desperate
adventurers, clothed with office by imbruted
black, and ia maintaining order, when
Grant most wanted a pretext to destroy the
State, they have deeply, offended him.
Grant intends to protract his reign and that
of his party, and the South has not hith
erto failed to lurnish, when required, a pre
text for some foul amp of demagogism by
which tho Northern rabble was marshaled
ia solid phalanx, against theso helpless
States. Grant's friends and family and
place-holders in New Orleans have rjrown
enormously rich, and sincu this could have
been accomplished only by stealing, and
New Orleans tells it that Grant's puppets
are rogues, Graat again is in a towering
rag! so ''infernally mad" that he cannot
restrain himaclf, even in a formal messige
to Congress, and tho President of the
United States, if such a thing as Grant
can be such. President, absolutely speaks
falsely. Thero ia no other word for it, and
the apology is given ia facts wo recite in
his defence (T). Then the people of Louisi
ana hate thieve?, and these arc Grant's spe
cial friends, and thus Grant hates and will
cruih Louisiana. Black Leagues hare ex
isted since the first carpet bagger bt-gsn to
desolate tho South, and Grant owes all
local power wielded ia the Sou.a by his
adherents to "Black Lcsgues; but while
a white league would com pel. honesty ia
cHlco, and invest civilization with power,
Grant is more and ruore enraged and a caJ
mia and a fool becomes his reprcaectative
ia New Orlci3. The whole ccustry cca-

TUB TUIBD TkBJI.
Will Graat achieve the third term Ii
so, what ia hi programme? To whom can
he look for support, and where can he count
his frienda! In our opinion, his prospect
for re-election wai, from its incipier.cv,
small now it has "grown smaller, and
more beautifully less." If 1 e desired and
expected to be made President sgain, it
seems that reason and common- seme would
have prompted him to pursue a policy com
mending itself to the American people,
characterized neither by truculent subserv
iency to partr, nor cringiug obedience to
prejudice.
At one time it was thought the President's
policy towards 'the Southern States would
be mild andjioffservative. We were told
that bis feelings had taken a "kindly.drif."
towards the Southern people, and that he
desired to see them reinvested with all their
right and privileges. How Southern hope
beat high when it was announced that the
day of deliverance dawned, and that of
bondage was drawing to a close. Alas, for
the vanity of human wishes? The dream
was soon dispelled, and hopes turned to
ashes! A few short months had scarcely
rolled by,' when the country was startled by
the perpetration of the grossest outrage
ever done .a free people. A military
satrap, armed with instruction from his
chief, proceeds to the capital of a sovereign
State, and, with sacrilegeoua hand, tears
down the most sacred rights and tramples
under foot tbe few remaining liberties of a
most unfortunate people.
Waa this wisdom was tins' policy Does
the President, by his action in the Louisi
ana affair, expect to commend himself to
tho favorable consideration of tho Ameri
can people Have the lessons of the third
of November been forgotten t Has tbe
warning that was thundered forth on that
day passed from his mind and left him in
blissful forgettulness of the dangers that
environ him
Quern Deus rult jvrdere, prius dementlt,
"whom God would destroy He first makes
mad." The President is enraged. Flatter
ies of fortune, of wealth, of friends and
fools, have turned his bead. The sands of
his lueJi have nearly run out. Fickle for tune
is tired of the stupid, blundering favor
ite. When history come to contemplate
the height to which accident raised Grant,
sho will be lost in amazement, till, looking
down, she Bees the depth into which he
fell. It ia said that "every dog ha his
day." Grant's has been a long one, and
every sunbeam has been moulded into glit
tering coin. He can retire with a plethoric
purse, and the proud satisfaction of hav
ing established the precedent that the presi
dential office is "to let" twelve months
from next March, and he will retire, if at
all, to friendly shades that shelter the
despised of his countrymen. The places
that knew h!u once will know him no more
forever, and his fondest hope will have
faded like the baseless fabric of a vision.'

An approved correspondent of tha Phila
delphia Presn, writing from Jackson, Missis
sippi, saya:
"Land can be bought for a mcrej song; in
fact, hundreds of men offer to give away
land, provided sctlers will live on it a few
years, and surely there are few places where
a greater chance seem9 to exist for a poor
man than on the beautifully rolling lands of
Hinds county, witii exhaustlcss store- of
marl to enrich them.
Jf the writer refer to "poor" white men,
as thottt to bo blest in Mississippi, he is
sadly mistaken. Poor whites do not flour
ish in the midst of blacks. In .Mississippi
negroes are supreme and land ia therefore
valueless. Radicalism reigns and migratory
populations may love Radicalism and tho
negro very tenderly but the latter only at
vast distances. It is astonishing wnat a
curious suspension bridge k this thing called
Radicalism or uegrophily. The greater its
length the greater its strength and durabil
ity. The wider and deeper the chasm sep
arating the two races the diviner the
blinding luster of negro worth and
citizenship. To an ordinary, spec
tacled d own-east er, pedagogue preacher
r politician, who looks out through icicled
window panes away down into the remote
jungles of African government and civiliza
tion in tbe- South, Sambo "shines like a
rotten mackerel by moonlight.' But he
never draws nigh unto tbe negro. H keeps
him at arm' length that he may continue
to love him. No Yankee or other sane per
son invests cash or would have a farm or
borne in a negro-governed State, city or
county, and it ii well that the truth some
times leaks out, even through the pores of
the iVe, as when it tells that land is value
less xbere the priceless boon of negro suf
frage invests civilization with all the de
lights ef savagery, and converts governments
into simple machinery for the robbery of in
dustry, f

The llrst speaker was Judgo I. W.
Ferris, nf Waxuhachie, the nominee of
Ellis county, by the IdUioaster conven
tion, for the position of delegute to the
Constitutional Convention. The Judge
commenced his address by referring to
a remark he had beard, to the ell'ect
that it wus not proper to sejd lawyers
to the Convention, ami contending
thai It was wrong; that the lawyers were
the proper persons to frame and make
a constitution, being better and more
fully acquainted with the principles,
needs and wants of a constitution
Xe favored a chuuge lu the Judiciary
of the State, by the adoption
of tho old County Court sys
tem, with Its chief justice, who
should have Jurisdiction lu civil cases
to the iinioiint of oue thousand dollars,
and in crluilnul cases to ull grades of
oll'enses below felony. He would take
the constitution of 1845 as the basis of
the now organic luw, With proper mod
lllcutloiis und changes to suit the
changed condition of the State. He
favored an uiury law, fixing the legal
rate of Interest at eight percent per
annum, with a limit of twelve per cent
ou special contract. Ho fuvored econ
omy in all departments of the govern
ment und a reductlou of salaries of all
ollleers to the lowest living rates;
yet be would not place
them at such ruinously low rates as
would debar bouest and competent
men from striving to secure them. Ho
fuvored bieuuial sessions of the legis
lature, and a reduction of per diem to
five dollars, with a fair compensation
for mileage. He favored the wiping
out of tho immigration bureau and the
office of assistant attorney general
was in favor of a thorough aud liberal
public school system. Upon educa
tion, he said, depended the future
greutness, happiness aud prosperity of
the people; without education, we
could do nothing. The Judge spoke at
considerable length upei this subject,
favoring a liberal and comprehensive
system that, while a blessing and ad
vantage to tho state, should, by tigiu
and equitable taxation, be made to
bear lightly on the people. He
was opposed to the people
being taxed by tbe State, county or
municipalities for the purpose of sub
sidizing auy system of iuterual im
provements. He favored a six years
term of olllce for judges, aud four years
for all other State and couuty officers,
aud concluded by .declaring bis oppo
sition to the scheme of ousting tho
present officials; tbey had been elected
by the people for a certain length of
time, and he, for oue, was not in favor
of depriving them of that to wbloU
tbey were justly entitled.
Judge Ferris was followed by Major
John Henry Brown, who, in a ten or
fifteen minutes address, reiterated his
former ideas aud views of what the
constitution should he.
Major Brown was followed by Col
onel John C. McCoy, who also briefly
reiterated his former views, replying,
in auswer to au interogatory, that be
favored the ousting of tbe present
State aud county officers, and desired
a new election ordered all around.

Result or tbe Precinct Meetings Yes
terday. In uccordanc with the call issued by
Judge Hord, the residents of the
various voting precincts assembled aud
appointed delegates to tbe convention,
which is called for the 3d of July.
In the first precinct, Judge H. Field
was called to the chair, and Baruett
Gibbs and G. A. Cutler were appointed
secretaries.
Ou motion, the following delegates
were appointed:
Burnet Glbbs, E. O. Bower, Thorp An
drews, Major R. E. Burke, Dr. J. W.
Crowdus, Robert Cowart, A. H. Stea
gull, Charles Morris, J. L. Williams,
aud Thomas Itanahao.

ing resolution:
Resolved, That the voters of eleo
tiou Preciuct No. I, are unequivo
cally opposed to the nomination of
auy candidate from this couuty to rep
resent us lu the Constitutional Con
vention. Ou motion of Mr. Bookbout, the res

of several gentleman as to what ques

tabled, and the delegates go tbe Cou-
ventlon unmsirucieu,
At preciuct No, 7, General W. L.

a W. 8. Dunoan appointed secretary,

Sweetman, David W. Adams, C. H.
Jenkins, A. H. Shepbard and Mr.
Buckland. Tbe delegates were In
structed to vote against a nomination
at tbe Couuty convention.
In the sixth precinot, Colonel A. T
Obencbain was called to the chair, and
Mr. William Sella was elected secre
tary. Messrs. Fearn, Goldthwalto,
Obencbain, Garllugton, Wright and
Kean were appointed delegates, with
out Instructions.
At the fourth precinct, on Saturday,
Messrs. P. G. Swor, James H. Mat
thews, W. Harris, J. H. Coebran and
Dr. a H. Gilbert were elected dele
gates to the county convention. Tbey
were instructed to vote against a nomi
nation, but in the event one was made.
to cast the vote of tbe precinct for Mr.
Veal as first choice, and Major John
Henry Brown as second.

A substitute to a bill, entitled an act to
encourage manufacturing establishments,
and to exempt the structures, machinery
and mechanical appliances thereof trajn
taxation for ten years, was introduced into
the nouse yesterday, and gave rise to con
siderable discussion, some of the opponents
taking the ground that it was unconstitu
tional. But we think that those who take
this position are Dot sustained by the Con
stitution. It is true that section second of
the bill of rights is as follows: "All free
men, when they form a social compact have
equal rights; and no man, or set of men,
is entitled to exclusive separate public
emoluments or privileges. Now if there
was no other clause or section in
the Constitution, then it might be
well said that the bill was contrary to the
organic law. Bat there is another section
which makes the point perfectly plain.
Section nineteen of article twelve, is as fol
lows: "Tuxation shall be equal and uniform
throughout the State. AH property in the
State Khali be taxed in proportion to its
value, to be ascertained as directed by law,
except uch jrroperty at tvo-thirJ of loth
howtet of tin Ley'uthiture may think proper to
exetTtjft from taxation." Can any proposition
be clearer? It absolutely and positively set
tles the question, as to the power of the
Legislature to.exempt property from taxa
tion. As to the propriety of exemp
ting the structures, machinery and
mechanical appliances, we will next con
sider. It is admitted by all who have
examined into the benefits resulting to a
State from the production of the varied
fabrics and machinery, etc., that manufac
tories are very desirable and profitable and
that it is an act of wisdom' to encourage
them that it is sound political economy to
build up every material pursuit by which
wealth and power are increased in the State
that in proportion to the increased taxable
property, by the same ?atio the taxes are
decreased to each tnembor of the common
wealth. Consequently, every law which
encoifrAges capitalists to bring their means
into the State for permanent investment is
a blessing to the people. But those who
oppose the law take the position that the
exemption from taxation prevents the
increase of revenue, and confers unequal
privileges, which are antagonistic tb the
well being of the citizen and prosperity of
the State contrary to sound Democratic
principles and subversive of the rights of
all. The conclusion would be logical and
unanswerable, if the exemption were per:
manent. But it is only for a penod of ten
years. It takes time for large manufacturing
establishments to get under full working
order, and to begin to get back some of the
money expended in fitting up suitable
structures and machinery! At first the in
come is not. equal to the expenditures.
Large sums are locked up, as it were, in the
buildings, looms, boilers, etc., in suchfac'
tones as produce cotton and woollen fabrics
and in a quantity of tools, machinery, etc., in
those which produce all kinds, ot vehicles,
furniture, etc,
If then they are compelled to pay taxes on
all of these, before there is any assurance
that the dividends will pay,' it would in new
States like Texas, amount almost to prohi
bition: For it is well known that capital
ia sensitive, easily alarmed, easily driven
off.' When, however, a spirit of liberality
is manifested, and capitalists are thus in
vited to come in among us and build fac
tones of all kinds, they are induced to
take hold, feeling that they can manage
their business without loss, even if the en
terprise should not prove a success. After
they get into successful operation, then the
tax gatherer collects an amount annually
which adds largely and permanently to the
revenues of the State. And not only are
manufactories built up, but a large amount
of money U expended in our midst.
We append the fallowing figures which
the Atlanta Conttituiion vouches for as hav
ing come from a source perfectly reliable

will build and furnish a factory of two
hundred and fifty looms. Such a factory
would weave 800,000 yards 4-4 sheeting a
week, fit twelve and a half cents a yard,
tnis amounts to $37,000.

REMOVAL OP JI IMIKS 4V ADDKKSS
11 V l.HPKiCU.MIs.T.
The removal of judges of the district
courts, on the address of two-thirds of the
members elected to each House of the Legis
lature, has iven rise to very erroneous
opinions. 1 here is a very great difference
between the removal by address and im
peachment. For a better illustration, it
may be said that the judges may be re
moved by address for a misdemeanor, but
by impeachment, only for a crime. Ia the
one of address, the Constitution holds that
they may be removed "for incompetency,
neglect of duty, or other .reasonable'causes,
which are not sufficient grounds for im
peachment." It will !e seen that no crimi
nal intent is necessary to be proved in the
removal by address. A rnan might be per
fectly honest, upright and honorable in all
his dealings and associations; he might Iks
a model citizen, a good neighbor, a devoted
and affectionate father, loved and respected
by all, and yet, might, if a judge, be sub
ject to rqraovul by address. And why?
Because, he might be incompetent, ne
might know but little of the civil andcrim
inal laws; might not be able to comprehend
the difference between murder and homi
cidc,. between positive and circumstantial
evidence;' between an action of ejectment
and unlawful detainer; between the chal
lenge of a juror for cause and peremptorily
between an action of assumpsit and a writ
of ad qvod damnVm. And, yet, such igno
rance wiuld be no reflection upon his
honesty, or disposition to rcnlor to every
man his just dues. But, on the question of
address, would be sufficient cause for re
moval. Again, being all that the most
scrupulous couhl require in hid daily walk
and conversation, he might for good cause
neglect his duties as a judge. Let us sup
pose that he was wealthy and very charita
ble, and that for the amelioration of the
condition of some unfortunate beings who
were suffering from disease, or hunger, or
any other cause, he should neglect to hold
his courts at the time required by law, and
instead, leave his home with his pockets
filled with money to be distributed for their
benefit. Tot he would be liable to removal
on address for neglect of duty. Could any
one say that he was a bad man Such
charge would be repelled as monstrous, ne
would be called, and wduld be, a philanthro
pist. But while he was doing a very praise
worthy deed, the wheels of justice would
be clogged, and important legal questions,
involving the rights of others, would remain
unsolved and, perhaps, parties charged
with grave crimes and misdemeanors cquld
not bo brought to justice. Again, by his
mild and charitable nature, and on account
of sympathy for the family of some crimi
nal, he might exercise undue forbearance,
and fail to have the stern mandates of the
law enforced." Under our Constitution such
forbearance and leniency would be cause
for removal by address. But Such removal
does not deprive him of any right or priv
ilege as a citizen of the State.
Now, let us examine into the causes of
and effects of removal by impeachment.
The House of Representatives act in the
capacity of our grand jury. The repre
sentatives draw up the bill of indict
ment, and present it to the Senate. The
Senators while sitting as a court of im
peachment shall be upon oath. Mark the
difference. In the trial on address, the
members of the two nouses are not put
upon their oaths. The question then is sim
ply presented for them to determine wheth
er or not the judge is suitable and qualified
to discharge his duties. But when on trial
by impeachment, the case becomes a very
serious one, and may work immense damage
to the accused for upon judgment, he is
disqualified from holding any office of honor,
trust or profit, under this Stte, 1 and shall
be subject to indictment, trial' and punish
ment, according to law. In other words.
judgment against him orthe indictment of
the nouse of Representatives disqualifies
him from holding office afterwards in the
State; and after he leaves the Senate Cham
ber the grand jurors of the different coun
ties in which the offense were committed,
may indict him, and ho may be tried, con
victed, and even sent to the penitentiary.
Thus it will be seen that the proceedings
in the two cases, and the results are totally
dissimilar. A nian might be tried on im
peachment and acquitted, and yet be one
of the most fit subjects in the State for re
moval by address. We do not propose to
discuss the propriety of proceeding against
a judge on address, after he has been ac
quitted on impeachment. It would be well
for the dignity of the State, and economy
of the finances, to look well to the charges
preferred against the judges before pro
ceeding. It is customary with lawyers to
examine the witnesses in their cases before
going to trial, so tliat they may know how
to proceed.
Without rellecticg upon the Legislature,
would it not be well before proceeding
against a judge in either of the modes, to
examine the principal .witnesses and ascer
tain the character of his offenditg Much
is involved, and thorough examination
iho'ild be instituted before commencing
proceedings.

times very tuffi

LATION TO LOriSIAMA.
UI
Concurrent resolutions have been intro
duced into the I' gislature expressing strong
sympathy for t! unfortunate and distressed
citizens of Lou L iana, praying Congress to
grant them a r publican government, and
instructing our Senators and Represents
tives in Congrt.-j to "use every honorable
effort in aiding tie intelligent tax paying
citizens of Louisuna, in the Congress, to ob
tain for the in seizes a republican form of
government and freedom from the ruinous
taxation imposed by the usurping, so-called
Kellogg government."
If there ever wrs a time ia the history of
these Southern States when they should be
united in feeling purpose and interest it is
upon them now. Having passed through a
long and exhaisting war, with society
broken up, peperty destroyed, confi
dence lost, finance ruined and genera
destruction staring them in the face,
it was not surnrisinfr that wicked and un
principled mea look advantage of the situ
ation and t-srae in with their minions for
the purpncf devising means whereby well
organized rings of robbers might fasten
themselves upon the government, and under
the guise of officers, rob at pleasure. It
was a great day for them. They did not
hesitate "to kill the goose that laid the
golden egg." What mattered it with them
if the States did lose their credit So
they could keep up the semblance of labor
ing with a will in their interest, and, in the
mean time, fill their carpet-bags with the
spoils, it was a matter of perfect indiffer
ence what became of the well being of the
people. Deep scftemes were perfected
to wrest the offices from the hands
of those who were entitled to them
which were generally successful. The voice
of the people was totally disregarded. Men
who had been legally and constitutionally
elected were set aside with all the coolness
of veteran highwaymen. The States were
subjected to their will, and preyed upon as
wolves would upon the sheepteld.
In the case of Louisiana, tho Federal
power was called in to aid them. Kellogg,
with his minions, overturned the legal State
government and by the aid of Federal
courts and, Federal bayonets, republican
government was destroyed, and the offices
and property of the State placed wholly at
their mercy. Taxation and plundering have
become so intolerable, life and property so
insecure, that the citizens "through their
committee of seventy have appealed to the
United Spates Congress, praying for the ful
Ailment of the constitutional guaranty to.
her of a republican form "of government."
OBSCENE AND VCLGAB LANGUAGE.
"An act to punish the use of obscene and
vulgar language," has been introduced into
the Senate, and referred to the Judiciary
Committee. The committee, through their
chairman, Judge Ireland, very properly "re'
turned the same, and recommended its pas
sage." We. are glad that the attention of
the Legislature has been called to this out
rageous practice. It is high timo that par
ties who have neither respect for themselves
nor other people should be made to fear the
consequences of a violation of the rules of
decency and good order. It is the most dis
gusting habit which man can fall into. It
is a violation of the first laws of society; a
disregard of the feelings of others, which
ought- to be severely punished.. There are
certain creatures who call themselves men
who helieve that such conduct is evidence of
manhood, of a knowledge of the ways of
the world, and that people will believe
that they are fine fellows, very smart, and
worthy of great consideration. But how
much mistaken I They are talked about as
the very dregs of society; as men who are
to be avoided as a moral leprosy. Fathers and
mothers would be shocked to know that
their sons kept their company. And well
they might be. Nothing but the most se
rious injury could result from such associa
tion. The" child would hear words which
would eat into his very soul, and perhaps
be instrumental in hardening his nature,
and preparing him to cut loose from all re
straints to plunge down into the depthsjof
infamy, where all social ties would be sev
ered, and be left to wander and drift away
from the presence of the good and pure, a
homeless vagrant, an outcast from society.
IRRIGATION IN EL PASO.
On the twenty-seventh inst., a bill to
encoirage the citizens of 1 Paso to irrigate
the valley of the Rio Grande and to grant
land to aid in the construction of canals for
such purposeswas introduced in the House
by Mr. Cardis of El Paso, 'and referred to
tho Co mmittee on Internal Improvements.
This imi provides mat any town or
association' of citizens, or municipality
shall, upon the completion of a ditch
of a general width of nine feet and an
average depth of at least four feet,
properly constructed for irrigation and put
in operation, be entitled to eight sections
of land to each niilo of such ditch so put in
operation. The bill also provides that the
water flowing in said described ditches
shall be free to all who will provide labor
to keep them clean and in repair. Such a
proposition as this came before the Thlr-
teentk Legislature, but was defeated be
cause El Paso had no one to represent its
interests. She had been sold to a set of
political knaves who worked more to its
detriment than, otherwise. The people of
that county now look for this justice to be
extended them by, their political friends
now in the Legislature, and we hope the bill
will pass in the shape they desire. They
ask it as an act of justice, having suffered
ittolcrable burdens and persecutions at the
bands of political vampires until the people
ire almost in a starving condition, with no
resources left. The country is rich in agri
cultural capacity, and all that is needed to
make it a paradise is an abundant supply of
water.

,The same paper gives nn account of a
terrible storm which, swept over that sec
tion on the sixteenth inst. The lightning
rod on Mr. Fletcher's house, one and a
halt miles east of town, .was struck, the
shock being so' severe as to completely par
alyze the whole famMy for a few minutes.
It is thought that the rod saved the house.
Mr. Keizer, three miles south of town, had
his barn struck by lightning and completely
consumed, notwithstanding the rain fell in
torrents. The burn contained some two
hundred bushels of corn, a considerable
amount of hay, a buggy and three or, four
head of good horses. Ills loss is estimated
at $1500. The lightning rod man will be
popular in that region hereafter, ashc de
serves to be.
CiiinuAutjA. We learn from the India
nola Bulletin, of the twenty-sixth instant,
that Messrs. Ileyck Bro., agents for the
Uhiuuahua Mexican trains, received on
Tuesday last a consignment of ninety
thousand Mexican silver dollars. This im
portant branch of our commerce is done en
tirely by wagons traveling several hundred
miles from Northern Mexico to nearest sea
port Indianola. 'The States of Chihuahua,
Coahuila, Nueva Leon and adjacent States,
are, by geographical position, naturally in
debted to Matagorda bay for their maritime
exports and importation-.. With railroad
facilities entended farther west, an im
mense trade would be built up between
Northern Mexico and this port.
Thk following special dispatch to the
Galveston Netr will throw a little more
light upoH the movements of certain disap
pointed Radicals, who desire to overthrow
the legitimate government of Texas that
they may be placed in power. The people
of the State should register their names, so
that in all time to come they may be rcmcm
bered:
Washington, March 24. Senator Morton
presented in' tbe Senate to-day a memorial
threo columns long, setting forth the recent
political history of X exas as viewed from a
Radical standpoint, and asking Congress to
interfere in overturning the present btate
government. The memorialists say
"We thought we had the right to ask for
assistance against this species of violence,
We have done so, but perhaps we did not
ask in the right way.
"There may have been some want of tor
mality in our appeal, or we may have been
forestalled by interested slanderers."
The memorial was siirned by Boulds
Baker, Sam W Johnson, Stanley Welch,
A. J. Hamilton, and about fifty others, and
was referred to the Committee on Priviter8s
and Elections.

There is great excitement in Massaehu
setts about the successor to Hon. Charles
Sumner. The difficulty seems to be to agree
u:vn some one who will be considered as
entirely worthy to fill his scat. It is even
rumored that Beast Butler is looking askant
at the seat, and revolving in his mind the
practicability of evolving some plan to foist
himself into the favorable notice of the
solid men and law makers of the Bay State,
so as to secure the coveted prize. Strange
things have happened. The spoons
man has been gradually working his way
up, to the alarm of a few, who still
have an idea that a public man roust not
tuve '"itching palms," and must demeau
himself like a gentleman... Could not Sim
mons give his friend Ben a little pushup
ward? Perhaps there' is an office or two,
of no particular use to anybody in particu
lar, which might bo thrown in to make mat
ters pleasant. An office sometimes does a
great deal to make men think; and then
they think so kindly if necessary, or so
fiercely, if somebody is to be injured. Dove
like innocence unselfish devotion!
The Louisville CvurierJvuriutl in an edi
torial upon the veto of Governor Kemper,
of Virginia, very pointedly says that
"those who burnt his effigy in Petersburg,
will yet regret their ebullition of passion
and will honort him for the courage and
manhood with which he has discharged an
exceedingly delicate and painful duty. He
has risen above the clamors around him to
the heights of a wise statesmanship. He
has done what is most difficult for any one
to do sacrificed personal feeling to public
duty and his vindication, will yet come, ns
it always comes to those who adhere unfal
tering to the dictates of principle.";
Read; the communication of "F. II." in
relation to the condition of the Indians
and what ought to be done, ne is a fluent
writer, and states some facts which might
be oflienefit to sentimental legislators who
are taught to look upon the Indians as a
brave, generous and much abu.-wd race.
Sentiment is lost upon them. If a few Con
gressmen, who now talk so' glibly about
then., could only be put down on thep1ain9
and had to run for life from a' few of the
noble red men, brandishing scalping knives
in their hands, it is more than probable
that they would go back and. take grounds
at once for placing positive and immediate
restrictions upon their movements.

under the ban of the Civilian and Mercury.

a back window on a rope ladder to fall into
her lover's arms, contrary to the wishes of
the 'old man.
Rise and explain, Colonel. If you haze
done wrong make a clean breast of it.
The communication signed "Taxpayers" is,
from the pen of a gentleman identified with
the interests of our city and desires harmony
between the city authorities and its citizens.
It is written for the purpose of inducing
discission on the part of the city council on
measures of Vital importance, affecting the
present and future prosperity of our young
and growing city. The question of finance
is a perplexing one, and no plan should be
adopted in haste. "Let us reason to
gether."

We regret to notice the action of the City
Council at its meeting of tho twenty-fourth.
AVe regret it, because so far from correcting
the existing evils complained of, thoy smw
fit to proceed further in tho same direction.
Wc hope this action will not Ih sustained,
but that wheu we can get a full council at
the board the same will bo corrected, ami
our finances, loth for the present an J in fu
ture, will be place! on a firm basis.
Three things are reported as occurring at
the meeting which attract attention.'
1. A petition f tho polico to have their
salaries raised, which was referred to the
Committee on tho Police. There was no
proposition to reduce tho police force nor to
reduce any expenditure wiiatcvor,
2. The City, Council agreed to Re'l
$'20,000 coupon bonds of the city bearing
ten per cent, interest and payable in twenty
years, to be negotiated at not dcs th.tn sev
nently Increasing the debt,' and calling for
au ultimate taxation of $(10,000 to pny those
bond and their interest.
3. Tliey created an addit itHml (Tac, mak
ing Forster Co. financial agents agreeing
to pay thutu one and one-half percent, upon
tho negotiation; by which means the
$20,000 lninds, exclusive of the expense of
nrintihff them, will oulv nav into the trea-
fury $14,700.
Then It sppears that for $14,700 to Ik
paid into the treasury now, we arc to be
taxed to tho extent of $00,000 within the
course of twenty years.
A proposition was offered and adoptod
that no work bo done or contracted for lilt
the city was able to pay. That was ft move
in the right direction. But how is tho city
to be ever able to pay for any work, unless
41 r t- 1
wie expense 01 iiiiiiniuining oincers ana
offices is reduced! We have already
shown that half of tho revenue goes that
way, and tliat by the charter it is imicratlvc
that half the revenue at least shall be appro
priated to the back debts, if such an amount
exists, and it appears that we have 6iieh a
debt to the amount of $l3,i00. So all the
revenue must now bo paid out,and the Coun
cil having reduced the tax to three-fourths
of ono percent., the sum to be raised under
the law will cover no more than to pay tho
debt now due and tho pay of the officers
and maintaining tho expenses attached to
their offices.
What is the consequence? Under tho
rule" adopted, that no work shall bo con
tracted for or dono unless the. money is at
hand, there will not be, from revenue alone,
a dollar to pay for any work whatever on
the streets or for improvements. Hence the
expedient is resorted to of borrowing money
again at high rates of interest and creating
an additional officer to procure the money.
For what purpose was our corporation
created? Was it for tho people, or for the
!cnefit of those who administer the uilairs?
If it was created for the people, they will
firfl rwnf.itpil wlipn thru non tln mnnnw tir.i.l
in taxes devoted to keeping up and repair
ins the streets erecting necessary bridges
ana puonc ouiiuings; wnen 11 is spent ior
the preservation of the city from tire to sujv
J 'II" 1
ply the city with water and light. These
arc the objects which the people of the city
want, but under the present auspices these
things are all to be postponed unless we can
borrow money at ruinous rates.
From the apnearanco of things, wo think
it was probably a mistake to reduce the tax
of ono per cent., and we know it was n mis
take to authorize borrowing more money;
for, practically, that ia done to continue,
our extravagant police, rents expenses and
salaries to officers.
If the police are not satisfied with the
pay they receive, let them resign; they are
not compelled to serve. Again, cut down
uic uuuiuvi vi cuijnvj vta iuuuw ui uwumi
all salaries where there are fees attached b
the office; curtail in stationery, printing,
etc., devote tho taxes to tlie payment of
the debts due, and appropriate the remain
der of the money to .the improvement of
tho city.
If this i3 done honestly and energetically,
we warrant that an amount of work will bo
done that has never been done before In this

city. 11 tnre-iounns 01 one per cent, ut

VOL. III.
T1IK LF.OISLATIRE.
The members of the Legislature are liarJ
at work and have already done a great deal.
Tliefe seems to be a very earnest desire to
do Uitir dutT. A exeat manr new bills
bare been introduced," and the old ones are
being taken up and acted opon as rapidly
as possible. In so large a body it is very
difficult, ordinarily, t get a majority of
the members to take tbe same view of a
bilL Hence it is that, oftentimes, bills of
importance are put off from day to day, on
accountof some slight difference of opinion.
In such cases the members generally are
blamed irhen thev should not be. The
whole machinery of legislation is, to some
extent, cumbrous, and success in disposing
of bilU is rather the result of good manage
ment. There are so manr wars for a
captious member, to block the way,
that parliamentary tactics, when used
by some skillful member, become a jvower
which is hard to overcome. Persons who
have never witnessed a contest over some
important bill which is opposed by able
members, cm have but little conception of
the power of a skilled debater. And when
there are irreconcilable differences between
the two houses, a conference committee is
appointed to bring alout harmony and con
cert of action. Tbe Legislature have
passed several important bills, the captions
of which have already been published, but
several more of great importance remain to
be acted upon. It is to be hoped that they
wjll not now be in too great a hurry to
get through and adjourn. It will not
be economy to do so. It always brings
confusion and disappointment. Better
take time and act coolly and deliberately,
even if some of the measures should
not be acted on at all. But there is
only one way for a Legislator to proceed to
discharge his duties well, and feel that he
can meet his constituents boldly and with-J
out the fear of censnre; and that is to first
find out what ought to be done, and march
right ahead to the best ot his ability, as
long as he can, until he shall accomplish
the work. Let no fears of being consid
ered extravagant or stubborn deter him
from doing his duty. In legislation as in
battle, he who is the coolest and most vigi-
ant gains the victory.- If a member suffers
limself to become excited by reports of in
activity, negligence, extravagance or incom
petency, he is not in a condition to examine
ills and determine what ought to be done.
Each day he should resolve to be calm and
watchful, ever ready to detect what is bad
and to advocate and uphold what is good.
A little experience in this way will give him
immense advantage and his experience will
enable him to successfully oppose such
measures as would be injurious to the pub
ic interest. Perfection and efficiency can
only be gained by close application, thor
ough examination and correct information.
f any measure should come up about which
a member is not informed, if it is one of
importance, he should lose no time in in
forming himself about every prominent fea
ture. And to this end, he ought to have it
postponed, until he can be informed and
ready to act advisedly upon it.. So much has
been said about adjournment that these gen
eral suggestions have been offered to show
the hecoBsity of avoiding haste and crude
egislation. Better take time, while here,
and legislate wisely, rather than hurry for.
fear of protracting the session too long.
he blunders made, through haste, are not
cured by adjournment.... And while we do
not advocate long sessions, ,it is evi
dent that it would be far better to
protract the session rather than neg-
cct any important measure. The peo
ple are far mora intelligent than many seem
willing to give them -credit for. "They can
be readily convinced of the necessity of re
maiuing, if any good is accomplished by so
oing. In a word, we hope that the mem
bers will go forward in the even tenor ot
their course and do what they feel con-
inccd ought to be done, and then adjourn
and go home to their constituents prepared
to give an account of their stewardship.

i:ologicai. srnvEV.
The communication of Prof. Buckley is
one of great interest and will well repay
perusal." Among the necessities of a State,
first and foremost, is that of a geological
survey. hen minerals exist in large quan
tities, according to their value will be the
wealth of the State; for, when it is satis
factorily ascertained that there are deposits
of the useful and precious metals, one or
both, means will lc used for developing
and using them. Hence it is, that a State
that abounds with minerals is wealthy, and
capitalists will always'furnish the money to
put up the necessary machinery, arid work
shops to bring them into market. But
before it is known that they do exist
in such quantities as will pay for
mining for them, but few persons
will run the risk of investing and forming
Companies for that purpose. So the first
thing to be done is to employ a competent
geologist to make a survey of the. whole
State and locate the various deposits and
give the necessary information about the
quantity, character and value of the miner
als. So soon as it is found that they do ex
ist in sufficient quantities to make them an
object to be sought out and brought into
use, so soon will there be parties ready to take
hold and work them to trie best advantage.
It can hardly be necessary to write anything
to convince our legislators of this fact. If
they are so ignorant as not to know of the
vast and speedy benefits which arise from
the development of rich mineral deposits, it
would be work, perhaps, thrown away to
try to convince them. And it. is not so ne
cessary to discover rich mines of gold and
silver as some would suppose.
The useful metals, such as iron, copper,
lead, platinum and quick silver, are sufficient
to add millions to the wealth of a State, if
they exist in large quantities. It is there
fore of great importance to find. out all
about them. It is not a waste of money to
pay a-geologist and assistants to examine
the "various localities of a State and find
out mineral deposits. On the contrary, as
a matter of economy, it is well to place one
in the field just so soon as can be done.
Soon the State gets back a hundred fold
more from increased taxes. Prof Buckley
truly writes that all of the States have
geologists in" the field, except a few. 1 See
how rich Pennsylvania has grown from her
iron and coat deposits. She is. a power in
the land. But how far will she bo behind
Texas, in a few years, if the proper work is
done to develop her mineral resources.
There is no doubt about it. And it would
be a burning shame if the Legislature should
not make the necessary appropriation to
carry out the survey In point of economy,
it would be a good move, for the money
would not be lost, but would soon come
back into the Treasury of the State doubled
and quadrupled.' Let us not then hear any
one say that it would cost the State too
much that the treasury is emptyv and that
no more appropriations will be made until
we get better off. Such logic is mere twad
dle, and is unworthy of repetition. The
man who came near drowning, and said he
would not go in bathing again' until he
learned how to swim, reasoned just as well
as the legislatbr who is opposed to such an
appropriation. Money must be paid, to get
money. Even the tax-gatherer is paid for
collecting the money. It is not asking
amiss to request that means be placed
at the disposal of Professor Buckley
so that he may go all over the' State, into
every nook and corner, and find out the de
posits of all the minerals, coal, etel By
so doing, we feel satisfied that manufacto-
ries, workshops and mills will be built in
many places where none now exist, or will
exist for years to come, 'without the infor
mation which he will place before the.
country. 1
IiAU useless to have such a vast empire
State if it is not developed. It will be no
better off than the. little State, which are
not larger than one ot our counties. Texas
can be made richer than New York,- with
all her commerce.

in State and co'-.ncil. Nctl.irg snort cf

a certain kingdom, where law and oruer,
founded in wisdom and executed and en
forced for the happiness and well be
ing of all, were galling to a minority
party, the ring leader (rings commenced
then and .there) feeling that his ambition
could not be tolerated, and knowing
that his hopes must be blasted so lon as
the ruler continued ta be beloved and
obeyed, determined to make war on
him, although be had made him all that be
was or could hope to be. Accordingly, an
attempt was made, by force, to take poiv
sionof the kingdom, so as to thrust outue
rightful owner. The work was sharp and
decisive, and he and his followers were cast
out and driven away into a strange place
where they were sorely amazed and afflicted.
But the good ones who stood by the right
ful owner, remained and lived in the en.
joyment of the most perfect happiness. In
gratitude will never gain friends and power,
Now just 6uch a spirit has become man
ifest with certain men. and is bound, if
indulged in, to bring confusion and dis
appointment upon their heads. It will
never do for any man or set of
men to attempt, in earnest, to divide
the State. lie or they will be doomed, and
had better leave beforehand. What, divide
this great State one destined to be the
mighty power in the land, to hold the bal
ance of power, to become the home of mil
lions, to make and unmake Presidents As it
is, the various sections can move on in har
mony and prosperity; for the productions
of each do not interfere with the others,
and they are all necessary to form one united
whole, great, progressive and happy. But
let the State be divided, and at once the
bright prospects of the near future will ba
blasted, and the lamentable picture of dis
cord and enmity between the new States
would be witnessed, which would re
tard the rapid growth of each. It
would takemany years to- Tecovef
from the prostration which it would
produce. Again, the burdens of increased
taxation would press heavily upon the citi
zens of each, for new public buildings
would have to be erected, such as a capitol,
buildings for the various asylums, Supreme
Court rooms, Comptroller's and Treasurer's
offices, ete. More money would have to be
raised by taxation for paying the salaries of
Governor, supreme judges, Secretary of
State, Comptroller,' Treasurer, Attorney
General, Commissioner of General Land
Office, district judges, attorneys, etc.,
which would amount to hundreds of thou
sands. Are the people ready to shoulder
such responsibilities, submit to such taxa
tion, endure such burdens just te satisfy a
few "soreheaded" hard heads, who care
not how much the masses suffer, so they
can revel in fat offices, drink their fine
wines, whiskies, braudies, etc., and live
ike lords. It would be difficult to estimate
all the damage which would be done. In
whatever light or aspect viewed the conclu
sion must be the same. "There would be
the advantages? None. Now we have a
government of the people's own choice, and
all the buildings absolutely necessary for
the various officers of State w ithout going
to the expense of building new ones. And
besides, the people recently elected Austin
as the permanent capital of the great Em
pire State by a large majority over all
others. By remaining together as a united
and homogeneous people, soon the present
burden of taxation will be lightened, and
very section can move on harmoniously,
free from the clogs and hinderances which
always attach to new States. As one State,
the different sections will be much sooner
developed, aa the money which would of
necessity be used for putting the machinery
of the new governments in motion could be
used in building manufactories, railroads,
machine shops, etc. In other words, the
taxes being less, the money thus saved in
the hands of the citizens would be applied
to works of internal improvement. It is
high time that this spirit of restlessness and
discontent should be at an end. Agitation
produces weakness and prevents the healthy
growth of the State. There is no
doubt about the future of Texas. The
capital has been permanently lo
cated at Austin; and it will be labor lost
to attempt to have it removed, or to divide
the State and require the people to build
new capitols and put new governments in
operation. And we would Say to those who
desire to tear the State in pieces that .they
can have their ambition for elevation satis
fied much more readily in a different role
and ppon a .different platform that their
talents will be sooner recognized and re-,
warded in some other pursuit. "We hope
that we will hear no more of the insane at
tempt to divide the State into several.
Let peace, order and unity prevail and let
the people of Texas look upon any man or
set of men who shall attempt to bring about
division, as an enemy opposed to the perma
nent interest and well being of Texas.
EXPANSION OF THE CFRBESCY.
The expansion of the currency is now at
tracting the most profound attention
all over the land. No question of
late has caused so much thought and
examination; but still there is the great
est diversity of opinions which cannot be
reconciled. The old financial problems
give no certain rule for the solution of a
question which equals in importance the
trading and productive capacity of the
whole country. And it is really curious to
note the advocates and opponents of the
measure. Instead of being settled by fixed
rules," the two parties, forand against, take
position according to the business in which
engaged. And here again an anomalous
condition is presented, not goverened by
the rules and customs which formerly pre
vailed with parties'engaged in the same oc
cupations. It lias leen noted that the
trading classes are gtnerally opposed to ex
pansion, holding that it would unsettle
prices and destroy or lessen trade. That
the nearer an approach ia made to a tpecie
basis the more steady and satisfactory will
trade be. While this is true, it U not the
same policy which was most generally
adopted in anU Itllura times; for then, as a
general rxile the trading classes advocated
a paper currency and "f.uAtlmen so that
they might the more readily and rapidly
draw men into s peculation, and thereby
11 their own pockets. And although
they generally fcaade money, ia too
ciany cases their victims were made to
suHer, tot cr.ly by the lo-s of credit, but
alio the loss cf property. Then the pro
ducicg cij5-ies advocated a specie circula
tion sad deplored a paper currency. As
evidence cf this, t.-e Southern States, which
were c;7'el to b .rAs and tirl-Ts, were
proJuccrs an.l c-pc-:-.l Jsptr as a circula
ting Medium. Rut now, all is cbiBged
and we ad the Southern and Western
States united ia demanding an expansion
or the currency, while the traders of New
York and Boston oppose any increase. The
New York representingthe trading
.and commercial classes of the North, loses
its dignity and, to some fiitent, powej, by
spasmodic appeals to the President to veto
the bill fixing the currency at 400, 000,000.

ishes. -iShreteixtrt Time.

connection with the old Stale, if it were
not under the full belief that their condi
tion will be very greatly improved. And
we have no doubt that it will be. Texas is
no longer cursed with carpet-bag govern-.
ment and ruin, financially, politically, so-.
cially and morally. .The. people are fiee
once more to choose their own officers, and
to keep house for themselves. The govern
ment at Washington at last concedes that a
State is sovereign, and that Congress cannot
interfere in her internal arrangements.
But poor Louisana was made to Buffer most
foully before this reserved right was fully
accorded and hence her citizens can better
their condition by uniting with Texas.
Their interests are identical and Louisiana
can very well afford to let these two coun
ties go, as it will only be in accordance with
tlie wishes of the people." Their annexation
will make the line between the two States
much straighter and more satisfactory.
Then if Texas can only get a small slip of
Southwestern Arkansas, our great State
will assume more magnificent proportions.
The members of our -Legislature will of
course do thoir part.

their localities. In regard to valuable min

least useful part of the knowledge imparttHl

maiuder. On the other hand, the disoovcrr

m-reases the wealth of the State, and also

hence all are interested in a geological sur
vey as a matter of economy.

STATE OMLICATIONS.
It ia uaJeretoo.1 that the Finance Commit
tee in the Senate is proposing to introduce
lull re viirins all State warrants, issued be
fore tbe fifteenth of January test, to be con
verted into bonds of the State, bearing eight
per cent, interest The effect of such a law
would be most unjust in its effects, and re
sult in the most serious injury to those who
have acted upon the faith of the State. To
prove this, let us consider what a State ob
lation is. Is it not to be viewed in just
O
the eame light as an individual contracw
Is not a warrant issued by the Comp
troller a solemn promise that the State will
pay its face value to the holder And
sheuld it not be the first duty of tho State
to provide means to pay all auch certificates
of indebtedness t Vie claim that the State
possesses no rightrto abridge the claims of
iu rightful creditors. Take, for instance,
the case of a clerk, who has receiyed war
rants for the last twelve months, and not
beins inclined to throw away the price of
his honest labor for sixty or seventy cents
on, tho' dollar recollect that he has bor
rowed money at exorbitant rates of interest,
hoping in the end to receive his money and
save at least a part of the depreciation upon
warrants. Then let us ask, was it not the
duty, and ia it not more particularly so now,
since we desire to prove to the world Jthe
justice of our cause, that money should be
placed in the treasury to meet these obliga
tions? Is this the most perfect measure
or justice, or. is it more proper to
say to the employee that he shall not
be paid as proposed, but that while money
is commanding two, three or lour per cent,
a mouth in the market, he shall put his
warrants into bonds, payable in ten or more
years' time, .with interest, in the mean time,
at only, eight per cent.. Such a proposi
tion might suit the capitalists of Wall
street, seeking investment for their surplus
funds, but not the poor, laborer for this
State. And if the State is to pay a per
cent.' upon the outstanding indebtedness,
why, in the name of justice, could it
not borrbw from these very capitalist
and thus pay tho debts owed to those who
nre. suffering in consequence of non-pay
tiieiit. Has the State a right even to force
its creditors to ncccpt payment in a differ
cut shape from that promised? And, out
Bide .of a few capitalists, who, that hold
State warrants, could afford, with safety to
themselves, to submit to a settlement in
bonds? "We repeat it it would bo a great
injustice, and more especially since such a
bill would be a respecter of persons. It
would at once create a difference in the
value of warrants issued before and after
the date named. For instance, take a war
rant dated the fourteenth, and another the
sixteenth of January, under the existence
of abondingbilL, and offer each of them for
sale in the market, what dealer- would put
money in the first for which he would be
compelled to wait ten years or more, and
receive eight per cent, per annum, when
he .could just as readily, by loaning
on the best of security, make twenty
nr thirtV. Tho warrant dated on the
sixteenth would have a ready market value,
and the one dated the .fourtcenlh. would
eally have a nominal one. The result then
would bo that tho original holder would
be forced to suffer a heavy loss, when it was
really the ctaty of the State to redeem its
solemn pledge at once in money. In the
name of justico we would say let the rich
and great Stato of Texas, with" its un
bounded resources, borrow money and pay
oH' its warrants, and not forco its em
ployees to suffer simply because they have
done work for it on a credit. The latter course
is just, and if this is not adopted, let
every ono who holds warrants keep them at
his own peril, and trust to chanco for relief.
The State, after its employees have suffered,
should .not drive them involuntarily into a
stato of bankruptcy. Let it remain option-
ary with individuals whether they will bond,
but do not force raany into such a course to
their utter ruin.
TOE PO URTEENT II LEGISLATfTR E
The rrescnt Legislature of Texas is re
garded, and we think, very justly, as the
best that has ever been assembled since the
orrmnization of the State government. In
o w
intelligence, earnest of purpose, industry,
and patriotism, it stands almost unequalled.
But in addition to these exalted recorn
mendations to popular favor, is its pure
moral tone.
It is a remarkable fact, worthy of com-
mendatiod, that all that character of reck
lessness and dissipation, which usually char.
acteri7.es legislative bodies, and 1 which
spreads its baneful influence upon society at
a State .capital, Cads a rebuke in the de
meanor of the chosen representatives of
tho. 'people.' Wo arc, proud of it. Every
Senator and Representative should feel it;
and we axo rejoiced that they recognize it.
Every man connected with the State gov
ernment seems to feel that he represents a
constituency to which he desires to carry
buck aa honorable, record, lie looks Jut
iumself alone aa an individual, and every
member regarding it in the sa.no light, a
rrfe:t result is attained.
Thj members of the Legislature meet in
the morning, and Becm devoted to duty and
thebusiness set lefore them. Committees are
prompt in their meetings, and unremitting
ia their duties. Certainly a more unselfish
Had devoted body of men never met to
gether, and wo sincerely trust that their la
bors will be 'as fruitful of good, results as
they intend.
That each men may differ, wo may rea
sonably expect, and perhaps on s?tvc ques
tions, but that there will bc any difference
which will lead to alliance or discord, we do
not. anticipate. There U work for all hon
cst patriotic, -earnest mcn for tho ensuing
four years, and wc sincerely hope all who
are connected with public affairs will bear
in mind that there is but one security for
good government,- and that ia ia dispassion
ate counsels, and unity of purpose.
Ia our present condition, it is like laying
down tho principles of a new government.

the path pointed out by honor, patriot-;
ism aad duty. No poIicyw men; no teai
poriiing; no fruitless, ignoble scheme of
expediency. No disgraceful lobbying, none
of the vile means employed by bribe", to de
bauch legislators, or betray ofHc-ril-?. All
these are banished, aDd we arc u-.v-kcUing
to a new life. Standing as a sentiac4 u the
watch tower, it gives us pleasure to bear
testimony to the honorable character and
bearing of the people's representatives. Vf e
congratulate them. Were it other ie, we
should not be slow to condemn their con
duct.
PARTY SPIKIT.
It is very well for the Galveston '.Km,
Houston 3fercury and kindred journals to
talk about what they term the independence
of the press, and to inveigh in vehement terms
anin.n jiarty spirit, and to deprecate tne
course of what are called partisan journals,
but they ought not, and surely the iatclli
geat men of the country will nut, tease to
remeniler the past and the present condi
tion of public affair3.'
If it were a mere" question .bet t en t.iq
"ins and outs' of office and officeholders,
then the course of these journals would be
proper; there would be a lofty spirit of pa
triotism exhibited in their apparent, self-
abnegation.
In all sincerity and candor, Lave these
cotemporaries forgotten the history of the
country for the last eight years, and partic
ularly for the past four If thoj haye, tho.
citizens of Texas, they may. -rest assured,;
hav not, and will never cease to remember
those ill-fated years as the.' darkest and

The story asptin pes tbrongh the jiress
that Gn. John II. Morgan was bctrajedbv
a Mr. Williams, a sister-in-law ot Jtijor
William of Uurgaa's aUfZ. Tbia lady,
afterwards diTorced, waa at the residence
i.f the mother-in-law, but left the bouse an
hour or two before Morgan's arrival and
dil uul kuow that be hail been in thai an
cient town till after he was killed. Morgan
was strongl attached to the Williams funn
ily, aud their residence was moaf, attractive
in all its incidents. Its walls within were
gctrniahed with all the appliance of taste
and art, and tbe mother of Major Williams
was an educated and admirable woman.
She was rich, too, and herself of famous
lineage, lier diseased husband was the
granaaon of tbe hero of Kings Mountain.;
Morgan was especially addicted to tbe good
tilings of this life. When he entered the
bouxe, tearing his command several miles
sway, the matronly lira. WiUiauia insisted
that neither ber sou nor the General should
remain, during the night; but Morgan
recked snore ot toe hot rolls, juicy steak,
ana Mocha coffee of the breakfast table
than of dangers of capture or death, and
the downy bed of the spacious apartment
ubovti.bUix fnvited him to deWyhtfaf re
pose, lie would not listen to the sug
gestions of Mrs. Willi tins or of Mis
N. X. Scott, a granddaughter of Hugh
L. White, then of the household. It
was Mibg Hcott who ran to Morgan's
room aud aroused him at sunrise next morn
ing with the announcement that the house
was s mounded by Federal soldiers. Mor
gan drew on his boots and pantaloons, and
ran down the stairs and out iato a vine
yard of two acres at the rear of the stately
old brick mansion. lie crossed this garden
and concealed hftnselt foi a time beneath a
cburcb built inside this garden by the Wil
liams family. Greenville was full of 'to
nes" so-called and Morgnn was discov
ered by one of these, and Federal soldiers
were directed to the spot. He came out,
and ran back across the garden towards the
residence, and was shot in the back while
crossing the garden by a soldier named
Moses Campbell, of Tennessee, who was on
the ouUi ie of the enclosure. Morgan fell,
lie was recognized, and his body taken by
tbe man who shot bim, aud placed across
tbe horse he rode, in front of the saddle,
and borne in triumph about tbe streets of
die village. It was said that Mor
gan was not yet dead when this
estiibition of the body, reekiug with
blood, wss made by those who slew
him. Tbe corpse was borne away towards
Gen. GiIImib's headquarters, eighteen miles
distant, and afterwards returned through
the intervention of Col. Jim Brownlow to
Mrs. William's residence, Gen. Gillem, in
this behalf, doing everything that became
a gentleman and soldier. He has stated,
iuanote heretofore published by the writer
of this, that he was advised of Morgan's
presence in Greenville by negroes and oth
ers, and that Mrs. Williams the daughter-in-law,
now of Little Rock, Arkansas bad
no agency whatever in procuring Morgan's
death. The story made her famous. She
never denied its truthfulness. Federal sol
diers soon after the event held all tbe towns
of East Tennessee, and Federal officers flat
tered the vain and attractive woman, in
vested with that degree of notoriety inci
dent to tbe supposed teat of traversing a
wild country eighteen miles to tell Gen.
Gillem that Morgan was in Greenville. She
did no such thing, and did not know that
Morgan was in Greenville till she heard of
his tragic death.
Major Williams, more fortunate than his
commander, escaped death and capture. In
stead of crossing the vineyard he concealed
himself beneath the straw that constituted
.the bed of a great Newfoundland dog that
slept under the at ps leading iuto tbe back
yard ana vineyard. i lie uog seeuieu to
comprehend the situation and rested quiet-
wlule the master rested Denina ana dc-
ntatii bim; but the position and odors aud
fleas were intolerable, and young Williams,
when he heard the huzzaing m tbe main
street, two hundred. yards distant, and when
silence reigued about the house, sought con
cealment iu a well in winch there was no
water. It was covered with logs and par
tially with stones aud earth.- lie had not
been there an hour till squads or soldiers
were searching for bim everywhere. He
heard their oaths and imprecations and oft
ix pressed amazement that he could have
disappeared so suddenly .and mysteri
ously. A soldier stood above the
well and said be was going to tire
his repeater into its depths; but an old
negrefte passing at the moment said to bim
that the well was more than five hundred
feet deep, and he might kill the devil if the
bullet w:nt to tha bottom. The soldier
was iuvitml to ahaie delicacies furnished by
l ho fat old qnren of the kitchen, and that
nkdit youuK Williams crawled out of the
well and was concealed by this faithful ne-
gresa oenettm me lioor oi uer caoin.
Greenville was evacuated at last by the free
booters, and Williams rejoined the headless
command of Morgan.
1 he writer has often stood on the spot
and plucked luscious fruit from the exotic
grapavine, enriched by tbe blood of the
Knllant Morgan. He has often heard, from
eye-witnesses of events narrated, the story
of Morgan's death. He haa slept in the
apartment and in tbe bed which Morgan
occupied, and, tbouKh several years have
elapsed, can lie mistaken by uo possibility.
in reierence to leading facta and lucideuts
ticre recited.
When wheat is selling $1.13 in Terrell
and at fifty cents about Comanche we don't
exactly see why representatives of Co
manche should not be always advocating
railway construction. And if public schools
and railways be slaughtered and the towns
of the whole State beggared by rabble suf
frage, and .divested of local independence
because the rabble is dreaded, why should
any citizen of any town vote for the
Constitution f Tha Convention dread-
lug and fearing to tax. the heads of
blacks and ot the nomadic rabble atill
tear to invest them with power to
tax towns and counties and the illustrious
lawmakers, cringing aud dodging, declare
that no debt sh-tll be created unless voted
for by the taxpayers. The kuavery of the
mob is confessed in this. It car vote early
and often and make elections disgraceful
and corrupt. It may pollute the ballot-box,
render the business of voting detestable in
tbe eyes of deceucy, revolting to patriotism,
and abhorred by every hooest gentleman
where the rabble reigns.' This hgfat to da
all this must4e conceded, and thus knaves
and thieves may still be invested with
office until honest men eschew offices as
disgraceful that were once honorable, slav
ing done all this, the Convention records a
confession ot the tact by declaring that
this same foul mob shall not vole when a
question involving t expenditure f
money or the creation cf a debt is con
templated. The ballot-box may be pol
luted by villains wao, the Convention says.
would steal and rob. When, by Toting, the
rabble may filch tax-pay ets money, the
roaring rabble shall not Tote, say tbe Rata
ga; bat when honest, decent peopte,
identified wittWhoee whom they must rep
resent, as well as tax, should be chosen to
rCie, then the Ruubsgw lift up their
horny hsnds and cll us that "the pcr
brave lellows ilt the country's battles,
and must be suffered, of course, to destroy
the country and rT.;e iu towns acd,p!un-
der its cotiLtie and reader wide liact
nninbabitable. Moreover, they are born
branded with the right" to vote iu this
peculiarly blessed land, and if we d con
fess on the face of the constitute n that
in money matters they are rogues, ia mat:
ters of morals and decency and. religion,
