Jackson was tbe defeat of the Statesman.
On tbe contrary, tbe election of Britton was
tbe disgraceful defeat of Democracy, and at
the same t'me it was the election of Mr.
Wheeler. One of the truest, stauncbest,
ablest Democrats of the capital was the pre
ferred candidate of the Statesman. He
was supported by two-thirds of the intelli
gent Democrats of Austin,, and his defeat
was due to the sale of the rabble made by
ex-Governor Davis to Wheeler's supporters
or Sunday .night, thanight that praeeaKr,,-1,, or satrapy and the black
tUo-day f tbe election. These facts were
seen and known and confessed, and nc one
denies them. They occurred even here in
the very presence of tbe State Convention,
wbirh, with sbamefacedness, is forced to
confess its folly when it so shapes the Slate's
organic law that the very offices of the
State capital are sold out and delivered over
according to contract, even as coolly and
nnblnshingly as were cattle yesterday at the
Fair Grounds.
What better illustration of theories persist
ently illustrated by the Statesman could
have been presented to tbe Ruttbagas
whose follies may make the new code of
organic law unworthy of the State and of
the men making this Constitution. Law
givers tremble in tbe presence of tbe frown
ing rabble and a newspaper published in
the State's commercial metropolis, unwor
thy of its mission and of the honorable po
sition it occupies in the world of letters,
diffuses the idea that the Statesman was
defeated iu the late Austin city elections,
and iu this the Netc is true to itself and
false to its readers. Why does it not tell
the whole truth and-nothing but the truth?
Why not say that the Statesman sought
to avert a partisan calamity, involved in
Britton'o triumph, but that a the venal
tnob intervened and set up masters over
the true people even of Austin. Galves
ton, by the same agencies, may be garroted,
and then we, too, may mock her calamity
and deride ber misfortunes.
We have so often shown, or sought to
show, that the only possible recourse for
statesmen, if they would perpetuate the
unity of Texas, consists in making local
freedom almost absolute, that this integral
idea of pure and true Democracy has not of
late been discussed by the Statesman.
Got. Coke, in his admirable message,
sought to avoid the evils of universal suf
frage by proposing to take from counties
and towns local sovereignty and invest it
in the legislature the Convention confesses
the necessity for the restriction of suffrage
by restricting it in special cases, and the
fundamental idea of Texan and of republi
can freedom is violated by the cowardice of
the Convention, which so dreads to restrict
suffrage in local elections that it absolutely
destroys the freedom of municipalities.. It
confesses the incapacity of tbe black and
yellow mob and of the white nomadic rab
ble for all tasks of government, and there
fore -eviscerates the soul of local govern
ment. It dares not purify the ballot box
and leave towns and counties to do with
their own as they please. Mankind expect
ed the loftiest possible developments of
moral courage, in tbe conduct of tbe
Texan Constitutional Convention; but man
kind finds the demagogue and politician
the same everywhere, and even now, when
we have an opportunity to perpetuate the
existence of Texas as an unit, by reconcil
ing antagonisms of remote districts by leav
ing each county and town free to pursue its
own aims of progress in its own way, moral
cowardice compels the Convention to strip
local governments of locnl freedom, and
Texas is staggered and then dissolved by
the blow. It is the ciime of the age, and
the folly of the century. No central gov
ernment, as this of Texas under this new
Constitution must, can enact local laws af
fecting all local, minute interests and live.
The strain of nerves and muscles in every
part of the system soon becomes intolera
ble and statehood dies of suffocation, and
unity of outraged and violated natural
diversities.
The Sn Abtonio J&rpres referring to the
Statesman's report that the people of
Mississippi are all degraded by agencies
made necessary to be employed in elections
because suffrage is universal, says
"Our people will learn that, aside from
the wrong of the thing, such equivocal vic
tories in the South turns the whole North
agtiinst them."
We do. not see why the whole North
should be turned gains' those Missis.-ippi-ans
who properly did not hesitate to resort
to all arts and acta necessary to overthrow
a system ot rovernmenx that was radically
and judically rotten and radically ruinous.
radically disgraceful and utterly intolera
ble. If the' necessity had supervened Ames
should have Ixen driven by force of arms
from the savagely ruled State.
If the starry correspondent of the Gal
veston Vhrutui AJtoottU who generally
succeeds, even when speaking, in writing
himself down an sa, in and oat ot the pul-
pit again re fen to the Statesman fn his
published letters, we may take occasion to
tell what we and others think of him. It
is the. peculiar province of the pulpit to
wag war npoa vice, and he may say what
he pleases about drunkenness and we wil'
concur with him but we do not propose lo
become preacher ot the gospel, or to in
vade the province of the pulpit, or to have
a fanatical idiot shape our cousre or con
demn it unjustly with impunity..'
The; PitUbnrg Kaput says that the ful
ore of the Constitutional Convention to
make payment ot a poll-tax, a prereqnuise
for voting, u a matter tbe people cacsot
and will not silent! y overlook. Talk about
republican governments as failures! How
can they be oiherwise, without some safe
guard tii to wo around the ballot box The
stability af uovernmeiits depend upon it,
and are worthies without it. Let the Con

death struggle, and so re
garded by the people. No lives were ta
ken',' no violence was done, no outrages per
petrated. If freedom from African domin
ation could be attained without resort to
violence its avoidance was eminently desir
abie but at any cost of "money and effort

won. It needless to disguise the fact that
Mississippian8 have been restrained for

mainly by considerations affecting adjacent
State". TheyJiayeendLured thaAm' w4
jinnissariea of the little adventurer whom
they loathe and hate, and whose

ties and towns. The cost of the State gov
ernment and of courts has been multiplied

the amount of property pays a sum five
times greater than when meu alone voted
who supported the government. Perhaps
Rutabagaism should make a note of these
facts and reflect that by its crimes one-third

The Navasota Tablet never compressed
more valuable truth in less space than when
it said that "Masonry is either soiaethingto
be practiced, or its teachings are valueless.
It teaches sublime truths it arrays ltseu in
gorgeous drapery it points grandly to tbe
beaut'.ful, the good aud the pure; it elevates
the evergreen of immortality it tells of
high duties and glorious hopes; it lifts the
veil of the future, and points to immortal
ity and a glorious resurrection and eternal
life; yet how few of these grand visions do
we practice. Can we hope longer to maic-
tam nur heritage, enjoy our birthrights,
uulcss we live Masonary, as well as teach lit
It is important that the minds-of the craft
be constantly called to tbe vital importance
of a more thorough investigation into the
character and qualifications of candidates.
You cannot take a candidate without he be
worthy and well qualified, and make bim a
Mason by conferring degrees. He must be
a man, Godlike, his vision undiinrned by
vice, his thoughts elevated, bis reason, by
bearing full sway, stamped with divine im
press, or degrees will never make him a
Mason. You may manipulate .bim, restrain
him for a time, but 'Truth is mighty, and
will prevail,' his true character will devel
op itself, and when too late you will find
that you have admitted either a pestiferous
meddler or useless drone. Where were
you tought to look first for the embodied
pinciples of Masonry t At the heart. Will
degrees make a Masan, and create what
does not exist in man? Never, never.
Principles, eternal principles,deeply planted
by the great I AM, can only make Masons.
Degrees muke members, Deity makes Ma
sons."

TUB SABBATH.
"Remember the Sabbath day to keep it
holy." Thi is tbe Divine injunction given
to man amid tbe fires and thunder of Sinai.
It is Jehovah's awful command to the wea..
aud erring of earth. Thi morn the S-ib-bath
bells are ringing throughout the Chris
tian world; the bells, the deep toned
chorcb bells, inviting the little children to
Sabbath school in every hamlef, village and
city throughout the land, where the word
of the merciful Father and the lore of tbe
Saviour ia taught. Inviting saint and sin
ner to those sanctuaries where tbe way of
salvation is made plain, and the glories of
futurity, to those who love God and obey
Ilia commandments, like beautiful visions
are nnvmled to fill the heart with rejoicing
and make glad the weary. How like Dead
sea fruits, tuft ted but to turn to ashes on the
lips, are the schemes, the baubles, the am
bitions and tbe poor, bitter pleasures of
this life, beside the crowns, the realms aud
the joys of eternity, that are the inherit
ance of "those who die in the Lord."
"Remember the Sabbath day to keep it
holy." Picnics, junketings, excursions,
and all the worldly things that make na for
get God and God's mercy, are infractions of
the letter and spirit of the law. The mere
attendance upon church services ia not all
that ia required. For those who go there
to see and be seen, the young men and the
old who go only because it ia respectable to
do so, the young women who go to show
their pretty ribbons, their lace handker
chiefs and twenty dollar bonnets, and to see
what that Sonhronia StuckuD has on. and
if Tabitha Gabbletongue wears that same
old blue silk, are in no wise benefited by
what tbe preacher says. The seed thit
is sown falls on atony places and barren
tpots, and it takes no root. The mind and
the heart are both unbent -fited. Tlie one be
comes tilled with thing of the earth earthy,
aud the other imbued with the vanity of
vanities bitterur.H, covetousuuas and un
charitableness. Such keeping of the Sab
bath day holy mitkea the angels weep in
sadness, while it tills the heart of the devil
with iflue, and niken him lling double
shuffles of delight. The sume bells that
ring out ho gladly each Sabbath morn, toll
in solemn notes above the funeral cortege.
"Remember the Sabbath day to keep it
holy." Ifouaton Telegraph.
We are affected even unto tears when
we remeuiler that the truly good man ho
wrote the above, and who with touching
puthos pleads w.th bis hearers to "Remein
ber the Sabbath day aud keep it holy," ha
never in the course of his whole life kept a
single Sabbath, nor refrained from its regu
lar weekly violation. How thta Apostolic
man can reconcile his precepts with bis
practice, we are unable to explain, but tbe
fact is as we have stated. While he is thuH
thrill'tngly eloquent npon the soul-destroying
effects of Sabbath-breading, he is and
boa always been a consistent, persistent
Sabbath-breaker. None of the uncircum
sized Gentiles, whether Phillistine, A male
kite, Moabite, Ammonite, Ilittite or Ilivite
has kept the law of the Sabbath less than be
The text of Scripture from which our
reverend brother of the Telegraph quotes is
commonly known as the fourth of the com
mands given at Mount Sinai to the Jews,
and it goes on to tell which day the Sab
bath day is, and hou it is to be kept holy.
As our saintly friund does not appear to be
very familiar with the Bible, we will repeat
the commandment,' to show how strangely
contradictory have been tbe teachings and
actions of this truly pious man Remem
ber the Sabbath day to keep it holy. Si
days shalt thou labor and do all thy work
but tho sevenh day is the Sabbath of the
Lord thy God in it thou shalt not do any
work, thou, nor thy son, nor thy daughter,
nor thy nifo-servsnt, nor thy maid servant,
nor thy cattle, nor the stranger that is with
in thy gates." Saturday is the particular
day assigned by this commandment tor
Sabbath observance, and the children of
Israel, to whom the command was given,
have always so observed it, and do to this
day, by resting from work on Saturday,
and holding their synagogue worship
There is one, and only one, weekly Sab
bath enjoined, described, or in the least
alludud to, in the whole Bible, whether He
brew or Christian the Saturday Sabbath.
No other day is bo designated; ne other
day can be the Bible Sabbath. Does the
pious oditor of the Telegraph keep Saturday
as a Sabbath t If not, then we grieve to
ssy that this truly good and venerable man
is a Sabbath-breaker, since he neglects the
very thing enjoined by that commandment,
the keeping of the seventh day, Saturday,
as a Sabbath. But the commandment re
quires something more. "Six days sbalt
thou labor and do all thy work." Thus this
eloquent moralist is doubly a violator of
the fourth commandment, neither resting
on Saturday, the seventh day, aa it enjoins,
nor working on Sunday, the first day,
which it equally enjoins. In the course of
a somewhat lengthened editorial career we
have seldom been called npon to chronicle
a more saddening illustration of human
frailty. This eminent and holy mat, actu
ally quotes to others, as binding, a law
which he himself openly disregards and
violatM iu all its parts. Let him, hence
forth, comprehend that neither Jesus, nor
any Apostle, nor any New Testament writer,
enjoins Sabbath-keeping, and that in the
Christian system there is no such thing as
Sabbath-breaking; for where no Sabbath is
to be observed there is no Sabbath to be
violated.
The high-toned Christian of the Telegraph
baa been so intensely engaged during the
last year in the unprofitable task of reading
the Statesman out of the Democratic party
that he baa become rather rusty in religious
matter, otherwise be might have learned
that ChritiniT- Jom vjx nrt a Ay
for religion; but it doe aotnethiug much
better; it requires that we apply religious
principle to the labor and rest and recrea
lion of every day. It teaches voluntary obe
dience, iu all the details of the business of
life, to w4iat is understood to be the will
of God. The Heavenly Fattier desires of us
daily obedieuce instead of weekly cere-
monies and professions. If the daily life
ia selfish, frivolous or vicious, a Sunday cer
emony added to it will not help the matter,
The thing needed la to rvform the daily
life, and to tpplj ourselves, trjr daj la the
week, to the work of reformation. The
Sabbatical ordinance of tbe Old Testament
were made for Jews, and for Jews only, and
to imit upon them now, to please God,
seenis t- a a 'much of blunder as the old
Jewish fashion of periodically setting hot
ore) and roast veal bw.xje him. The New
Testament leaves' 'he ue-s of Sunday just as
much to the choice of each person aa those
of Monday. Therefore, he who demands
or another special acta or rpeciat omlwiona
a the Christian duty of Sunday, of who re
bukes another for acts or omissions oa that
day which would be right on any other day.
makes of himself a one-horse Pope, and seta
m a claim of lordsbi p over God's heritage.
So fax M the customary observances of Sun
da are Rood, accordant with reason, suited
to promote me weii-
let them be '.inued on those ground.
VOL. V.
Let ua keep such of the Sunday customs as
are salutary leeaute they are salutary and
of this sort are rest, recreation, tbe social
meeting of relatives and friends, and public
as '"Mage for religious instruction. In
this way all classes can have benefit from
the common freedom, each obtaining tbe
particular solace ha requires, undisturbed
by the fact that other are differently em
ployed. Those who want ceremonies f
worship can assemble for that purpose in
tbe churches; those who want rebgious in
struction, can meet for that purpose those
who want simply rest, or social enjoy
ment, can stay at home; those who want
quiet recreation abroad can seek it. While
if any one chooses to spend that day in giv
ing help, physical, mental or spiritual, to
the many wbo are in need, he will have
made tbe best use of Sunday the
best use, .whether he shall have spent it
in sawing wood for the sick widow, or in
taking poor children out into tbe country
for recreation, or in explaining the love of
God to one wbo has been left in doubt and
darkness by tbe preaching of some theology
of the lachrymose, bilious and dismal sort,
such as from time to time gives spells of
spiritual colic to tbe godly man of the Tele
graph. 1
NORTH
AND SOSJTH SOCIAL CON
BMTIONS.
The New York Herald says that the
condition of affairs in the mining regior
ot Pennsylvania is deplorable. Murder
stalks red-banded through the couDtry, and
men are shot down in broad daylight by
the agents of a powerful secret association
that rules over tbe Uvea of all the inhabi
tants. Such a state of thinga is disgrace
lul to our civilization, and we are glad to
see that the frequency with which these
Crimea are committed is arousing a general
spirit of indignation among the law-respecting
portion ot the con, muni ty. If the peo
pie will only take the matter earnestly in
hand they can cure tbe evil."
With this little paragraph the Herald dis
poses of facts which, if they affected any
of the Gulf States, would give employment
to a corps of special correspondents. Grant's
intervention would be invoked and maitial
litw proclaimed. But those fellows knocked
on the head are only white men, and the
only irreparable calamity that can befall an
individual is to be born white. Lamar and
General Gordon fall in with the passion of
the hour and would pave the way to uni
versal blackuess or an universal haziness.
The Herald closes its brief but suggestive
article on the desperate condition of affairs
in wide districts of Pennsylvania with this
sentence:
Besides, this trouble among the miners
gives the speculators a chance to run up the
prices iaf fuel, and so beat heavily upon the
poor everywhere."
Plainly enough, if the murder of these
white fellows didn't augment the cost of
c ml, the Herald would nsver have adverted
to the subject. The very next editorial
paragraph of the Herald is significant of the
fact that however terrible and deplorable
the condition of affairs in Mississippi, soci
ety is in no bettsr condition in New, York.
The Herald says:
Murder in the slums of the city is be
coming so frequent that killing must soon
be looked on in the light of a slight offense.
It is certain that the rough element of our
population no longer look npon murder as
anything more than a venial sin against pub
lic morality. On the slightest provocation
the knife is brought into requisition, with
the most deadly results. Something must
be done by the courts to stop this wholesale,
covardly assassination."
The posthumous advantage arising from
generosity to newspaper men is well illus
trated in facts incident to Ralston's death.
Creditors of his bark and officers have sought
in vain to derogate from his worth and
blacken his name. Even George Alfred
Townsend does not forget "poor" Ralston's
generous, boundless hespitahty, and the
newspapers are building a monument to
Ralston more enduring and prouder far
than he dreamed of erecting even when he
proposed to beautify and enrich the coun
try and city he deemed his own and such as
was never reared save by mightiest mon-
arena. Ralston is one of the few debtors
who, dying, lives immortal in that he is
lauded as all others like him have been
cursed as infamous, and simply because the
press will not be silenced and continues to
bed lustre upon his deeds and virtues.
Most men expect to die, and they who
would live after death should not forget
the lesson taught by Ralston's deeds and
posthumous luck. Deal generously with
the newspapers is the one great lesson
taught by Ralston's life and death.
People are having a free fight on the
usury question. Wegive an excellent letter
on this subject this morning, and part of
ita excellence consists in its brevity. Words
mean something and are worth printing
when "Josh" slings them around. 4 Of
his philosophy others may speak. Another
correspondent, writing of this subject, says:
"The question is whether laws on the sub
ject should not be fised by the Legislature,
and whether the Constitutional Convention
can properly do more than say that the rate
of interest shall be regulated by law. Dur
ing the next twenty or fifty years the neces-
aitiea of trade and abundance and scarcity
of money may be greatly different at differ'
ent periods, and tbe .Legislature, it any
agency of power, changeful in purpose with
changeful necessities of the .country, should
rrgulate rates of interest." We propose to
lot every body talk th chooses on this QUes
tion if they will only write as briifly and
sensibly aa these two correspondents,
GEN. W. MOMGAN SHE SOLDIER
STATESMAN OP CUIO.

Gen. G. W, (Cumberland Gap) Morgan, the
truest Democrat of Ohio, sayt:
"Texas occupies a warm place in my- af
lections. I entered ber service as a private
soldier at aixteea. and held a captain's com
mission at eighteen. I was personally ac
quainted with ber leading soldiers and
statesmen, sad nave never Knows s nooier
body of men."
Very certainly the heroes of Texan history
never were lauded by a irenUemaa whose
own worth makes his assertions mors vain
able to tho fans of those whom o com
meads, and there would be a singular pro
priety In having Geni Morgan address the
veterasa of Texas, at their next convccMi m
at the Stats Fair la this pity. V J

if Grant's re-election require it there wiil

KOCTIIEBM STANDPOINT.
The condition of the Gulf States as af
fected by the poverty of the people, by
tbe impossibility of obtaining advances on
crops as in former years, and tbe universal
ity, of petty depredations committed; by
hungry negroe these are facta that be
get enmities and discontent, thus giving
origin to collisions that will be continuous
and irrepressible. It is "also, true, that in
counties and towns in which the negroes
are absolutely omnipotent the white people
are impatient of incurable poverty wrought
by thieves invested with office by the s'.u
pid blacks. In New England it is all very
well if the cx-planter'a estate is practically
confiicated by tbe ex-slave. It it quite
pleasing in New England that po.-erty is
made the law, and comfort and decency tbe
exception in wide negro-populated districts
of Mississippi, Alabama, Georgia and South
Carolina. But it so happens that the whiie
people of these impoverished counties, see
ing no hope for themselves, and that it is
quite impossible to make their own condi
tion worse, are more than willing to have
military masters substituted for thieves and
knaves placed in office by the blacks.
Therefore, the evils of which Senator Al
corn has been talking are perhaps incurable.
We do not know how tamely New Eogland
vrs would submit if tbe descendants of
the negroes they sold the South were
shipped back to Boston to supplant GaS
toua, Quincys and other mayors of the
ancient captital, or how well Boston would
be pleased if tbe very dregs of Southern
society were sent thither to shape the offi
cial political action of Africans in posses
sion of the Hub. We are much inclined to
think that Winthropa and Appletons and
Parkers and Lawrences would become ex
ceedingly "rebellious," and that Governor
Gaston would be forced to call on Grant for
assistance in the maintenance of 'order and
quiet even in the patriotic precincts of Fan
euil Hall. But it is ourtx that is gored
and not theirs of New England, and there
is no help for Africanized districts of the
South, save in tbe substitution of military
despotism for negro despotism, and the white
people prefer bayonets to blacks. There have
been disturbances begotten, solely for the
purpose, we doubt not, of precipitating the
President's intervention. State rights and
State freedom would signify nothing to
Bostonians if a negro rabble managed the
city, making property on Washington and
Tremont streets valueless and investing so-
iety iu all its aspects with such disgusting
features that strangers entering the city
turned away in horror. Would Boston
weep if Grant sent soldiers, commanded by
white men and by educated gentlemen, to
subvert the established black system in Bos
ton? Why does not Senator Alcorn present
the plain facts, and why should' Lainar and
Gordon go thundering vapid nonsense be
fore the million. Lamar has just reached
conclubions and asserted purposes for the
announcement of which, eight years ago,
Alcorn was proposed to be denied ad
mission to a Southern city, and now
Gordon and Lamar cling to the .vain
delusion that unity and peace would
be possible in Boston if Timbuctoo were
set up in absolute supremacy in tbe old
State House on Kins street. Mr. Attor
ney-General Pierrepont and Secretary Fish
need not go through the senseless forms of
consultation about it. There will and can
be, and should be, no peace in Boston. Let
the soldiers be sent forward at once. Let
the statehood of Massachusetts, sacred as it
is in the eyes of those who were taught from
childhood to revere memories that Peter
Parley embalmed in our hearts, wonderful
as were the deeds and w4sdom of the patri
ots of the venerable commonwealth let
them disappear that Timbuctoo may ravage
Boston and that savagery may supplant civ
ilization and that the bfack may become
partially white. We pity Boston but "hu-
manitarianism" demands the sacrifice and
the troops must move upon Boston.
We have stated the facts as they are,
save that "Boston" is substituted where the
word "Mississippi" should occur, and we
speak of that which we do know,, that how
ever terrible the calamity to other states,
the sooner Missi sippi ceases to exist and
becomes in fact and in form a satrapy gov
erned by military masters, in substitution
for negroes and carpet-baggers, the butter
for the hapless white people. They would
not bring down upon other communities
wo?s that befall themselves, but when one
star,whether Massachusetts or Mississippi,
is stricken from its place in the galaxy of
states, the reign of darkness has begun and
the splendor of the heavens is slowly but
surely dimmed forever. We see to-day the
beginning of the end.
President Walker, of the Alabama
Constitutional Convention, among other
things, advises the restriction of the powers
of bounty courts to create county indebted
ness.. The absolute necessities of counties
cannot be foreseen, and their- deprivation
of power is as nowise as the same conduct

TEXAS.
Judge John Q. Reagaa made a most sen
sible and thoughtful speech yesterday, as
admirable aa brief. It was literal truth as
every journalist of the country, worthy the
name, must confess. The distinguished
gentleman said that organs" had become
f little value and were little esteemed, and I
hethex aet ap to promote the aims ot a I
party or of s chqne or ring or any Individ- I
ual deserved little confidence. Tbe M-ple I
presume that these organs are paid for ser-
vices rendered, and declarations of policy
and opinion made by an organ are mere
copies of decrees issued by their masters,
or by the ring or clique which the organ
represents. Independent journalism does
not involve independence of yirty, but of
party leader. Parties we must have.
These must subsist while people
iffer in reference to the theory
of the American system of government.
Our theory is that of the original Jefferson-
ian Democracy; but inside the party, as
thus defined by this leading principle, the
Statesman is the organ of no man or set of
men in or out of Texas. Therefore the suc
cess of the Statesman. It is denounced
with the utmost bitterness by men who have
been accustomed to have "organs" do all
their dirty work and serve them with profit
less but unfaltering fidelity. But the peo
ple approve the very facts and incidents
and peculiarities of journalism which the
whippers-in of the party and officeholders
are apt to condemn. If the paper be true
to the principles and philosophy of govern
ment in which liemocracy consists, the
masses of the people ask no more. Beyond
this they would have fearless independence
and capacity and boldness to assail any
leader or condemn any act of personal sel
fishness or personal ambition,however great
the idol guilty of the crime or blunder.
The people never questioned the Democracy
of the Statesman because it disapproved
the policy of Judge Ireland. The circula
tion of the Statesman still grew apace;
but party leaders howled and their hired or
gans growled and we were formally extru
ded from the Democratic church by an
'organ." It was bmtum fultnen in the ears
of the people and independence won popu
lar confidence. Sensible, intelligent gen
tlemen, personally attached to Judge Ire
land and life-long Democrats differed from
us in opinion, and adhering to the Judge,
were pleased that a newspaper had the
courage to tell what it conceived to be the
truth in defiance of the clamor of placemen
and of organ-grinders. Judge Reagaa is
right. An "organ" doea not deserve popular
respect or confidence, and cannot secure it.
The fortunes of the Statesman in this city,
and throughout the State demonstrate this
fact. In truth Democratic newspapers in
Texas having that independence which has
marked our course, as we have earnestly
and honestly sought to shape it, are the
successful newspapers of Texas.. It would
perhaps be invidious to name them, but re
cent events have made them quite conspic
uous, and illustrate as well the truth of
Judge Reagan's declaration that "organs1
are undone, and their day is1 past.' The
people prefer the unbought, unrestrained,

ries in Texas that has never been known

uals. Should the Convention, fail to make

guish until it will be only1 a question of

banking. The farmer does not make more
than five, or six per cent, per annum

and a half per cent, per month is the

This demoralizes the f aimer when we ought
to encourage tbe farmintr interest. Ihe
price for the use of money should be lim
ited so that a farmer could borrow money
to invest in farming with some bope of be
ing rewarded for his labor. When this is
done there are numbers of enterprising men
in the country that will go back to their

lowing tbe collection of any debt by law,

per annum. Old and prosperous States
have such laws.- Ia Tennessee and Penn
sylvania six per cent, is the highest rate
that can be collected by law, and most of
the older States, with but a tew exceptions.
fa little higher) are tbe same. So long as
the moneyed man can lend his money at
tbe present price, just so long will the State
of Texas know nothing of manufacturing
establishments, otherwise there will re ac
tivity in Texas, land and real estate and
manufactures that has not been known
since the absence of a usury law. All
Christian nationa have fixed by Uw the rate

THE COST OP THE CONTENTION.
The spirit of economy and reform that
has abcady, and very properly, manif.: od
itself ia the Convention indicates that the
expense of forming a new constitution wQI
not be so great by far as has leen contem
plated. The saving in the cutting down
of the per diem of mem'oers and officers of
the Convention alone will be' alwut 30,-
000, allowing sixty days for the seaaioa.
Counting all the clerks, doorkeepers, pages,
etc, at ten members, and the entire body
therefore, as one hundred, the per diem of
the Convention amounts to $500 a day,
or 30,000 for sixty days. The printing,
stationery and incidental expenses will
in all probability not exceed $20,000, and
the mileage much less than 1 0,000, mak
ing, as a sum total for a sixty days' session,
less than (00,000. Still many enemies of a
Constitutional Convention prated about an
expense being entaile i upon tbe State, in
consequence of one, aggregating hundreds
of thousands of dollars. The people can
rest satisfied about this expenditure of
money, for agieatdeal more, prospectively,
is to be saved.

The Decatur Guard says "Iu the voice
of Texas and s new deal and clean sweep
as demanded from bottom to top." What's
the use when the same men would be re
elected f Tne only evil we fear Is that tho
rage for "reform" will be too violent. We
want capable men to hold ths offices of
Texas, and think the honest people willing
to pay a fair price for honesty and first rate
ability! Sam Houston lost money by serv
ing the State as Governor, and so has Gov.
Coke lost heavily, pecuniarily but fame
and honor make up deficiencies nnleoa
children be robbed when death comes to
greatness. Subordinatebflice-holders, how
ever, must have fair and adequate compen
sation for their toils. It costs more to live
here than on a farm, and siycly an honest
gentleman would not proffer a premium to
roguery and incompetency by proffering a
mean pittance, instead of a decent salary,
to fitness and honesty.
A private letter from ex-Gov. Stockdale
to Hon. E. D. Linn states that "if the
Convention doea not realise the hopes of its
friends it will be a great misfortune. I am
sure that .if the spirit which governs the
delegation from this district prevails, the
Constitution will be all that could be hoped.
So far as we are concerned, we tire not only
ready to adjust fairly its provisions in view
of other interests and notions th in our own,
but ws are willing to work faithfully and
all the time to make the instrument all it
should be. There are, no doubt, greater
expectations uwn the par, of some than can
be realized. Many seem to regard humaa
government as omnipotent, capable of cur
inc all evila. Of course, tbey will be dis
appointed. But as far aa conflicting views
and interests can be harmonized into an ex
pression of the general wisdom of the peo
ple, I think and hope and pray that our
work may prove successful."
When a journal, known not to be an
"organ" does speak kindly and approvingly
of an office-holder is worth something to
him; but the causeless, vapid, senseless,
ever-recurring laudation and horrible adula
tion of an "organ" is disgusting to decency
and common intelligence. Judge Reagan
bears a good head on his shoulders, and has
thought to some purpose and kept his eyes
open while toddling about through this
rugged world. Other party leaders shsuld
learn what he is prepared to teach, that a
good cause never needs nn "orgau" while
good newspapers exist.
Under technical rules of law governing
courts, the Georgia negroes charged with
"conspiracy," etc., have been acquitted.
The New York Herahr reporters evoked
rtnany confessions from many negroes, show
ing the designs of the worst blacks to have
been infamous, but the Herald now sayt
editorially that "there was no general in
tention to murder or commie violence."
This is no doubt true. The masses were
blind dupes of designing knaves, as in all
political acts of these good people.
Thb Comanche Chief reproduces and ap
proves a very laudatory sketch of the life
and deeds and virtues of Hon. James R.
Fleming, a member of the Constitutional
Convention. He was a Confederate soldier
at thirteen, and remained threugh the war
a follower of Gen. Forrest. Peace restored,
he became a lawyer, and stands foremost
among lawyers for thorough integrity and
sound, practical, good sense.
In Jasper county they confine jail-birds
all in one room that has no window. Men
are not entitled, when accused of crime, to
have access even to the air of haaven. Is
the State to suffer such things to bet
Should there not be a prison inspector f
Men are murdered outright by jailors of
Texas, and these jailors are helpless vic
tims of tbe thoughtless folly of those that
constructed these horrible denit.
There are counties densely populated
that support and need public schools.
Sparsely populated and new counties can
not. If those wbo paid, expended taxes,
tbe school question might te solved by
transferring the matter almost wholly to
the several counties. But they who dance
should pay the piper, and those who pay
should expend taxes, even in matters of
schools.
A PABAonApn is going through Texan
papers to the effect that tbe school, asylum
and university lands have been placed on
the market, and are now in process of tale
to aid settlement by actual settlers. In the
present condition of the money market, if
for no other fact, the sale of these uni
versity lands, if begun, should not go on.
In fact this land sbould not be sold at alL
The Democratic party is gaming strength
everywhere on the financial question. Even
in New York City bankers and merchants
are' changing their tack, and confess tbe
absolute impossibility of specie resumption
as provided by the present law. Every
where the prospect of a Democratic triumph
in tbe next presidential election grows
brighter.
Jndge Edward Dougherty ermtori!y re
moved the Brownsville clerk of the district
court, A. Lavicke, who voted for Ford,
Murphy andCardis, the independent ticket,
while the Judge was for the nominees. Is
this true! Such is the story that comes to
us, aad CoL Ford will be apt to inquire into
tbe facta.
The Corpus ChrUti GazetUitji tbst "tbe
result of tbe election in this district has not
been officially ascertained as yet, but enough
is known to. render certain tiie election 01
tbe entire independent ticket, by majorities
ranging from 500 to lowo.
says that s private letter from Col. Joba 8.
Ford states that he will leavs for the Aus
tin Convention by tbe next Morgan steamer.
"Our delegate, lion. Joha B. Murphy, will
not leave ontil bs receives his certificate of
election."
It isn't best for as editor to have cliques
and rings and placemen to control bim.
His position is judicial, aad he should
know, professionally, neither friends nor
enemies, sad have no entangling alliances.
4 Oca f: correspoBdeat, "SuUscriber,' is
mistaken. Tbe "conventional' usury law
prevails in Tennessee, and when no rate is
fixed by sgreementsix per omt. is collect
able. The Cleburne Chronicle complains of ths
intolerable burdens imposed npon tbe judge
of thai district. He occupies ths wool
sack ten months and travel two months
each year.
The lste overflow f the Mississippi cov
ered more conntry.Iinflaltely, oa paper than
oa land.
The Sothiterm Jtdet will, appear regu
larly daring ths session ofth Convention.

Everybody's business is nobo'ly'a busi
ness," and therefore Lalf dozen persons
organized and directed the celebmtiou of
the Centennial Fourth in Austin. Every
body attended, and everybody applauded
8eech8 and events and listened with p o
found interest when the Declaration of In
dependence was rea, ami while Judye
Bell pronounced a taatcful and patriotic:
oration instead of a lnstnrii-al recitation of
hic.il facts which the public expected at his
hands. But this profouuil pulilic interest
in event of the day anil the earnest devo
tion manifested and appluusc? so jjener'tmly
aAarded not only the reader but the ora
tor, both steadfast Union men through all
their lives, should surely convince our
Northtru fellow-countrymen that the peo
ple of the capital of Texas are not only de
voted to the Union and its hallowed nteiiio
riea but have absolutely forgotten, if they
ever existed, those unworthy, section
al and personal prejudice!) that spring
from the fact and are only forceful to
tue extent that the trus people ol
the North and South are ignorant of
each other's peculiarities. Very many peo
ple of the South have only seen one phase
of Northern character us developed in a
class that was pensioned off, for st.rvices to
Radicalism, in thr South, and came simply
to gather gold and return with it; but rail
ways and travel and the iutlux of people
from all the States and nations rapidly cor
rects errors of ignorance and of local preju
dice, and the people learn that honesty and
virtue, like knavery and crime, are not lim
ited by parallels of latitude or longitude,
but are of equal force, where grades of in
telligence are vqual, in all cliuieu and all
countries. Of those who sat through four
or rive hours iu the hall of the House and
listened intently and applauded earnestly
the patriotic assertions of Judge Bull and
Governor Hubbard, ninety-nine hundredths
were "rebels," and yet the vices of the
times in Texas are ascribed by Northe'n
partisanship to the dormant but excitable
spirit of secessionism that "still pervades
and makes a very volcano of the South."
Austin, Tuesday and Tuesday night, was
surely volcanic of Federal as of local pa"
triotism.
Perhaps the most pleasing incidunt of the
day, next after this double assurance of
fealty to the Union, and to unionism as de
veloped in those who led in the celebration,
was the social and moral t licet of the
splendid entertainment given by our G; ruiun
fellow cit'zens to the Legislature and to
citizens of the capital. The tiring of can
nons, the Centennial ball in which our
fathers and mothers of the olden time re
appeared, the wonderful exhibition of the
Turners, the patriotic speech of an eloquent
German barrister, the fireworks all these,
however effective, sink iuto insignificance
iu local Bocial results and in accomplishing
a defined purpose when contrasted with the
festival tendered by German tuste and Ger
m in generosity to lawgivers and people.
Five hundred invited guests sat at endless
tables burdeued with every conceivable
delicacy aud with every dish iu the prepara
tion of which German culinary art was ex
hausted, there wre sous ami toasts and
infinite good humor and rich wines from
Khineland vineyards and music aud fair
women, and Germans and Texans were an
undistinguishable mass and there was a de
gree of good fellowship enforced establish
ing a degree of oneness am ing the people of
the capital that must affect, fur long years to
come, through representative men and
women, the social conditions of the com
monwealth. There is nothing truer, how
ever we may dislike to confess it, than the
oft made assertion that the shortest route
lo a man's good will runs down his ttsoph
agus. Our German fellow-citizens recog
n zed the force of the apothegm at ti e
supper table, and theu captivated popular
fancy by graces aud delights and delightful
melodies of the splendid bull room. The
good accomplished on the by-gone anuiver
sniy by speeches and by the peculiar history
of men who uttered them, and by the tttste
and it ti lligeucc of Germany, developed in
rexas, will be surely fe't "through the
century thnt is oetore us, and when all
these years have fl. d the living of the sec
ond Centennial, reviving Mivmories of Tues
day, will have reason to bless the m u aud
women who love not Texas less became
their tongues have accents significant of
hallowed memories of Faderlaud.
a.tNT..
ANvAS DMIH-HIS
HIS AND 'ICKS.
VIH-
Santa Anna, who died a few days ago,
was born in Jalapa, about the year 171)8,
though he said, when cap'ured at Sin Ja
cinto, that he was then "48 years old, and
was leaving public life when most men ol
his fnine and triuin, hs were just entering
it. He theu suposed that his Career had
culminated, and that his sun had set. Forty
years have elapsi d. ami we m iy now do a
share of justice to the m mory of a in u:
whose name in Texas lias been the syno
nym of infamy. That he was a gr?at sol
dier, none deuy; that he wan faithless and
treacherous, posterity must ever confess.
When he went a pnsoucr to Washington,
accompanied by the late Gen. Bee, of tins
State, iu the winter of 18;lt5-7, wheuce he
was sent to Vera Ciux by Gen. Jackson, ou
a Federal ship of war, he ngreed with Gen.
B- lo bear half the cost of the journey
made ou hotsetutck and in stasre coaches.
At Washitifcton. when accounts were ad
justed between B e And the Mexicau Gen
eral, he gave the Texan a draft 01 his
bankers at Vera Cruz for MO. Tnis draft
was returned protested, aud paid by Gen.
Ua, and on it xi the endorsement ot
Snta Anna: "I recognize no obligation
to pay this "debt, since I was a prisoner at
the time it was created." Tne Republic of
Texas relieved Geo. Bee, but hardly forgot
the treason to asserted personal friendship
ol which the Mexican was guilty. Sxnta
Anna spent many recent year of hit life at
St Thomas, and British steamers plying
between that islaud and South American
State were accustomed to gratify the taste
and minuter to the pleasures of the old
master of Mexico by supplying him with
game cocks and with bevies of pretty
women with which he constantly stocked
hi aeraglio. Of private personal virtues he
knew nothing but be waa the ablest and
wit eat ruler, not even excepting Juarez,
that ever governed Mexico. lie moved
SO, 000 men without rest or food, eighty miles
to fight Gen. Taylor at Buena Vista. Hi
toldiera died of starvation when on the bat
tlefield atd the stomachs of the -slain
were wholly empty. Beaten and utterly
overwhelmed at Buena V'uta on the twenty-
second of February, he was again at the
head of a powerful army and fought a des
perate battle at CVrro Gordo on the eigh
teenth of April. The man seemed ubiqui
tous and ilmost omnipotent. Routed at
CVrro Gordo he reappeared with fresh
armies iu defense of his capital, and, what
ever may be said to tfce contrary, with
forces dial iplined, provisioned and equip
ped a were those of the United States,
(ieu. Scott's triumphs would hardly have
been as speedy ami brilliant and ruinous to
Mexico in history tells. In reference to the
uj is.-atru at the Alamo it is due to truth to
say that when Sauta Anna demanded a sur
render his dig of truce waa tiled on,
and if war have laws there is one which
justified the massacre as at Fort Pil
low. Stnta Anna ever denied that he
authorized the bu chery at Goliad. But
before he left his capital he had issued a
proclamation denouncing foreigners in the
armies of Texas aud ordering all these to
be put to death. He declared, however,
that if he had known that Fannin had made
terms of capitulation, of which tact Urrea
fulled to inform him, the massacre at Go
liad would never have occurred. Urrea
denied the innocence in this behalf of his
wily master. But the time has hardly
come, even yet, when the history of the
warrior president of Mexico can be
properly written. Passions were aroused by
terribly events iu eai ly Texan history which
survive the great leaders in a terrible con
flict, anil we would only advert to leading
fact in the life of a m-in whose personal
vices were us shocking as his statesmanship
and genius for war were admirable.
His failures were the fault of his race and
people; his depravity, of the country in
w hich he was born, aud of the moral at
mosphere w hich he breathed from child

American military leaders should recog
nizj the vior and ability of Escoledo, il
luslratetl iu organizing and leading an
army. His recent triumphs are due wholly
lo the celerity of his movements, and
though the soubriq let Orejoic (long-eared)
Urn been giveu him he is neither a hare nor
an ass. Originally an unlettered teamster
or driver of puck mules, he has won his
way to distinction by inherent forces of
genius. It was Escobedo who gave the
first terrible blow to the French masters of
his country when, with a handful of fol
lower, he captured treasure trains bearing
live m Ilious ot Austrian gold. Uj moves
his sandaled soldiers, shod with strips of
rawhide, twenty leagues, fifty miles, each
day, and on his recent ni ircli from
Central Mexico he fought a battle
and ninrched twenty-eight leagues (a
league is two and five-eighths utiles).
Alatorre, next in command to the
President, marched, not long ago, fifteen
leagues iu one day, with two thousand five
hundred men, and stormed and captured
heights in Oixaca, very like Lookout
Mountain, held by seven thousand "rebel"
Mexicans. Mexicaus, under the training
given by the French aud Austrians, have
become most effective soldiers, and of Mex
ican leaders Escobedo and Alatorre are
hardly surpassed in genius for war by Diaz,
w hose fame is greatest on this side of the
Rio Grande.

The Western Senators and Representa
tives have a good show of reason for op
posing the general incorporation act, but it
is believed they will support a measure of
this sort in the end. A failure to pass such
a bill will place every community in Texas
at the mercy of corporations already oper
ating in the State. No matter how great
may be the need of any community, and
how great the desire of its people to invest
money in a railroad enterprise, they will be
required, iu the event such a bill be not
pasted, to pay ti ibute to men hold
ing charters tor the right to carry on
works of internal improvement. Should
Austin, Sin Antonio, Dallas or any
other community desire to have rail
way L-ading towards the rich agricultural
lands in reach of them, and there is no gen
eral incorporation act in force, they will
be approached by parties simply holding
charters, and they w ill be forced to psy ex
orbitant sums for the mere privilege of
spending- money necessary for their advance
ment mid protection. Men without a dol
lar, having charters, will demand one, two,
three, four, or even rive thousand dollars
per mile for the use of them, and the masses
will be subjected to a more terrible
monopoly than has ever had or assumed the
protection of law. Certainly the Legisla
ture cannot fall to pass this necessary bill,
cwntcuiplat'-'d, aud even marked out by the
Constitution.

Bismarck, D. T., July 7. Gen. Custer
left Roaelind on the twenty-second of June
with twelve companies of the Seventh Cav
alry, aud on the twenty-fourth a fresh trtil
was reported. On the morning of the
twenty-fifth an Indian Tillage three miles
long aud half a mile wide was reported,
fifteen mites off. Custer pushed for iu They
had made seventy-eight miles in twenty
four hours preceding the battle. When
near the village the Indians appeared to be
moving in hot haste, as if retreating. Major
Reno, with seven companies, was ordered
to attack the right and Cuater, with five
compauies, vigorously attacked the left of
the camp, Keuo felt them with three com
panies, and was immediately surrounded,
andAfter an hour's fighting, losing Lieuts.
fcjp-ufeson ana Mcintosh and twelve men
aod several Indian scouts and many wouud
ed, tr cut bis way out and. gained a bluff
three hundred feet high, where he en
trenched himself, and where he was soon
ined by Col. Benton with four companies.
Here the Iudians made repeated assaults,
but tney were as often repulsed, with
great slaughter. The Indians finally
gained higher ground than Reno, and, with
linger range guns than the cavalry, kept
up a galling nre till night.
I he Indians renewed the attack at dav-
liglit. Reno had lost forty odd killed be
fore reaching the bluff. There were many
hand to hand conflicts. The Indians out
numbered them ten to one. The men were
without water thirty-six hours; they were
letermined to reaclt water at all hazards.
L'ol. Benton made a sally aud routed the
main body, guarding the approach to the
water; the water was gained with a loss of
oue killed and seven wounded.
The fighting ceased for tue night, during
which time Reno prepared to resist further
attacks. There had now been forty-eight
hours of fighting with no word from Cus
ter. Twenty-four hours more of suspense
and the lighting ended, when the In
oians abandoned their village. Iu
great haste, Gen. Terry with Gib
bons commanding his own in
fantry, had arrived, and as the comrades
met, men wept on each others' necks. In
quiry was then made for Custer, but none
could tell where he was. Soon an officer
came rushing into camp and related thar.
he hatl found Custer dead, stripped naked.
but not mutilated, and near him was his
two brothers, Col. Tom and Boston Cus
ter; his brother-in-law, Col. Calhound his
nephew, Col. Yates; Col. Keagh, Capt.
Smith, Lieut. Critteuden, Lieut. Sturges,
Col. Cooke, Lieut. Porter, Lieut. Harring
ton, Dr. Lord, Kellogg, the Bismarck Tri
bune correspondent, and 1U0 men and a
scout.
Cus'er went into the battle with compa
nies C, L, I, F, and E, of the Seventh Cav
alry and the staff and non-commissioned staff
of his regiment aud a number of t cout a.
and only oue of the Crow scou'8 remained
to tell the tale. All are dead.
Custer was surrounded on every side by
Indians, aud the horses fell as they fought
ou'the skirmish liuc or in Hue of battle.
Custer was among the last who fell, but
when his cheering voice was no longer
heard the Indians made easy work of the
remainder. The bodies of all, save the
newtpaper correspondents, were stripped
and most of them were horribly mutilated.
Custer was shot through the body and
through the head. The troops cared for
the wounded anil buried the dead, and re
turned to their base for supplies and instruc
tions from the general of the army. Col.
Smith arrived at Bismarck last night with
thirty-five of the wounded. The Indians
lost heavily in the battle. The Crow scout
survived the battle by hiding in a ravine.
He believes the Indians lost more thati the
whites. The village numbered eighteen
hundred lodges, and it is thought there
were four thousand warriors. Gen. Custer
was directed by Gen. Terry to find and feel
of the Indians but not to tight unless Terry
arrived with the infantry aud with Gib
bons's column.
The Herald correspondent, Kellogg, was
killed.

very well for the city authorities to exter
minate dogs, bnt if a business man, having
no biting dog ab-ut the premises, resorts to
firearms and kills a Galveston drummer or
so he is liable to be fined by the rolicking
recorder.
There is likely to be a great row in Dal
las. The discoverer of a cheap method of
converting corn into' sugar and molasses
talks about setting up his machinery in
that blessed town. Whether he will be
suffered to use corn in such a sacrilegious
manner we are not permitted to say.
Henry TackeU shot and killed John
Leonard in the San Jacinto Hotel, Wednes
day, in Dalla, and the Legislature contem
plates the event sorrowfully enough, no
doubt, but has not th nerve to frighten
knaves by uplifting the utmost terrors of
outraged law and decency.
An Austin correspondent of the Brenham
Banner says that Austin and Galveston co
operate to compel the nomination of Hon.
John Hancock for Congress, and, therefore,
"the ring" had the convention called to
meet in Austin, that outside pressure might
aquetze out Clint Giddiugs and Seth Shep
ard. The Waxy Enterjrue says "that Galves
ton wants a break-water, and Dallas needs
a wind-breaker. Ilouaton wauts a ship
channel, and San Autouio wauts to be the
capital of a new State. Jefferson wants a
peuiteutiary, and Tyler would take nearly
anything down to the capital of Eastern
Texas."
It is proposed to gather in Austin all the
Sunday schools of the city aud of adjacent
towns, aud even from Houston, anu have
them move through the streets of the capi
tal, all singing the Centennial hymn of
American ireedoui. The bands of the city
aud of the several towns represented are to
accompany tin in.
In De-nisou those pious, good people
over there have got Tilden and Tilton hor
ribly confounded, and most of them swear
they won't stick to Tilden if he don't go
back to his own Betsy Jane, when every
body knows that Tilden, like old Buck,
never had any Betsy Jane. Diana rixhticira
wouldn't blush iu Tilden's moBt gracious
presence.
The Bolton Journal says that the railroad
will surely be extended to that place, and
that Mr. G roes beck, vice president of the
Central, agreed to visit Belton und see what
terms could lie made with the people. Mr.
Groesbeck will be tl.e e next Tuesday, and

rangement can be made with him to build
the road.
A letter trom Cleburne says: "Riinia
abundance. Wheat wilt average in the
neighborhood of ten bushels; com has a

outs cannot be beat; cottou looks rather

iu abuudauce if they matuie."
The source ot much crime in the West
results from confusion of great cattle herds,
autl theu, too, the Free Yarn says: "In
the portions of the country that are being
rapidly settled up, the ire of stockmen is
kindled against all who, in any way. tres
pass on what they consider their natural

Dr. Sinoot stands among the great st
and best men of Kentucky. He is fully en
dorsed by such men as Dr. Stuart Robin
son, of Louisville; Dr. W. E. Bogus, of
Memphis; Kev. Win. Dubose, Judges Un
derwood and Graham aud by his whole
church and presbytery, and by the good
and reapwnsib'e men of Bowling Green,
Kentucky. He is a noble man, and his
Christian character is unimpeached.
Inspired with hopefulness by recent po
litical and partisan eveuis, uiej aauy- ai
Corsicaua, on the fourth, with wondrous

If y country! 'tl of thee,
r-snot land ot llhertj.
Of I bee I alng
Land where my father died,
Lant of the p lifilm's pnde

Let freedom riug."
Mr. Joseph Sherwin, late architect of
this cry, now swells around iu Denver.
Some Michael Angelo, in making up Sher
win' architectural outlines, put the dome
in front of him, but the underpiuuiug is
stout and firm to resist the desolations of
time, and around the shining summit eter
nity must play. ll is said by a Denver
paper to weigh about two tous and to look
like Pike's Peak on wheels.
The people of Denison celebrated the
Fourth by having a silver half-dollar ticked
on top ot a greased pole forty feet high,
the money to If the propeity ol the climb
ing captor. We haven't learned whether
the postmaster or the John Knox preach
er of that town raked iu the whole pi'e
ami the whole glory or uot. If they dldu'l
they can't compete with their klud down
in these parts.
The San Antonio Eifires is informed
that Pierce's road is progressing finely, and
that by the end of July It will reach the
Guadalujie. All hands are workiug vigor
ously, aud the Etprtm was assured that Mr.
Pierce would continue to build after tbe
Guadalupe bridge was finished. Its in
torniaot was an intimate friend of the i rli
cers ol the road, and has made up his mind
upon this point from the knowledge gleaned
iu private conversations.
The Old Folks' Association of Western
Tennessee celebrated the Centennial Fourth
of July with bonfire aud illumination.
Charles D. McLean, ninety year of age, a
boon compauion of Divid Crockett, Sam
Houston aud Andrew Jackson iu the early
years of the urrent century, read the Dec
laration ol Independence, and n Congress
man Avery prououueed the historical ora
tion. Col. McLeau has descendants in this
county.
Elder W. J. Jones, of the Christian
Church, write July 4, 1878, from Wash
ington county "We had forty x addi
tion': thirty-five immersions, eight from
the B iptiats, ope from the Methodists, and
two restored. We had a glorioa time, in
deed. Two old Texas veteran (oae seventy
year of age) were immersed. One Catho

be all tue glory. I expect to legin a pr
1 1 acted meeting at Elgia bbout tuxi Satur
day evening."

Latly Gay center of fashion in a place ol
which w have heard occasionally, named
in honor vf a sedate, quiet tdd fellow who
was once Vice President of the United
Stales iu that vtgon.u. troerea'ive city s

left aim-sat dead on thaatreet, and the Leg
islature still talk sentimentally of the ter
rors and barbarities of the laab and whip

Dr. J. Maury Ogdra, only a week ago,
was strutting along tbe street of Jones
ville, Michigan, wearing the identical $40

He swindled somebody here out of a stove
and fixtnre. It ma at have been red hot
since he only carried away the stove-pipe

ItMtenfeldt. J. Maury is still operating oo

cently robbed in a bog's eye of $250,
and ioduced a Chapter La Michigan to pay

What is a gentleman t The queation hat
been put and answered more than once, yet
there remains considerable doubt upon the
subject. People are not disposed to accept
the verdict ot tbe poet that a gentleman. is
an individual who never does a mean action
and never tells a lie, but conscientiously
performs his duty by bis neighbor. ,r
are they prepared to accede to tbe conten
tion that a gentleman is a being who comt-t
of a noble stock, and soils not his hands by
selling calico by the yard or tea by the
pound. Yet, if they were ked to define
bat a gentleman is the majority wouia.
probably, only be able to say that a gentle
man is a gentleman, or, in otner words,
an indefinable something. Nevertheless,
they seem to be able to instinctively point
out those whom they consider to be gentle
men. .Now, of what sort are the persons
who are honored by their favorable verdict f
Well, it is to be feared that the fortunate
ones are judged principally upon the tcore
of their exteriors snd their clothes. A niiu
may be notoriously a hard swearer, a deep
driuker, and immoral he may tie known to
be utterly wrapped up in himself, aud to
have coarse aud vitiated tastes yet his
claims to the title of geutleman are often
readily admitted, provided that he keeps
his face cleaner thau he does hi oonacieuce,
that he has a ct-rtaiu air, and that be modu
lates his speech in a particular manner. Oa
the other hand; people who are virtuous,,
well-read aud of temperate habits, are
placed among thecommou herd unless they
are masters ot those little arts which look
so simple and are yet so tlifiicult of acquire
ment. It matters not what garb they have
ou, unless they wear their magnificent ap
parel with a peculiar grace. Indeed, you
may dress two men alike, and when they
are sent into promiscuous company one will
be set down as a gentleman and the other
as something else. Yet neither may com
mit a pot-itive breach of manners or do any
thing exceptionally brilliant in the way of
politeness. They are judged upon the
principle that a straw suffices to show which
way the wind blows. The angles at which
they hold their arms, the manner in which
they shake bauds, the way in which they
walk and stand, the method which they
adopt of usiug their pocket hantlkerchiefs,
and other little things, all tend to form the
judgmeut which is passed upou them. At
the same time, it is doubtful whether the.
judges, unless they are of a strongly analyt
ical turn of mind, note iu detail the signs
upon which the verdicts are formed; the
ngus, so to speak, being more palpable to
the instinct thau the understanding. The
signs that we have indicated are, of course,
supplemented by other. When men talk
they are bouud to show, to a greater or
lesser extent, the stuff of which they are
uiade. Thus, after the two persons in ques
tion have relieved themselves of their senti
ments, anil have joked for a certain time,
the grounds upou w hich they are judged
become somewhat clearer than they are at
first i-tart. It will be found that one in
dividual is proue to indulge in loud laugh
or awkward smirks, while the tone of his
conversation is low if not absolutely coarse,
aud tends to indicate that he possesses a
somewhat eity disposition. The other,
however, though he may be petty aud
coarse-minded, has the good sense to keep
his pettiness and coarseness to himself, and
to assume that he does things ou a grand
scale, and looks at matter iu a grand way.
Nor will you find him giving utterance to
sentiments which, thougti possibly laudable
in themselves, are apt to incur ridicule.. It
may be asked how It is that it is easy to
make some people, shabbily dressed and not
particularly blest by fortune, pass for gentle
men with very little trouble, while ap
parently more favored individuals can by
no means be mado to do so, however much
they may be drilled. Is there any difference
between the blood and bone of ons man and
those of another) Certainly the being who
has a abort, squabby figure, a pug nose, and
a coarse moist skin, is liable to bu set down
as being of plebeian origin. But he need
UOI, of uXMlt ai.,, H.
ing many men who, though thus heavily
handicapped, are freely accepted as gentle
mer. At tha same time it must be stated
that the .n lividuai who is slim and grace
fully built, end has a -straight nose and a
bright skin, stand a much better chance of
being declared a gentleman thau does such
a personage as the one whom we have re
ferred to above. This, however, is by the
way. The truth appears to be that certain
occupations aud associations tend to form
in people fault of per n, manner and
thought, and that these fault bucotne so
ingrained iu the persons whom they inflict,
that they grow almost ineradicable, and aro
transmitted trom generation to generation.
Thus, the man who has to indulge in much
rough labor become utterly slouching in
his gait, while he who is giveu to small
transactions iu barter loses aiht of large
matters in petty calculations. jlei le thi,
it is to be feared that those whose liveli
hoods are dependent, upon others acquire a
truck'ing dumeunor which never leaves
them, though it may be set off with a dis
position to play the tyrant to those to whom
it would be simply a waste of time, in a
pecuniury sense, to lie subservient. Thus,
when they leave their shops, offices and
warehouses behind them, they carry with
them, unconsciously perhaps, a disposition
to pay homage in quarters were it can but
be out of place ami excite contempt. Of
course, the children who. are under their
care aie affected by the example which is
set them, and thus the taint that we have
explained clings to families with what
seems singular but is really peifcctly natural
tenacity. From all this it will be seen thnt
the tendency of the, age Is to dub gentle
men those who display the most taste rather
than tbe best moral and greatest sterling
worth. This may be ground for satisfac
tion or it may not; but there i some com
fort iu the thought that those w hom society
is pleased to houor are, after a fashion, the
beat trained of their specie. It may be
urged that there is au undue tendency to
consider trivialities at the expense of great
oue when uien are in question; and thit is
undoubtedly o. But it imy be replied that
the Instinct which induce men to do this
i a natural rather than an acquired one.
For instance; a costerniouger who has no
love for an sloe racy in the abatiact will, in
spite of himself, pay much more deference
to one whom he recognizes a a gentleman
than t an individual w ho, he feels, claims
kindred with bint. At any rate, a very
obvious kston maybe learuej from all this,
and that lesson i this those persons who
wish in the future to stand well in the eyes
of the world mutt uot racbew self-culture
while they are in pursuit of wealth. Lon
don Liberal llccute.

Tlie Constitution is practically annulk-il
D4 the Federal governuieut ia ooly oryan
izei misrule. Individual frtedutn, like
tlmt of S ate, ia habitually violnted bd1
Federal, State and local indebtedness has
been created till taxation ia intolerable,
property valueless, and industry uaeless,
and thus ullenefs, crime and robbery and
outrige atalk abroud everywhere. Public
burdens are imposed, not to maintain
government and law and order, but to
foster apecial organ i z -d interests, anil labor
ia robbed of its rewards by a depreciated
curisucy. So terriole and streujj have
these calamities become that con upt'mn is
universal, and the very durability of our
ouce admirable federal t-y.tem becomes iues
tiouable. Reform or revolution are the
issues present" d by everj public fact of the
paat ten years. Kiirli and poor alike must
combine autinwt the robbers or money and
nuuiljers achieving a triumph in the com
ing presidential eiectiou, our whole syteui
and philosophy of government must be
transfoimed. While bowing befire the
majesty of the negro's virtues and woith
the yoke of despotism has been fastened
upon our own necks and "in freeing the
slave America is enslaved." We are soou
to choose a President, and in designating
thechxice of Texas the representative body
now defining a code of laws, under a new
government, for this commnweulth, may
exert a strong and bentliceut influence.
The convention at St. Louis sits at an
early day and the Democratic party, a
represented here at the capital, should
assert its purposes aud aspirations aud
designate its preferred candidate. We
should declare for a president who has
illustrated in public an 1 private life those
virtues that will as-ure the speedy- extirpa
tion of those p cubar vices and evil, that
make the feiteral system abboiretit to
honesty aud ruinous to industry. The de
mand for the hniiest administration of
public aifairs and for obedience to the plain
edicts of consn'utionill law renders the
currency question comparatively- insignifi
cant and the; moral and intellectual forces
and virtues of men must be the strong
elements of fact and character to
shape the preference of the Democracy of
Texas. Samuel J. Tilden, of New York,
has been tried everywhere. He has been
found wanting in nothing. He recognises
his oath of otlice. lie overturned Tammany,
sent Tweed into exile and consigned his
pals, with the canal thieves of the Empire
State, to the peniteutiaiy. He turns neither
to the right nor left, despising alike the
many and the few, when they obstruct the
course of honesty and reform. Demanding
obedience to law at the hands of others,
he concedes ooedience iu all his. public acts
to the country's law aud exercises no
doubtful powers. He is to day t'le very
impersonation of Amciicun hatred of wrong
ami rubbery aud nepotism uvt vulgar snob
ocrncy. He has lessened pu'ttic burdens as
was never done before by any governor of
Nj. Voik, aud-byhls pleudid virtues,
illustrated in his viori tin and just admin
istration of the goveruuieul ol the greatest
Sia'e, he has shown his finicss to shupe the
destinies of. the graudcsl confederation of
Scates, and Sumuel J. Tilden should be the
next President. That his ticket may boast
of incomparable intellectual and moral
strength and draw to its support the vast
multitude of people who reck little of
parties if proper men lead them, it occurs
to us that ex-Senator Trumbull, the ablest
iawyei of the Wes, the defender of An
drew Johnson when impeached, and fore
most juiist that ever presided over the
Judiciary Committee of the United States
S nnte, should be the Democratic nominee
for the Vice Presidency. The South will
surely vote the Democratic tick'et; we
wou'd win the West, and Trumbull draws
Schuiz after him, and the German aud
Conservative Republican vote of the West.
Tne ticket should be Tilden and Trumbull.
Tiieiie are now no newspaper or foreign
deadbeats preying upon the capital and
upon the innocent literary glow worms of

Tilden seems still to lead the list of Dem
ocratic candidates, and the chances, just
now, evidently favor bis nomination. Til
den hus the tremendous advantage of being
sustained by the whole Conservative and
Democratic press of New York City. There
is no means of measuring its all pervading
power. The commercial metropolis of the
country, the focus of American intelligence,
the depository of its gold and the central
seat of newspaper power can hardly be re
sisted by any representative body gathered
even in St. Louis. Omnipotent and ubiqui
tous New York newspapers shape the action
of the party even before its managers ant
convoked in the new "Hnb," and each del
egate, going to the big fridge, crosses it
with the conviction that Tilden is the
strongest of all aspirants, and each repre
sentative of a district would have it said
that he foresaw results, and, from the be
ginning, stood by the winning candidate.
Especially is this true when each delegate
goes to St. Louis with the hope that some
thing will come of glory or honor or office
to himself from his action in this conven
tion. He would have some benefaction at
the hand of power, and therefore would
clasp it tightly, even before it wields
the scepter of presidential omnipotence!
It thus happens that all the time-serving',
timid members of the convention will
speedily rally about the banner of the hero
whose praises are most loud y chanted by
the ubiquitous newspapers of the East.
There is a fact of this, sort most unfor
tunate for our party. Not only does Pres
ideut Grant wield the consolidated power
of 03,000 placeholders, and of their kindred
and satellites, but government patronage
has been used to create newspapers and en
rich and gather intellectual resources and
give potency to the Republican press un
til its aggregate power is superior to that
which Tilden, if nominated, may wield.
Every United States marshal and the post
masters and placemen of his district is re
quired to maintain a well-oiled party organ.
Sharp, shrewd appointees of Radical place
holders turn the crank and there are no po
litical melodies chanted in Northern States
that do re echo the story of Southern mis
deeds, crimes and enormities. At this
hour there are countless myriads of listen
ers who believe that wails of murdered Af
ricans burden the very air breathed by the
people of Texas. While, therefore, the pow
erful press of New York will probably
nominate the candidate of the Democratic
patty, it is iqually true tlmt the great
danger of defeat incurred by Democracy
consists in the extraordinary force aud
power given Republican newspapers by the
Federal Treasury. Compared with organs
of Republican faith, those of the Democratic
party are less numerous everywhere iu the
North and they are unable to employ the
agencies of popularizing themselves to
which the President's exponent may resort.
Offices themselves consolidate a mighty
army about the President, and this terrible
organization exerts despotic sway through
the press it euriches over the opinions and
passions of the masses of Northern people.
It follow;, whatever may be said to the
contrary, that if the New York press nomi
nate, it must also elect the next President
or confess defeat at the hands of Tribunes,
Gozcttes and Inter-Oceans and Glube-Dono-erats.

We have written of the scheme of thoee

mand a million nore? When a city induces

property absolutely secure that cost less be

officers appointed by the G iveruor, iu every
ci.unty iu the State, clothed with the same
powers that peace officer possess, with au
thority to pnxs'ie fugitives from justice
from one end of the State to the other,
whenever necessary, would be far more effi
cient to the protection of the people as well
as far more satisfactory Nhan a re-enactment
of the so justly di tested police law.
To expect this would require thorough or
ganization. The Governor should appoint tbe
officers and have authority to remove ihetn
for neglect of duty or other proper cause,
and such laws should govern ihe militia as
apply to tegular military organ izitions.
The State should pay all expenses incurred
when the tniiitia leave their own county in
pursuit of criminals. The sheriff of each
county should have authority to calj upon
militia officers for a detail to guard the jail
or prisoners whenever he may deem it nic
essary. 1
In addition to the militia the Governor
should be empowered to employ- special
agents to ferret out the perpetrator of
crime and to ascertain the whereabout ol
escaped criminals or fugitive from justice.
To do this effectually, an appropriation
should be placed in his hands to be ex
pended for this purpose, and also for the
payment of militia when they pursue crimi
nals outside of their own county. The
special agents to ferret out' crime and the
militia could act together when necessary
II ml such an organization existed in
Guadalupe county when the mail was
tubbed a few a ay ago some one would
have immediately notified the nearest mili
tia officer, whose duiy it would have been
to hae ki once taken tbe field with hie
men, and by sending couriers to every point
ia the vicinity, the whole country would
have been aroused, and tbe pursuit would
not have cased until the robbers were cap
tured or driven out of the State.
An objectii n tliat will be urged to this
system is that the people will not submit
to drills, or to the labor imposed upon them
by such a law. It must be remembered
that drills are almost unnecessary, for the
simple reason that the people of Texas are
veteran who have paaaed through a four
yeara' war, and are, with Iut lew excep
tions, well drilled. The loss of time and
the arduous dutie imposed by ucc a sys
tern are more serious otj ction, and should
be fatal, were not the necessity for eome
system so serious a it i at tbe present time.
The Legislature hoc Id remember that the
first duty u to provide adequate protection
for life and property, nod that a failure to
do so now will ma be forgotten by tbe
people whom they represent. Of all matter
before the Fifteenth Legislature none is o
important as this, and all other should
give way to it. Ia t no paltry considera
tion of ripen e govern their minds or in
fluence their conduct. The cry of tbe peo
ple hi for protection, and immediate de
uutnd is made upon tbe Legislature for it.

That the power to pass a fence law, except

22 of general provisions. Tbe Legislature

a Ihe varied and conflicting interests of the

A general law for the inspection of cat

No no. ice, as provided in siction 57, arti

The undersigued, a member of Judiciary

the resolution of the Committee on Agri

laws, begs leave respectfully to report that
he cannot agree with the majority cf tbe
committee, for while it is true that under

the passing of a fence law applicable only

couuties, as may be needed to meet the

else where was the necessity for in

not be doubted that, if it were
not in the Constitution, the Legislature

applicable to any subdivision of the State.
But if the Constitution had been silent

be passed without publication of the notice
for the application, as prescribed in aiticle
a, section 57. But when full power is

tbe wants of the people" iu any subdivis

quiieuieuts respecting other local fats.

lias not the Legislature passed law mak

Ct mstitutiouf Who u r quireo to publish
uotice of an intention to apply for such a
Ism Not the Legislature surely. But if
such uotice is necessary, it would seem o,
for it alone is made the sole judge of what
"may needed to meet the want of tbe
people." There is no "local option" noth
ing left tirthe people and no notice can be
necessary where all power is given. The
minority of your committee, being fully
persuaded that this Legislature has to-day
full power to pass any fence law necessary
to meet the want of the people of tbe
whole State, or any part of it. i con
strained to differ (modestly be trusts) with

Nobody but Henry Wa'erson could have
drawn this faultleaa sketch of Henry A
Wis. J
It is common to hear him spoken of
ualee be be abused outright aa "the
venerable Henry A. Wise." There is noth
ing venerable, about him. A tall, thin,
little figure; flaxen bair parted in the mid
dle; a keen, pale, clear cut visage piercing,
ever-reatle eye, a shrill, treble voice, tb
veteran agitator doe not carry about with
him any of the sanctity of age. In hia duet
with Cocke in S2, a U.1. Starboitle
would ay hi antagonist undertook to
jockey him a they were placed via -vi by fi
whispering to hi second loud enough for
Wise to her, "I call you and Ood Almighty
to witnea that I am guiltless of ibi man's
blood," to which Wise quickly rejoined,
"You had better leave GJd-a-inighty alone
and look to your own blood, damn you,"
putting bis bullet into Cocke's groin as soon
aa tbe word wa given, and coming off -him-elf
without a scratch. He ataped James
K. Polk in the faice for a trifling offenae,
and be ha bulled hia way through life
noma how in spite of uudeniablo genius and
oonraire. J
Tbe ex-governor has been in Washington
of lste, and appeared last Thursday before
the House Committee en Elections as
counsel for Piatt, who is con test iug the seal
of Uoode. After he bad closed his argument
he took occasion, by leave of a recent allu
