lengthy peroration, political and xraonal.
It was, the corrcoudeut of the Cincinnati
Vovmunvial tells us, "full of the old tire,"
abounding in' "curious, quuiut and strong
expressions,! tinted with the egotism that ia
not unbecoming or distasteful in a man of
the Governor' fame aud years." A sum-
nirizd by the same correspondent. Gover
nor Wise's views are certainly entitled to
this description. He declared, to liegin with,
that be was neither a Conservative, nor
Republican. Ho described the,Conserva-
tive party of irgiuia s old-fashioned
Democrats, old-fashioned Whigs, Know-"
Nothings, Locofocos, Ned Yeskit'Demo-
crat of the Jefferson stamp, or Muer-kreut
Democrat, a they are called lu lennsyl-
vania, and Greeleyite. He averred -that
while he wa governor of Virginia, If he
could have caught Greeley be would have
hanged him and pardoned John. Urowu;
and yet Greely was the niao the Conserva
tive party in Virginia voted for for preai-
dent. He said he bad tried to kef p Goode
from going into the Grn-ley party, but
Goode had disregarded hi advice and bad
gone over to the, Black Republicans, and
voted for Greeley. Which is the worst,
cried the old man, a black Republican or a
black Conservative? Some one sked if he
wa uot fatigued, and if he would not like
some refreshments. Hen plied: "If you
can bring me a glass of good, pure, sweet
milk, I will take it, but I curse whisky,
brandy and rum." He praised Grant ror
hi honor and magnanimity in instating that'J 1
the parola given at Appomattox liould lie
kept in good iaith. He considered Uraut a
great chieftain and worthy to receive the
sword of Robert E. Lee. His denunciation
of tbe Conservative party of Viiglnla was
t rribly .scathing, and referring lotlieac- T
tion of Goode, against whom the contest
is carried on, using one of old-fashioned,
fiery expressions, he exclaimed; "By the
Eternal Gods, I would have my right hand
cut off at the wrist before 1 would bold my
pen to ign the test oath."
The tenor of all this I not very credita
ble i to Governor Wise' discretion, and
might bn dismissed aa first -class raving but
it is sincere, and, if its author were required
to support it by a logical argument, be I
capable of making one that would be plau
sible. There is in it just that admixture of
truth and error which enables a shrew ed
bandier of quidditrs to confuse the simple
mind by dextrous play. That has been
al way the forte, the metier, of Governor
Wise. He is an honest deluder of himself
and other people. He came on the scene -a
Randolph of Roanoke passed away, and
at a time when tbe ambition of every aspir
ng young Virginian was to imitate tliftt
eccentric statesman.- None succeeded quite. j:
so well a Wise. He had courage, b had

florid, out-door orators, of the cultivated,

ciallzed Queen Anne's men he wa the

absurdity so far. and save it.: No man
could take such license with court, juries.
jegislative assemblies and popular gather-
ings.'. tie was a sensationalist inacso tw
hi track were not the tracks of tbe
mountebank. He wa an eloquent pleader i

The begi- aing of the troubles oa the Iiio
Grar-i'e Hitea back to the war of Texas in
prmlcnce in 1S3C. Annexation did nnttlose
that war. T"ie Uuited Slates tccepteJ an
empire, fiecl ofTered bj the heroic men
who achicTed auccess against fearful odds,
and also accepted all open questions .be
tween Texas and Mexico. Ooe of the re
sults of annexation was the war of 1846-7-8.
These two conflicts inaugurated s war of
races placed the Anglo-American and tbe
Spanish-American- in direct antagonism.
On the side of tbe American there ws
much to remember blood wantonly spilt;
'irothers massacred in old blood. After
hostilities had ceased, tbe gorernmeot of
the United States adopted a coarse of concili
ation proper towards a sister republic. She
Juried resentments, and exhibited a Chris
tian forbearance where 6he might hav de
manded justice at tbe cannon's month.
During tho war of intervention, when for
eigners impot-ed an emperor npon a republi
can people, tint United Btates stood by
Mexico, furnishing arms, and, throwing her
weight into the scale, effected by dip'otnucy
what Mexico could never have accomplish
by arms. Secretary Seward did more to
consummate the overthrow of Maximilian's
empire than Juarez with his legions, though
seconded by the treachery of Lopez.
The government and the people of the
United States thus endeavored to win the
friendship of Mexico. Tbey failed signally.
Hatred for Americans increased, and it wn
not restrained by acts of kindness or abated
by time, but grew into a deep-seated senti
ment of reveuge an infernal thirst for hu-'
mtn blood. Americans were assassinated,
outraged and robbed. The "legion-of-honor
men" who followed the republican flag of
Mexico until it waved in triumph from the
Atlantic to the Pacific were defrauded,
swindled out of their pay; and this act of
black ingratitude was indorsed .by the
Juarez government, and covers its memory
with infamy.
Oa the Rio Grande, -where the two races
stood and still stand, foot to foot, the hos
tility of the Mexican has been manifested
by legislative enactments, and by deeds of
violence and bloodshed. The passage of
the zona libra law the establishment of a
free belt, six miles in width, extending from
the mouth of the Iiio Grande to a point
above New Laredo was a blow aimed at the
commerce of the United States. It ruined
the business of American merchants In the
valley of the Rio Grande. It has encour
aged smuggling into the United States. To
pass a law permitting the introduction of
goods, free of duty, all along the Rio
Grande, was a discrimination against the
United 8tates.
About the stme time, and tinder the im
pulse of the same feeling, another system
of aggression and injury was adopted.
Tbe first raid made upon the people of
Texas from Mexico was set on foot in 1853.
The Tampacuas 'Indians inyaded Texas,
killed a number of people, robbed others,
carried cattle, and other property, acquired
by force, into 3Iexico. In 185 9 Cortina in
vaded this State, bince then raids have
been frequent, and the people of Western
Texas have been robbed of property worth
millions of dollars.
Tho principles of international law in this
connection are very plain and very positive.
Nations are compelled to restrain their citi
zens, or to assume the responsibilities of
their lawless acts. Bands of robbers, or
ganized upon the soil of a State for the
purpose of making incursions into a neigh
boring State, must be checked, broken u-,
and punished,-by their own government, or
iho injured 8tato has the Tight to pursue
them to their places of refuge and inflict
summary punishment. The government of
Mexico has given an asylum to the raiders.
Th-y have carried their stolen property
within bcr limits and sold it there udc-r
the protection of Mexican authorities.
Mexico has sinned against the United St tea
since 1853, and has indicated no feeling of
regret; on the contrary, her excesses have
become moro intolerable with time, and
despite complaints and remonstrance.
Tbe laws of nations accord to the United
States the right to institute whatever meas
ures she may deem appropriate to the occa
sion. After more than twenty years of
wrong and outrage on the part of her citi
zens, Mexico is estopped from interposing
any plea of justification. She cannot es
cape on the ground of ignorauce she can
not claim to have been actuated by good
intentions. She stands at the bar of pub
lic opinion convicted of bad faith she has
iuiled upon thu United States, whispered
words of friendship and stabbed at the
same time. ITer skirts are red with Ameri
can blood; millions of dollars1 worth of
American property, Required by force, fraui
and crime, are now within her confines.
The questions which interest thousands
of despoiled and impoverished citizens are,
what steps will our Legislature take Will
the United States indemnify us for our losses
and protect us in the future f May not the
illustrious soldier now garroting an Ameri
can State at New Orleans be more patriwti
c.llj employed on the Rio Grande t

People, and editors are of the people,
are apt to go m id. They get an idea into
their beads, like tueCredirMobilier or back
salary grab, and there is not room enough
for another. It is an axiom in mathematics,
applicable in this case, that no two sub
stances can occupy the same space at the
same time. Crania are not all capacious
enough to contain the two propositions
that if a ring have plundered the State, all
who ask the State's assistance are not neces
sarily rogues, and all railway companies are
not constituted of thieves. Besides there
are very ignorant people in the world who
have not yet learned what the country owes
to railways. One great highway in Texas
would be better than none, even though it
might rob and wrong the people and con
trol the Legislature and absolutely rule the
State. It should have rivals to regulate its
rates of charges and watch it when its
lobby comes down to Austiu. Therefore
the Jefferson Ltxir lectures on the subject
of railways and says:
Why is the whole North, east of the Mis
sissippi, so much more prosperous than the
South? Why are lands there so mach more
valuable? What bas built up her towns and
cities; erected bcr factories and mills, de
veloped her agricultural and mineral re
sources? What has given employment to
countless thousands of her people, and
made the busy hum of industry heard on
every side? Take the State of Illinois for
instance. When her great. Central Road
was incorporated, a large land grant was
niM.de to it. This land was then worth an
aver.ge of two dollars per acre. When
the road was constructed, these lands
were sold by the company at an
average of five dollars per acre, on long
payments. To-day every acre of thi.t
land will bring from twenty -five to fifty
dollars per acre. Thriviug towns and
populous cities have grown up along the
line of this road, and Illinois, with her
magnificent system of railroads, has become
the empire State of the West, and the govj
eminent has received back in taxes, etc.,
moro than the value of the lands she gave
to aid in building trese roads. The history
of Illinois in this icpcct is the history of
every State where railroads exist. The
roads we now have have done fsr more to
advance our interest, to develop oar re
sources,' than all other things combined
would have done. Let the Texas and Pa
cific road be completed, the International
finished from Jefferson to tbe Rio Grande,
and no min i can contemplate, no pen do
justice to the future greatness and gi mdeur
of tht, the Empiiu State of the Union.
Is it not the duty and. interest of- both
State and nation to foster and aid these
works? And is it not about time that the
8eal of the people's d'Bapprobation should
be placed upon the narrow-minded, selfish
and anti-progressive class, who are standing
in tho way and opposing the consummation
of these great enterprise'', in which the
State and people are so vitally inttrf-!. ed?
Let us then unite and urge with all .our
power tho granting of aid by Congria to
the Texas and Pacific road, and demau-, in
a way that will not be misunderstood, the
iruru"diate settlement, by our Legislature,
of the International bond question, tnd

Jur.es B. Ends is still urging Congress to
give him ten millions of Federal bonds in
consideration 'of the erection of tu
parallel walls of wood or stone or iren, le
tkctn which-the vast outspread mouths of
the Mississippi are to be compressed. :"T: e
current, we are told, will be strengtheuet i
and eject delrltcs swept down from mcu;;
tain slopes and alluvial Uins far out ir
the sea.- True enough, but' there is enough
mu i brought down from the R .cky Moun
tains to make a mall continent in each dec
ade. Erti..'i j -Uies must be extended for
ever, and each year. the cot.will.be forever
tenewed, and thu woik must be forever
done over ngain. Why not end the
task at oncc? Nature has already cut deep
channels, now r.avignble for the largest
steamers, two-thirds of the distance from
N;w Orleans to deep water in Barrataria
Bay, west of the Mississippi. A ship canal
is indispensable from the Crescent City to
this -mighty harbor. It was fortified, years
ago, even by President Jefferson, because
there were navigable streams or bayous from
this harbor to New Orleans. Open these,
convert them into a elack-water ship canal,
the cost will be trifling, and once dug, the
work endures forever. It will not
even need dredging once in five or ten
years. A locomotive on its banks would

rope, the Indies and to Mexico and South
America, would bo lessened more than oue
third. Build Eads's jetties, as proposed by
our correspondent, and the tow-boat com
pany will still prey upon and destroy the
commerce of the West. It now costs a
sailing vessel of two thousand tons four
thousand dollars to get in and cut of the
river. 'Eads's jettios will double the diffl
cultjr of ascending the current and the short
can4l from Fort St. Philip perpetuates it.
Therefore let Texan Congressmen remember
that the commercial distance between Gal
veston and- New Orleans will be lessened
oie-half by this canal from New Orleans to
Barrataria B.ty and that Texas, an empire
of itself, is made part and parcel of the
Federal Union as never before. Dethrone
Ames and Kellogg, 'hang the murderers of
Btates 'and dig us a deep "canal from New
Orleans itself to tho sea, and then frighten
old women and fools with dismal howling
of Radical knaves who tyrannize over the
South, plunder the people, destroy States,
refuse to aid our Pacific railway and when
they pretend to serve the South, some eham
like this of Eads is resorted to to pacifj
and appease while deluding and plundering
tho victims of a remorseless despotism.

Hero is a return not of obedience to the

The constitution in violated in order to pre

butes.
Sheridan, unlike General Wilkinson, seems

The Statesman baa reported 3Ir. Mc
Comb improperly npon the qiectioa as to
whether thia it a regular or biennial scioo
of the Legislature. Ill remaika were in
faror of the mij'irity report. Ha aaid
there might be three kinds of session. reg
ular aeseiona, as to time provided by law;
called or special aeaaioDa; aud ex-raonli
nary gessiona, oot prortrled for by law or
called by any authority, uch a came ai
reci ly from the people through the late con
vention, ry an ordinance and uft a perpet
anl law that according t he.se deliattio i
thia mast be an irregular biftininl t-t-s-aion,
becausa not a-Hfinbk-d by any
law fixing the "regular" time of meeting;
tlitt it ia nt t a F-f irtl Meg-ntn, ralktl by the
0 Tcrnor; that it ia an extraordiuory aex
ion, called by the people acting in their
-tovereiun capacity; that delegate with ex
inordinary p iwers, restrained by nothing
but the orxanio law of these United States.
They aaid by ordinance thut thia session
should be held at an unusual time, the
eighteenth of April an extra period of
time tacked on to the present term of
office, assembled for purposes for which no
other aession can be assembled until we
have another constitutional convention
that the power which convened this session
waa extraordinary, as it did not meet at a
regular time of meeting, while the leading
purposes for which this session was con
vened were extraordinary, and it cannot be
a "regular" biennial Beesion, nor yet a spe
cial session called by the Governor; that it
must be an extraordinary session, yet with
the authority of a regular session because
the power which called it gave it the au
thority to do what may be done in regular
session. That convention had the right to
(confer thia authority because it hail su
premo political power.
When a robber presxuta n.s pistol, de
manding one's purse, or when highwaymen
appear, as when Mr. Fry's 400 cattle were
driven from his home at Burnet, or when
the gentle horse thief enters a farmer's in
cloquie to deprive industry of ineuns of ex
ertion in each instance the plunderer
comes tinned cap-ajtie. lie has already
committed murder in his heart. The eepit
et uxperhivit "taking ami carrying away"
constituting the gravamen of the offense
at common law, are really the least
criminal of its incidents. The law,
aa now operative, punishes the wrong done
property-rights; it should also punish the
bloody deed already consummated in the
purposes of the highwayman. He who
does these terrible deeds of robbery and
outrage is the- very prince of murderers,
and should bo hanged. Thu South, and
not t'ne North, was the scene of a terrible
war, and here tbe choicest villains, born of
the foul womb of war, were left likeTioiu
ulus and Remus, to be nursed by wolves
of famine and to prty upon order and
society. Here, too, are the impover
ished victims of ghastly governments that
have desolated the Gulf States. Here
are aggregated tht knaves and desperate,
reckless adventurers of a continent. To
destroy the whole brood or drive it across
the Rio Grande or turn backward the title
that fl iwed into the South through Federal
offices, we must not only make Tilden Presi
dent but so amend our criminal code that the
highway robber and cattle thict shall be
punished as a murtierer, and iu order that
the laws may be executed, those counties of
the west, in which public officers are mur
derers aud thieves, must cease to txiiit a
corporations. Reform we must have and
the remedy must be assimilated iu its
fierceness and violence to the disease to be
cured.
TnE Democratic party will be wisi
ecough when convened at S Louis to con
fess at once, that the whole wurld may un
derstand ii, that we do not propose to carry
Ohio and Indiana. Local elections in these
two States occur in September, and they
are joined to their idols. Tiiey utterly
demolished Greeley, because Greelev's
managers pretended to believe that they
would vote for him. Failiug, Greeley wa.i
ignominiously beaten, and mainly, because
of the moral effect of local Republican Sep
tember vicioriea in Indiana and Ohio.
There will be no repetition of this folly, and
by nominating Tilden aud Trumbull at St.
Louis, the country will be assured that
Democracy reliea for victory upon New
York, Connecticut, New Jersey and upon
the conBervaiism of Illinois, aud of the
Northwest. We do not need Ohio and In
diana and enter upon no bootless contest to
win them. Wo can well all rd to lose
them and for the reason that the very facts
that cauee thia loss make assurance of vic
tory douoly sure in the States assigned to
Tdden and Trumbull. A Washington letter
writer grates pirunently that a "lurge num
ber in the Republican party will vote for
Governor Tuden Itecniisu of his splendid re
cord aa retonuer, who will not vote for
any other Deinocract. It is asserted with
ureat confidence that he Can carry Mussa-chut-etta,
and he is the only candidate
named who can certainly carry New York.
These cominnu-seute views have made a
uYep iuipr. a-Mtiu here, as they have dont.
eUew here, ami there is a growing increase
of Tilden sentiment."
Thk Dallas and Wichita, like the Austin,
Lhtnpasaa and Delion narrow gauge, Is in
dispensable to the prosperity of D.dias.
B-oad gauge roads, having remote termini,
would Carrv freights tbe greatest possible
distance, and Tom Scott aud the Central
will soon have no termini at Dallas.' So
with the broad gauge road a converging at
Auaiin. They exiet for New York and Su
Louis, and Austin is only a way station to
gather in and transport tbe most and leave
here the least possible wealth and popula
tion. Nothing ctn compel the reduc
tion of freight and passenger rates,
in promotion of Austin interests, ex
cept the narrow gauge. The old roadi
know tbia, and, it ia alleged, even hire men
to take stock in the narrow gauge ia order
that these pretended friends, fighting from
within tbe company, may excite hostility to
it and IU active, earnest friends. The
scheme must not be abandoned. We must
have the narrow gauge, with branches to
Helton, Salad a, Lampasas and to the min
eral diatricta of Llano and Mason counties.
Austin need only build twenty fire miles of
tbe road and these will build the rest. But
we only proposed to say that Dallas is as
sured of the construction of its narrow
gauge line, and with it this from Austin
must be clumate'y connected and tbe two
an unit will become part cf tbe Denver and
Rio Grande Railway, coming down like a
Doi'her from Pike'a Peak.
Brcuham J5.1 a twr thinks "the private
st;i.- the pot of bonor for Liza wkxe
uuutiiu V d tt the newspapers."
Whex the article entitled "Jefferson
Davis" was written and published in its
regular order, and by the regularly em
ployed contributor to Appleton's Cyclo
pedia, it attracted hardly a proper share
of attention. It happened, however, that
the writer in question was a devout Unionist,
as were the Messrs. Appleton, and that the
sketch was deep'y colored by prejudices
which time will surely heal. Every absurd
story and ridiculous allegation ever pub
lished, affecting the conduct and character
of Mr. Davis, was accepted by the writer
for Appleton. Not long ago Mr. Walthall,
cf Mobile, editor of the Cycle, prepared and
printed a careful review of the article in the
Cyclopedia. The cyclopedia.' statements
were hown to be untruthful and his spirit
niHuifeatly unjust. Messrs. Appleton were
induced, like honest men as they are, to
confess the wrong done and to suppress the
ciictilatiou of the volume condemned by
Mr. Walthall. It is. proper that the
public should comprehend these facts,
and understand at what enormous cost to
themselves the Messrs. Appleton, having
unwittingly wronged the South, undertake
to apply the proper and only remedy, by
employing Mr. Walthall to rewrite the ar
ticle bffecting Mr. Davis. The genius and
worth of the Mobile editor will surely be
properly recognized by his own people, and
as thoroughly that of the great New York
publishing house.
Ai.TnouoH the Tom Scott influence is
just now active, it will fail to convince
members of the Legislature that an im
mense public domain should always be held
at his disposal. It will also fail to prove
that it is right for Texas to use a clerical
force for years, and to supply a place for
the wife keeping of Texas and Pacific land
papers for an indefinite period, until this
railroad magnate shall see fit to turn the
State's labor to his account. He will fail
in these schemes as effectually as he did m
foisting Blaine, the bitter enemy of tbe
South, upon the people of this country a
their chief magistrate.
Charles Scott, a negro, outraged a white
child twelve years old Saturday near Waco.
The child, the daughter of a poor widow, is
now a maniac. Scott will be captured and
properly executed on the spot, and yet North
ern newspapers, because the war rotted here,
and because the North generously concedes to
the South all peculiar blessings that flow from
degraded citizenship, are ever amazed that
deeds of lawless violence are done in Texas.
But will not the Inter-Ocean be good enough
to publish the story of Scott's enormities? If
Scott were white his photograph would be tbe
frontispiece of the next Ilnrper't Weekly.
Morgan's steamers at Clinton will gatbertn
the whole trade of San Antonio and of the
Sunset route. They capture the cotton and
wheat of Dallas and the only recourse left
Galveston is the extension of the Gulf, Colo
rado and Santa Fe road to Austin. A ring
btirsted this concern, in the interest of the
Houston and Galveston road, and the scheme
must be revived or Galveston moved up to tbe
head of the canal. Leeds and the Clyde tell
Houston of the trifling task she must accom
plish. TnK late District Attorney f Philadel
phia, when leaving Cincinnati, after the
nominations were made, said: "There is
one thing that mortifies me beyond meas
ure, and that is the total want of princi
ple evinced by the Southern delegates."
The representatives from Texas will please
rise and explain. Did the shrewd prose
cutor of kuaves blunder or did he know
what he was talking about
It is proven by Mulligan, Fisher and
Charles Francis Adams that the good man
Blaine did not read the full text of bis let
ters produced before the House. But we
only proposed to say that, as we predicted,
this irrepressible'Blaine was first to express
his supreme delight that he wasn't and oth
ers were nominated at Cincinnati. He, is
a fmuk, honest, good fellow especially
frauk.
iOse huudreti and twenty-eight million
ninety-nine thousand two hundred and
forty acres of public land have been granted
by leg slatures of Texas to railway corpo
rations, to say nothing of breakwaters, big
ditches and cleaning out imaginary and
other streams. The State never had more
than 87,000,000 acres, and tbe rest may
well be thrown into the sea at Galveston.
Bristow withdraws from Grant's cabi
net, with the purpose, we would fain hope,
of securing re-employment at the hands of
Iliyes. The purpose, if approved by
Hiyes, is a good one; but Bristow's chances
for future public service are not brilliant,
unless be have an understanding with Til
den, and why not? He and Tilden are the
practical reformers of modern times.
The Examiiter is wholly at fault. A lare
majority of the newspapers ef Texas, if we
are not grievously misled, favor t!e nomina
tion of Tiltlen. A vigorous administration,
by a capable and fearless reformer and no
good-natured soldier or namby-pamby good
fellow like Hayes, will be accepted by the
plundered taxpaying people of the United
States.
It was the Tixas delegation at Cincin
nati that tu-ncd the tide, and, lifting up
H.yes, turned down and drowned poor
Maine. B'.ainc, contemplating the bloody
shirt hanging on a nail in the wardrobe
whence he draws the Sunday clothea of his
eloquence, weeps bemuse of the ingfati
tuoe of his part? in Texas.
To silence further inquiry we wonld
state that the Wheeler tacked rn to nyes
to defeat Tilden in New Y rk is not the
M ivor of Aut.n or the inventor of ''look
stitch" in the rent garaieuts of red-shirted
Radicalism. Ha ia tbe standing Colfax
Blaine-appointed chairman of tbe Railroad
Committee of Congreaa.
It is said that Hayes need lo be a Know
Nothing with Deacon Smith -and those

THK Jl KUESf Y QrESTlOX.
Wo do not profess to be learned or pro
found on nutters of finance. We have
never been crvr-fred in tr-vl? or ronrsect-O
r t
with a bank of any kind. But wo have
some plain, old-fashioned notions about cur
rency, which wc desire to lay before our
readers for what they arc worth. The
world, a it becaire peopled and began to
have commerce and trade in connection
with aa acknowledged and permanent right
of property, required some standard of
values, and, by common consent, the pre
cious metals, silver and gold, were fixed
upon for this purpose. These, for thou
sands of years, have been used as measures
of value, from the little change they havo
undergone and the scarcity of their produc
tion, and they are still considered the com
mon standard of the world. In times
of trouble and financial embarrass
ment, a resort has been had by some
nations to a paper currency and a
forced recognition thereof, but this has
never been done except under a sup
posed necessity to answer a temporary pur
pose, and as soon as possible there has been
a return to the common specie currency of
the world. There never has been a resort
to paper money without innumerable diffi
culties attending it, and especial injury to
the mass of tha people using it. A few
shrewd, cunning and not over-fastidiously
honest men manage to profit by it, and therc
foro they are always anxious to keep up the
system and prevent a return to specie vab
ucs. The colonies in this country from the
old continent, being often hard run by the
heavy expenses of their Indian wars, some
times resorted to this, tempting expedient of
extending their means and credit, but the
results were always more or less injurious.
So, too, when these colonics confederated
together in a noble struggle to obtain their
liberty aad independence, they resorted to the
issue of paper money, and kept on issuing it,
as it depreciated, in still larger quantities,
until it became utterly worthless and died in
the hand? of the people. A bushel of con
tinental money would not have procured its
a single meal. Our forefathers, after
the gixatsITugicas over, when they met
in convention to establish "a moro perfect
specic, gold and silver, were alono recog
nized as the legal standard of values. For
one we have never doubted tho wisdom of
their determinations on this or any other
point. But notwithstanding this, banks
were established and paper money issued;
and made more and moro plentiful, as the
trading people demanded it; and from time
to timo the currency was inflated, and the
nominal value of everything, including la
bor, raised, and everybody wa3 supposed to
lc rich and flourishing, until something
brought on a panic, and the banks failed, and
their paper promises became worthless and
the great mass of the pcoplo suffered a3 they
always will from such a system and such a
practice. During the late unfortunate civil
war both sides resorted to paper money, or
running their credit for the time being, and
the result was, as it always has been and
always will be, tho depreciation and con
stant change in value of tho notes issued,
until, by the surrender of the Confederacy,
its portion became worthless. The United
States was enabled, by its superior wealth
and facilities, to hold up its rag currency
better than tho Coafcdcracy, but it, too,
went up aad down with the varying phases
of tho war, sometimes being quoted and
used as three or four to one. During this
period a few pcoplo at tho North and West
realized largo fortunes, and became mil
lionaires, but no actual wealth was added
to the nation, and the ordinary classes were
not permanently benefitted thereby, but in
the ead seriously suffered. Thi3 is history
Siace the war this paper currency has had
no fixed value, but has gone up and down,
at the pleasure of speculators, until, when at
it3 highest worth ns quoted, the late panic
occurred, and the whole country felt a
shock as if from an earthquake. With all
the paper money afloat, and at an estimated
value nearly the same as ever, there i3 com
plaint of a want of money business has
suspended, lalwrers have nothing to do aad
arc clamorous for work, and disorder aad
tumult aro common ia our large cities. It
is said that Congress must do something for
the people. This is always the cry whea
people arc ia aey financial distress. What
6hall the Congress do? Shall it continue
the evil which lias beca brought on the
people by the use of paper money not
founded oa specie basis? Shall it issue mere
cf the "filthy lucre" to relievo the people
from their present embarrassments? Mem
bers of the Congress, Republicans and Dem
ocrats, arc divided tad split up oa this
question. Some are for giving temporary
relief by the lsue of more paper money,
others deem this a favorblo time to return
t a specio basis and to specie payments.
Thf.t is our opinion. Let there be a rcsior
atioa of the constitutional currency of the
republic It must corae to that at last. Lit
it bo now. There will never be a more fa
vorablj tirac

LEGISLATION.
The making of laws is of all things the
most important which can affect men In a
state of society. Laws aro tho fret neces
sity of organized humanity. It would never
do, in tho fallen etateof .man, to let all act
according to their pleasure, without any re
strictions or -any regard, to the pleasure br
rights bf others. But even Adam and Eve,
before their, fall, while In the garden of
Eden were under tho law of their Creator,
and their expulsirn from Taradise was the
result of a violation of that law. Since then,
in the progress of humanity, a still greater
reason has existed for curbing and restrict
ing tho evil propensities and passions of
mankind. The,' first laws known, and the
most important as Affecting our future life,
are the laws of God. Theso can never be
wrong, and should never be questioned.
The law-maker is all-wise and the enact
nirnts mnst be perfection. Then come tho
laws, of man, which "are. tho result cf
experience In 1 the wants of society,
and which, prococdinjr; from Imper
fect natures, must necessarily be more
or less imperfect, but still they must be
made for a profound thinker and great
writer has said that even "bad lawa are
better than none." These necessary laws
have been the work of either one strong
will, which has obtained ascendancy and
control over others, or the combined action
of a few wiso men. having the same power
over tho masses, or by the general consent
of the .whole people, expressed in a body,
or through their chosen representatives, for
the sake of convenience. Despotic mon
archs make laws by the issuing of edicts,
which are regarded as. such and must be
obeyed. An aristocracy make them by the
sanction of a majority of their own order,
irrespective of tho wishes. and will, of the
balance of a nation, and the whole people,
without distinction of classes, ordain and
enforce laws by common consent, through
their representatives, in a free and real re
public The State of Texas is to-day an il
lustration of a -freai.' people governing
themselves. They do not make laws
in a body, being' bound by the de
termination of the majority, aa did
the old Athenian democracy, because, from
their great number, this would be found in
convenient and impracticable, but they make
them through their agents, elected for that
purpose, to carry out their wishes. Hence
the duties of a Texas. Lctrjslature, or any
other similar body, are plain so plain, that
any ono can seo and understand them. The
Representatives, as tho very name implies,
do not' assemble together tQ make known
their own individual views and feelings,
and put them into the shape of laws for
their own advantage, but to carry out the
will of the whole sovereign people, whose
agents they are. Strange to. say, where the
people govern themselves by making their
own laws in the manner indicated, the
greatest danger is that tho laws will be too
numerous aud complicated that they will
should bo as few, a3 simplo, and as, plain as
possible for the well being of thoso for
whom they areanteuded. Too much legis-r
lation generally- does moro harm than
too little. The natural liberty of
mankind should not be restrained be
yond what is .necessary for the order,
peace and security of society. They should
be left to take care of themselves, as much as
possible, with as little interference as possi
ble for .the attainment: of tho foregoing ob
jects of government. It is not the duty of
legislators to make pcoplo rich, or to keep
them poor, or to, pay off their debts, or to
regulate, their way of living, or their reli
gious observances. Individuals who make
up a people or nation, do not like to be
dictated to in such particulars they prefer
to be let alone, so that they do not interfere
with others in their natural and constitu
tional right3." All legislative bodies should
recollect and heed these plain rules of
legislation.'
In conclusion wc would respectfully sub
mit that in this article no reflection is
meant against members of tho Legislature.
It is written simply to bring an important
subject prominently: forward, hoping that
tho ball, kept moving in the right direc
tion, will gather power, and that mind upon
minl will earnestly accomplish what the
public so much desire Let ns have less and
better legislation in the future, and the peo
ple will be much better satisfied.

FBOXTIEtt DEFENSE.
In another column the reader will find a
communication from Palo Pinto on this
subject. The writer has been misinformed
as to the details of the bill before the
present Legislature. There appears to be
no disposition to continue the home com
pany system.
Tho remarks of Palo Pinto are, in the
main, appropriate and judicious. Ho is
quite correct in assuming that there should
bo" unity of purpose and concert of action
to render tho operations of a frontier force
effective. In order to obtain these, there
must be an officer placed over them as com
mander, and he must possess the necessary
qualifications to give efficiency to his com
mand. He must be. acquainted with the
frontier, and with Indian character. He
must have had experience as an Indian
fighter. lie should possess the confidence
of the border settlers, and bo enabled to
secure their co-operation in furnishing news
of Indian' forays, guides for particnlar lo
calities, and in other ways.
Experience has proven that'pursuit of In
dians from near the immediate scene of a
depredation has been a failure in nine cases
out of ten. The Indians move with great
celerity. They drive stolen horses before
them at a gallop, and when the horses upon
which they are mounted becomo fatigued,
they halt long enough to lasso and saddle
others.
In this manner they travel from sixty to
one hundred miles per day. They 'have
scouts in their rear who ascend hills, and
ascertain whether Ihcy are pursued. In
ithat -event JVyJ n y y dr-tard -r-'r -i1 4
takb every "tje-g o gharu hgiuubt'otfr
w .-rt tin n-rinv-iTf j i J
prise, and to throw their pursuers off the
trail. This course gives the Indians great
advantages. They gain a night's march in
every twenty-four hoars trails cannot be
followed at night.
Tho better plan is to follow marauding
bands mora slowly. Let them have a day
br two the start. After having gone beyond
the line of settlers, and having seen no one
in pursuit they grow, more or less careless,
stop to rest their jaded horses, and to recu
perate themselves by eating and sleeping.
They may bo reached while thus engaged.
An officer in pursuit of Indians should leave
camp prepared to follow them to their
lodges, and to fight them among their
wives.' and children. Tho Indian carries
war to tho domestic hearth of the white
man, and gloats over tho deeds of blood ho
can perpetrate there, but when the pale
face retorts upon' tho red man, tho dismay
and terror consequent are truly intense'
Carry tho war into Africa let tho rifle
speak to. the cars of the squaw and the
papoose-T-ict the warrior fall beforo his wig
wam, and his surviving relations and friends
will think seriously of peace, and when
made they .will not break it for trival
causes. i
In order to do this tho frontier forces
should be well armed and mounted. They
should be drilled, else they could not be
handled in the field. This drill should in
clude the tactics requisite to fight Indians
with success. The soldiers should be good
horsemen, good shots, and inured to life in
the woods.
A great deal has been said about tho
rations of Texas rangers, and much of it is
gammon.' The old time rangers were capa
ble of bearing fatigue, hunger and priva
tion, without murmuring, but they loved
good eating as well as others, and would
grumble when their rations were cut short
without good reason. Men most be prop
erly fed if good service is expected of them.
Ilcnce the coraiaissariat of frontier troops
ehonld be adequately eapplied.
We say this because efficiency cannot be
attained by a raist&kcn sense of economy.
11 oar legislators mean to call out troops,
they must appropriate eadcicnt funds to
place them in the field with all the modern
means and appliances to raako war. Any
appropriation folding short cf this would be
a waaton wicio of the pallia racney.

try aud Cauadus are Hooded with

Mk.mimus, October 7.-The following
special appeared in the Memphis Ap
peal of this morning
Khiau'k 1'oint, October I), 1ST).
Have just arrived per steamer Julia.
At eleven o'clock a. in. the citizens
were all under arms and anticipate an
overwhelming attack from the negroes
unless three hundred men can come
here to deter thu negroes I'roiii further
violence.
liusinesft Is suspended, mid the citi-ze-i:i
are in a dreadful state of alarm.
The negroes, live hundred strong, at
tacked Friar's Point yesterday, but
were repulsed by thu while forces un
der (ieiiernl Chalmers. Thev threat
ened to burn tho town, ami murder the
women and children.
The light commenced within three
miles of the town, ut a point where the
negroes made a stand. The lighting
was kept up to Clarkshades. As yet,
there in no reliable.statenieiit as to the
losses.
Pickets just In report lighting In the
vicinity of Oak Ridge road, eight ne
groes reported wounded in yesterday's
tight mid one white mall.
Sherill' Drown is now a', Jonestown,
fourteen miles cast of here, reorganiz
ing his party for the supposed purpose
ol auothei Jdtaek upon Friar's Point,
where the negroes are said to be
rendezvousing in large numbers.
So far as 1 can ascertain, this Is no
party movement, both Democrats and
Republicans being united to resist, mi
incendiary attempt or. tin- part ot
Sberill lirowii, who has been making
imllaiiiinalorv speeches to the negroes
ami organizing tliem Into armed
bodies, upon Ihe idea that the whites
intended to kill them, when in fact
tile w hites liad not uttered any such
intentions, nor had any meeiings lor
such a purpose, nor did they hold
meetings of any kind other
than the nominating convention.
I am assured of this by Hemilor Al
corn, Judge 1 larrison, 1'. 10. lteid and
oilier prominent citizens, who believe
that the presence of aid would prevent
a fatal collision between the whites
and blacks. The former dread such an
occurrence, and will only net in de
fense of their lives and property.
This telegram is forwarded by special
courier lo Helena. The courier will
wait there to know what action bad or
may be taken by the citizens of Mem
phi's, (iciicral Cbnliners and his cav
alry have not yet returned to town.
'i he special has created very little

at Friar's Point anil as yet no men

(ieneral Chalmers lias two Hundred

Annie Waite and William II. Leake
ure not upstarts in their chosen pro
fesslsn they have made It the Btudy
of their lives to approximate perfec
tion, and at tlie same time to elevate
the standard. Although still on the
sunny side of life, they have lived to
reup the rewards of their patient en
deavors, not only In the rapturous
plaudits of admirinir thousands, but In
the more substantial, though not more
satisfactory, matter of dollars and
cetils. For some years Mr. Leake has
been lessee and manager of the Opera
House at Indianapolis, and has made
ii 0110 01 tne most popular and success
ful institutions of the kind in thecoun
try. Meanwhile Anulo Waito has been
playing star engagements at Chicago,
v-iiK.iiniBii, i iiisourg, ana tne princl
pal hasten cities.
Annie Waite is comparatively
young woman, of medium size, cast In
nature's most perfect mould. In her
impersonations sue never rants and
ireis, nor (toes sue ullect to "split the
ears of the groHnullngs;" yet she Is the
reverse 01 lame, in lUe most quiet
scenes she is always Intense, aud fulth-
luuy represents tlie character she has
assumed no more or less. She Is inore
lliau an actress she Is an artiste. For
tlie information of those who attend
theatres uo less to feast the eye than
tbe ear, we may state that her ward
robe is simply magnificent.
William H. Leake's actlug In many
respects Is similar to that of Miss
Waite. At times be rises to the sub
lime in the most iru passionate scenes,
aud bis burning eloquence bursts forth
In fiery, irresistible torrents; anon be
gracefully subsides Into the style and
tone of ordiuary conversation, leaving
no gaps for the imagination to supply,
and bearing no traces of the fierce
storm of passion that swayed him but
a moment before. It can truthfully be
said of him that be never overdoes, his
part.

(t immigration, is beyond computation. The
8'eat S:ate of Texas embraces within its
linits a territory of 237,504 square miles,

ettent of this empire-like domain.' Take
tie five New England States, New York,

islation. Little South Carolina is agitating

a? the genius of immigration, pointing to
to her fertile prairies, proclaiming to

and figures dhow! Does it not most em
phatically exhibit the benefits of immigra
tion; that i) umbtrt create values.
This is not an age for standing still, and
stationary progress signifies want of vitality
and decay. The men who sit in our Legis
lative halls to-day are sent direct from the
people and are pledged to support this
measure. It was one of the main planks in
the Democratic platform. Governor Coke
has in, words that have thrilled the heart of
every good Texan with a responsive echo,
plainly and clearly marked out his wishes
and desires. That palpitating, indomita
ble, incarnate energy that is only satisfied
by great results, haa been roused in the
heart of the people and they ask, nay, de
mand, that their Representatives shall stand
firm to their pledges.
TUB CONTESTED ELECTION CASE
FltOn Till ItTEESTII DISTUICT.
The pending question in the Senate on
Thursday was that of the contested election
case from the 'Thirteenth Senatorial District,
involving the rights of Z. Hunt, contestant,
and Walter M. Burton (colored), contestee,
to ft seat in the Senate. Tho committee has
had this case before them for a 'ong time.
Senators Dillard and Parker reported in favor
of seating Hunt, and Senators Hobby and
Davenport reported in favor of Burton. The
Thirteenth District is composed of the coun
ties of Wharton, Fort Bend and Waller.
Hunt was the Democratic candidate, from
Waller county, and Burton the Radical
candidate from Fort Bend. In Fort Bend
county 973 votes were cast for W. 31. Burton
and ISO for -Win. Burton, while Hunt re
ceived 253 voles. In Wharton county Burton
received 108 votes printed "W. M. Burton,"
and 134 printed "William Burton," while
nunt received 281 votes. In Waller county
Burton received CC4 votes printed William
Burton," while Hunt received 208 votes. There
were four Senators on the committee and two
reported iu favor of seating Hunt on account
of their inability, under the law, to go behind
the election returns, while the other two
claimed that since there were but two candi
dates for the Senate in said district, and but
one Burton, it was evident to them that all the
votes cast for Burton, by whatever initials,
were intended for Walter M. Burton,
nnd that in the entire vote cast in the
three counties, ho had an undoubted ma
majority over Hunt. On Thursday, as
stated, the case came up for argument be
fore the Senate. Col. Delany and Mr. Mc
Leary, both members of the House, were ap
pointed as counsel for Burton, and Col. Peeler,
of this city for Hunt. The argument con
sumed several hours, and elicited the talent of
all the very able gentlemen engaged. We have
not room to even give a condensed report of
their speeches, hut must say that each side put
the case ia tho strongest colors, and did full
credit to it. The Senate adjourned pending
the discussion to again take up the case on
Friday.

we know not, but we do know that if the
vr.ice of the intelligent and virtuous men of
his district could exercise a potential influ
ence, lie would not remain in his present po
sition an hour. The country needs and de
mands a pure juJitiary, and the first step is
to rcniove thoie who are ignorant and un

The cases of impeachment and addressing
out of district judges, from present appear
ances, bids fair lo become a matter o gigantic
prcrjortions. It is universally acknowledged
that many of the judges are men of utter in
competency, that some of them are very cor
lupt, and that the safety and dignity of the
State depends upon other men filling their
places. All this is trae, and the popular
expression is to this elToct. We repeat, that
the matter of removing these judges is becom
ing ponderous. A host of witnesses are being
summoned, and their mileage and per uicui
will have to be paid out of the State treasury.
Not only this, but the time consumed by the
Legislature in disposing of these cases will be
considerable, and to each day so consumed
there will be added to the above named expense
twelve hundred dollars. To these heavy ex
penditures addtiie bills for printing and other
costs consequent upon the investigations, and
the expense of a constitutional convention will
be surpassed in the one item of removing six
or eight judges. A constitutional convention
would cover this entire ground, and at the
same time give the people that which they
expected, and for which they now clamor.
We overheard one of the Senators opposed to
the convention remark, in conversation on the
floor of the Senate during tho discussion of
the subject of allowance to the above named
witnesses, We had just as well nave a con
vention." In that Temark he struck the popu
lar string, and it is to be hoped that the re
sponse of the majority of the Legislature will
accord with it. Let us have the conven
tion. It will at once raise the stand
ard of our judiciary it will give us a
basis for sound legislation, one not laid down
under military dictation and indorsed by a
power at the time "prone to oppress, but one
coming up from the hearts of Texans, broth
ers who are free, and who in their next sacred
compact, if it be permitted, will build up a
standard of citizenship, which will exalt so
ciety and add character and solidity to our
State which would not be easily shaken. In
the light of advancement a convention becomes
necessary; in the light of justice it appears to be
due; while in the item of expense consequent
alone upon the legislative adjustment of judi
cial incumbents, "we had just as well have a
convention," which would correct everything,
as to otherwiso cover only one-tenth of the
ground at the same expense, or most probably
a greater one, than that consequent upon the
holding of a convention. This Is a serious
question for consideration, nnd after all the
expense the full ends, in all probability, would
not be reached.
RRVEM'E HEFOirri.
The revenue question is to-day the one of
paramount importance before our Legislature.
What is required more than anything else is an
effective revenue system.
We believe it may safely be nsserted that
taxes to the amount of not less than one mil
lion of dollars are now due to our State treas
ury. A large proportion of the taxes due, re
main uncollected in many counties. We have
nothing approaching a thorough system of col
lecting taxes. This is the true reason why
we have an empty treasury at present, and
why the warrants of the State are being hawked
about the streets and sold at ruinously low
rates. Speculators in government paper have
always flourished iu times of financial distress.
Nothing can be more important to the State
than to maintain its credit, and its reputation
for good faith untarnished. Texas is amply
able to pay one hundred cents on the dollar
on tho whole of its floating and unfunded
debt within a comparatively short period of
time.
We take the liberty of suggesting to our
Legislature, the appointment of a joint com
mittee from both houses selected from among
those most familiar with financial questions,
whose duty it shall be to prepare and report
at an early day the best possible revenue bill
for the State. Let our tax system be simplified
and let everything bearing thereupon be em
braced in one act, and all others be repealed.
Nothing is so essential to efficacy as simplici
ty. Should such a committee as that sug
gested, be appointed, we doubt not but that
they couW readily bring to their assistance in
the solution of the difficult problems before
them, tho best financial talent in the State.
The subject of taxation is always tho most
difficult and delicate one that comes before a
legislative body. In some of the older States
men who have made finance" the subject
of life-long study, have been appointed under
authority of the Legislatures, commissioners to
propose reports upon the best system of taxa
tion and the one that would be least oppressive
upon all branches of industry.
The report of lion. D. A. Wells, commis
sioner for the State ef New York, is very ex
haustive and instructive. Mr. Wells was for
merly commissioner in the same capacity for
the United States government, and he has long
been recognized as one of the ablest writers
upon the subject of taxation. We believe
that the object sought may be accomplished
by the appointment cf the joint committee as
suggested above.
We have an able and honest Comptroller,
but he must be armed with the means for see
ing that the taxes are fairly and promptly as
sessed, and honestly collected and promptly
paid into the treasury of tho State, We be
lieve that the true way to raise the credit of
our State and relieve us from our present un
fortunate situation, Is to create a simple and
effective revenue system.

In addition to these, ve fin I the conviction
of two men cf previous respectability, who
murdered Green Butler, at Clear Creek, on
the nineteenth of May, 1S72. It. was a cold-Mo-xled
an 1 dastardly assassination, for which
V: was no excuse or palliation, and yet,
blraiige to siy, the jury, Instead of i-eud'ntr
them to the scaffold, rendered n verdict con
signing them to the penitentiary for life.
This is the first step in good government
and in retribute justice.
It has been too common, North and South,
to exhibit every sympathy for a criminal,
whoHO hands are imbued in blood, and none
for the man who has perished by the hand of
assassination. There is no commiseration for
the widowed wife or her orphans. She set
solitary and unenmmiserated in her poverty
and grief.
We rejoice that all e ver the land crime is meet
ing its just reward. And while we feel deeply
for the suffering, there is a relief in tho re
flection that f-oeiety is benefited by their
punishment. Law, justice, and an enlight
ened public opinion arc exercising a potential
sway, and will teach bad men that tho gallows
and the penitentiary arc before them, and that
money, however freely expended, cannot
save them from punishment.

TUB SABBATH.
"Remember the Sabbath day to keep it
holy." Thi is tbe Divine injunction given
to man amid tbe fires and thunder of Sinai.
It is Jehovah's awful command to the wea..
aud erring of earth. Thi morn the S-ib-bath
bells are ringing throughout the Chris
tian world; the bells, the deep toned
chorcb bells, inviting the little children to
Sabbath school in every hamlef, village and
city throughout the land, where the word
of the merciful Father and the lore of tbe
Saviour ia taught. Inviting saint and sin
ner to those sanctuaries where tbe way of
salvation is made plain, and the glories of
futurity, to those who love God and obey
Ilia commandments, like beautiful visions
are nnvmled to fill the heart with rejoicing
and make glad the weary. How like Dead
sea fruits, tuft ted but to turn to ashes on the
lips, are the schemes, the baubles, the am
bitions and tbe poor, bitter pleasures of
this life, beside the crowns, the realms aud
the joys of eternity, that are the inherit
ance of "those who die in the Lord."
"Remember the Sabbath day to keep it
holy." Picnics, junketings, excursions,
and all the worldly things that make na for
get God and God's mercy, are infractions of
the letter and spirit of the law. The mere
attendance upon church services ia not all
that ia required. For those who go there
to see and be seen, the young men and the
old who go only because it ia respectable to
do so, the young women who go to show
their pretty ribbons, their lace handker
chiefs and twenty dollar bonnets, and to see
what that Sonhronia StuckuD has on. and
if Tabitha Gabbletongue wears that same
old blue silk, are in no wise benefited by
what tbe preacher says. The seed thit
is sown falls on atony places and barren
tpots, and it takes no root. The mind and
the heart are both unbent -fited. Tlie one be
comes tilled with thing of the earth earthy,
aud the other imbued with the vanity of
vanities bitterur.H, covetousuuas and un
charitableness. Such keeping of the Sab
bath day holy mitkea the angels weep in
sadness, while it tills the heart of the devil
with iflue, and niken him lling double
shuffles of delight. The sume bells that
ring out ho gladly each Sabbath morn, toll
in solemn notes above the funeral cortege.
"Remember the Sabbath day to keep it
holy." Ifouaton Telegraph.
We are affected even unto tears when
we remeuiler that the truly good man ho
wrote the above, and who with touching
puthos pleads w.th bis hearers to "Remein
ber the Sabbath day aud keep it holy," ha
never in the course of his whole life kept a
single Sabbath, nor refrained from its regu
lar weekly violation. How thta Apostolic
man can reconcile his precepts with bis
practice, we are unable to explain, but tbe
fact is as we have stated. While he is thuH
thrill'tngly eloquent npon the soul-destroying
effects of Sabbath-breading, he is and
boa always been a consistent, persistent
Sabbath-breaker. None of the uncircum
sized Gentiles, whether Phillistine, A male
kite, Moabite, Ammonite, Ilittite or Ilivite
has kept the law of the Sabbath less than be
The text of Scripture from which our
reverend brother of the Telegraph quotes is
commonly known as the fourth of the com
mands given at Mount Sinai to the Jews,
and it goes on to tell which day the Sab
bath day is, and hou it is to be kept holy.
As our saintly friund does not appear to be
very familiar with the Bible, we will repeat
the commandment,' to show how strangely
contradictory have been tbe teachings and
actions of this truly pious man Remem
ber the Sabbath day to keep it holy. Si
days shalt thou labor and do all thy work
but tho sevenh day is the Sabbath of the
Lord thy God in it thou shalt not do any
work, thou, nor thy son, nor thy daughter,
nor thy nifo-servsnt, nor thy maid servant,
nor thy cattle, nor the stranger that is with
in thy gates." Saturday is the particular
day assigned by this commandment tor
Sabbath observance, and the children of
Israel, to whom the command was given,
have always so observed it, and do to this
day, by resting from work on Saturday,
and holding their synagogue worship
There is one, and only one, weekly Sab
bath enjoined, described, or in the least
alludud to, in the whole Bible, whether He
brew or Christian the Saturday Sabbath.
No other day is bo designated; ne other
day can be the Bible Sabbath. Does the
pious oditor of the Telegraph keep Saturday
as a Sabbath t If not, then we grieve to
ssy that this truly good and venerable man
is a Sabbath-breaker, since he neglects the
very thing enjoined by that commandment,
the keeping of the seventh day, Saturday,
as a Sabbath. But the commandment re
quires something more. "Six days sbalt
thou labor and do all thy work." Thus this
eloquent moralist is doubly a violator of
the fourth commandment, neither resting
on Saturday, the seventh day, aa it enjoins,
nor working on Sunday, the first day,
which it equally enjoins. In the course of
a somewhat lengthened editorial career we
have seldom been called npon to chronicle
a more saddening illustration of human
frailty. This eminent and holy mat, actu
ally quotes to others, as binding, a law
which he himself openly disregards and

The posthumous advantage arising from
generosity to newspaper men is well illus
trated in facts incident to Ralston's death.
Creditors of his bark and officers have sought
in vain to derogate from his worth and
blacken his name. Even George Alfred
Townsend does not forget "poor" Ralston's
generous, boundless hespitahty, and the
newspapers are building a monument to
Ralston more enduring and prouder far
than he dreamed of erecting even when he
proposed to beautify and enrich the coun
try and city he deemed his own and such as
was never reared save by mightiest mon-
arena. Ralston is one of the few debtors
who, dying, lives immortal in that he is
lauded as all others like him have been
cursed as infamous, and simply because the
press will not be silenced and continues to
bed lustre upon his deeds and virtues.
Most men expect to die, and they who
would live after death should not forget
the lesson taught by Ralston's deeds and
posthumous luck. Deal generously with
the newspapers is the one great lesson
taught by Ralston's life and death.

Steele State. Mississippi is doomed.
must either be negro-governed, or a milita.
ry despotism. Some people prefer ths relga
of the miUtary tyrant.
JCDGB REAGAN AND TUB PR ENS OP
TEXAS.
Judge John Q. Reagaa made a most sen
sible and thoughtful speech yesterday, as
admirable aa brief. It was literal truth as
every journalist of the country, worthy the
name, must confess. The distinguished
gentleman said that organs" had become
f little value and were little esteemed, and I
hethex aet ap to promote the aims ot a I
party or of s chqne or ring or any Individ- I
ual deserved little confidence. Tbe M-ple I
presume that these organs are paid for ser-
vices rendered, and declarations of policy
and opinion made by an organ are mere
copies of decrees issued by their masters,
or by the ring or clique which the organ
represents. Independent journalism does
not involve independence of yirty, but of
party leader. Parties we must have.
These must subsist while people
iffer in reference to the theory
of the American system of government.
Our theory is that of the original Jefferson-
ian Democracy; but inside the party, as
thus defined by this leading principle, the
Statesman is the organ of no man or set of
men in or out of Texas. Therefore the suc
cess of the Statesman. It is denounced
with the utmost bitterness by men who have
been accustomed to have "organs" do all
their dirty work and serve them with profit
less but unfaltering fidelity. But the peo
ple approve the very facts and incidents
and peculiarities of journalism which the
whippers-in of the party and officeholders
are apt to condemn. If the paper be true
to the principles and philosophy of govern
ment in which liemocracy consists, the
masses of the people ask no more. Beyond
this they would have fearless independence
and capacity and boldness to assail any
leader or condemn any act of personal sel
fishness or personal ambition,however great
the idol guilty of the crime or blunder.
The people never questioned the Democracy
of the Statesman because it disapproved
the policy of Judge Ireland. The circula
tion of the Statesman still grew apace;
but party leaders howled and their hired or
gans growled and we were formally extru
ded from the Democratic church by an
'organ." It was bmtum fultnen in the ears
of the people and independence won popu
lar confidence. Sensible, intelligent gen
tlemen, personally attached to Judge Ire
land and life-long Democrats differed from
us in opinion, and adhering to the Judge,
were pleased that a newspaper had the
courage to tell what it conceived to be the
truth in defiance of the clamor of placemen
and of organ-grinders. Judge Reagaa is
right. An "organ" doea not deserve popular
respect or confidence, and cannot secure it.
The fortunes of the Statesman in this city,
and throughout the State demonstrate this
fact. In truth Democratic newspapers in
Texas having that independence which has
marked our course, as we have earnestly
and honestly sought to shape it, are the
successful newspapers of Texas.. It would
perhaps be invidious to name them, but re
cent events have made them quite conspic
uous, and illustrate as well the truth of
Judge Reagan's declaration that "organs1
are undone, and their day is1 past.' The
people prefer the unbought, unrestrained,
ll it l., 1 J 1
uuucsi ii UMBiaikcu, upiuiuus auu uccitua-
tions or an untrammeied newspaper press.
THE CSCBV CODE.
A valued correspondent writes
from
Washington county .as follows:
"If the Convention -enacts an usury law
there will be a revolution1 in farming and
real estate and the establishment of facto
ries in Texas that has never been known
before. Until this is done -our State will
be a stranger to anything of the kind.
Give the country a stringent usury law
if you desire to see the country and the
farming interest prosper instead of individ
uals. Should the Convention, fail to make
such a law, the farming interest will lan
guish until it will be only1 a question of
time as to wbo will be tbe owners of this
county. There are numbers of farmers in
Washington county (though one of tbe
best farming counties in the State) who are
anxious to dispose of their farms and move
to town and embark in tbe business of
banking. The farmer does not make more
than five, or six per cent, per annum
on his investment; tbe banker, two
and a half per cent, per month is the
price he charges for the use of his money.
This demoralizes the f aimer when we ought
to encourage tbe farmintr interest. Ihe
price for the use of money should be lim
ited so that a farmer could borrow money
to invest in farming with some bope of be
ing rewarded for his labor. When this is
done there are numbers of enterprising men
in the country that will go back to their
old avocations, make a usury law not al
lowing tbe collection of any debt by law,
with the rate of interest above six per cent.
per annum. Old and prosperous States
have such laws.- Ia Tennessee and Penn
sylvania six per cent, is the highest rate
that can be collected by law, and most of
the older States, with but a tew exceptions.
fa little higher) are tbe same. So long as
the moneyed man can lend his money at
tbe present price, just so long will the State
of Texas know nothing of manufacturing
establishments, otherwise there will re ac
tivity in Texas, land and real estate and
manufactures that has not been known
since the absence of a usury law. All
Christian nationa have fixed by Uw the rate
of compensation for the use of money.

THE COST OP THE CONTENTION.
The spirit of economy and reform that
has abcady, and very properly, manif.: od
itself ia the Convention indicates that the
expense of forming a new constitution wQI
not be so great by far as has leen contem
plated. The saving in the cutting down
of the per diem of mem'oers and officers of
the Convention alone will be' alwut 30,-
000, allowing sixty days for the seaaioa.
Counting all the clerks, doorkeepers, pages,
etc, at ten members, and the entire body
therefore, as one hundred, the per diem of
the Convention amounts to $500 a day,
or 30,000 for sixty days. The printing,
stationery and incidental expenses will
in all probability not exceed $20,000, and
the mileage much less than 1 0,000, mak
ing, as a sum total for a sixty days' session,
less than (00,000. Still many enemies of a
Constitutional Convention prated about an
expense being entaile i upon tbe State, in
consequence of one, aggregating hundreds
of thousands of dollars. The people can
rest satisfied about this expenditure of
money, for agieatdeal more, prospectively,
is to be saved.

Ir anybody wants to know why the free
school system is not popular in Bonham,
they will find an exposition of the mystery
in tbe statement of tbe Enterprise, to the
effect that tbe establishment of a negro
school within the corporate limits a few
years ago, brought several families of dark
ies from farms in the country to town to
educate their children, and now to give
Bonham special privileges on the free school
question would bring the rest of that
population to the town. We don't object
to the cost of educating a fair share of the
peripatetic darkies who pay no taxes, but to
have the whole colored population of the
country drawn into the town limits is rather
rough on corporation tax-payers. Then,
too, such a mass of negroes may be inducted
into the place that they will gain control of
the offices and ravage and destroy till Bon-
bam becomes desolate. This evil may be
provided against by tbe Convention, which
may restrict suffrage in corporation elec
tions to tax-payers.

A private letter from ex-Gov. Stockdale

When a journal, known not to be an
"organ" does speak kindly and approvingly
of an office-holder is worth something to

ever-recurring laudation and horrible adula

and common intelligence. Judge Reagan
bears a good head on his shoulders, and has
thought to some purpose and kept his eyes
open while toddling about through this
rugged world. Other party leaders shsuld
learn what he is prepared to teach, that a
good cause never needs nn "orgau" while
good newspapers exist.

This is no doubt true. The masses were

Thb Comanche Chief reproduces and ap

Fleming, a member of the Constitutional
Convention. He was a Confederate soldier
at thirteen, and remained threugh the war

he became a lawyer, and stands foremost
among lawyers for thorough integrity and
sound, practical, good sense.

artus vt lthiu tbe bouoUaricS of LUtuet o Uu

J. Fought has quit fightiug-, having fought

.Once upon a lime there was a man travel
ing througn a strange couutry ana came
upon a wide ana muddy stream, lie paused
uoou the. brink of that river, and hesitated
to trust himself to its drum lie current. But
his "was a ground bog case" and,. sink or
a in, be must go; so lie got on. us nurse,
took off all his clothes, tied them securely
to the top of bis head, remounted, said bis
"now I lay me, etc., ana plunged iu. .1 be
water was not over kuco deep on any part
of tbe ford. Bo it was with me on leaving
Burnet for San Saba, a distance of sixty
miles.- One who has never passed, over tbe
routa before expects at any turn of the road
to pUingo'iuttt,a-howling wilderness with
Indians and grinning "varmints" all around
bim. Every rustle of the twigs by an inno
cent bog causes each separate hair to stand
on end, and be is afraid, of the sound of his
horse s hoofs upon the solid ground. Hut
each succeeding turn of the road brings to
his astonished view neat farm houses and
cultivated fields, lowing herds and bleating
flocks. About twelve miles from Burnet
as you reach the summit of tbe "divide" b
twoen tbe Lampasas and the Colorado
three distinct peaks of tbe Colorado 3ipin
tains rise to view in the plain before you
liko the pyramids of Egypt. These eaks

Buch people as you delight to happen in up
on after a hard day's ride on a mustang,
nite Murcnison a. on this road you strike

ly's Bend." This is a splendid body of just
such' land aa tbat lying below Austin. The

Between the river and this place, i a dis

alone and be sball undertake to furnish a
help meet for turn. But more of Mr. Trip

Chicago, October 7, 1875. I
Editor Democratic Slatetman Having
wandered around this lively city collecting
a few items' (and consideracle mud) your
correspondent will proceed to inform you
what he knows about Chicago:
The Exposition is the principal sensation
at present. The Exposition is considered
much better than that of last year, and the
articles exhibited attract a. great deal of at
tention. The "Power Building" supplies
the power oi running tne maciunerv which
is on exhibition, and also runs the machine
ry used by the manufacturers who are giving
exhibitions of their work. There is a large
variety of agricultural machinery exhibited
also several kinds of steam and compressed
air pumps. Amon the latter is a miner's
Compressed air pump which is claimed to be
capable of pumping 120, 000 gallons per hour.
This pump is supplied with water by a pipe
laid to tbe lake. Two working models are
exhibited a n mr null and grain elevator.
The null, fciinds, tbe wheat into flour
and packs it into miniature barrels in tbe
most perfect manner. .The grain elevator
goes through tbe process of railing the
grain. into the bins, the wbole operation
being visible to tne observer. A toy firm
exhibits some cut ii. us toys, including an
alligator mat' crawls very naturally im
Muttes sweetlj, and a doll that pushes a
wagon around. A tin can factory in the
building turns out caus tiy the hundred,
the machinery being run by boys. There
is a very ingenious brick machine exhibited,
the bricks being forced up through an iron
table which revolves. Tbe display of cereals
from Nebraska acd other Western States
and Territories is very fine.- The grasshop
pers failed to send delegates.
t The Chicago Academy of Science exhibit
some finely mounted specimens of the mor
tal frames of animals, including a mastodon
and other extinct aaiwala, also a -aLack ed
whale.. The sight to be seen ia tlie exhi
bition are so numerous that considerable
time is required to fully examine everything
which is to be teen there. The fair sex is
generally well represented at the Exposition
toe attention oi tte ladies being attracted
principally by the display of femi&ie cos
tumes by Gassage Jt Co., and the fine candy
stand ot Guotber. Tbe hotels have been
very lull siace trie rbow opened, the Palmer
House having Pill names on its regisur yes
terday.
One of the arm-trie, of Chicago is the
"fst mail" araio al.icli runs fr.wn Cbicauw
Vo New York in twenty-ail tioura. Tbe
cars are pain. ted while bdJ each Car i
named after some Governor. There is con
siderable buildir-g bring done here this year.
Tbe district bururd vvcr las yeas taving
built up rapidly with brick and stoue Imiid-
logs." Tho geutU-men here wear very long
frock coati, and a "casual obervet,,,"wHUld
itadgiue be saw a procession of clergymen.
The city authorities are lowering the water
Siains in tbe streets as they v.tre frozen up
la t winter.
The American Bonrd of Missi.ms is in
aesaiiin at present in Chicago, dUcuaaing tbe
moral condition of tha heathen and the
price of red flanneL There is a great nuui
oer of missionaries in the city in prime con
dition.' There art alo about 800 burglars
in tbe city who appropriate tbe plate and
jewelry wbtlo tbe family is at tbe exposi
tion. Trade is quite lively at preseuc aud
the streets are prt-ity crowued.
As you have probably had enolgh of
Chicago, your correspondent will proceed
to "give you a rest," hoping you have eo
dured tbe ibfiiciion with met kuess.

Davenport, Iowa, ou the banks of the
iiisippaJ there is an artificial cave ie u
out oC tirti solid rock, its entrance covered
by a huge stone, reached by stoue steps.
Tbe floor of this subterranean cavity, which
had undoubtedly been made thousands of
years ago, was thickly strewn with ashes,,
the- charred remaius of bonesv'und a sub
stance which tbey took to be leaves, first
wetted, then pressed together, and resem
bling in color and solidity the cake from oat-
mpn.1 AiufinB -tha dry ashes Ihej j)icfced
op three tuyks about "the size of boar's
teeth, and flill lower the boat hta.k came
iu contact with a ban! substauce which
proved to be a skull, brown as polished,
walnut,' periect in every respect, aud of ex
traordinary size. On further examination,
almost a complete skeleton was discovered.
But the most singular part came to light lu
the hardened and almost petrified leather
scrapH, 'bronze buckles, aud a wooden leg
which continued the right extremity, that
limb having been removed about midway
between the hip and knee. ,Tbia is a very,
interesting discovery, proving that a knowl
edge of brorze was among the learning of
tbe aboriginal Americans, and that mechan
ical surgery in those days was equal at least
to the adoption of a timber, extremity.-:.

1 Several yearn ago considerable excitement
was crcaced in Fairmount, West Virginia,'
by tbe 'discovery of traces of an old macad
amized road, extending along the right
bank of tbe Monongahela,"bppostte tbat
place. Recently the government 'engineer
ing parly engaged in the'strvey-of the upper
Monongabela river, with the view Of slack
watering that stream from Morgantown to
Fairmount examined this old road, and
from the members of the party we learn
tbat there1 can be no doubt but that road
rariisbes- another trace' of the long race
tht once dominated the continent long be
frre the Indians eame into-possession,
There are traces. of the road lor nearly
eleven miles along the.narrqw strip of bot
tom land between the hills' and the river,
though at many points the river, having
shifted its channel and cut into the hills.
baa entirely obliterated every evidence of
it. Generally the road is covered by from
two to five feet of alluvium, either deposited
through the course of ages by the river
doods, or else in tha slow' washing off of
the hills skirting it... Its width is said to
be about 'fifteen feet. The most. curious
features about the road is tbat tl.e stone
from which' it is formed appears invariably
to have been' burnt. Wherever the road
shows, itself in the cutting banks, it' is
marked by the black substratum of. .ashes.,i
in aeptn tne broken stone vari.es jrom .six
to eighteen' inches, of irregular fragment.'
The material employed appears to havfe been
largely composed' of boulders-of ired sand
atone, and tbe,coDjeoturo ia Ahat tbey. were
broken alter being made, not,; by water
thrown on them. -There are low (if any)
boulders- in the river Or adjacent' country J
the query arises, Wherecould the- material
bave been obtained to mako this road; i At
ths crossings of the streams there are no
traces of bridge abutments or piers, which
lead to the conclusion tbat the streams
were crossed on wooden bridges.' At many
points the road is covered, with' trees-over
150 years old, which makes it certain that
it was constructed long before the advent
of tbe white man and there are other cir
cumstances surrounding the case which lead
inevitably to tbe conclusion it waa made in
those early ages when a semi-civilized peo
ple inhabited North America, i No one ac
quainted with the habits 'of -the .North
American Indians would give-them -credit
for constructing such a work, of art as this.

very

When the July term of the court passed

preseut time.'. No arrests have been made,

The preachers of Savannah, Georgia,
concur in signing a paper which ad
verts to a subject the Statesman has
discussed. They say that extravagant
customs prevail which entail unon
families in moderate circumstances
debts which harass them for years, and
lead in many cases to intolerable bur
dens. The circumstances of funeral
occasions are such that the nearest
relatives are deprived of all power to
judge with calmness or control expen
ditures with prudence. Often they
satisfy themselves with giving the mat
ter into the hands of others with the
single request that such a burial be
given as custom demands. It needs,
therefore, that custom be wisely regu
lated. After a thoughtful considera
tion of tbe reforms needed and practi
cable, it is suggested that in procuring
coffins and carriages simplicity and
utility be chiefly regarded, and that
display on such occasions belongs to
paganism rather than to Christianity.
The reform proposed of most imme
diate value is that the duty
ef attendance on the part of the
public at funerals should be universal
ly considered as ended when the relig
ious service is concluded at the church
or residence of deceased that the pro
gress to the cemetery and the inter
ment should be private that the fami
mj f w M" wiugn, e&vepi tor pri
vate use. Wealthy Christian men will
readily practice this self-denial when
assured that the keen and pressing
wants of widows and orphans all aboat
us plead for this alleviation. It often
occurs that the fond desire of friends
to obtain the latest possible sight of
their dead prevents the permanent
fastening of the coffin lid until after the
close of the religious service, when a
space is given for those who may wish
to approach the corpse for a last look.
For two reasons is recommended the
abolition of this practice. First, medi
cal science defines numberless dangers
attendant upon tbe presence of exposed
dead Irodies in crowded rooms. Sec-
ndly, our own hearts indicate to us
that the preferable time for this last
look is before the assembling of the
people who wait upon the funeral ser
vice. That in all cases the coffin be
closed before the service, and that the
body be not detained for what often
becomes not only a trying but danger
ous tax upon the already overstrained
nerves of sorrowing families.
