TBE CO.tll.f C HIGHWAY.
With tbe assurance given by recent
rainstorms that crops will be unsur
passed and bread and beef abundant,
the flood-tide of immigration will be
anprecedented, and money, as com
pared with the condition of other
States, must abound in Texas. Brighter
days dawn upon us. Secretary Sher
a still twists the thumbscrews of
contraction and aggravates the press
ure of "hard times" in older States,
driving discontented populations into
Texas. It will be easy to live. Bread
will be cheap, and beef and bacon will
attract tbe hungry and industrious of
all lands. Trade wilj revive, and it is
only needfnl that Austin provide high
ways for its coming. These local im
provements now prosecuted by the
Board of Trade, which has induced
tha oo-operatlon of the county court
the court duplicating the amount gath
ered by private subscriptions these
local highways, when perfected, will
demonstrate the necessity for more
effective agencies for the concentration
of trade in the capital. Along these
local roads farms will advance in value.
The savin? in the cost of the transfer
of each crop to market is the interest
on the sum added to the value of each
acre ot each farm along the improved
highway. A good turnpike commonly
adds from twenty-flye to fifty per cent.
to the value of a farm. A narrow-
gauge railway, reducing the cost
of travel and transportation to
minimum, commonly duplicates
or quadruples the salable value of a
farm. The reason is that the turnpike
must be forever repaired, and to this
cost must be added thitt of horses,
mules, oxen, wagons aid drivers, to be
maintained forever by the farmer. The
narrow gauge repairs and forever re
produces itself, and saves the farmer
time, money and the maintenance of
wagons and teams and teamsters. In
these simple facts consist the reasons
for the preference given by all intelli
gent towns to the railway, and in these
facts we discover the reason why older
communities have abandoned turnpikes
and substituted the cheaper and leBs
costly and more profitable narrow
gauge railways. But now that the
Chamber of Commerce and county
court have absolutely become public
benefactors and accomplished a great
and good work, property values will
be affected by popular confidence in
this public spirit. For the first time
the sluggish capital is aroused. Great
crops to be garnered will demand im
proved facilities for transportation, and
the people of the country, co-operating
with those of the city, will surely be
enabled at last to begin the construc
tion of the cheap railway towards
Brownwood. We only need a Cone
or Bremond or Hurley, and the work
will be speedily begun and the task
easily executed.

Commencing exploraMoos in the park

ered with dwellings,. grouped appar
ently in communities. A little careful

latter generally with depressed centre,
showing excavation for some purpose.
Tbe greater portion of the mound-like

most entirely concealed from casual
observation. Sure indicatiens of tl'eir
proximity, however, was the vast quan

nously indented, painted and glazed

the scattered broken (tottery. Among
tbem one building was found of

lay within a lew yaraa-Af the banks of
the stream, was apparently about ten

could be determined, of nearly all tbe
separate rooms or bouses in tbe larger
blocks, there being no bouses larger,
and Bianv not more than five feet
square. The atones en posed were each
about seven by twelve inches square
and four inches thick, those in their
original position retaining correct an
gles, but when thrown down worn
away and rounded by attrition to
shapeless boulders. Being so exposed
to the elements, the cementing materi
al which bound the masonry together
was entirely worn awny upon tbe sur
face, but upon tearing away a lew
courses it was found binding the rocks
together quite firmly. It wss not,
however, anything more than clay
cement. Farther up the canon, ruins
were found on all sides, tbe great ma
jority consisting of beapt of debris.
with always a central mass considera
bly higher and more massive than tbe
surrounding lines or subdivided
squares. Square buildings, not more
than eight feet square, were often
found standing alone apparently; ao
trace ef any other being detected in

To the right, the long slopes of pro

Jackson made the explorations of the
ruins of Southwestern Colorado his
special study, and one can ponder for
days over tbe tiny representations he
has made of them. Tbe Professor's
account of the incidents of the tour
reads almost like a romance. He as-
serts that the evideuces of civiliration
displayed in these groups of ruins are
far lyond that of any of the present
inhabitants of Colorado or adjacent
territories, though this, of course, mutt
he taken with due crams of allowance
lor the enthusiasm oi au ardeut student
of antiquities. One of the most pecu
liar features which he describes is tbe
system of cave dwellings that prevailed
among these ancient tuoiUR At first
tbe existence of these cuvc dwellings
was unknown to the paity, bin one of
them, sharper eye. I thuu the others.'
suddenly ili-scricd, away up near tha
top or tin; bluir, fully a thousand feet
from their base, perfect little houses
sanelwiclied iu among the creyices ot
the horizontal strata of the rock of
which ihe bluffs were composed. At
the peril of his life, one of the ptrty
scaled tha precipice, and, letting him
self down into one of the bouses, found
the masonry as firm and solid as whoa
first constructed, and so smooth and
even that the casual observer from the
canyon below would not notice the dif
ference between it and the rock itself
once in fifty times passing. But
I neglected liuishing Jackson's de
scription of the massive structure
found in the McElmo, or rather. I did
not tell you all regarding tho surround
ings. Upon tbe came level as the ruins
of the immense building, with ita
monstrous walls and inner circle, and
xtending back some distance, wera
grouped, line after line of foundation
aud mounds, the great mass o( which
wasstoue, but not one remaining upoa
nothcr iselow these croups, some
two hundred yards distant, and crm-
municstinjr by indbtlnct lines of Vc-
Inix, was auditor great v. all, inclos nt
space of two hundred yards or mors
square. Only a small section of this
wall was well enough preserved to pci
mit anything like accuracy in judgirg
ita character and dimensions, tla
greater portion consisting of largo
idgcs, flattened down to such an ex
tent ss to measure some thirty feet
across tbn base and five to six feet in
height. Tbe better preserved portion
standii g was about fifty feet in length.
mn or eight lout in height, and
twenty feet thick, the two exterior
surfaces of well dressed and evenly
laid courses, and the center packed in.
jlidly with rubblo masonry of entirely
liferent charter than tbe rooms hlled
with debris, though it was difficult to
assign any reason for its being so mas
sively constructed. The city built
sgirrV,ftugMcY,iflirj ruo'er-Jiir-ing
buildings in the center, while all'
about were scattered and grouped, the"
smaller structures comprising the sub-.
urbs. And people talk about going
from their own country to find ruins,
hoary-headed as it were with agr, pic
turesque in detail, and above all,
flavored with superstition. But I have
given you barely a hint of one-half the
wonders Jackson revealed in my chat
with him. Indeed, it would take a
book to do it. His description of Man
cos Canyon was peculiarly fascinating, i
many of tbe ruins there rivaling in in-
ten bo interest those oi McElmo. Tha
Cliff House, in the Mancos, wai one of
the wonders of their discoveries, it be
ing first seen one evening just as the
sun was sinking behind the western
walls of the canyon. Perched in ita
little nook, like a swallow's nest, tho'
Cliff Hottso was found to be two stories
in height, and evidently an edifice ot
considerable distinction, as its upper
windows command an extended view
down the canyon, and its position gen
erally that of pre-eminence over tho
entire section thereabouts. 1
The interior was found to le ar
ranged far more elaborately than ia
any other building jet explored; the
windows encased in wood, neatly fash
ioned, the sill and lintels still showing;
evidences of having been stained in
imitation of dark woods, uml most ef
it in roinirkable fair stnte of preserva
tion. Home dihtnnce from this build
in if. and ii on cither ledges, the ruins
of quite a number of smaller buildings
ere found, and all of them ranging.
from seven hundred to a thousand feet
from the bottom of the canon below.
The H v. Arthur Tooth, the Rit-
ualialic martyr, has returned to Lon
don, and religion has broken out
afresh at ht. James's, Hatcbam. He at
once announced that all services which
had been conducted there since his re
moval were schismatical, and the ve
nous appointments which bad been
forced upon his parishioners were null
and void. On Sunday morning, May
13, he entered the church at 8 o'clock,
wearing au alb and white silk chasu-
ble. and accompanied by another
clergyman and a lay assistant, and be
gan the celebration oi tne noiy com i
muoioa. About AOO person WCTA
in the pews. Thcro were two lighted
tapers on the altar. There was no)
cboir, and consequently no singing, it
being what is termed by the Ritualists
a low celebration. Tho service pro
ceeded quietly, according to the us
of ht. James s, until the prayer for tha
Church Militant, when Mr. Fry, tha
Protestant churchwarden, entered tbe
church hastily, accompanied by twe
policemen. Having reached the chan
cel gates, Mr. Fry was met by one of
the congregation, who detained bint -until
Mr. Webb, tbe vicar's warden,
came forward. Mr. Fry loudly called
upon tbe police to take Mr. Tooth into
custody for breaking into tbe church,
but Mr. Webb forbade them to act
without bis consent. Mr. Fry threat
ened to prosecute bis brother war
den for refusing to act, adding that
be alone had tbe keys snd custody
of the cburcb, and all those present
were trespassers, which tbe vicar's
warden warmly denied. After further
altercation between the two cburcb
wardena, Mr. Fry and tbe policemen
left tbe cburcb. After service a large
body of men formed two lins outside
tbe cburcb, between whom Mr. Tooth
was escorted to the vicarage. At the
11 o'clock service a clergyman, who
announced that be was specially sent
by tbe bishop, officiated. After ser
vice several members of the Protestant
League attempted to demolish the
confessional box which stood in tha
baptistry, but after doing some dam
age were corup'lied to desist upon the
appearance of Jr police and one of the
wardens.

Here it is again. The Houston Tel
eerapb. values the good weather at
$100,000 per diem. Some of our ex
chauges say it isn't worth a cent so far
as the cotton crop Iscoucerued. Where
so many doctors diflor, how is a true
diagnosis to be reached? The Jiuiplc
cute will note the following, and let us
hear from it again. We ure uu earn
est seeker after light on this subject:
A great many who have been enjoy
ing the magnificent weather with
which we have beeu blessed since the
1st of October, little dreamed what a
source of wealth it wus to our state.
Every day of lino weather during the
mouth of October was worth 100,000
to Texas, and everyday of flue weutber
ut to the 20th of the present month
will average the same. Thirty days of
fine weather last month added aixty
thousand bales to our crop of cotton,
worth $3,000,000 to our planters, and in
the same proportion will it be up to
the 24th of November. Good weather
is quite an item with us at this season
of the year.
Our dispatches of yesterday stated
that there had been a collision atEu
faula, Alabama. The following is the
fullest information we have been able
to gather about It;
The disturbance was quite sanguin
ary; six whites wounded, one of them
(William Keith) mortally; three ne
groes killed outright, and as far as
heard from seventy-four were wounded,
ten or fifteen pf the number mortally.
Bince the fight five have died, and
three or four more will die during the
night. The difficulty arose out of the

Twenty years ago General Bam
Houston delivered a speech from the
old Capitol Hill In Austin, and in pic
turing the future of Texas to the vast
audience that bung upon the old war
rior's words, he raised himself op to
the full measure of his commanding
height, and looking, as it were, out in
the time to come, said "I see a grand
empire stretched out before me, with
Its busy cities and towns. Its fleecy
fields, its golden grain, lis herds upon
an hundred hills and plains, its looms
and factories, its countless wealth and
laughing prosperity, and I thsnk God
that to your children and mine this
heritage shall come, when we who
have borne the beat and burden of the
day In the dark hours of the republic,
shall have paused from this world's
stage, and are at rest from our labors."
The hero of Ban Jacinto, and many
more of those who laid the foundation
of our present greatness and prosperity
nave fallen asleep, butt be magnificent
empire that Houston saw In the vis
ion which he had on old Capitol Hill,
is beginning to be realized by the chil
dren, and the thousands who are pour
ing Into oar state are rapidly develop
ing Its resources until we can almost
see the fulfillment of bis propbeay.
From New England, from the middle
states, from the west and from oar al
most ruined sister states of the south,
we are dally receiving new clticens,
and the better class of immigrants
from abroad are being attracted to our
borders. We have been at work in
the right way hitherto, but now we are
at work In earnest, and the prospects
are w shall bavs such aa accession to
our population as will be surprising
even to the most sanguine of those
who have lately put their shoulders to
the wheel. The roads are already lined
with Immigrants coming to Texas.
A geutlemau Informs us to-day that,
ou his way from Missouri to this slate,
he saw a very lurge number of movers
with substautlaloutllts.and unmistaka
ble evidences of belug thrifty, who
were bound to different portions of our
territory. The tidal wave of Immigra
tion sets toward oum more than to uuy
other state or the union.
Every section of Texas has awaken
ml tntlin militant nf nhtaluinir nonula
tlon for our fertile lauds, and all pro
per and liberal inducements are being
ottered to those who are looKing ior
homes in a country where none need
want, and all can be independent.
Our future is to be a glorious one,
Already is our state pointed to as one
thai is to shine more brilliantly man
all the others In this great constella
tion of states, and it will not belong
before, to its fullest extent, we shall
see what Houston fancied he saw just
twenty years ago.

No congressional district in Texas is
more honored in the selection of its
representative than the third. In the
person of j: W. Throckmorton we
have a man who, in point of ability,
In our opinion, has no superior, and
verv few eounls in Texas. No man in
the state has received such a flattering
testimonial of regard from the people
as did Throckmorton in the unani
mous nomination by the democracy of
the third district.
The vote polled Tuesday, although a
very small oue, is nearly all for him.
No man in the district could have
beaten him for the office. The people
were determined to reward him, by a
seat In congress, for his yerrs of work
in their service.
Governor Throckmorton came to
Texas when quite a boy, before Texas
bad been admitted to the union, lie
was elected to the legislature as repre
sentative of Collin and Denton coun
ties in 1857, soon after reaching his
maioritv. His course in the legisla
ture was highly satisfactory to bis con
stituency and he was reelected several
times. He held other Important posi
tions of trust previous to the war, and
iu 1866. on the reorganization of the
state government, was elected gover
nor. His career as governor was snort
but brilliant. He was removed on the
ground that he was an "impediment
to reconstruction" iu Texas. Under
his administration Texas, so lately
emerged from the scenes of the wur,
began to build up tier waste places ana
repair her' shattered fortunes. The
judiciary was reorganized; taxeslevied;
the public (lent largely 'decreased.
wholesome laws enacted the ne-
cro. fresh from the bonds of
slavery, was invested by law
Willi all tliu rignis and privi
leges of the white man, and the state
once more placed on the road to pros
perity. J5ut tuis state oi nnairs was
not to last long. The radicals who had
been defeated for office clamored
loudly for the removal of Throckmor
ton, aud General P. H. Sheridan is
sued his order removing him, and ap
pointing E. M. Pease in his stead.
Upon retiring trom onico uoveruor
Throckmorton Issued an address to
the people of Texas, reviewing bis ad
ministration, aud stating the causes
leading to his removal. This address
to the people as he gave up the office
to which they had elected li Ira, in obe
dience to the order of the military
officer commanding the district, will
live in the history of Texas, as the
ablest vindication of her people from
the wrongs of military oppression ever
published. It U a scathing rebuke of
the course pursued by General Sheri
dan, and at the same time breathes the
spirit of true patriotism and submission
to tue laws oi tne iauu.
Besides his eminent service as a pub
lic officer, Governor Throckmorton
has been au earnest worker iu the
cause of internal improvement in our
state, and to his untiring labors we are
indebted for much of the prosperity
we enjoy to-day.
Well may we congratulate ourselves
on the relection of a man of such un
doubted honesty aud splendid ability
to represent our district in congress.

Intelligent people, and particularly
Journalists, who are the most instru
mental of all men in giving publicity
to the merits of a place, are accustom
ed now to judge of tlio enterprise, de
gree of civilization and business thrift
of a community solely by the patron
age or support the community gives its
local press.
We venture to say that the Herald
has done more to mako known the ad
vantages of north Texas,and particular
ly of Dallas, than any fifty men in this
section. The attention of emigrants and
capitalists has beeu riveted upon our
city, as much chrough the press as
through individual effort. It must be
borne in mind, however, that the
sums coming into our cosh drawer are
small in themselves, still iu the aggre
gate the amount received and disburs
ed is considerable. To publish even
so small a paper as the Herald re
quires an expenditure of a thousand
dollars a month a much larger sura
than many large bouses are run for.
Every business man, then, will see tbe
uecessity of paying promptly his
printing aud advertising bills.
There are a clans of people who will
beat a railroad, a hotel, or a newspa
per, whenever they can, and do not
consider themselves swindlers either,
only "smart" or "cute." Now jour
nalism is as much a profession as law
or medicine, and the publishing of a
newspaper quite as legitimate a busi
ness as merchandising, planting or
banking. It requires more general in
formation, as much patience and is
more exacting than any of them. It
must not suffer itself to be beaten.
The profession of Journalism, like
that of law, is cursed with Its "shys
ters." Fellows who have no local
habitation or name, dissolute creatures
who haunt bar-rooms, ten pin alleys
and shows, and prostitute their preca
rious and irresponsible connection
with some disreputable sheet for
drinks, hats, diuners, suppers, free
rides on railways and free lodgings at
hotels bummers whose highest atubi
tiou is an excursion and the ephemeral
association with decent people and re
spectable city authorities. But it would
be as unjust to judge gentlemen of edu
cation and .position engaged In Jour
nalism by such a standard, as it would
be to compare the average city police
court "shyster" with men like Charles
O'Connor, or Beverdy Johnson. Let
us judge men rather by themselves, by
their work, their lives, their talents.
We have heard Journalists sneered at
by business men for not being "good
business men." This depends, bow
ever, upon whether they succeed or
not. Success is never sneered at. It
is always invested with business capac
ity, whether it has it or not That
journalists are not generally "business
men," is owing to the fact that most
professional men are not as methodi
cal, exact and grasping as persons en
gaged In trade, and whose minds are
never occupied with anything but bus
iness and money getting. All profes
sions requiring intellectual ellort and
vulture have tbe effect of making men
careless about money matters, and
the routine of a counting house stand
ard of work. The Journalist, however,
can, and does display as much mere
business capacity, and administratrlve
talent, at the bead of a large city daily,
as tbe lawyer with his multifarious
cases, the physician with his long list
of difierent cares, the broker with his
financial ramifications, or tbe mer
chant with his. array of clerks, and
warehouses of goods to sell and replen
ish. Let each do his duty in his own sta
tion without urrogallng ull the busi
ness tulent to himself or his purtlular
trade, business or profession, and there
will be fewer conceited asses In the
world.
Houston has beeu conceded by Gal
veston to be far ahead of her in mu
sical taste and vulture. This is rather
a mortifying admission for the Venice
of Texas to make. She muy console
herself with the reflection that Sun
Antonio and Dallas can competo with
the Bayou City for the laurel wreath.

There ure many very well meaning
but misguided people, who entertain
the most erroneous ideus and the most
preposterous and arbitrary opinions
about the relations of the press to the
public. We propose to disabuse their
minds of some of tho fallacies they en
tertain, and to define tho duties of the
press to the publio and the duties of
the public to the press.
First The press is constituted by the
publio tbe gfinlians of morality, the
sentinel ou the ramparts of liberty's
citadel. It Is expected to defend the
weak against oppression and to pro'
tect truth uguinst falsehood. The presn
is expected to be always on the side of
religion, law, justice and liberty. The
conscientious journalist is in the posi
tion of a sworn judge, who is to weigh
all the evidence before charging the
public, which Is to bring in the verdict
of the people, on ull mutters of interest
to the commonwealth. As a chronicler
of the events of the day, he should be as
careful as a sworn witness to separate
what he knows from what is common
report. Truth is thu foundation of the
character of a puper. As the publica
tion of a newspaper is a matter of bus
iness whereby men are clothed and fed,
thepublichas no more right to expect
anything to be done gratuitously than
it has to expect the merchant to give
his goods away. A contribution is one
thin?, an exaction or au extortion
founded on a custom is another. Tho
press should aud does do its share to
ward every laudable enterprise, or
worthy churity. But demands of the
public upon its money, time, lubor und
material are onerous, iu the way of polit
ical curds, charitable enterprises, per
sonal nollcej of men in business, which
should come under tbeheailoi'udvertise
nieuts (for every time u business man
is noticed otherwise than apart from
his business, It is virtually an adver
tisement.) Whatever bus the least
odor of the shop about it, is au adver
tisement whether about a politician, n
professional man, a merchant, u rail
road man, a mechanic or farmer. If
the notice is likely to be of any ben
efit it must be charged for as an adver
meut, in the regular way of business.
The press Is bound, by tho terms of
subscription and advertising, to give
all of the local and'geuerul news, to
give intelligent and instructive com
ments on the current topics of the day,
and to protect the subscriber only from
tbe encroachments of the advertiser by
keeping out of tho editorial aud local
columns, pioper, everything of au ad
vertising character, for the subscriber
and geueral reader is most Interested
in the news, and it is not fair to trick
him into reading an advertisement
whether be vvuiils to or not, by insert
ing one in the editorial or local col

A quarterly clrculur of failures for
tlie lirst three months of the year, just
issued by Messrs. Dun, Harlow Co.,
represents, a more favorable view of
the trade of the country than might
have been looked for. There Is evi
dently a wonderful elasticity in our
commercial alluirs and a favorable
outlook for revival of prosperity. Tlie
figures show 1,1.13 failures, with gross
liabilities of $38,873,000, being only
about $73,000 more than one quurter of
the amount of tho previous year, and
neurly $;0,000,000 less than for a cor
responding period In 1873.
From the circular It appears that
thus far In 1875 the failures huve not
becu largely lu excess of previous
years, while the amount of liabilities
shows uo increase whatever above the
average of years. Considering tlie
continued depression in numerous in
terests, the exceeding severity of the
winter, und the lateness of the spring,
the conclusion Is ou the whole encour
aging. It was to be expected thut
numerous wrecks from the panlo of
1873 would be discovered long after the
storm had passed, aud probably not a
few of the above failures can be thus
characterized. The ability to stand
the pressure of these continued hard
times, and the steady ryaiuteuauce of
credit which the above figures imply,
under all the adverse circumstances of
the season, is subject for congratu
lation. AM-OTIIF.RM HEE IS.

Our New Orleans ilispaeli announces

She was a lady of unusual character
and Intelligence, who passed a long
and eventful life us the companion of
un exceptionally great and good man.
Her husband fell us ho would have
chosen to full had he been given the
choice of his taking off, with hi face
to the foe und a eiinnon hall through
Ills heart. He wus in every tense, u
model of the legentlery chevalier,
pair cleans rrjirwhe. A Jupiter To
uuns under tire, iu rest ti simple
minded, child-like man, General Polk
possessed the confidence unil love of
his troops, spite of the jokes they were
fond of getting oil' at his ex
pense, more thoroughly than ai.y
commander in tlie west. His wife
kept constantly near the theater of his
operations, a useful Helpmate no less
than a loving watcher. Since tlie fatal
shot which laid him dead almost ut he.
feet she has liveil in closest retirement,
waiting with Christian resignation the
call to follow her lost chieftain. Her
death, however, fnr all tlie seclnsive
uess of her life, will raise up ninny sad

legrets to thousands of Christian

It is said that Thk Dallas Herald
Is to be purchased by the Texas and
Pacifio rullroad officials. This may ac
count for its recent hostility to demo
cratic usage. Austin Gazette.
The publisher of The Herald is cer
tainly unaware of any such contem
plated purchase. As regards The
Herald's hostility to democratic
usage," it has only to refer to its record
of over a quarter of a century to refute
any Insinuation In regard to its political
faith or honesty. While true to its party
and friends, It has never yet beeu sub
servient to the behests of any man or
clique, or so lost to self respect and in
dependence as lo fear to criticise cor
ruption, di-ljt, ui-sty or error among
members of its own political faith, or
render just praise and credit to au oppo
nent when merited. Tub Herald is
content to rest its reputation with the
people of Texas. No charge of bribery,
sorruptlon or false dealing taints its
skirts In either national, state or mu
nicipal matters. Can the Gazette say
as much?

Ornuue flowers.
Hulls, parties, picnics ami weddings
seem to I io the order of llio day ill Mar
shall. Terpsichore, Cupid uud Hymen
have no more ardent worshippers
than can bo found In that pretty Utile
city, und It must be true us England'
poet laureate warbles, "In tho spring
llmcnyoiing man's fancy lightly turns
to thoughts of love" and, let us add,
matrimony.
Tho last event wus tho wedding of
Charles H. Pepper, chief operator and
train dispatcher of the Texus and Pa
cific railway, to Miss Elizabeth It.
Itoss, youngest daughter of Colouel
I. W. Itoss, proprietor of the Capitol
hotel.
Tho luvlted guests embraced almost
till tho chiefs of tho dillcrcut depart
ments of the Texas and Puclllc, with
whom both bride aud groom ure uni
versal favorites, aud the excursion
party of distinguished railway hieu
which visited Dallas lust 'Friday.
Among them were Colonel Thomas A.
Scott, president of tho Texus aud Pa
cific, uud railway king generally; Hon.
Gulusha A. Grow, president of the Iu
teriiutlonnl und Great Northern rail
way, and ut ouo tlmo speuker of tho
national house of representatives; Hon.
William Walter Phelps, ex-member of
congress from New Jersey; II. M.
Hoxle, superintendent of tho Interna
tional; John DurUMil, superintendent
of the Houston aud Texas Central rail
way; Colonel George Noble, superin
tendent of the Texus and Puclllc; J. P.
Muckny, prlvnto sccretury of Colonel
Scott, and others.
The marriage ceremony was per
formed by Itev. E. A. Wagner, rector
of tho F.piseopal church, at U o'clock iu
the evening, and at midnight the party
seated themselves at tlie hospitable
mahogany of tho bride's father, who
had prepared a feast for tho occasion
Lucullus might huve envied. The par
lors of tho hotel were beautifully
adorned with flowers, tastefully ar
ranged iu the form of wreaths and
bouquets.
Tho bride was tho recipient of a
number of presents from her friends,
and when the happy pair left for Phll-
adelphiuutil A. M., the sleeping car, and
particularly the bridal berth, had also
received their share of floral tributes
from loving hands. The Herald
wishes brido aud groom a pleasant
journey to tho Quaker City, and a safe
return to the home of their adoption iu
tlie Lone Star state.
Homicide Near Nherinnn.
A man named Biddlecome shot and
killed a gentleman named Euds, on
Saturday last. It seems that the mur
derer made bis bot.sts that be would
kill his victim, and quietiy went to
where he was plowing iu a field and
shot him down. He was arrested and
placed iu Jul). From the way things
are going on in Grayson county, a few
llrst-cluss hanging matches might have
a decidedly beneficial effect.

money-Order Rales.

people that there has been a change
mudo in the money-order rates,
which is to lake effect on the first
of July. It seems that by tho
present low rates tho money-order
department has lost a largo sum of
money unnuully. Five cents lias
only been chargod for sums less
than ten dollars, which includes
about seven-eighths of the orders
issued. I!y this rate the depart

conducted nt an annual loss of $100,
000. The now law fixes the rates at
ten cents for anv sum nn to fifteen

BBE3STHAM, TEXAS, FEED AY, JULY 26, 1878.

Rates of Advertising:
Transient and Legal advertisements Insert
ed at 81.30 per square for first Insertion, and
73 rente per square for each subsequent Inser
tion, Nardage and Obltuarv notices, exceeding
etybt lines, Jialf price. Editorial mrticesofa
imrr'y bnsiuess character, 10 cents a line each
insertion.
Announcements will be Inserted at the fol-
loninicra'es: State and District oHm, 810;
County, 37.50; rrecinct, 45. The money to
accompany the announcement, ia eTcry instance.
The woman's rights women are
holding a .meeting at Rochester,
N.T. Lucretia Mott, Elizabeth
Cady SShtDnrPhoebe Cousins
and other distinguishe'd"yourg"
ladies are present.
The; hot weather continues
unabated at St Louis.
Yellow fevor is reported in
the navy yard at New York.
m m n
Thc Pomological exhibition
at Houston closed on Friday.
Thr Potter investigation is
still in progress in New Orleans.
Work on the Santa Fe rail
road bridge across the Brazos
began on Monday.
The Nationals or Grecnback
crs at Memphis, Tenn,, have
frone over to the Rads.
The congressional conven
tion for the Fourth District
meets atBryan, August 15.
Maj J. D. Sayers delivered an
eloquent and forcible address at
the opening of the convention.

The Galveston News wrestles
with the problem: "Is univer
'sal suffrage a failure?" It thinks
it -is not.
Tile, Houston Age pays a
high and well deserved com
pliment to the Democracy of
the Northern States.
Paper flour barrels are now'
made and sold at one-th'ird of
the cost of wooden barrels.
They are also much better.
The Houston. Telegram thinks
.the advcnt.ofanewr apd vigor
ous party in Texas would be a
Godsend to the Democracy.
One of the strong points
made against Ireland was the
fact that he was once a Know
Nothing. This killed him with
the Germans.
A speclu. to the Neil's from
Scguin says the people are very
much pleased with thc action
of the minority in the San An
tonio convention and will go
solid for Ireland. Just so.
j
In the convention, on Friday f
Lang's name was withdrawn af
ter the third hallot. The tenth
and last ballot of the Jay was:
Throckmorton 714 'Hubbard
754 necessary to choice 996.
The Dcnison Herald comes
squarely in favor of thc whip
ping post. We will never be
satisfied that the whipping post
is a failure until thorough tried
in Texas and, found wanting.
The Sherman Register says
that Hon. Sam Evart, of Tarrant,
is the Greenback candidate for
congress in that district. He
proposes to run independent
a la Wash Jones. He will be
left.
Gov. Hampton invites all
citizens of South Carolina now
away from the State on account
of their kuklux doings to re
turn home. The only condi
tion is that they behave them
selves. The present State convention
is too numerous and cumber
some. The resolutions submit
ted propose a reduction of dele
gates to one for every 300 votes
or fraction over 1 50 votes cast
at the next election.
THE CONVENTION.
The heated term still contin
ues at St Louis. On Thursday
there ..was forty-two sunstruck
patients at the dispersary at
noon.
A wild Irishman created a
sensation in a Tennessee town
by running stark naked into a
church. He is a religious mono-
.maniac.

A rumor is in circulation in
Austin that the Central will
build railways from Brenham
-and Waco to Belton and thus
forestall the Santa Fe.
An impression prevails in San
Antonio that a hostile move
across the Rio Grande will be
made on the occasion of the
next raid from Mexico.
1
The committee on platform
declare that is the duty of the
legislature of the state to regu
late the rate of freight tariff on
all railroads in this state.
Wm. E. Gray, known in Tex
as as Dr. Geo. Colletso, is now
in thc New York jail on a
charge of fogery. He raised
only 310,000 on forged papers.
The first bale of new cotton
was received at Galveston on
Wednesday and sold on Thurs
day at 16 cents. It wae raised
by L. E. Newhaus, of Lavaca
county.
The outcome of the San An
tonio congressional fiasco will
be the political death of Hon.
John Ireland. His political
friends had as well prepare his
obituary.
It is claimed, and with a reas
onable show of truth, that the
express companies realize three
fourths of the value of the fruit
crop of Texas. Tariffs are enor
mously high.
The Navasota Tablet says
papers in the "old States" will
confer a favor on young lawyers
by advising them that Texas is
raising her own. supply, and will
hive a large surplus
The jury on the trial of the
tram robbers at Austin, on the
18th, returned a. verdict of guil
ty as to Pipes and Hcrndon,
.principals, and acquittedthose
accessories who are held as be
ing accessory to Bass and oth
ers.
E. A. Oakly, in 1870, 'stole
300,000 from a New York
bank. He succeeded in elud
ing arrest until a few days ago.
At the time of the theft he
was cashier of thc bank. Since
the crime he has at times been
so destitute as to be forced to
beg berad.
Gen. Ord gives it as his opin
ion that a failure to re-elect
Schleicher would be regarded
at Washington as evidence that
the 'people of western Texas
were opposed to vigrous meas
ures to repress raiding, as
Schleicher had been the fore
most champion of an aggressive
policy.

The Dallas Herald says:
"Though supporting Mr.
Throckmorton as a candidate
for governor, it has cast no stain
upon the honor or integrity of
Gov. Hubbard or Mr. Lang."
A contrary policy has been
pursued by no small number of
Texas papers.
The Galveston Nevis has a
fearful time with its "N. A. T."
letters. N. A. T. hardly ever
writes a letter that does not
call forth a reply correcting
some wild assertions. The last
correction comes from Fayette
county, and is a defense of its
fruit growing industry.

Work on the Washington
monument has been resumed.
The foundations will be strength
ened and the unfinished shaft
completed. It has been decid
ed to place on each of the four
sides of the monument panels
of bronze thirty by fifteen feet,
representing bas-relief scenes in
the life of Washington.
The following summary of
the proceedings on the second
day of the State Democratic
convention isv compiled from
thc dispatches to the Galveston
News: Both delegations from
Brazos county were admitted.
Permanent organization was ef
fected by the election of M. D.
K. Taylor, of Marion, president;
John Bookhout, of Dallas, sec
retary; G. B. Zimpleman, Ser-geant-at-Arms.
Committee on
permanent organization report
ed that there being no data fur
nished by which to determine
the representation of counties
organized since 1876, they be
excluded. A minority report
was offered, claiming the right
of lately organized counties to
representation. Amid much
confusion the unorganized
counties were ruled out. A
committee on platform was
then appointed.
The convention was called
to order at 8:30 in the evening.
The chairman said unless the
convention decided otherwise
the two-thirds rule will prevail.
Joel Bryan, of Brazoria, nomi
nated Throckmorton. Gen.
Young put in nomination R. B.
Hubbard:
Bassett of Washington sec
onding the nomination of Hub
bard, asked: Who have been
your governors? Have they not
worthily performed ther duties?
Why was Runnells made gov
ernor in 1857? Because he had
performed the duties of lieuten
ant governor. Why was it, at
a subsequent time, Frank Lub
bock was made governor? Be
cause he had been placed in
the line of promotion, discharge
ing the duties of lieutenant gov
ernor. He was nominated and
elected lieutenant governor in
1863, and subsequently that de
cree was ratified m 1865 at Gal
veston and elected by an unpar
alleled majority by the Demo
cracy making Texas-the banner
state. Now will you undo
what your predecessors have
done in the past to displace
Hubbard? for so the canvass has
been shaped. Are you prepar
ed to register such an edict?
Campbell, of Fannin, nomi
nated W. W. Lang. Mr. E. A.
Jones of McLennan said: Mr.
Lang is charged with being a
class candidate, I would not
support him as such. He rises
superior to cliques and classes."
The first ballot resulted as fol
lows: Lang 210; Throckmor
ton 560 13-20; Hubbard 679
146-165. Dark Horse 4 3-15.
The Brenham correspondent
of the Galveston Netrs, upon
the adjournment of the county
convention at this place, tele
graphed to the Nnt's that a ma
jority of the delegates elected
to the State convention were
Throckmorton men. Again, a
day or two ago, he telegraphed
that three-quarters of the peo
ple of this county were for
Throckmorton. Now Ictus ex
amine the first ballot in the
State convention and see how
near the A'ifi'i' correspondent
came to guessing the vote of
this county: Lang 6 1-2 Hub
bard 13; Throckmorton 2 1-2.
"A majority of the delegates
are for Throckmorton," says the
reliable correspondent. Com
ment is unnecessary. The del
egates are fresh from thc peo
ple and are among the best men
in thc countv.
Jackson is a populous coun
ty in Missouri, and composes a
Congressional district. There
were three candidates, all Dem
ocrats, for the position of con
gressman. It was decided to
hold a primary election and each
Democrat was permitted to vote
for the man of his choice, or
rather, to make his selection of
a candidate by a direct vote.
The plan seems to possess the
merit of simplicity and does
away with the possibility of
packing county and manipula
ting district conventions. It
arrives directly at the choice of
the people.

The Galveston Journal of
Comma ce says live cattle are
being shipped from New York
to London, and asks why the
same cannot be shipped from
Galveston. In central and
southern Texas corn is abund
ant and very cheap. Beef cat
tle can be fed cheaper in this sec
tion of country than any where
else because of the mildness of
the climate and pasturage the
whole year round. If the en
terprise be inaugurated it would
be a source of large revenue.
Cattle sell as readily for cash as
cotton.
The Santa To Read.
The Galveston News of the
19th says Col Flournoy return
ed yesterday from New York
and reports that his mission was
entirely successful. The con
tract for the sale of the Santa
Fe bonds has been satisfactorily
arranged. He and Mr. Kop
perl are highly satisfied with
all they have achieved. All
that remains now to be done is
the acceptance of the contract
by the stockholders of the
company.
That the stockholders will
accept thc terms agreed upon
by Messrs. Flournoy and Kop
perl we have no doubt. This
being done, the .vork will be
commenced vigorously, and it
is within the range of possibili
ties that thc cars on this road
will reach Brenham before
Christmas. The merchants and
business men of Galveston are
thoroughty in earnest about
this enterprise. We shall re
gard the completion of this
road to Brenham as the dawn
of a new era in her onward
progress.
The pomological at Houston
is attracting a good many v.si
tors. The displays of fruits,
flowers and vegetables are very
creditable. Thc Telegram ex
presses surprise that no entries
have been made from Washing
ton county to compete for the
Sioo prize. So far, Fort Bend
county has been the most suc
cessful competitor.
It is has been suggested that
at the congressional convention
the candidates be made to
pledge themselves to support
the nominee of the convention.
will fight it out to the bitter j Delegates will do well to press
end 1 this matter
The platform adopted by the
State Democratic convention
now in session is sufficiently
greenback to suit the views of
the most advanced Grcenback
crs and should do away with
the necessity of a seperatc or
ganization. The difference be
tween the Greenbackers and
the Democrats are so small that
they should be harmonized. The
idea of having "absolute mon
ey" is ignored in thc platform;
the scheme is so visionary as to
be unworthy of consideration.
Public Opinion.
N. A. T who furnishes the
political opinions of the Galves
ton News, has discovered that,
in spite 0. the fact that the del
egates to the District Nomina
ting Convention from the coun
ties of Colorado and Burnet
instructed for Shepard, the peo
pie of those counties, to the ex
tent of three-fourths, are for
John Hancock for congress.
Tim is one of the po!iticalpar
adoxes for whicti the A'cics is so
famous; and, while it may elicit
some remark as a mere curiosi
ty, it will excite-no special in
terest, for the reason that no
body believes that such a state
of things as the Nnos describes
exists. Houston Telegram
N. A. T. has succeeded in ac
quiring an unenviable reputation
for reliability. He always finds
out things that are unknown,
save in his imagination. During
his perigrinations in this con
gressional district he was una
ble to find any but Hancock
men. Everybody with whom
he came in contact was enthu
siastic for Hancock, he found
no Shepard men anywhere,
yet, notwithstanding this una
nimity for his man Hancock,
but one county (Fayetle) in
stated for Hancock, and in that
county Shepard has many
friends and supporters. From
the tenor of N. A. T. letters it
would seem as though his chief
business had been to manufac
ture public opinion in favor of
his man. How far he has suc
ceeded remains to be seen. In
our opinicn, he has done Han
cock no good, but on the con
trary has made friends for Shep
ard. This was not his intention;
however, we can stand it if he
can. The people are abundant
ly able to manage their own af
fairs, and always instruct dele
gations who will represent their
sentiments; hence the instruc
tions for Shepard instead of
Hancock, who is the choice of a
few newspapers and not of the
people.

ex-minister,
ored

STATE NEWS.
Williamson county has no
o deputy sheriffs.
Williamson county
ds will shortly be sold
year
caring
old almond
in Navarro
:ver
the
as-
obliter-
tneir.
ttftlie trades, mechanicl
riculture. He deprccr.
crowding themselves
cities. He said:
we are a necessary cl;
nation all preiuaicesv
suaged, if not entirely
ated." In conclusion he gave
the following sterling advice
"Never advance any man po
litically because he is one of
your color, and never vote for
any one who is not qualified for
the position sought by capacity,
ability and integrity."
Our colored friends in this
county, who ire now beset by
chronic office-seekers, will do
well to cut thc above out and
paste it in their hats for future
reference.
The Hempstead Messenger
acknowledges the receipt of a
call from the venerable father of
Judge McCrimaion of the Bell
villc Beacon. It says thc old
gentleman's hair and beard
were as white as snow. The
Messenger has been imposed
upon. It was not the judge's
father but" the judge himself.
Judge McCrimmon has done
one of two things; he has made
made arrangements to get mar
ried or he has abandoned the
idia of getting married. In
either case it was unnecesary to
paint his hair and beard; hence,
he turned sudenly grey and
was mistaken for his own father.
w
Grow Your Own &Ieat.
Hon. Gustav Schleicher was
given a banquet by his freinds at
the Menger hotel. He an
nounced his purpose of making
ing an aggressive race. "Cast
ing the sheath of his sword be
hind him, he would hereafter
deal his blows with the naked
blade. He would enter the
race unhandicapped. It would
not be so with his opponent"
Ireland's friends assert that he
The Galveston Nezos publish
es a letter from a correspondent
who has very recently travelled
through the counties of Milam,
Williamson, Burleson, Washing
ton, Burnet, Lee and Colorado,
all of which have instructed for
Shepard and the peopl generally
are enthusiastic over the result
This does not agree w ith the
manifactured reports that Col
orado and Burnet counties are
dissatisfied with thc result.
This writer says that the De
mocracy of thc district arc
sound and that there has been
no abuse of the convention sys
tem. He says further that the
nominee of the Brenham con
vention will be elected.
An account of Bass' ap
pearance at Round Rock and
the killing of one of his men as
well as that of a deputy sheriff
has already been published. A
squad of Lieut. Reynold's men
captured Bass about seven miles
north of Round Rock. Bass is
now at that place fatally woun
ded. The Galveston Ncios giv
es a sketch of his life: He was
born in Southern Indiana in
185 1 came to Texas eight
years ago, locating in Denton
county and engagingas a herder.
His first celebrated exploit was
the Union Pacific robbery in
September 1877. His subse
quent exploits arc well known.
The Beltonwniiconsid
crs thc railroad prospects as
flattering. More cotton will be
raised than can be picked out
The committee on platform
and resolutions in the State
convention made their report
on Saturday. The advance
copy of the report is quite vol
uminous. The financial planks
are sufficiently greenback to
satisfy any reasonable man.
The 7th resolution is as fol
lows "We favor one currency
for the government and the peo
ple, the laborer and the office
holder, the pensioner and the
soldier, the producer and the
bondholder." It is also de
manded that the bonds of the
United States be paid in legal
tender notes, except where spe
cific contracts have been made
otherwise.

Nearly all the swindlers,
cappers, intrigures and fraud and
fogery manipulators in the Flor
ida and Louisiana electoral
frauds have been rewarded with
positions by which they can
live on the public crib. Nota
bly among them is Noyes, of
Ohio, who has been made min
ister to France. He is describ
ed as "a red-faced, beefy, sham
bling specimen of a Cheap
John polititian. truly a nice
speciman to represent the Um
ted States at Paris.
Waco has two ice factories;
the consequence is that the citi
zens buy ice at one cent a
pound. Herein Brenham we
have no ice factories; and, if a
man is good looking and behav
es himself politely he can get
a few pounds of ice as a mat
ter of accomodation at six cents
a pound. Brenham needs an
ice factory.
The Denison Herald, a quasi
Greenback paper says the
Greenbackers of Missouri are in
a quandary. The Democrats
have stolen all their thunder
and now they don't know wheth
er to return to the Democratic
ranks or fight it out on the in
dependent line.
The Democracy of Grimes
county are wide-awake and have
gone actively to work. A num
ber of Demecratic clubs have
been organized in different parts
of the county and the white
man's parly feels sanguine of
success
Hogs pay well. They are es
pecially worthy of attention
from Southern farmers. The
crowding of many swine togeth
er, as is usual among large swine
growers of the West, may lead
to disease, as it has in many in
stances; but it is far different
with those farmers who aim to
grow their own meat, with a
small quantity in excess. All
over the South hogs have lately
been raised, and the loss from
disease has been far less than in
any other section. Good atten
tion will do much to ward off dis
ease, while our more favored
climate, with the aid of chufa tu
bers, sweet potatoes, &c, will
enable the Southern farmers to
grow their own meat at a very
small expense of cither capital
or labor. A good cow, it is es
timated, will earn about fifty dol
lais per annum clear of expen
ses of keeping, and a good sow
will produce about forty dollars
per annum, where the increase
is sold off, or even double that
amount where the pigs are fed
and fattened for meat; and this
can easily be done, and at very
little expense where there are
cows, with skim milk to spare.
There is very little food that is
marketable that hogs will eat
twelve months, and yet many
can be grown on almost every
farm, and without adding any
thing to the running expenses
of the same.
Let every southern farmer re
sohe to procure at least one
good brood sow, and to grow
his own bacon, and thus become
perfectly independent of the
pork-packing, trichina-meat pig
gerys that every year make for
tunes by packing and speculat
ing upon the misfortunes and
improvidence of the Southern
farmers. When every one shall
gro'v his own hogs wc will hear
of no more horrible deaths from
eating unsound pork, meat pack
ed for Southern consumption,
of diseased and worm-infested
hojs.
The plan agreed upon to
bring Mr. Schleicher out as a can
didate for congress is to present
him with a formal and official
copy of thc proceedings of the
convention, showing a ballot of
120 to 85, and a letter asking
hunt take the field w ithout fur
ther ado. This he will do, pre
ceded or followed quickly by
Judge Ireland announcing him
self, and then there will be mu
sic by the band. Houston Tcl-fga--
ClosjING out of Summer Dress
goods, brown Linen 15c per
yard, regular price 25c Black
Grenedine 20, 30, 35 40 and 50c;
former price 25 j 40 50 and
65c; Colored Grcnedines 12c
and upwards, rare bargains, at
D W Bloombargh's.
is still- shipping
melons and vegetables to
e North.
BelT county is making ac
tive preparations for a fair in
the fall.
The Comanche Greenback
Appeal is no more. It was very
short lived.
A firm at Navasota propose
handling cotton this seas
on at 25 cents a bale.
Iola Greenback club, of
Grimes county, w ill vote with
thc White Man's party,
A Greenback barbecue
was given in the vicinity of Coon
Bend in Austin county.
A sausage maker is wan
ted in Comanche; the unlicen
sed dogs are to be killed.
The citizens of Sar. Saba
county are now having law and
order the rangers are there.
Four prisoners in the Jeff
erson jail overpowered the jailor
and escaped, on the 19th.
Dallas is being surveyed by
a corps of engineers prelimina
ry to system ofsewage.
Houston is recovering from
its hydrophobia scare. The
Telegiam thinks sunstrokes will
be next in order.
The Beaco'i and Argus are
at war. It would be more seem
ly that they dwell together in
peace and harmony.
The Greenback club at
Houston had fifteen converts on
Friday night. The Age says
they were "taken in."
S. S. Gabert's livery stable
at Navasota was burned on
Thursday. Incendiary. Loss
S 15,000; no insurance.
Thc Bcllville Beacon has a
good word to say for D. G.
Bowers our candidate for tax
collector in this county.
Mr. Joe Turner, while out
collecting bills in Houston, was
sunstruck. By the application
of ice and ice-water he was restored.
The town of Oakwood is be
ing moved a mile from its old
location to a new town of the
same name on the I. and G. N.
railroad.
Another shooting scrape
has taken place in Bell county.
One Taft was shot bvhis broth
er-in-law, Llewllyn. Cause,
tarantula juice.
The opinion is gaining
ground in Dallas that the Mexi
can, Devarra, did not commit
suicide, but was poisoned and
then hung.
The Navcsota 7abret man
weeps and says prize watermel
ons come in very slow. The
producers of melons can't be
bulldozed.
At Navasota a villian at
tempted to chloroform a young
girl 12 or 15 years of age. She
gave the alarm and the scoun
drel escaped.
Thc Chief 'is magnanimous.
It says as the Greenbackers have
no organ in Comanche county
to defend their nomineks it will
remain ncutal on that point.
The BtllvUlc Beacon says
its all a mistake to suppose that
a lie well told and stuck to is
as good as thc truth. The truth
is mighty and will prevail.
The Comanch Chief bran
dishes its tomahawk in thc
air and shouts that as long as it
has one- Democaratic subscri
ber on its list it will will battle
against Radicalism.
Denisori Herald: Texas
newspapers have all come to
the conclusion that they don't
know anything about candi
dates who do not advertise.
The Huntsville Item re
ports that a ruffian from' -'oh:
west" thought to take the townT
A Huntsvillian stepped up and
told the westerner to keep quiet
or he'd knock him down. The
gentleman from out west subsi
ded. Thc old Magnolia House,
near the Union depot at Hous
ton, was burned on the 1 8th
inst, It is supposed to have
been accidently set on fire by
tramps. The building was un
occupied, Loss, giooo; no in
surance. Last week a negro man,
named Wm. Thomas killed a
negro woman.named Celia De
lany, on her return from a part',
near Columbus. He shot the
woman as she was riding be
hind another fellow. Cause
jealousy.
Colorado county will hold-
a primary election on the 3d
prox. to ascertain the choice of
the Democracy for county offi
cers and also congressman and
representative. It will be defin
itily determined whether Shep
ard or Hancock is the choice of
the- people.
A cording to the States
man there is scarcely an end
to the wants of that place, she
wants a woolen miLl, a cotton'
factor', a tannery and many
other things, chief among whii
is a want of enterprise.
That's what is the matter
with Austin.
J. M. Gibbs, a prominent;
citizen of Grimes county, ancf
a leading Republican, publish-'
es a card in thc 7 ablet 'm which,
in response to the earnest so
licitation of a number of the
best citizens, hewithdraws from
the present canvass, lie says?
'And I now surrender these"
prospective honors and emolu
ments in difference; first to the
social welfare of my wife- ancf
children.' "etc.. etc.
On Friday evening when
the south bound train was'
about two miles- below Hemp-i
sterd a white- man and. negro
were observed some distance
a head standing near the track.
As the train approached them?
the white man suddenly sprang'
upon the track in front of the
engine. He was killed in an
instant The man was a rail-'
road laborer, but was unknown
to any of the section hands.
He was about 50 years of age.
It was a deliberate case of
suicide.
A passenger on the freight
train was set upon by seven
other passengers, while the train
was at Hockley and relieved of
35". Four men were captured
and taken to Houston.
The police of Dallas have
been ordered to attend strictly
to duty; they are not to go in
to saloons unless called to make
an arrest; are not to strike any
one unless in self defense.
A mysterious man with
manacles on his wrists has been
camping m the vicinity of 1 run
ty cemetery, at Dafla'?. The
man eludes arrest and is the
cause of much speculation.
The Beacon learns that
the director- of the Santa Fe
railway have instructed the con
tractor to proceed with grading
the road to Bovine Bend in the
lower portion of Austin county.
The Georgetown railroad
is becoming a reality, the grad
ing is nearly finished, and'a large
number of ties have been distri
buted along the line. The con
tractor has gone to New York
t" bin- iron
The National Democratic
Committee met last evening; 34
members were present- The'
political situation was again dis
cussed, and the following resolu
tion adopted:
Resolved, That the action of the
House of Represented ires map
pointing a committee fully em
powered to investigatcanof re
port upon the frauds alleged ttf
have been committed in the late
presidential election, to, the end
that the truth may be known to
the people and the repetition of
such frauds be prevented in the
future, meets the approval of
this committee.
No action was taken on the
subject of issuing an address to
the people, though Mr. Prince,,
secretary, had one already pre
pared, which he was willing tt
have altered. The fo. jwing
committee was appointed to co
operatewith the congressiorraf
committee in managing the con'"
gressional elections this fall:
Senator-Barnum, chairman; Sen
ator Ransom, of North Corblfna)
Representative Ross, ot New"
Jersey; John G. Thompson, of
I Ohio; and Mr. Scott, of Pennsyl
vania. The national committee
then adjourned sine die. Wash
iigton Stat.

The lectures of Professor Proctor, the
English astronomer and scientist, excite ti.e
wonder and admiration of learned people
everywhere. lie telU that it i quite im
poMible, u all must admit who are acca
totntrd to contemplate the bnlliantl atar-lit
heaven of thia low latitude, to contemplate
the heaven! bodiea and not confeaa that
many of them must be preferable aa abiding
place for aentient being to this earth of
oura. Bat it is painfully true that research
es of astronomers can only throw light on
the economy of the universe, and all the
combined learning of physicists, biologut
ana philosophers fails to discover the con
ditions or even facta of life in other worlds.
The theory of the steady progressive devel
opment, growth and extension of planet
life has aroused endless opposition among
good people who imagine that Mr. Proctor's
philosophy is in couflict with their fixed
religious prejudices or convictions. But
this seeming, rather than actual conflict
between religion and science has frequently
arisen, and as often has it been found that
each discovery of true science, instead of
militating against, confirms the statements
of theologists. Mr. Proctor applies facts
and reasoning, evolved here on this, to

Tlien, as the cooling process progresses, the

ulood courses more and more blowly
through the veins of the old of our race.
The pale-faced dying world then struggles
for a time for existence, it grows lustreless,

economy of the universe than any other

o'iscard the idea that the present time, in

tral than any other? Bo tar as planets and
worlds are concerned, this period in the lapse
of iulin te ages is no more central or im
portant than any of the future or the past.
Perhaps the large planets have a longer

ferent stage of development or decay. Be
cause of finite understandings and narrow
views and restricted vision vain and extrav
agant importance is given to the period of
our lives, to the period in the world's ex
istence of which we are aware, aud to the

aSect by their forces the movements of the

tlie'people would-' have reproduced the

gate slmken by a child fell' and broke the
child's leg. A suit for damages followed tinu

A man has no right to remove bis, fence and
leave a ditch or dangerous bdlelortravelers
to stumble into at night. The conditions
of a lawful fence are expressed bv Statute.
An ordinary fence is the common fence of
tiie country, aud is such as will restrain or
derly cattle. Unruly, animals, such as can
not be restrained by an ordinary fence,
breaking iu aiid destroying crops, the owner
is liable for their '"damage The line of
boundary in a board fence is between the
boards ami post; in :t rqil fence Jhe center
of the post; in a stone wall, if not more
than three width at the base, the cxtia
width must be on the soil of the man build
ing the fence. Bounds upon a brook take
the center of the stream upon a tiutural
pond to the water; upon an artificial pond
the line goes in the middle or over the bed
of the old stream. A man may not divert a
stream flowing. through his land in such a
way as to iuterfcre with'the accuftonicd use
of water bv the neighbors below.
The title to land goes downward to the
center of the earth and upward to the
heavens. If your title is good you own the
soil and everything beneath it and above it.
Your neighbor may build up to your divi
sion line, but if the eaves of his house over
hang your land so that the water drips npon
it you may sawttit-iimfTif vou choose. But
if you allow it to go ou without remou
stranee for fifteen years, heacquires the right
to discharge his roof water upon your land
forever after. You may not dig upon your
own land so nearyour neighbor's houe as to
endanger its foundation unless you wall up
or otherwise make safe, in which case you
may dig square up. The whole of a tree
and its fruit belongs to the person in whose
soil are the roots. If the branches of your
neighbor's tree overhang your land you may
cut them oil, but you caunot use them for
fuel or take the fruit if any fallsupon your
laud.
In describing a line, if a deed says due
east from a certain point 150 rods, to a
etuke and stone, and a subsequent measure
ment makes the distance but 100 rods, still
the stake and stone, remain the "bound."
The principle tiling that the measurements
remain aad are to be considered s author-ity-
If a man digs a well upon his own land
and thus drains away the .water from his
neighbor's well, lying at a higher level, the
lat'er has no rcdres, us the law does not
take coyn'zince of underground water
courses, aud a man has a right to dig where
he will and as deep as he pleases upon his
own soil..

Within the last few dnys I have had the
pleasure of inspecting the troutteau of a Prin
cess a real Princess that is to say, oue of
undoubted royal blood, the damsel in ques

of S.txe-Weimar, niece to the King of Hol
land. She is shortly to be married to the
Prince of Keuss. and the firm of Godcherand
Co., on the HueNeuve desCapucines, has
had the honor of prepuring the bridal outfit

Of course, only a portion of this immense

I due crepe de China was trimmed with

A varnett velvet dress was decorated with

beauty ami fluent-. The wedding dreas

was attached to the waisUmnd of the skirt.
It was bordered all around with tbe rows

Tbe pattern was dahliea, forget-me-nots and

For the bride's mother, the Grand Duchess

The other day we were shown what
promises- to be tbe most wondei f ul invention
of the age, and one which promises to revo
lutionize our tintir -duuMaiic and-commer
cial economy. It is nothing less than asub-i.
sillute lor luel, produced at comparatively
no expense,' and by the applicatior of tbe
simplest scK-nlinc contrivance. J no mvett
tiou is deucminated the hydro-carbon burner
and gus generator. By the application of'
the iuveutioii, ordinary coal oil and water
uuiy do made to produce heat enough to run
su engine,' propel machinery, heat a cook
ing stove, illuminate a mansion, or perform
any other result usually accomplished by
menus of wood or ooal. it is an entirely
safe aud practicable method. By' its use
the most difficult mechanical problems of
the age are destiued to' be solved in tbe fa
ture. The. burden of gas monopolies will
be abolished, and the new system of gene
rating' heat from water and oil will astonish
the scientist as well as the unschooled and
undisciplined mind. The process is hot
complicated or mysterious; It is simply
tlie result which overwhelms the mind with,
its wonderful possibilities. Professor Ken
dall, the well-known chemist, who has ex
pel i men ted with this invention, says that
by the use of it he was enabled to produce
all tne fire to do the cooking, washing and
ironing for six persons, for seven' days, (or
fifty cents. The apparatus burns from one
fourth to seveu-eighihs as much water as it
does any combustible fluid, and prod aces a
result w bich cannot fail to fill everyone with
amazement who witnesses its operations.'
For household purposes it is unexcelled.
There is no dust or tilth, smoke or disagree
able scent about it. Gas can be furnished
from any cooking stove for illuminating a
house for fifty cents per thousand feet. The
light produced is clear, bright and steady,
far superior in quality and illuminating
properties to the ordinary gas produced
troui coke, which costs from $3 to $3.50
per thousand feet. The attachment can be
made to any ordinary stove, range or fur
nace or motive power, with entirely satis
factory results. The invention only needs
to be examihad to have its merits fully ap
preciated. Capitalists and persons desiring
Iroin curiosity or other motives to see the
invention in operation ore cordially re
quested to call nud witness Its result.' A
patent baa teen procured, and tbe inventor,
a very eminent scientific gentleman, ia pow
prepared to enter the held, and capture tbe
public with an invention obvious on sight,
and more useful to civilized man than could
be the realization of the wildest dreams cre
ated ity the alleged claim of the Keeley mo
tor, which has heretofore created so much
comment and interest in the public mind.
The simplicity and practicability of this ap
paratus can be determinated on sight,- and
it is destined to go into general nse as soon
as its merits become known to the public--.

A stout, ferocious-looking fellow stepped
forward, with a common wicker basket 'of
the country, which he begged we would
carefully txumine. This we accordingly
did it was of the slightest texture, and ad
mitted the light through a thousand aper
tures. Under this fragile covering be placed
a child about eight years old, an interest
ing little girl, habited in tbe only garb
which nature had provided for her, perfect
of frame and elastic of limb a model for a
cherub, and scarcely darker than a child of
Southern France. When she was properly
secured, tbe mnn, with a lowering aspect,
asked her some questions, which she in
stantly answeied, and, as the thing was
done within a few feet from the spot on
which we were seated, tha voioa appeared
to come so distinctly from tbe basket that
felt at once satithed there was no decep
tion.
They held a conversation for some mo
ments, wben the juggler, almot with a
scream of pxssion, threatened to kill her.
The child was heard to beg for mercy, wben
the juggler seized a sword, placed bis foot
upon the frail wicker covering under which
Ins supposed victim was so piteously sup
plicating his forbearance, and, to my abso
lute consternation and horror, plnnged it
through, withdrawing it several time, aad
repeating the plunge with all the blind fe
rocity ol an ex:ded demon. By this time
bis countenance exhibited an expression
learlully indicative of the most frantic of
human passions. Tbe shrieks of tha child
were s reul and ao distracting that tbey
altnoat curdled for a few momenta the whole
mass of my blood; my fi;at impulse was to
riihh upon the monster ani fell him to tbe
earth; but he was armed and I defenseless.
I looked at ray companions they appeared
to tie pale aud paralyzed with terror; and
yet tht sc feeuuus wer somewhat neutral
ized tiy the conociousuess that the man could
not dure to comn.it a deliberate murder in
tbe broad rves of dav, st d In-fore so many
witnesses; aiill the whole thing was appall
ing.
The blood ran in streams from tbe baa
ket the child was heard to struggle noder
it; her croana fell horribly on tbe ear; ber
struggles smote painfully npon tha heart.
The former were gradually subdued into a
faint moan, aad tbe latter into a slight rast-
lin sound we seemed to bear tha last coo-
vuUive gasp which was to set her innocent
soul free from the cored body, when, to our
inexpressible astonishment and relief, after
muttering a few cabalistic words, the jug-
frier took no toe traaket; tout no cnua m
to be seen. The spot was indted dyed with
blood but tb re were no mortal remains,
and, after a few moments of nndisasmbled
wonder, we perceived the little object of
oar alarm coming toward aa among tha
crowd. She advanced and sainted us, bold-
ire ont ber band lor onr donations, which
we bestowed with hearty good-will; she re
ceived tbetn with a most graceful salaam.
and the puty left ns well aatiafied with ear
more than expeetea vratoity. what its
dored the decrptioo anora extraordinary
was that the man stood aloof from th
crowd during the whole performance there
was not a person within several feet ef
him. Front FtMCi "Live f th Cvnpirm.

One other scene may properly be added
to! this brief record of the struggle and tri-
umphs.of old .New York.: Tiiers came a
sunshiny day in April. 1789. when George
Washington, president-elect of tbe United
states by tbe unanimous voice of the people.
stood on a Daieony in front or tne ben ate
Chamber in the old Federal Hall on Wall
street, to take tbe oath of office, An im
mense multitude filled the streets and tbe
windows and roofs of the adjoining bouses.
Clad In' a suit ef dark brown cloth of Amer
ican manufacture, with bair powdered, and -with',
white silk stockings, silver shoe
buckles and steel-hilted dress sword, the
hero" who bad led the colonies to their In
dependence came modestly forward to take
np -the burdens that peace hsd brought.
Profound silence fell npon tbe multitude aa
Washington responded solemnly, "I swear
to belp me God." Then, amid cheers,
the display of flags, and tbe ringing of all
the' betls ia the city, our first President
turned to see tbe duties his countrymen
bad imposed upon him. In sight of those
who wpuld have made an Idol of him.
Washington's first act was to seek the aid
of other aid than his own. Ia the calm
sunshine ef that April afternoon, fragrant
ith the presence of seed-time and tbe
promise of harvest,' we leave htm on his
knees in old Bt. Paul's, bowed with the
simplicity of a child at tbe feet of tbe Su
preme -Kuler of the universe. beribner.

i "Tjtn. pabcocx. in conversation witn nis

VOL. IV.
THE FCTCRE CONDITION AND 8TA-
TIS OF THE NEGBOLS.
Now that the Democrats bare swept the
field, and the glad shouts of rictory echo
and re-echo over the broad land now that
the fierce passions which were kindled by
the war and made more bitter by the out
rages of an usurpiBg corernment haregiTen
way to fraternal feelings, and the white
wings of peace stretch over the country;
now that crime and dishonesty ba; been
rebuked, and the minions of power signally
condemned, it becomes necessary for the
Democracy to prepare to grapple and deal
with the great questions of the day in a
broad and statesmanlike manner.
Much of the trouble and outrages to
which the South has been subjected by
Radical greed and hate can be traced to
the enfranchised negroes. That party
sought the control ol the eeyoral
States as a necessity for the protection
of the negroes. This was only a pretext,
as we know. Their real purpose was to get
In position, so that they could manipulate
the treasuries. But all ef these issues from
a Radical stand-point have passed into his
tory, except the fate of the civil rights bill,
which ma or may not be acted upon by
Congress at the next session. As the mem
bers elected on the third instant do not
take their seats before the fourth of March
next, in tho second session of the Forty
third Congress the Radicals will have ample
time to show what they have in store for the
country.
The eyes of the world will be turned to
wards the Democratic party so soon as they
shall come into power, in order to find out
their policy and purposes. Among the
great questions which will require solution,
will be the one of the condition and status
of. the negroes. It has been all along con
tended by the Radicals that Democratic
successes would destroy the liberty, rights
and privileges of the colored people and
they were for many years deterred
from voting and acting with them
on account of this assertion, which was
persistently dinned into their cars. But
Democrats as pertinaciously declared that
such was not the fact, and that they would
be as safe and free under Democracy as in
the embrace of Radicalism. In the course
of time Georgia and Texas overthrew Radi
calism, and thus removed the shield which,
it was alleged, was the only safeguard of
the negroes. At first we verily believe that
tho poor deluded creatures expected to feel
the bloody hand of the Ku Klux, and
looked for them through the black shades
of night But, aa weeks and months rolled
on, and they were aa secure, and much more
prosperous, than under the spoils-bearing
party, they began to see that tho whites of
the South were not the terrible and vindictive
enemies that they were induced to believe.
Tho great speech of the Hon. Carl Schurz,
of Missouri, also did a vo3t amount Osgood
for them, and pointed eut, in clear and
plain language the true road to political
freedom and the security of personal rights.
We have reason to believe that that speech
did much good. It was a great light at a
point and from a source which carried con
viction of tho honesty of purpose and wis
dom of the plan proposed. The negroes at
the elections held on the third instant
proved by their votes that they appreciated
the situation and intended in the future to
maintain their political as well as their
personal freedom. Louisiana would have
been lost to the Conservatives, had it not
been for the votes of the colored people.
Caddo Parish had twelve hundred majority
of colored voters, and yet that parish gave
six hundred Conservative majority. In
New Orleans there were several colored
Conservative clubs, and the Democratic
majority of the city was largely increased
by their votes. In other portions of the
State there is indisputablo evidence which
shows that they voted with the whites.
Jn Alabama great many did bkewise.
The riot in Eufaula was caused by a col
ored man who voted the Democratic ticket,
and was about to bo mobbed for so doing
by men of his own color, but the whites
defended him at the risk of their lives and
severely punished tho aggressors. The col
ored people having given proof of their sin
cere desire to work out their political fu
ture to tho best advantago for their race,
they now stand before tho American people,
as a body, asking that their condition and
status may bo wisely and humanely deter
mined.' While it is true that the fourteenth and
fifteenth amendments were not adopted ac
cording to-tho provisions of the Constitu
tion, still, by acquiescence and indorsement,
we have recognized their validity and it
would not be wise or well to disturb them.
The great dam&go has already been done.
We have passed through the cruciblo of ne
gro suffrage and have reached tho broad
plane of human rights with a full conscious
ness of their importance and influence.
We cannot turn backwards, if we would.
But, fortunately, there is now no necessity
for such a course. The colored voters can
be led to see, as many of them do, that
whatever is good ia the government for the
whites, will also bo for their benefit; that
their interests are identical that when the
whites suUer, they will suffer; and when
the whites prosper, they will also. The
only difficulty which remains for solution,
is tjie one f qualified suffrage, which
would be applicable alike to alL But this
would bo attended with dangers which we
may not now be able to avoid.
Therefore we think that it will be wise
for tho Democrats not to disturb the condi
tions of suffrage by an educational or a
property qualification but to dlcUt Ld qve
tim to 1 rcttd upon eto jhHU Utxitt ivn
the election of all officers and representa
tives, there should be no restriction upon
voters, except for conviction lot some fel
onious or infamous offenie.
