SHALL, THE NEGROES BE EDO
CATED f
If the positions assumed by us in relation
to the fourteenth and fifteenth amendments
are correct, then it follows as a corollary that
tho negroes should be educated. This is
one of the leading questions which Demo
cratic administrations in the several Stales
will be called upon to solve in accordance
with the progress of the age.
Before the war, when they were held as
servants, their owners were prevented, by a
stern necessity, forced upon them by med
dling abolitionists, from educating them,
because by so doing they would have crea
ted a magazine on every plantation, which
would have been liable to explosion at any
moment, followed by scenes of rapine and
murder that would have been appalling.
Incendiary documents, pamphlets, and
papers would have been secretly distributed
among them which would have advised
and urged them to rise np as one
man and overcome their owners. But as
they could not generally read, the abolition;
ists could only operate by the underground
railroad, which only carried the negroes off
to the loss of their value to their owners. It
was a source of great regret to very many
who owned them that, even then, they
were prevented from educating them and
some did, at their own risk," teach them to
read and write. Had it not been for the in
terference of the fanatics of the North,
they would have been taught the elemen
tary branches of an English education.
But those days have passed away forever;
and the question, stripped of itaorbidding
aspects, comes up before the Southern peo
ple for solution. We say, without the fear
of successful contradiction, that the colored
people will find in the South friends who
will be pleased to do all in their power for
their intellectual, social and political ad
vancement. But in saying this we would
not have any one to infer that we would
advocate mixed schools or the amal
gamation of the two races. Goci created
them as a separate and distinct race, and
gave them a country that waa divided
from the rest of the world by natural boun
daries which, m the early historr of man
kind, prevented in a great degree tfie ming
ling of the races. Looking upon nature
as perfect in her works, and not wishing to
disturb the perfect harmony of those great
laws that are everywhere manifest, from
the smallest blade of grass to tho loftiest
mountain range, we desire to give to the
negroes every advantage that may enable
them to improve themselves intellectually,
morally and physically, and mount to the
highest standard of perfection for which
their natures are capable. In thip, the
white man has nothing to lose and every
thing to gain.
Tho colored people, "when educated as
they should be, will make better citizens
and better laborers. They will learn to
look upon civilization aa something more
than the restraint of the vicious and punish
ment of the criminal. The rewards and
pleasures that spring from the order and
harmony Qx;iety will be new incentives
to progress. Instead of falling into all
kinds of excesses, and filling our jails and
penitentiaries with convicts, they, will
struggle to gain a place and a name with
the good, honest and industrious citizens,
to become useful members of the communi
ties in which they live.
Tho money that will be expended in
their education will be well spent. The
property which will bo taxed for the pur
pose will bo made, to the extent of the
taxe3 paid, that much more valuable, and
the advantages that will be reaped from
their improved labor will be the source of
additional profit. There will also bo the
time and expense of attending courts, as
witnesses and jurors, in the trial of negro
criminals, saved. Property and persons
will be safer, and better men will be chosen
by them as officers, where they may have
the majority.
In becoming better citizens, they will
own more property, and will have greater
inducements to assist in suppressing crime
and enforcing the laws.
If, then, it is a settled fact that the ballot
will never be taken from them, it should
also be established as a corollary that they
shall bo educated. The one should not be
allowed unless the other Is guaranteed. As
there is no intention to deprive them of the
ballot, educational facilities will be pro
vided, and thus the questions which have
disturbed the country will be fully and"
finally settled in favor of universal Bullragc
and education, with such exceptions as are
usually found in all general rules.
But it may bo asked, who doubts the
willingness of Democrats to educate the
negro I Suffice it to say that there are
doubts expressed. Some persons are moved
to make accusations against Democracy
from hatred and revenge some on account
of ignorance of principles, and others be
cause they desire to see Democrats stumble
and f all.
It is much easier to retain powcrThan to
regain it when lost. The Democrats, after
fourteen long years of misfortune and dis
tress, have prospectively regained the gov
ernment. They cannot expect to rule un
less they show capability and fairness.
-Democracy governs for the good of alL
The humblest citizen in the land is entitled
before the law to the same protection as
would be awarded to a millionaire and
President.
Let us make no mistakes; and, above all,
let us fulfill our-promises. By so doing,
tho people of the whole land will be bene
fited, peace and prosperity assured, and
Democracy established forever.

to all arguments in favor of a law to restrict
exorbitant rates, we quote the following:
The Statean (for which we entertain
the highest regard) has labored industrious
ly to produce some argument with which to
convince itself and others, that usury is an
evil that ought to be suppressed by law.
From the tenor of all the articles that have
appeared in its columns, it was plain to be
seen, there was not a firm conviction upon
its part that the selling of money as other
property is boM, ouht to be prohibited, or
demanded that species of class legislation
which it advocated.
now cool and refreshing! Ah I now we
have it. The TiUgraj perhaps, only
wishes us to understand that the opposi
tion of that paper to an usury law is not
real, but feigned. If we were not firmly
convinced of the necessity of Buch a law,
then we doubt our own existence and the
sanity of tho editor of the TtUgraph. Bufc
we will accept of the proposition of that
paper and make our readers the jury to de
cide between us. What then? How many
have sustained the Telegraph. Two or three
papers while the large majority that havo
expressed an opinion, coincide with the
Statesman and sustain our views and as
for the people, except the money lenders,
tne7i generally, warmly sustain our position.
We have published tho opinions of several,
and have more of the same Bort which will
be given to our readers.
But the TUgrap has attempted te cx-1
plain the difference between money as a
"medium of exchange" and money when
not a "medium of exchange." To show
the extremity to which that paper is driven,
we give another extract, as follows
We havo tried to show the Statesman
that when a dollar was in hand it was not
a "medium of exchange," but property.
That it only became a medium of exchange
when it was put in circulation that if A
sold a dollar to B, and B sold it to C, and
it passed as property, being bought and
sold from A down to Z, it was not a medium
of exchange at any time during all these
transactions. Its functions were suspended.
When Z paid his butcher or his laborer, or
his wife shopped with it, its functions were
restored. That, being property, it would
be doing violence to the property owners'
rights to say he should not have what his
property was worth. That is our position,
in very few words, and cannot be mistaken.
Now, the above is a remarkable distinc
tion, and makes tho whole question lumi
nous Now we see. If a man pays ever bo
high for money, it is all right, if he will
only hold it lock it up and keep it like other
property in other words, makes no use of it
then it is not a "medium of exchange", but
if he uses it as a "medium of exchange,"
then it is not like other property, and
should not be let out at high rates. If this
is not the legitimate construction of the
extract, then it means nothing. But we all
know that men do not borrow money to
keep in hand, to lay up in banks, to look
at as a. fine horse or house for which a rich
man may have no particular use. When
money is borrowed, it is for the purpose of
being used as a "meilium of exchange" in
the transaction of the business pursuits of
life. Then aa such medium it should not
be taxed with high rates, because it is a
public necessity and is not at all like other
property. The higher the rate, the dearer
will be the price of every necessary of -life,
if it should be generally loaned at high rates
and if not generally so loaned, then it will
fall with crushing power upon the man who
is compelled to borrow at exorbitant rates.
We only called the attention of the Tele
graph to the opinions of other papers beside
the Statesman for the purpose of showing
that paper that there were various reasons
for enacting an usury law, and that we were
not singular in our views. But we do not
agree with the Telegraph that tho article of
the Fort Worth Democrat was "timid" nor
that of the Commercial a "tirade." They
were- founded upon facts and the necessities
of the people. The article from the Ennis
Argus was good and so was the letter of
Mr. Kcmble. Surely they will have rcn?
weight with our cotemporaryt
We are not writing these articles for
amusement, but to call the attention of our
leading men to the necessity of protecting
our citizens from one of thejblighting curses
of our present Constitution which was
framed by men who gambled in money,
reputation and political positions; whose
god was gain; and who, as a general
rule, cared not for the necessities of the
citizens nor the development of the State.
Again, we quoto from the Telegraph, as
follows
For the extortioner, the Shylock, the
man who preys upon the wants and necessi
ties of his fellows. the human spider, who
weaves his web and without remorse de
stroys the human fly caught in its meshes
for all these we have as holy a horror as
either of the highly respectable journals
named; but they are found in all trades,
professions and avocations. We may hate
Shylock and his brethren, but "let us be
just, though the heavens fall;" and, in ac
cordance with that maxim, we are opposed
to usury laws.
Now, our cotcmporary must excuse us if
we doubt his sincerity in the above. He
may believe that he is sincere, but the posi
tions assumed do not warrant such a conclu
sion. Men who put out their money at ex
orbitant rates invariably become Shylocks,
however humane they may be at first, un
less they decline to collect from the needy
and lose by their transactions. We mean
men wo make it their business to lend
money. Experience and facts prove this.
If, then, the law may be invoked to punish
a man for forgery, counterfeiting or swind
ling, it is equally necessary to pass a law to
puuish a man, in some way, who gambles
in the necessaries of life. Money is a ne
cessity cf trade, and of exchange, and should
be guarded by wholesome restrictions.

CAPITA!, AND LABOR.
History proves that there is not that har
mony between capital and labor which
should exist. For the last few years .there
has been a very strong tendency to increased
antagonism, which has filled the Old and,
to some cxt.nt, the N.w Werld with
gloomy anticipations. The majority of
mankind are poor and dependent upon their
daily labor for support. Tho decree to this
effect went forth when Adam was driven
from the Garden of Eden. He was told
that in the sweat of his face he should earn
his bread. This is considered by many as a
great calamity, and they vainly look for
some expedient which will relieve them
from toil. It is an error, which generally
prevails, that the man who owns his mil
lions and revels in splendor and luxury
Is tho happiest of mortals. Without
stopping to investigate facts, and car
ried away by the gaudy trappings
and glare of costly equipage, they axe
led to the conclusion thar riches are indis
pensable to enjoyment; and therefore fall
out with their own condition, and. complain
of the cruel injustice of society and the ac
cidents of fortune. Such men do not de
serve wealth, for if they were possessed of
a competency it would not be wisely dis
bursed. The evidence of their incapacity
is made apparent by their senseless com
plaints. We would not'be understood to take the
position that riches are not desirable. By
no means for if properly and wisely used,
they can be made instrumental in doing a
vast amount of good, and thus -be the
source of genuine and lasting pleasure to
the owner. But when used simply in the
gratification of sensual appetites and vain
pursuits, they bring pain and Borrow, dis
appointment and disease.
While we have no sympathy for the
constitutional croaker, who would have
riches bestowed upon him by some
freak of fortune, we do feel for the
laboring millions, who, by divine decree,
are entitled to just consideration and com
pensation for their labors. Not such men
and women as form tho Communes of
France and fiercely scowl at those who
chance to be able to surround themselves
with the comforts of life, but for the pa
tient, the needy, the industrious, who ask
for an equivalent for their labor, which is to
them an absolute necessity. It is just here
that avarice and injustice creep in and at
tempt to defraud the sons and daughters of
toil of a right coeval with the necessity for
labor. Here is where the real antagonism
begins which has no justification or pallia
tion. It is carried into the business of life it
is studied, so as to enable the man of means
to obtain the work of his poor neighbor at
prices wholly inadequate to the labor per
formed and the necessities which exist.
Here is where the great crime bcgins'to
crop out, which threatens tho peace of the
world and the welfare of mankind. If the
cases were only occasional, and confined
to a few individuals, their wrongs might
be redressed by tho local courts of justice
and the wisdom of the communities. But
it is organized into companies, and under
the forms of law sanctioned by our legis
lators, pervades the land and gathers the
money, much of which should remain in
the hands of tho laborers. Then it hides
behind forms and ceremonies which mis
lead the simple, and secure immunity from
censure and punishment.
Witness the huge monopolies of the
land tho banks, high tariffs and manufac
tories where money is unjustly taken from
the individual, and weary hours of toil de
manded for stinted compensation, which
leaves tho poor creature with the marks of
pinching care and craving. want. Again,
tho exorbitant rates demanded for the use
of money of the toiling masses call out for
redress.
This latter evil did not formerly exist to
any great extent. And when an individual
so far forgot the right as to wring from his
fellow-man extortionate rates for the use of
money, ho was dramatized as a Shylock,
and held up to tho execration1 of mankind
as a miser who would tnd the face of the
poor, or take thelast pound oJkah for un
holy gain.
There should not .exist any conflict be
tween capital and labor. A just compensa
tion for work done is all that ought to be
demanded. We neod both. Labor is a ne
cessity which cannot be dispensed with.
In agriculture, in the workshops, factories,
mines, gardens, orchards in building, and
in many other occupations, labor is essen
tial, and should receive ample compensa
tion. It is true wisdom to act upon this
principle; its good effects will everywhere
be manifest in the increased and superior
productions. More efficiency, more faith
fulness,' will be tho result. There will be
fewer jars and mere harmony ia soci
ety, and a higher order of prosperity.
The poor will not then look upon
the rich as their natural oppressors, but as
friends who have a proper regard for their
rights and a correct appreciation of their
wants. We, at the South, do not yet fully
comprehend the evils which threaten the
body politic from the clashing of capital
and labor. It should, therefore, be the aim
of our people to take time by the forelock,
and so regulate the relation that when a
vast population shall cover our fertile
plains, there will be order, harmony and
unity in the land.
The North is now threatened with serious
consequences from this cause, and many
look forward with apprehension and fear to
the future.
Samtei. Cox, who goes by the name of
"Sunset," and ia a representative in Con
gress from New York, has the profound

THE RING OF TRIE METAL.
The New Orleans Picayune of the tenth
instant has the following article in relation
to the returning board, which has the ring
of true metal:
From the official notice published in an
other column, it will be seen that the re
turning board is convened to assemble on
Wednesday, the elsventh, and inaugurate
the canvassing of the returns of the lata elec
tion in Louisiana. Let us all hope that this
work, the final and crowning act of the
great political campaign through which we
have just passed, may be consummated in
peace and quiet. No public body ever met
in this State with a more grave and respon
sible duty to perform. As it behooves the
canvassers to be wise and just and fair, bo,
also, is it needful for us to be patient, cau
tious and watchful. It is well understood
that our people do not intend to be wronged
out of a single jot or tittle of their rights.
They do not mean to give up the fair fruits
of their hard Won victory without a strug
gle, compared with which other struggles
are as child's play. Nevertheless, they will
be calm and temperate in their judgment?,
avoiding excitement and eschewing rash
conclusions. The returning board shall do
its work in quiet, free from molestation,
and the people's verdict on the result be
formed in cool and dispassionate delibera
tion. Momentous things hang upon this
issue. Our people do not condemn or im
peach the returning board beforehand; they
only remember 1872, and are resolved to
have justice this time at any cost.
The Picayuna is right. The citizens of
Louisiana have gained another victory at
the ballot box, and they should not permit
any set of men to defraud them out of the
fruits. Let the citizens be firm. The coun
try will sustain them.
LUNATIC ASYLU9X.
We have been favored with the report of
the board of directors and medical superin
tendent of the. Lunatic Asylum of the
State of Texas, from the tenth of February,
1874, to thirtieth September, 1874, D. R.
Wallace, superintendent, which show's that
tho institution is doing great good for the
poor unfortunates who have been bereft in
whole or in. part of reason. The number
of cures -effected will compare favoiably
with similar institutions of this country
and Europe. But the buildings are not
near large enough to accommodate the
number who will apply for admission, if
we may judge by statistics, which
give the average number of insane
as one to every five hundred inhabitants.
Tho Legislature should, therefore, make
ample provision at once, so that the af
flicted may receive prompt attention and
relief before too late. We append table of
the -number on hand, restored, im
proved, etc.

One reason why we feared the result of
a Democratic victory, such as we have just
witnessed, and why we labored to prevent
it, was because of the Buffering negroes and
white Republicans of the South. No mat
ter how much the reports of the outrages
upon these classes may have been exag
gerated, and no matter how ignorant the
one and corrupt the other, the fact still re
mains that they have bcon mercilessly and
Unjustly persecuted. The Democratic
party Las professed that it only desired
power to do justice to the whole people,
and because the Republican administration
had oppressed the Southern whites. We
have a chance now to compare its profes
sions with its performances, and to see what
equal and exact justice, as measured in
Democratic scales, means. Philadelphia
Press.
The Press need not be concerned about
the negroes of the South and tho Republi
cans have hitherto shown a wonderful ca
pacity for taking care of themselves. The
Democrats intend to do what is right, re
gardless of the hypocritical tears f such
papers as the Press. They are perfectly
competent to administer the government
for the good of all who really desire peace,
harmony and prosperity.
When wo need advice, such papers as
the Press will not bo consulted. The fu
ture will provo how basely the Southern
Democrats have been slandered.

The Hon. J. Frazer Brown, a member of
the Fourteenth Legislature from Saa Saba
county, visited our office yesterday. Mr.
Brown is a man of ability and a fine
speaker. He favors the speedy settlement
of the International Railroad muddle; and
believes that the company should be dealt
with in a spirit of morality and fairness.
He also warmly favors an usury law as in
dispensable to the welfare of the people and
the development of the State.
Dkatii of Mr. W. J. Cain. Tho Houston

Bastrop AJccrtiser, and also at one time
connected with tho Christian AJeocaU, of
Galveston, at Bastrop last Saturday even
ing, the seventh inst. He was buried by tho
Masons and Odd Fellows.
The Western Stock Journal has been pur
chased by Messrs. J. M. B. and W. W.
Lawhon, and will be edited by Colonel J.
D. Logan, an old and successful editor of
the West. Tho paper will be neutral in
politics, but devoted to the 6tock interest
and the development of that section. We
wish the Stoci Journal the most abundant
and ample success.

Tho overwhelming defeat of the ad
ministration In the lute Connecticut
election is attributed solely to the third
term question, by the administration
papers, and Is made the subject of a
long article iu the Indianapolis Jour
nal, the accredited organ of Senator
Morton. It allects to believe that but
for this incubus, the result would have
been different, weak us the stato ticket
was and, with profound respect for
the motives, diameter and military
and civic services of General Grant, is
conlideut thutu brilliant victory would
have been achieved had the party de
clared boldly and emphatically against
the third term, and nominated for
governor a man of pronounced opposi
tion thereto. In other words, this
usually astute political journal, lufer
eutiully, is confident that the action of
the administration in Louisiana and
Arkansas allairs, and the known policy
of hate of the leading republicans iu
both branches of congress toward the
south, have been endorsed in Connec
ticut, so fur as they were issues in the
canvass, by a pronounced voteof "si, (MX)
against 4-1,000, the number of votes re
ceived by Greene, Oie republican can
didate and third term partizan.
Such nonsensical twaddle, offered lor
the double purpose of screening the re
buke administered to the policy of
Senator Morton uud cleuriug the presi
dential field for its apostle of hate and
venom, is an insult to the intelligence
of the country. The third term ques
tion was hy no moans an issue hi the
canvass. It was forced in hy the op
position, not as an Issue for it could
be passed upon directly when forced
upon the people of Connecticut by the
radical party buj simply as a tantal
izing question to Mr. lilalue and other
republicans who were suspected of
anti-administration tendencies. The
great pertinent Issue and, outside of
trifling local questions, the only one iu
the canvass was emphasized by the
,-cpublican party in the nomina
tion of Mr. Greene, the r.eaious
the batttle was joined, and upon that
issue alone was it decided, and the
true American, both north and south,
rejoices that the verdict was in favor
of peace, reconciliation and good will.
While unalterably opposed to a third
U-rm, the intelligence of the country
will scout the idea that that question
was the cause of the defeat of the ad
ministration party in Connecticut in
tlie late election,

Three years and nine months ago
to-day 1 became a citizen of this (own,
ami huve closely watched its growth
ever since. Plain fuels ure better ar
guments than hypothetical sugges
tions. How stootl Dallas then
Within the present municipal limits
there were not exceeding from twilvo
to fifteen hundred inhabitants. Now,
by fair estimate from the best data, we
have full twelve thousand. Then,
there wus not a business house oil the
court house square. Now, the center
of business is four hundred yard- from
it, and tiiere arc numerous respectable
establishments on four different streets
widely upurt, more than a mile from
tlie court house. Then, the present
residences of three-fourths of the ex
isting p ip.ilation were c ivured with
forest trees, brush, branibleand briars.
Then, tlie nearest railway depot was ut
Groesbeck, one hundred and twenty
five miles distant by the road, audi
wus tight days iu moving from there
to Dallas.
Now, by three different routes, we
have semi-ilaily connections by mil
with every state in the union und Can
ada. Then, we hud four churches,
now we have fifteen. Then, a rise in
the Trinity stopped truvel. Now, with
the rlvc three-quarters of a mile over
the bottom, tiie iron bridge mid cross
ways bid defiance to Hoods. Then, we
had no foundry. Now, the third one
is ubout starling. Then, about three
blacksmith shops; now, about twenty-live.
Then, one weekly uewspnper.
Now, two duilies and four weeklies.
Then, no bank, now four. Then, no
court house, now the finest one iu the
state. Then, two or three feeble Sun
day schools, now ut least fifteen large
ones. Then, a few struggling schools,
now a large number of excellent grade.
Then, two flouring mills, now five.
Then, our receipts iu cotton were next
to nothing now, we have a magnifi
cent cotton compress and cur ship
ments rank .next to Galveston, and
Houston iu the state.
Then we had two hotels, old style
now we boast several first-class and
one being finished. Then we had twu
livery stablci now ten or twelve.
Then we had not over ten indifferent
brick houses of nil sorts now proba
bly two hundred, and many of them
flue and costly. The ostofllce, Pacific
depot and many larve business houses
now stand in hut was then a field.
Tiie market bouse (nil other large es
tablishments now etaud where then
was a large, impassable barauca In
the suburbs. Our planing mills stand
now in the midst of business and resi
dences, where thickets then abounded.
Our cut ire suburbs now, then barren
prairie or copses of wood, are dotted
with pala'ial residences, beautifigl gar
dens and thriving orchards.
Look where we may, we find the'e
and kindled changes from woodlands,
ravines and thickets to centers of busi
ness, of ehurches, shops, residence,
gardens, tc If any town of the west
or south has ever made greater progress
in the same length of time, 1 am un
able to recall it Besides this wonder
ful change has taken plane wilbin less
tban three years, when the first cars
reached Dallas in IS72.
With such facts, supplemented hy
the great aggregation of capital in our
town the great enterprise of our mer
chants, mechanics and business men
of all pursuits, and the general unity
and spirit of our people, is there left
any ground for the vocation of croak
ers, set-fasts or pull-backs 7
Keep on in tbe same way let unity
prevail and oar career will continue
to be upward and onward,

Gen. Bragg was in the city several days
during the put week. lie referred, in the
course of a conversation Friday, to the re
cent assertion of Gen. Sherman that he bad
suspected Mr. Jefferson Davis of complicity
with the assassins of Abraham Lincoln.
Gen. Bragg said that he had seen Mr. Davis
tested in cases of thin sort, and while two
effort were made by hired asuassius in 1804
to destroy Mr. Davis's life, he never mani
fested the least signs of resentful feeling.
It waa in June, 130-1, if we uiiituke not,
that Mr. Davis, when riding, as was his
habit, each afternoon, along the defences of
Richmond, waa fired at, the bullets passing
very near his person. Once, afterwards,
he waa fcbot at when in the outHkirta of
Sicbtnond. But we propose to set tie' this
question finally. Dahlxrea'a raid w ill not
soon be forgot'en. We publish a paper
furnished by Gen. Bragg. It is a photo
graphed copy of Col. Dahlgreu's address
to his followers. The address, as written
and signed by him, was memorized and
spoken or read to his followers. They were
disguised aa spies, and were captured as
spies inside of the Confederate lines. They
came, as stated, to murder Mr. Davis.
When they were captured and this paper
was found on the person of this man
Dahlgren, Mr.. Davis's Cabinet and
his military staff concurred in declaring
that the prisoners should be executed in
accordance with the laws of war. Presi
dent Davis, after reading the paper and
finding that he especially was designed to
be assassinated by Dihlgren, peremptorily
forbade other treatment than that accorded
to prisoners of war. Benjamin, the wily
Hebrew, it was who caused these papers,
takei. from Dahlgren's person, to be photo
graphed and copies given to several gentle
men in Richmond Gen. Bragg preserved
this, given verbatim as follows:
Headquarters Third Division,
Cavauit Cows, 18G4.
Officer and Men You have been se
lected from brigades and regiments as a
picked command, to attempt a desperate
undertaking an undertaking which if suc
cessful will write your names on the hearts
of your countrymen in letters that can never
be erased, and which will cause the prayers
of your fellow soldiers now confined in
loathsome prisons to follow you and yours
wherever you may go.
We hope to release the prisoners from
Belle Island first, and haing seen them
fairly started we will cross the James river
into Richmond, destroying the bridges after
us and exhorting the released prisoners to
destroy and burn the hateful city and do
not allow the rebel leader Davis and his
traitorous crew to escape. The prisoners
must render great assistance as you can
not leave your ranks too far or become
too much scattered or you will be lost. Do
not allow any personal gain to lead you oil
which would only bring you to an igno
minious aeatn at tne nana ot citizens.
Keep well together and obey orders strictly
and all will be well, but on no account
scatter too far, for in union there is
strength. With strict obedience to orders
and fearlessness in the execution, you will
be sure to succeed.
We will join the main force on the other
side of the city, or perhaps meet them in
side. Many of you may fall, but if there is any
man here not willing to sacrifice his life in
such a great and glorious undertaking, or
who does not I eel capable of meeting the
enemy in such a desperate fight as shall fol-
ow, let him step out and he may go home
to the arms of his sweetheart and read of
the braves who swept thiough the city of
Kchmond.
We want no man who cannot feel sure of
success in such a holy cause.
We will have a desperate fight, but stand
up to it when it does come and all will be
well.
Ask the blessing of the Almighty, and do
not fear the enemy. U. Dahlgren,
Col. Uom'd.
Guides, Pioneers (with Oakum, Torpedoes
and Turpentine) Signal Oftr. Qr. Mr.
Com. scouts and picked men in Rebel
uniform.
Men will remain on N. bank and move
down with the force on S. bank, not get
ting ahead of them, and if the communica
tion can be kept up without giving an
alarm, it must be done, but everything
depends upon a surprise and no one must
be allowed to pass a Dead of tne column.
Information must be gathered in regard to
the crossings of the river so that should we
be repulsea on tne 8. side, we will know
where to re-cross at the nearest point. All
mill must be burned and the canal destroyed j
and also everything which can be used by
the rebels must' be destroyed, including
the boats in the river. Should a ferry bout
be seized, and can be worked, have it moved
down. Keep tha force on the S. side
posted of any important movement of
the enemy, and in case of danger, some
of the scouts most swim the river and

This age, like every other, has its pecu
liur advantages, to beset against the unde
niable losses which have occurred to man
kind from the decay of authority and
reverence for law from the absorption and
one-sideduess of a busy and material epoch;
from the disturbance which physical science
has given to traditional beliefs; and from
the general spirit of questioning, which for
the moment involves truth as well as error
in suspicion, and weakens the fastenings of
morals.
The gains which maik our era are so
large and decided, as to leave the balance
greatly in favor of our own time as com
pared with any previous period. As the
latest born, we are the heirs of the latest
and longest experience. If there be any
progress in the growth of a common wis
dom if the varied experiments of succes
sive civ.lization are of any worth; if all
past history, literature and poetry have
not been thrown away then, aa the' heirs
of all preceding times and epochs, we have
the vast capital and the accumulated wealth
of knowledge and practice which the past
has garnered.
The spirit of the age, like its politics; has
broadened into true democracy. It is fav
orable, as never before, to free and univer
sal inquiry. In every time students and
philosophers have sought to fathom the
laws of nature and of mind, but it was re
served to our generation to popularize this
knowledge and turn the contributions of a
few into the common enrichment of all.
When a branch of knowledge has been cul
tivated for ages and still remains inaccessic
ble to the world at large, it shows that
something radically deeper, richer and more
beautiful must supervene. This law is il
lustrated by many things'. All books were
once perpetuated by copying with the hand
whoever would possess a velume must pay
for the tedious toil of transcribing it. The
utmost rapidity and cheapness in this mode
of multiplying books could not bring them
within reach of the public. IIow was the
difficulty to bo met? By some meaner pro
cess, which should at once cheapen books
and dcterioiate their beauty and value to
the sa.Tie degree? Far from it. The means
of making the poor man a reader and owner
books lay- in a glorious new art that
clothed all literature in a bodily frame of
surpassing beauty, and placed it in the
hands of the people in a form that the great
est kings would have coveted and which
even Homer or Cicero would have welcomed
as the material pedestal of their immortality.
This art, bo simple and universal, was not
lower, but immeasurably higher, than its
predecessor, whose services were for the
narrow circle of the learned.
Up to a recent date -coaches and high
roads furnished nearly the only modes of
locomotion. The more these modes were
perfected, the more expensive and select
they became, and journeys were restricted
to a small portion of the community. To
popularise traveling, make it democratic,
and turn all men into travelers, an' inven
tion grander and cheaper than had then
traversed space waa required. To move
the rich was needed only a four-horse
coach, running six miles an hour;' but to
move the masses railways were required,
compared with which the Appian and other
ways of the journeying Caesars were value
less. So it must be with all human knowl-
enge. A petty magnet is snrncicnt to taxe
up a small class of isolated persons; but
when the people are to be attracted, there
nothing less than the earth that will
draw their feet. It is not too much to say
that more has been done in fifty years than
in all past ages to enrich human life with
knowledge and dis vvery, inuring directly
to the pleasure and profit of the masses of
mankind.
There is such a t hi rig as evolution. The
turnpike evolves the railroad, the twelve
passenger car the eighty passenger car, the
home spinning wheel the thousand spindle
factory, the school house singing class the
oratorio and Boston jubilee. The spirit of
change and of advancement and elevation
is literally in the air. The destruction of
the old is merely the evolution of the new,
and wise men, instead of experiencing de
spair and alarm, will adjust themselves to
new conditions until scientific feudalism
shall have passed away, and all knowledge
belongs directly to the broad industry of
mankind. We have seen this process going
on in the history of 'civilization and of re
ligion, and are about to witness the same
thins in the progress of science and of
thought.
THE Tit UK FOUNDATION OP COOD
COVBKK.VIErsT IN TEXAS.
The Rusk Obterter is wise and
just
and expounds popular opinion, as
de-
fined by the press of Texas, when it says it
would gladly have the sphere of operation
of the exemption laws enlarged rather than
restricted. This will be done. The time
may come when children will be reared with
the distinct conviction that while they are
honest and truthful their credit is illimita
ble. Then the country's laws will proffer a
premium to honesty, and civil courts will
constitute no refuge for rogus, Men will
pay, everywhere, as in cities, as a matter of
honor, and none will have credit unless
known to deserve it. The moral tone of
society will be elevated by the credit sys
tem as it will then exist, and a man's word
will at last be as good as his bond. There
fore, until Radicalism shaped the policy of
the law, it waa directed to the repression
and prevention of litigation now, that
courts and sheriff and constables and judges
and witnesses and idlers may be multiplied
and the people compelled to maintain them.
these costly courts are multiplied and the
gateway for fools to enter is widened and
people spend time and money in suits cost
ing the industry. of the country infinitely
more each year than all sums collected by
sheriffs and paid over ia tea years to plain
tiffs. In practice and la morals the whote
coarse and results of litigation, like lie ex
istence of civil conrts fur tie col
lection of debt, are wholly unfotunate,
alike to society and individuals. There
fore, in Civilized countries, litigxion is
heavily taxed and a lawyer detected "n pro
moting it is stricken from the rolls. Here,
in the South, to the amazement of maikind,
the people are governed by those wh pro
mote litigation even by legislation. These
would repeal all exemption acts and abso
lutely have the whole community conast of
two classes known in the books as lam
tiffs and defendants. It is in refereice to
this question that we are disposed t dis
trust a class of lawyers. Their interests
may lead, as they vainly imagine, iu one,
and those of the masses of people is the
opposite direction. It is to this class that
the South, while ruled by adventirers,
owes that change in public policy vhich
has proffered premiums for litigation.
These have multiplied courts until, in
Mississippi, their number has been doibled
and cost quadrupled and whole districts
and communities are 'together by the
ears" literally "woolling" one another.
The lawyers, and those happy fellows who
pocket fees and costs, gain all that the
ignorant devils lose, and a delightful state
of society is generated commonly described
by letter writers and real estate brokers as
terrestrial hell.
The selfishness of creditors constantlv over
leaps itself and defeats its own purposes.
When a debtor becomes insolvent it is only
needful, in order to render ultimate pay
ment forever impossible, to turn him over
to the tender mercies of constables, 'bailiffs
and shysters. He first hates, then contemns
his race, and finally loses respect for himself.
In ninety-nine cases out of each hundred
-the citizen once utterly crushed by raven
ous creditors never rises again. Society and
law lose a friend, and gain an enemy, and
family of miscreants supplants one which,
left with means of subsistence, would toil
to regain all of money and fame that had
been lost. If the law be changed let it be
irected in prevention of litigation, in re
pression of the gambling spirit begotten
of courts and in rendering the beggary of
the poor of Texas utterly impossible.
God has given this people peculiar bless
ings. Statesmen alone are wanting' to
make Texans the truest patriots. The first
measure to assure this result is to create and
protect the family. The household in its
unity and strength and purity is the State,
and each household in Texas should be an
indestructible independent republic, and
thus make Texas the greatest and freest and'
most blessed of commonwealths. Pro
tected in property, and a premium thus
proffered to marriages, families will be mul
tiplied and the number multiplied who are
deeply interested in maintaining law and
order and good government. Protected in
property, each family can educate its own
children, and thus the State is not substi
tuted, as in Prussia, for the family, and we
need not accept Bismarck's philosophy and
make the State a despotism because it is
everything supplanting both the family and
the church.

MONEY I OTEKETH MOT SIN.
Strange and fearful news often, flashes
over the wires, but never stranger or more
fearful than that which comes now, an
nounoiog that Henry Ward Beecher's sal
ary, as pastor ot Plymouth Uhurch, has
been raised from twenty thousand to one
hundred thousand dollars. In the first
place what did a preacher want with
twenty thousand dollars, and why, in the
'name of Christianity, must this man's re
ward, just at this time, be placed at an
enormous sum. Its magnitude, .piled up
from year to year,' dwindles, into signifi
cance beside the sad lesson it may teach.
But a month ago it was announced that
Plymouth Church had, lately, during the
Beecher-Tilton trial, increased its comma
nicants one thousand, and yet the world
well knows there has been no revival of re
ligion, in any community, at all commen
surate with the increase of membership in
this church. In truth, it may be doubted
whether Gotham has one more Christian to
day than it possessed twelve months ago.
A thousand more communicants, and a bun-'
dred thousand dollar salary, is the brilliant
result of the great church scandal. If
Christianity has not advanced in New York
and Brooklyn; if the good cause has not
been-growing and adding scores of thou
sands to its ranks elsewhere, what can it be
that has made religion so popular,- as
viewed through the portals (of Plymouth
Church. A thousand new communicants in
a few months, added to one congregation
alone, and that congregation singular in its
increase, is something wonderful to con
template. The question arises, is it amoral
or immoral impetus that has given a new
life to Plymouth Church? If moral, why
have not all the churches grown as 'it has,
and why have not communicants been added
to them in general? The Beecher-Tilton
trial has been one of the most unblushing
passages in the history of society in this
country, and whether Mi. Beecher is inno-
nt or guilty does not. alter, the case.
An intense degree of interest has
been thrown, into it, and, the social
condition of the country bas been damaged
by it. The scandals connected with this
have been tbe subject, for months,of refined
intercourse. It has been the popular theme,
liberally discussed between tbe sexes, and
the press has been filled with the recitals of
offensive evidence. It is said that the tele
graph companies alone have realized be
tween three and four millions of dollars
from the transmission of messages concern
ing it. The excitement produced has been
well measured, and we are free to say that
it is not of a divine nature. Can it be sup
posed that the one thousand converts have
been brought to Mr. Beecher's church in
consequence of his purity and approach to
divinity? Not so. Plymouth Church has
grown through a morbid excitement, a
strange fascination, that has lately gathered
about it, in consequence of impure, as we
opine, rather than pure and holy thoughts.
And as a sequel to all of this, to tbe scan
dalous and -immoral "teachings practiced
upon society through the trial, Plymouth
congregation votes Mr. Beecher an annual
salary of one hundred thousand dollars.
Must he be rewarded with gold for his sup
posed puntv, or must his implied guilt be
hidden beneath the wealth of a Croesus I
This last act of Plymouth congregation is
a disgrace to Christianity,, and should be
treated as each by united Christian com
munity. If Mr. Beecher is pure, he needs
no reward greater then that he has hereto
fore received, and if he is a good Christian
he will not accept it. If he be impure, dne
hundred thousand dollars a year will only
add to the" moral deformities of himself and
thousands of others. '.If crime is fastened
upon him, and many believe it, this great
salary will popularize tne features mat nave
lately made Mr. Beecher so notorious. It
were best could the Christian world, just
now. draw a veil over the enormities of
Plymouth Church, and consign them to ut
ter oblivion.

to state theixjriews andiikdge themselves
to some course. We want the old Constitu
tion with what more is neceasary oa the
negro freedom points; then we want all
State and eouaty offices filled by tha vote of
the people, and noae. except judges, elected
for a longer time than two years, and think
four years tone- enough for judges. Short
terms are -IeMsocrsatev-aa4 aaake mea feet
that they are servants of tba people. t
want a change in tbe school law and tba
road law, and we think about one-naif of
tba number of judges we now
have is
enough, and that the old salary in good
money is enough and that ninety members
in the Legislature is enough, and fire dol
lars per dy sufficient pay. 1 he stale debt
should be limited.
I am afraid that lawyers, who charge such
fees aa they do for a little work, won't
favor retrenchment, and will want to pay
Urge salaries. Where tbe carcass is, there
will the eagles be gathered. I do know,
from near aixty years of experience, thar an
increase of; salaries has made a.largein
creaseof thieves iu 6fffce, for the love of
money is the root of alleviL1 Now' with
out a pledge of a man's course in the con
vent ion, our side of the river will not sup-
nAlti.n.li.).(u 1.... I a.
LivikMuuiuavw ,1 v .RU1 UUiil-Sb, lUUUgllt-
ful men, that will lay down and carry out
a good, plain foundation of government.
We don't want demagogues or buncombe
speakers, but deep thinkinr men and smart
-enough to tell their thoughts in plain
words. I think a good constitution can be
made in twenty or thirty days at most, and
that the Legislature should only meet once
in two years; and that a three months' ses
sion is long enough. Changes in laws, so
much, keep farmers and mechanics in the
dark. I could say much more but think it
unnecessary; but if the substance of what
I have written is not attended to, we shall
vote no convention and if a constitution
is formed not on these principles, when
submitted we will reject it.
lours, A JKrvKBSON Democrat.
IIOW TUB STRESS IS WRECKED.
It was he of the Navasota Tablet, we be
lieve, who. made the famous race for the
mayoralty of the town. Properly, despite
his many virtues of intellect and morals,
he was sadly defeated. No man conduct
ing a newspaper, worthy the name, with
honors and power incident to the position
when well and worthily filled, -would aban
don it for the chief magistracy of the town
or city. The moral power and actual oper
ative influence wielded by half a dozen
newspapers of Texas and the press is yet
in its infancy and unorganized and uncon
scious of its vast consolidated force the
active influence for good or evil of half a
dozen newspapers is infinitely greater than
that wrought by any politician or office
holder. There is honor for a journalist in
cultivating good taste in letters and in
morals, in shaping wisely the course of
pablic opinion, in elevating, popular aspira
tions, in making the country conscious that
there are loftier aims and higher purposes
than those of party leaders who, when
about to create a new government for' a
mighty and growing people, cannot rise
above the unworthy selfishness and narrow
prejudices begotten of struggles for offices
and spoils of office. Even as the office
sought by a candidate for a place in the
constitutional convention is higher and
more honorable to him who fills it well than
that of the statute law-makers, so the office
ot an editor is higher and a more responsi
ble trnst than that discharged by any local
placeholder whose- administration is dis
tinguished only by the promptness with
which he draws his wretched stipend.
Occasional messages or vetoes are impotent
for good or evil while editorials constitute
themes for-daily thought and conversation
of the town and country. They shape pub
lic opinion, and, intangible and trifling as
they may seem, are transmitted from one to
another conversationalist and from one to
another generation, and break at last, like
the ocean's billows, on the remotest shores
of futurity. But we were only going to say,
wCen the first sentence of these was writ
ten, that the Press. Association of Texas
will never command the respect of me peo
pie till its memoers respect themselves and
each other. They toil, many of them, with
tireless assiduity to destroy their own po
tency and their own respectability. "The
profession, in popular apprehension, is an
unit, and whenever an editor destroys an
other in public esteem he wounds himself
and degrades his trade and whenever an
editor would abandon bis profession and
newspaper to become a mayor he should be
lectured by the fair editor of the Fort
Worth Standard, or whacked on the head
by the moral poker and power of the in
imitable Leader.
SOUND DodTRINES IN THE HELTON
DISTRICT CONTENTION.
There is sound sense in the platform
pdopted by. the late Senatorial district con
vention at Belton. It attains the end, and
very nearly by the same means, proposed to
be secured by the restriction of suffrage in
corporation to freeholder. The Belton dis-
tnct convention when nominating candi
dates for 'the constitutional convention,' re
r
solved
"That no municipal government in this
State shall incur debt for internal improve
ment, or otherwise, except by a vote of a
majority of those owning real estate lu such
municipalities."
Those who must pay can alone create tbe
debt. This effected, Governor Coke's prop
osition to restrict the powers of counties
and cities, vesting these powers in the Leg
islature, may well be withdrawn. People
know better what they want, and how to
accomplish it, than their representatives at
the State capital, and therefore the need
for the broadest local freedom, and there
fore the absolute need for the restriction of
suffrage in corporate elections. Everybody
In Texas, free, white and twenty-one, will
approve this proposed scheme of reform
when it is comprehended, and we are pleased
to see tbe district convention at Belton mov
ing in the right direction. It was not un
wise when it declared that no man should
vote, in any election, who has not paid his
poll tax..; Bat this law might operate to
lessen our representative strength in Con
gress by lessening the number voting for
members of tha Legislature. Xo such evil
befalls us when we declare that none save
freeholders shall vote in municipal elec
tions.
Tax Lampaaaa Ditpatch Is done for. It
will catch it now. Jt has dared to suggest
tba possibility of 1 wrong in the king, and
the king can do no wrong neither the
Governor nor Ireland. We bold our breath
to bear the "Organ." The Dupatch says:
"The Democracy must retrench, or ac
knowledge the corn and quit."
Tbe Dupatch becomes at once in tbe four
eyes of the two organs a Radical organ.
No man can differ from Ireland or Gov
ernor Coke even about local matters and be
otber than a Radical, and then again this
same Ditpatch dares to lay its unholy hands
upon offices that Governor Coke has filled
with good and true men. By neceasary
inference bis Excellency is condemned
when an office be baa irourisbed is pro
posed ta ba abolished. Tbe Dupatch says
Tb abolishment of tbe offices of Adju
tant General, Assistant Attorney, Superin
tendent oi ruinc ins trocuon and Superin
tendent of Immigrattoa wilL verv likely.
ba effected by tha approaching convention.
Tbev are supposed to be offices of doubtful
propriety ami tbe people are tired of sach
Vf
curses.
Tns. Marshall Herald says of our pur
poses when we have sought to make people
comprehend the infinite importance of mak
ing the wisest men in Texas members of the
constitutional convention
Hon. John Ireland Is merely used as a
means to an end tbe disintegration of tha
Democratic party. The Statesman and its
allies, aim. at a Jiigher- object than the de
feat merely of Mr. Ireland's political aspl-
rations.. ..Their 'object cannot be accom
plished without the disorganization of the
Democracy, and they attack Judge Ireland
because be advised the preservation of the
party organisationi
We condemned Ireland because the peo
ple contemplated the creation of a new
government for Texas and Ireland sought
to make the process an ordinary, vulgar,
little party conflict, even when Radicalism
only lifts -up its hideous head in Texas to
swallow occasional crumbs from the White
House table. There is no need for parti
sanship, and only for statesmanship. But
Ireland is head and front of the Democracy
of Texas, and he liked to show himself in
this capacity and be conspicuous and noisy
and active, and thus patriotic to an eminent
degree. But it is all welL It has made
the people tiilnk and evoked good men,
and proper nominations have been generally
made. w
Tub Telegraph man suffers his temper to
get the better of his discretion.: The pub
lic is 'apt to crown him victor who main
tains his good humor. But when a Tellow
gets to be an "organ" his feelings have
double force and it can hardly be expected
that he would maintain perfect equanimity
when his honest pocket and honest opin
ions are both assailed. The extravagant
seal and nervous sensitiveness of the Hous
ton Organ overleap themselves. It resorts
to epithets, and none are more intolerable
to a Democrat than to have that of "Radi
cal" applied to him, and thus the Houston
Organ, when sorely puzzlod and maddened,
calls the Statesman "Radical," and in
doing this confesses its consciousness of our
devotion to Democracy. If the man were
not crazed with passion, and because of
thwarted purposes, he would have thought
of this. But Ireland's luck and this finan
cial fog raised about the State Treasury
have crazed the Telegraph.
Would it not be well to authorizo the
Governor, in the new Constitution, to veto
any clause in a general appropriation bill,
as well as the whole bill! The President
of the United States has not this power, and
the consequence is he is forced to approve
confessed frauds iu order to keep the wheels
of government moving. The spirit of re
form is most exacting, and since it is de
manded that sessions of the Legislature shall
be few and brief, nothing will be more dan
gerous pr ruinous to the people than the
hurried legislation of the last days of each
session. Kentucky's Constitution comr.
short, very short, legislative sessions, and
it therefore happened, at the last of these,
that fifteen hundred bills were left undis
posed of, and those passed went through ia
unutterable confusion. The Governor, in
view of these facts, should have the un
restricted veto power.
Tot bonded debt of Houston is $1,530,-
207.85 the annual interest thereon $140,
000.52; the revenues of the city amount to
$164,300; this leaves the small sum of
$24,203.48 to defray the annual expenses.
which, of course, is not sufficient, tbe ex
penses amounting mi io, vou. i Uia muvw
an annual deficiency of $93,730,53. And
Honston pays one and one-half per cent, oa
real and personal property.
And such must bo, sooner or later, tho
condition of any city whose government
falls into the hands of those who are only
interested in one side of the account be
tween the people and the placemen.
A San Marcos correspondent writes on
the Fourth as follows: "The millennial
era of tbe centennial is already being fore
shadowed. The Stars and Stripes were
yesterday morning unfurled to the breeze,
for the first time since tho war, from Har
per's Hall, by Major Donalson, a veteran
of the Mexican war, assisted -by an ex-Confederate
soldier. The "old flag" was
saluted by the brass band with various pa
triotic airs, amid general manifestations of
satisfaction. The old fires of Seventy-six
are rekindled."
Thk San Antonio Herald man is always
writing pulls to be swallowed by the own
era of lager beer factories, and then he
swallows the factory. We know it is this
way, for if he swallowed tbe factory first,
the readers ot tbe Herald would never en
counter such difficulties ia worrying down
those yarns that so tickle the throats and
fancies of the Herald' t readers. But the
funniness of the right hand side of the
Herald is due to tbe solemnity that reigns
on the left. Look at it.
Mr. Wheeler, a fierce reformer and
candidate for a place iu the convention, at
Denton, would abolish all corporations of
less than three thousand inhabitants.
Either abolish them, and vest their powers
in the Legislature, and keep tbe Legislature
In perpetual session to attend to their
wants, or make these corporations capable
of self-government and of self-preservation
by restricting suffrage in them to free
holders. Ton see, Wheeler is half right
about it
Tax Memphis Atalanehe has passed into
tbe hands of two of the best newspaper
mea in tbe country, A. J. Eellar and R. A.
Thompson. D. A, Brower. also still writes
ringing, clear, concise editorials for the
Avalanche. The venerable Appeal still pros
pers as of yore, and is magnificent, and the
Keening Ledger is full of attractiveness.
Tbe city is hardly worthy, in spirit and en
terprise, of its newspaper press.
Hccn jealousy, perhaps a share of hatred,
has grown up between Russia aad Prussia.
Bismarck Is very mad, and thus Prussia is
enraged. Bismarck may well say L'Etat
e,et tnoL I am the Bute, and when I sneeze
Prussia is shocked and shakes. Bat it Is
tbe Telegraph, and not Texas, that trembles
when his Excellency nods.
Tna Dallas Herald thinks it possibly im-
possible to restrict suffrage in county aud
town elections to freeholders. There Is
nothing to prevent. It bas been dona
often, and can be done again, and will be,
or Texaa corporations will go tho way of
those of other hap lea State east of the
Mississippi.
Tua towns and villages of Western
Texas are making up liberal subscriptions
to support McNelly's Texaa scouts. This
shows the needs of the West, and what two
governments owe that horribly wronged
district of Texas.'

The story asptin pes tbrongh the jiress
that Gn. John II. Morgan was bctrajedbv
a Mr. Williams, a sister-in-law ot Jtijor
William of Uurgaa's aUfZ. Tbia lady,
afterwards diTorced, waa at the residence
i.f the mother-in-law, but left the bouse an
hour or two before Morgan's arrival and
dil uul kuow that be hail been in thai an
cient town till after he was killed. Morgan
was strongl attached to the Williams funn
ily, aud their residence was moaf, attractive
in all its incidents. Its walls within were
gctrniahed with all the appliance of taste
and art, and tbe mother of Major Williams
was an educated and admirable woman.
She was rich, too, and herself of famous
lineage, lier diseased husband was the
granaaon of tbe hero of Kings Mountain.;
Morgan was especially addicted to tbe good
tilings of this life. When he entered the
bouxe, tearing his command several miles
sway, the matronly lira. WiUiauia insisted
that neither ber sou nor the General should
remain, during the night; but Morgan
recked snore ot toe hot rolls, juicy steak,
ana Mocha coffee of the breakfast table
than of dangers of capture or death, and
the downy bed of the spacious apartment
ubovti.bUix fnvited him to deWyhtfaf re
pose, lie would not listen to the sug
gestions of Mrs. Willi tins or of Mis
N. X. Scott, a granddaughter of Hugh
L. White, then of the household. It
was Mibg Hcott who ran to Morgan's
room aud aroused him at sunrise next morn
ing with the announcement that the house
was s mounded by Federal soldiers. Mor
gan drew on his boots and pantaloons, and
ran down the stairs and out iato a vine
yard of two acres at the rear of the stately
old brick mansion. lie crossed this garden
and concealed hftnselt foi a time beneath a
cburcb built inside this garden by the Wil
liams family. Greenville was full of 'to
nes" so-called and Morgnn was discov
ered by one of these, and Federal soldiers
were directed to the spot. He came out,
and ran back across the garden towards the
residence, and was shot in the back while
crossing the garden by a soldier named
Moses Campbell, of Tennessee, who was on
the ouUi ie of the enclosure. Morgan fell,
lie was recognized, and his body taken by
tbe man who shot bim, aud placed across
tbe horse he rode, in front of the saddle,
and borne in triumph about tbe streets of
die village. It was said that Mor
gan was not yet dead when this
estiibition of the body, reekiug with
blood, wss made by those who slew
him. Tbe corpse was borne away towards
Gen. GiIImib's headquarters, eighteen miles
distant, and afterwards returned through
the intervention of Col. Jim Brownlow to
Mrs. William's residence, Gen. Gillem, in
this behalf, doing everything that became
a gentleman and soldier. He has stated,
iuanote heretofore published by the writer
of this, that he was advised of Morgan's
presence in Greenville by negroes and oth
ers, and that Mrs. Williams the daughter-in-law,
now of Little Rock, Arkansas bad
no agency whatever in procuring Morgan's
death. The story made her famous. She
never denied its truthfulness. Federal sol
diers soon after the event held all tbe towns
of East Tennessee, and Federal officers flat
tered the vain and attractive woman, in
vested with that degree of notoriety inci
dent to tbe supposed teat of traversing a
wild country eighteen miles to tell Gen.
Gillem that Morgan was in Greenville. She
did no such thing, and did not know that
Morgan was in Greenville till she heard of
his tragic death.
Major Williams, more fortunate than his
commander, escaped death and capture. In
stead of crossing the vineyard he concealed
himself beneath the straw that constituted
.the bed of a great Newfoundland dog that
slept under the at ps leading iuto tbe back
yard ana vineyard. i lie uog seeuieu to

The Navasota Tablet never compressed
more valuable truth in less space than when
it said that "Masonry is either soiaethingto
be practiced, or its teachings are valueless.
It teaches sublime truths it arrays ltseu in
gorgeous drapery it points grandly to tbe
beaut'.ful, the good aud the pure; it elevates
the evergreen of immortality it tells of
high duties and glorious hopes; it lifts the
veil of the future, and points to immortal
ity and a glorious resurrection and eternal
life; yet how few of these grand visions do
we practice. Can we hope longer to maic-
tam nur heritage, enjoy our birthrights,
uulcss we live Masonary, as well as teach lit
It is important that the minds-of the craft
be constantly called to tbe vital importance
of a more thorough investigation into the
character and qualifications of candidates.
You cannot take a candidate without he be
worthy and well qualified, and make bim a
Mason by conferring degrees. He must be
a man, Godlike, his vision undiinrned by
vice, his thoughts elevated, bis reason, by
bearing full sway, stamped with divine im
press, or degrees will never make him a
Mason. You may manipulate .bim, restrain
him for a time, but 'Truth is mighty, and
will prevail,' his true character will devel
op itself, and when too late you will find
that you have admitted either a pestiferous
meddler or useless drone. Where were
you tought to look first for the embodied
pinciples of Masonry t At the heart. Will
degrees make a Masan, and create what
does not exist in man? Never, never.
Principles, eternal principles,deeply planted
by the great I AM, can only make Masons.
Degrees muke members, Deity makes Ma
sons."

THE MILITARY t'KVEK AOMJ I'M.
We understand that a call will be
shortly made for a meeting to organize
a military company by a number of
the officers and men who have seen
service in both amries during, the
late war, and received honorable dis
charges, as well by some of the young
er generation who have yet to smell
gunpowder. The Importance to our
city of a thoroughly drilled and officer
ed volunteer military company cannot
be overestimated. It will afford ex
cruise and a knowledge of arms and
military routine to mauy. It will en
able the veterans who have hitherto
been arrayed in hostility to march
shoulder to shoulder and practically to
"shake hand over the bloody chasm."
Galveston, Houston, Austin and San
Antonio have already organized com
panies, which are an honor to them,
and Dallas should not be behind them
in this respect. Weliave in our worthy
mayor a graduate cf West Point, in
Dr. Thurston one of the best colonels
of the army of northern Virginia; in
Oapt. Odell a galhtut ex-federal officer,
who has in his day "heard great guns
roar;" in Capt. G. C. Rivers, a pupil of
a Virginia military school, who grad
uated "wearing the grey," and many
others. Here now then, is splendid
material for rank and tile, and with
that splendid hall Major Blades is
building on Commerce street for an
armory, there is no telling wnata
erack compauy might not begotten up.
The ladies, who "dearly love the
military," are taking an interest in
"The Rifles." Of course there will
come in due time, the first parades, the
presentation of colors by the ladies and
the first grand ball. When all this
comes off, the muu in plain clothes
will have to take a hack sent for those
handsome fellows in killing uniforms
will be all the rage, and not to belong
to "The Rifles" will be suicidal in any
pm bilious youth. The old soldiers will,
many of them, be pleased to review
the familiar sights and sounds of mili
tary life even in time of peace, while
we are quite sure the majority of our
citizens would have just cause to be
proutl of the due body of men Dallas
could put in the ranks.

A Brier Sketch or Our New Religious
und Kilutatlonal Institution.
Tho order of cloistered nuns, known
as Ursullnes, or "Daughters or St. Ur
sula," was rounded In 1535 by Angela
McricI, who wus horn March 21, 1474,
at Dezauzeno, a .little town lu Italy,
situated on lake Garda, in Bressaus,
six leagues from lirescla. She was fam
ous for her piety, learning aud zeal Iu
the education of young girls, to which
she devoted her life By her precept,
example and work, she did much to
promote purity or life and culture. Iu
1807 Pope r ins VII. canonized tills holy
woman. In every civilized country
tho order is now represented by con
vents in which young glrlsare educated
and brought up under the eyes of pious
nuns, who devote their time to tbe In
struction of them iu all spiritual infor
mation, and in habits of neatness,
truth, virtue and modesty.
The building just erected here Is
large, commodious aud well veutilated.
There Is au air of simple comfort and
cleanliness about It which shows disci
pline, order aud intelligent manage
ment. Tiie convent proper is a two
story frame building, with a garret ex
tending the whole length. Ou either,
side of the main entrance are winding
stairs leading up iuto the second story.
The vestibulo, on the first floor, opens
immediately into the school room,
which is a handsome, airy, well-lighted
apartment, furnished with the latest
improved school room furniture, and
properly warmed with stoves. A door
opens on to a back gallery, nearly the
length of the room, with small rooms
on either end of it. There is a large
cistern under one end of the school
room, convenient to the kitchen and
the dining room, which is 120x11-1.
THK SKCONfc HTOKY
Is sub-divided iuto a room for the
nurses aud infirmary, and a dormitory
for scholars boarding iu the convent
Corridors 0x31 divide the dormitory
from tho Infirmary ami nurses' room,
and another corridor 5x2S divides the
infirmary from the sisters' room. On
either side of the front of the second
story arc small rooms 10x1 occupied
by the nuns.

ance, under the charge of a Mother Su

Habits of neatness, punctuality, modes
ty, Immunity and piety are inculcated.
The children tire cared for with parent
al solicitude by the holy womeu who
have dedicated their lives to the noble
work of religion and education.

The 'Examiner, true to its instincts and
incapable of reason and addicted only
to appeals to prejudice, would have the
Democratic Convention at Galveston pro
nounce those papers organs of Radicalism
which oppose the adoption of the new
Constitution. Texas is not full of fools,
and tbe Convention will not recognize this
absurd touchstone of fealty to party.
There are countless Democrats who would
resent such an insult to common sense and
common houcsty. What has Democracy to
do with Rutabagaism Is the party re
sponsible for the follies of Johnson of Col
lin, and of those who bowed before the
majesty of his autocracy in tho Constitu
tional Convention We did desire, above
all things, to approve the new Constitution.
We expected to find it creative at least
of such an improvement on the gov
ern unt we have that no negro or Rad
ical could accept it. But there is not
a sane Radical in Texas who does not pre
fer this horrible Rutabaga code, save to the
extent that it may be amended with fa
cility, to that under which we are now liv
ing. The Examiner knows not what it is
saying. It never thinks. It is pulled
along blindly by the nose, sometimes by
the GVange, sometimes by the Governor;
now and then John Ireland tweaks it, and
sometimes Lang gives its much twisted
nasal promontory a terrible wrench. But
now it is the San Antonio Expresi, the lead
ing Republican organ cf the Southwest,
which has the proboscis of the Examiner
between its thumb and iudex finger. The
Examiner and Expren are running in the
same groove. They are delightfully asso
ciated, and botb well pleased with the
new Constitution. Both papers, for the
same reasons, laud the new code, and since
the Erpren is wise and discreet, and an able
organ of its party, and accepts the asser
tions of the Examiner, may we not suspect
the Examiner of Radicalism J It would
have Democracy insert a new plank, an ex
post facto rule of faith iu its creed, and un
church recusant members of the Democratic
church. The Exprtm, hand in hand with
the Examiner, would enter the very holy of
holies of Democracy on this new plank,
which is neither broad nor strong enough
to bear the Statesman. Approve the Con
stitution, as doesthcStyrey and the Exami
ner, if you like. We cau stand it if the people
cr.n; but for heaven's sake do not make
Democracy disgusting to intelligence and
patriotism by making the party responsible
for follies and absurdities of Rutabagaism.
If thus burdened, and tbe party still tri
umphs, we are content to bide our time.
The new code will not be in operation a
single year when the people will curse those
who conceived and promulgated it. Espe
cially will they be anathematized who not
only constructed this fearful code, but ab
solutely fasten a system npon Democracy
by making it responsible for crude incon
gruities and theories and facts of govern
ment which, when reduced to practice,
must become intolerable.

Considerable discussion prevails as to the
practicability of the nominating convention
for this precinct succeeding, in any reason
able time, in making nominations. It is
alleged that five hundred or a thousand
men will come to this meeting, and if this
prove to be so, there remains no doubt,
with all the work on hand, that great con
fusion will ensue, and, in the event of nom
inations being made, serious dissatisfaction
may arise at the results. Tbe matter must
be handled well. There must be no disposi
tion to crowd, or carry nominations, cither by
storm or by tiring out the crowd. We
would be glad to have the candidates get
together and agree upon a plan which will
give reasonable satisfaction to all. It is
suggested that ward meetings be held in
this precinct, and that each of them send
up to the county convention a representa
tion, which will collectively represent the
precinct in that body. As matters look
now there must be confusion, and
we would be pleased to see suggestions made
from among the candidates or their friends
which will lead to ultimata harmony. The
safety of those whom the Democrats of
Travis county may select as candidates is
the qaestion involved, and which should in
duce the most careful action on the part of
those who have authority, or are assuming
it.

The homestead provision of the new
constitution concedes to the head of a fam
ily tweny farms of ten acres each in
different counties, nnlimited in value, or
a residence and business houses iu town, of
unlimited value. This, comprising three
fourths of tbe improved real property of
the State, cannot be mortgaged or used in
any way as a basis of credit. Under this
law it is not safe to lend money on any im
proved real estate. This is certainly a
measure calculated to repel foreign capital
and make civil courts for the collection of
debt wh illy needless.

not, however, to be- of a tir.-esitondiug
character, pi-niaps the situation in. one re
spect is nut altogether appropriately choser
for either the thotisauds oTtiesta who are
expected will have a weary ascent to It, orji
system of hat-kney couch-service, which
does uot at present exist at Bayreuth, must
be created. The. jow;s of t-Under trees
which hue the way, too, will yield but slight
protection to the predestrian from the rays
of the afternoon sua. But tbe master had
a reason of his own for choosing this place,
lie needed a good foundation and ample
space beneath' his stage; and uo other place
in the valley answered this purpose ao well,
on account of the subterraneous waters.
The exterior of the theatre is- very simple;
the purt of the building designed for the
audience is ttmie-d toward the eity; ou the
corners are four small pavilions; iu the cen
tre rises the powerful Schnurhoden, some
what like the Leipzig theatre. When you
t.pproach the intutior from the stage you are
astonished et its' vast proportions. Iu i.he
dinieusionsof heiubt,depth and width, it ex
ceeds any other German theatre. The pul
leys for drawing the scenes are twice the
height of the ttigc, and as tbe depth of thirty-six
feet below the stage is sufficient to
receive the scenes, they vanish below instead
of being rolled up. Them is a row of these
arrangements for ii rawing up and down 011
diZerent parts of the stage. Rooms for
stage decoration ate provided i n either side
as in the nave of a cl uicli, and they are ad
mirably adapted to this purpose. Almost
every spot upon 1 he' vn-t podium isarraiigdd
to be sunk w-heu. iie.tx-nt.tu y, aud numbeileiss
little openings are provided to allow articles
of dress to vanish below. The spuce Umler
ground is divided iuto two stories, of which
the upper, in Borne plaei'a, may b rised to
the level ot the stage. The great peculiari
ty of tbe theatre centres, however, in t-he
orchestra and the arrangement for accommo
dating spectators." The latter consists of
forty amplntlieatiic rows tif seats divided
into sections, euch tf which furnishes' about
fifty sittings.. T bete is no prosccuium ex
cept the row of boxes for royalty. A wall
in the shape of act II,' 'overlapping the stage
as it were, completely' hides the oreheatra
from the public The wall extends some
distance uuder' 1 tie ftnge wherer-the wind
iustiuments are disposed, la this way the
musician's atttntion is never distracted by
the peiforiiKtuce. Even the violiuists, whose
places are next to the director, can scarcely
see the stage. The complaint is often made
by singers that they aie obliged to sing
louder than the orcbuMra to lieurd. If
this innovation proves successful, Wagner
will deserve to be regarded as a reformer in
theatrical archi-eciure, as well as in 111 u 41c.
We may possibly also owe to htm a reform
in the system of lighting theatres. His first
step in that direction tins been to remove
the foo'.lightiirom. tluj sta'giv tius avoiding
the unfavorable shadows which they throw
upon the faces of actors who inadvertently
step too ner thfm. The principal light
will he supplied (nun bImivs. However, all
this is as yet only iu the experimental stage.
The question of possible aecideuts from lire
arises. Thissulij.'ct h is not been overlooked.
Beneath the theatre is a well which, on the
one band, collects all the suuterroecus wai
ter, thus injuring a dry fouudatiqn, aud at
the same time forms a reservoir from which
water may be raist d by an eDgino, provided
for the purpose, to the very top of the edifice.
From there it is conducted ley pipes in all
directions. The steam auto will be used for
sceuic efforts, Several iron hpmhIis open un
der the stage, through, which. the steaiu or
vapor may ascend" to the podium. This
whole enterprise, front the const: action of
the vast edifice to the gathering inio it of
all the highest, talent of Germany, repre
sents something unusually grand iu design
and energetic' in performance and' if some
smile at tho uncertainties ami' impractica
bilities of the scheaie.: they most lay their
blame upon the fanatical friends of Mr.
Wagner, who have so liberally provided
means for it.' Now that the work is so far
advanced (for at the present time they are
already bating leliearsals in it with a full
orchestra), it is not premature to speculate
upon the efferf'wf -Tt -attraction. Four
years ago thousands fl icitd to. view tbe
performance of .the "Post-ion Plaj" at Ober
atnmergaui I- is not. too much to expect
at least an equal number to visit Bayreuth.
If anything is calculated to insure the sym
pathy of the German people in the highest
degree for this festival, it will be the reflec
tion that the whole creation (the Nibelun
gen) being its foundation, and tbe way it is
to be carried ont bears a purely German
character. Of course the decision of the
public will depend upon, the "Tone Pic
tures" of the master; but the Nibelungen
promises sometbingvery different from what

"Maistersanger."

A few days ago. tbe ReicJu Anteiger, or
Official Gazette, contained a statement of
the principle, or rather practice, according
to which the Imperial "government will
henceforth interpret the naturalization treaty
of 1868. According to Article IV. of that
treaty, a person naturalized In cither coun
try, who afterwards n turns to his nstive
land and makes it his place of residence,

the person iia.uraiizl in the one country
ret ides more than two J ear iu the other
country." The application of this rule is
permissive not imperative.' The object of
tlie article was to nx tbe status of some Ger
mans aad American who profess to be citi.
zensof one country, while they escape all
the obligations of citizenship by living ia
another. Two gears' residence wan deemed
a reasonable limit," and was designated fr
the guidsnce of the Imperial authorities. In
the course of tinte, -however, this option si
featare became a great source of confnaion
and difficulty. Naturalized citizens, re
turning to the Fat her land to paae the rest
of their days,' found themselves after two
years invariably in confhet with the interior
and the w sfdfT arttteErx: TTsei r goods
were seized for taxes, and they themselves,
if within tbe military age, were enrolled in
the army. They were rot fimbte for mili-'

emigration, 'uaiesa' they aJ actually de
serted, but were held bound to give such
service aa is exacted by law from peraoosof
tbe age at wh-h they -wef aaaajaird to bave
again become Prnsataa ot-. Oertnau subjects.
The result always was. in such esse, an ap
peal to the A met lean LagsUon. Under Mr.
Bancroft, who as the author of the" treaty
may be presomed ta kmov what U eoeatt,
as well aa onoer bis excellent successor, Mr.
Bancroft Davie, toe Ltfgsxioa has alwtiji
