and freely interceded to a reasonable extent
for this class of persons, and has, of course,
helped many of them out of deserved or un
deserved euibirrasnient. But, except lu a
limited number ot cases,' where the zeal of
Prussian officials had led them to claim pet
sous as Oertnaneubj.es before the txpua
tiou of the two years' grace, but little could
be done. While the authorities could justi
fy their claim to applytne teo years' reg
ulation, the Legation cull only rvquesf
from the foreign office, it suspenriou juiar
ticular instances."
It had never been formally d eclated b
the government that, under the option
given by that article, it would invariably re
gard two years' residence after returning a
a renunciation of American citizenship. In
some canes the police bad observed such a
rule, and drawn the victim into tbe army.
In other cases, where tveu after the two
years the pvrsi n could prove an intent to
return, he bad been released on the repre
sentation of tbe American minister to tho
foreign office. But this method wa arbitra
ry and capricious. It was as unjust to nat
uralized cit tons who, without warning, and
in the absence of any oblifation imposed on

fined for non-performance of military duty
aa it waa vtxatious to the legation and the
foreign office. The Heitk-Anaeiger there
fore announces the rule by wtucU tbe Ger
man authorities will henceforth tie governed.
On tbe expiration of the two years of grace
the returning emigrants will uot at once be
ensnared- as in a, trap without any chwice.
Their intent not to retain to America afti
that time will indeed be presumed. They
will oecome theoretically liable at once as
new German subject. But they will. bave
the alternative of actually leaving tho coun
try within a prescribed period, or of remain
ing aa German suhjec'a. The intent to re
turn ia to be proved by the act of returning;
the intent to remain is proved by the fact
of remaining. This rule, which is doubt
less fully within the competence of the Gar
hian authorities, will greatly simplify the

Among the most popular of Prof. Tyn
d all's descriptions is his history ot a drooof
water. He traces it as it is lifted by tho
suu's heat from the surface of the sea; car
ried aloft as an invisible vapor; wa'ted over
the laud cooled by and piecipiiated upon
some mountain peak, possibly in the snow
and ice of a glacier, or perhaps sinking be
low ine surface aa nan, aud reapjieartug in
the spring that bubbles up in a valley; and
at last, through rill and rivulet and river.
finding iu way back to ooeau again. Some
thing tike this has been done for ether sub
stances than water, by Prof. Punioellv. iua
discourse before the National Academy of
1 1 1 1
umouuvo. aa uh toug oven reoognizeti mat
all, the materials ot. which the earth ia
formed,-are iu course of being gradually
washed into the sea. Modern discovery has
thrown much littlit on the processes bv
which they are transformed iuto the rocks
and minerals of good dry land again. The
cycle ot these changes is, of course, enor
mously greater than that which Prof. Tyn-
dall described. It requires eras of time in
which mountains must wear away and ocean
floors rise to the surface; compared with
which tbe whole. known history of the hu
man race is an affair of yesterday. But though
so slow, -it is continuous, and is going ou at
this f our all over the globe, just as it has
been ever since there was any land lifted
above tho waters. If that excellent lady ol
science, Mr. Mary Somerville, was still
living, she might add a notable chapter to
her instances of the counection of 'he phys
ical sciences. The geologist, dealing witb
the formation of dead matter rocks and
stones and dry land is suddenly furnished
with auswers labia problems by students of
natural history, who are busy with animals
aud planets living in the sea. Thecbemiata,
too, bave helped largely in the research it
would have been impossible to get the facts
of the story without their aid. They have
found in tbe ashes of sea plants and animals,
not only the alkaline uaiths, but tbe heavy
metals, lead, copper, zinc, iron, and
even silver. These- were secreted by the
vital processes. Eventually, when the plants
and. animal are dead, they fall on the
ocean's floor. The naturalists have ascer
tained, to some extent, what vast quantities
of these 'plants and animals are concen
trated iu quiet eddies off the edge of ocean
currents. In some future age, when the sea
bottom of those localities shall have became
bard rock aud dry land, another Comstock
lode may be found where the precious met
als have thus been slowly garnered from the
sea..' One of the most curious results of
these investigations is the theory that man's
work in mining and burning coal, so far
from deranging the scheme of nature, w ill
help In the preservation of life upon earth.
It appears that carbon is, when deposited in
the form of coal, too slowly soluble by the
winds and rain to be restored in time to the
air and sea. In the course of untold age
since the first cretaceous seas were formed,
this carbon had been quietly tucked away
under ground. The plauta of the sea an 1
land .were beginning to need ii. If the
plants could not live, all animals must die.
The only way to bring the carbon to the
plants is to convert it into carbonic acid,
and spread that in the air, where forest
leaves can decompose it, or rain drop can
carry it down to the weeds of the sea. Man,
digging the coal from tbe mine, and burn
ing it under' steam boilers and on the do
mestic hearth, is really restoring the lost
balance to nature's work. Tbe colliers of
England and Pennsylvania are helping to
prolong tbe existence of every creature on
the globe. It is a very comlortmg doctrine
to the apostles of labor, however it may
stand tbe further tests of science.

WHAT IS ElETIOCRACTf
The TcUyraph critieiaes the platforto of
the Democracy of the United SUtei, as the
Stateaxax defines it, as too contracted.
Broad or nirrow, aa wc present it, it em
bodiei the on!j ide in reference to 'which
there is almost universal concurrence. The
S ta tesjias wonld willingly make Free
Trade a farther test of Democratic sound
ness,1 but the Granges the farmers of. the
country mercilessly robbed from the gov
ernment's loundation to this good hour
that all other classes may be enriched by
their poverty even the Granges have gone
off on a false scent-after railways, their
greatest benefactors, and dare not assert
adhesion to Free Trade. Georgia is a
model Democratic State; bat if we make
"free trade" "the issue" as an aJJtndum to
local freedom, the wealth and intelligence
of Georgia would combat the idea. In
very truth cotton manufacturing establish
ments, sooner or later, must bo transferred
to the cotton fields,' and when this occurs
the South may find opposition to "protec
tion" less prevalent and infinitely less vio
lent than to-day. As regards other ques
tions the honest, intelligent masses of the
people concur to an infinitely greater ex
tent than we Imagine. A politician goes
about anxious to quarrel. He is always
belligerent and finds enemies, who, if 'ap
proached differently, would differ from him
in nothing. In fact, partisanship, when
resolved into its original elements, consists
more of prejudices and false preconcep
tions than in facts or convictions or opin
ions, and the most violent partisan is either
a man of unreasoning self-conceit, of terri
ble prejudices,. iron-clad, by ignorance,. or,
be has his own "fish to fry" and, therefore,
keeps the fires of hate perpetually ablaze.
It would not do for. a standing candidate
for Congress in a Radical district to suffer
the people to have time to think. The ex
citement must bo kept at fever heat, other
wise Democratic truth would penetrate the
thin veil of prejudice, the party would be
disorganized, and the Democratic rival win
the salary and perquisites and honors of the
distinguished position. The rule,- it
may be imagined, and mode of reason
ing operates in both directions and
we concur in the .necessity for
party compat tness to the extent, thit the
fortunes of party leaders themselves are
involved. Their interests are promoted
when we are always fighting aad always
appealing to the passions and prejudices of
ignorance. "We see the necessity as plainly
as any gentleman anxious to w in- the Gov
ernor's pUce or even a' seat in the Senate.
"Whether popular interests and those of the
party leaders are always concurrent Ja a very
different question. We happen to believe
that at this particular juncture ia the for
tunes of Texas, the interests of the people
would be moat promoted by the subordina
tion of partisan to popular interests. We
would rear p. perfect fabric of government
from foundation stone to the "blazing dome
to remind as of the durability and glory of
the bended heavens, and can see no neces
sity, at this moment, for the intervention
of Democracy, and still leu of Radicalism.
The occasion,' too, impels us to forget the
passions and hates and instincts of parti
sans. 'Not long ago the wholo population
of the country were condensed into one
mighty incarnation of valor, and,' for the
first time n the annals of humanity, men,
women and children were joint participants
in a grand conflict and joint heirs of its il
lustrious renown. Towards .one another,
towards our political institutions we are all
republicans the same republicans as when,
in 1789, we shaped the Federal Constitution
to be the embodiment of the' popular will.
By the lame lofty impulse and heroic aims
Texans should now be guided when entering
upon a task pregnant with results as' vast
and enduring to ourselves as wero those in
rolved" in the construction of the Federal
framework of government to the people of
the continent.-. -r- -1 f v i
Civilization never outgrows a great prin
clple; circumstances never lessen its value,
time, ruthless in all else, never dooms It to
decay. A great principle is not an invca
tion, not a discovery, not a creation. It is
revelation the thought of God commu
nicated to man; and as Tie pleases, at dif
ferent intervals,1 page after page, chapter
after chapter, are added. The very nature
of such a principle renders it supreme In-
rested with supremacy it subordinates all
other principles to itself, infuses into them
its vitality and reigns as sovereign in the
wtirld of thought. Rule it will,' rule it
rousts because" Almighty ,God is in it.
i Buctt a principle ia local .freedom in gov
ernment, and such a principle is the living
soul of Democracy.!

nial New England. It has swi red to our
owa time with very important results. It
is calculated, beyond almost any other po
litical organization, to foster local patriot
ism, and to the mass of men. there is no
patriotism but a local patriotism. The men
whose political aspirations and political at
tachments can really take in so extensive
and various a domain as the United States
now comprise are uncommon men. If a com
mon man gets as far in his political regard
as to follow the Mosaic precept of loving
the people who are actually and literally his
neighbors, he has as much patriotism as can
reasonably be expected of him. Of course
his patriotism is a provincialism,' but pro
vincialism has its political uses. The pa
triotism of few men goes beyond provin
cialism, while only the very best and wisest
of our race, if even they, are able to "ele
vate their vision" so as to take in more
than their own land, and make general
philanthropy an effective substitute for a
limited and local patriotism.
It is at least in part because the political
unit of Massachusetts has been circum
scribed that public spirit is there so strong
as to make an impressive commemoration
out of what in most other States would have
been apt to turn out a farce. Any intelli
gent observer who goes to Boston; or to any
principal town in Massachusetts, finds on
every band of him monuments and memo
rials of local worthies at which he is apt to
thrust out the tongue of "cosmopolitan." de
rision. Undoubtedly the local deities of
Massachusetts are in many cases made out of
questionable material, and even the pan
theon of the State House enshrines some
curious objects of veneration. But the
Massachusetts memorials are' alike witnesses
of public spirit, and oat of a desire to do
honor to what seems most honorable. It is
not that other communities have not men
equally worthy of honor, which is the com
placent and obvious explanation of the dif
ference which the first Massachusetts man
you meet will favor jou with. It is simply
that in other States the desire to do honor
to honorable men does not exist -in the
same strength. This feeling is in its
turn the result of the local patriot
ism "and the local pride which have
been fostered in New England more than
elsewhere by the political organization
which remits to every- community the
charge of its own affairs.
This is a very practical consideration, as
matters stand now. In Boston a Tweed
ring would have been almost impossible,
for the reason that ia Boston every citizen
feels his own share of political responsibil-
ty for municipal affairs, and would have
regarded a band of plunderers in office as a
personal1 disgrace to himself. This is im
possible in a community denied self-government
and governed through commissions
set over it by 4 central authority. Bteara
and "the facility of, transit are tending all
the while to obliterate local divisions. 11 To
preserve public spirit it is necessary to pre-:
serve those local divisions' in politics so far
as may be. During the last century Massa
chusetts has been changed from a- common
wealth-of isolated .farms into a seething
factory. Yet the town meeting and the
political traditions derived from it have so
far triumphed over the changed conditions
of life as to make still possible a celebra
tion in Massachusetts which would be al
most impossible out of Massachusetts, and
to keep alive in Massachusetts a. municipal
patriotism .long since dead in the metropolis
of New York and other creat American
cities. '.-r

Pierrepont'a appointment to the Attor
ney Generalship is Grant's first concession
made, of late, to decency. The moral
effect of centennial celebrations North and
South Is illustrated in orations recently pro
nounced in Confederate and Federal ceme
teries, when these are decked by fair
hands with fragrant flowers. Everywhere,
as in Augusta, -Georgia, ana Columbus,
Mississippi, the tributes of honor and affec
tion were tendered alike to Northern and
Southern soldierly worth, and when the
women of the South have done this" the
war is. at an end.. -.Grant cannot. revive it
or reproduce its passions. Federal soldiers
of the Army of Tennessee, even Sherman's
remorseless legions, who knew so well what
war was, how terrible and how speedily,
and'. thus,'' mercifully perhaps, to end it,
even these dashing soldiers,' who' went
smashing things to the sea even these
bare invited the men who confronted them,
from Chattanooga to Charlotte Court House
in the old Noith State, to sit at the commu
nion table of patriotism and celebrate hal-
owed memories of the olden time. We
see in these results of the revival bf blessed
recoIlccti6ns""at Philadelphia, by' such a
celebration of memorable deeds of heroic
patriotism as the world never witnessed, a
happy augury of a more .blessed future.
Grant must make concessions' to the su
preme passion of this'' next more glorious
than the year, of jubilee or go out of office
abhorred of his countrymen! ne yields
unwillingly to the forces of popular feeling,
but never before has a whole people been
induced, as by a single impulse, to' listen
to the voices of dead years, and, reciting
their lessons,' become one mighty, incarna
tion of patriotisrxi Grant,' nnimpassioca-
ble,' undemonstrative, and accustomed' to
gain his aims at any cost of blood and even
in defiance of right, dared neither to im
pale Arkansas nor persist, .after witness
ing: the demonstration' of -reproduced
ocal -patriotism in New J Engta'nd, in his
violations of every instinct and pas ion
that gave birth to, and at this hour vitalize?
American freedom. lie "must -take steps
backward. There Is. qo supreme power, in
America, save-popular opinion, which, like
some mighty pendulum, swinging back and
orth forever, rests at last precisely.wbere it
stood. Stock' still one hundred years ago.
All the power of old England could not
move ity and the- index finger' of the old
time-piece" of history points steadily. to the
same spot, to "the Bame'. facta, to the -same
grand old men and mem6ries and reprodecs
the same passions and instincts and impulses
almost divine, that fired Amer'can enthusi
asm and American heroism one hundred
years ago. 1 ue ruae soldier, narsn oy na
ture, remorseless by training, selfish and
greedy of power and gain, stands appalled
in the presence of this resistless, ghostly
moral force demanding the reproduction of
the simple," honest republican government
of our fathers. If Grant cannot see these
facts he is. more drunken than Sardan
apalus, j and saw and heard, nothing
in New .England. But he was' sober. He
appeared in publie. He Was sane,, since
he discarded Williams and substituted
Pierrepont.

These States won the rigid to freedom in

1876, '.and who. may measure 'the debt of
gratitude due bur fathers who first won in
dependence by their valor, "and when we
had lost, they restore it by sheer force of
that. moral power wielded by the story of
their lives and deaths, r

That was a legitimate stroke of wit and
wisdom made by John .Bright day before
yesterday when, he said it was very absurd
for America to ask England to send hither
manufactures for the Philadelphia exposi
tion and yet prohibit, by tariffs, the impor

other words, 'said the" philosopher and
statesman, repeal your laws of Chinese cx
clusiveness, practice what you preach and
wc will compete With v you for cheapness
and excellence in your own markets. If
this may not be, there is. surely nothing
gained by avaia purposeless exposition.
And why not now act on Mr. Blight's sug
gestion t tWhy not adopt free trade It
mst come. The unity of the nations de
mf.nds it, even as does their diversity.
Clay's philosophy has -accomplished its
aims, and American manufactured cotton
goods are sold to day in this' market for
less than In Liverpool.' Production has
been stimulated till every natural advan
tage of America has, been; utilized," and
cheap foot?, genial climate and proximity of
the raw material enables us to undersell the
English. Since, therefore, there is no lon
ger any necessity for protection, why not
resort to the honest, direct mode of collect
ing Federal taxes t r Why all those vast ex
penditures of the people's wealth in mas
sive piles of brick and mortar, denominated
custom houses t Why such armies of col
lectors preying upon honest-: industry I
Why not find a solution of the difficulty of
robbing the people without their knowing
it in honest direct taxation t Then we
need not pay $9 for a pair ef shoes that
never cost' the manufacturer $2.50, and
then the cost of collecting would .not ex
ceed sums', wrenched by the mighty mass
of placemen from' the country's beggared
industry.' The same. men. who collect the
State should collect the Federal "taxes...

It is not flattering to Grant that Andrew

the Senate by the voice of the people and
despite the bitterest hostility of Radical

would elect Grant to any office,' and jet, be
fore the whole country, such is the curse of
partisanship, Grant is absolute master of
one, and the greatest terror to the other,
half of the whole" people. TTIe is not will

aud we must needs amend the Federal. Con

love will bo excited when Grant,v tooi be

strument which makes Cuffee a citizen and

Association In Baltimore, on Saturday even
in? last. Mr. S. Teackle Wallace read tho
following heretofore unpublished letter 01
Gen. R. E. Lee
And here I am" permitted, by tho kind
ness of a friend,-' to read some extracts from
a letter of the illustrious soldier, which has
never seen the light beforo, and which will
show through what sad strucrleg, of both
heart and mind,' he passed to what lie felt
to be his duty.- 'I doubt not nay, 1 know
that many a gallant gentleman who fought
beside him, and many another in tho op
posing host, grieved, with as deep grief as
Lee, to draw his sword. Tho letter that I
speak of bears the date of January 10, 1851,
and was written from Fort Mason, near San
Antonio, in Texas. It was addressed to a
young lady, a relative of his, for whom he had
great affection, and the passages of which I
speak were written as a message to her
father Alluding to the homes of two fami
lies of friends, he said
I. think of tho occupants of both very
often, and hope some day to scothetn aain.
I may have the opportunity soon: for if tho
Union is dissolved I shall return to lrgiuia
to share the fortune of my people. But be
fore so great a calamity befalls tbo country
I hope all honorable means of maintaining
the Constitution and the equal rights of tho
people will be first exhausted. Tell your
father he must not allow Maryland to bo
tacked on to South Carolina before tho jutt
demands of the South have been fairly pre
sented to the North and rejected. Then if
the Tights guaranteed by the Constitution
are denied us, and the citizens of cno por
tion of the country are granted privileges
not extended to tho other, we can, witli a
clear conscience, separate. I am for main
taining all our rights, not for abandoning
all for the sake of one our national rights,
liberty at home and security abroad, our
lands, navy, forts, dock-yards, arsenals and
institutions of every kind. It will result in
war, I know fierce, bloody war. But so
will secession, for it is revolution and war
at last, and cannot be otherwise, end we
might as well look at it in its true charac
ter, There is a long message, A for
your father, sod a grave one, w hich I had
not intended to put in jiiy letter to you,
but it is a subject on which my serious
thoughts often turn, for, as- an America
citizen, I prize my government and country
highly, and there ia no sacriflco I am cot
willing to make for their preservation, savu
that of honor. I trust there is wisdom and
patriotism enough ia the country to saro
kitcw, ivr a, iaui,ub ttuin n'-r tji,.
calamity to the cation as the dissolution of
the Union.

mocracy and violative of the c ctd of our

to Democracy, and is thus doubly worse

ary but their pockets are deftly picked

cotch, this new Constitution.

Senor Walker Martinez, the Chilian Min
ister to Bolivia, bus sent to the Exposition
at Santiago an interesting relic, fouud in an
Indian grave on the slopes of the Iliimani,

and in the form of a plume. The golden
liara was met with iu a nuaca or Indian grave

of a mummy in the manner of a crown, with
all the appearance of having been deposited
as fouud, many centuries ao. At the same
place were also fouud ol j .cu made of cop
per, but the most ol buiui clay, aud several
of gold, all more or less similar to the
relics inet with among the ruin of Titicacn,
Cuzce, etc. It is well known that ludiaus
buried their dead with their most portable
property, such as jars, idols, ornaments and
arms. So much so was this custom, that
old Spanish chroniclers cf the conquest tell
of a Spanish soldier finding iu one .sepulchre
deposits valued ax oO,0(H castellanos, anu
another becoming rich by finding a cot
lection of goldeu vases wonh 80,000 ducats.
At the preseut day the interest that at
taches to those resting places of extinct
races of men is of a moral and scientific
value, in so far as they serve to throw light
cn the manners and customs of people w ho
iu lunr way uiu great iniHgs, tne lesuit Ol
a civilization peculiar to the epoch. The
relics they left and discovered at this day
become the stepping stones of history that
extendkiureseaiches back into those period
of prehistoric obscurity. Tiie t-panisl -American
government, as a rule, have hith
erto paid little attention to mat ers of the
kind, an indiilereuce which doubtless in
ternational exhibitions and museums will go
a great way to remedy, it were to be de
sired that a reapeot for such relics should
be inculcated by the school, and even from
the pulpit," The Ignorant, we know, are
apt to retard with contempt the finely ex
ecuted earthenware vessels, the idols that
look like those of Egypt, as well as the arms
made of stone and copper, as things of no
value. .The mummies themselves, found so
numeroas over all upper Peru those solemn
bnt interesting inhabitants of the Indian
tombs, while they proclaim the existeuce of
that feeling of repugnance in human nature
to be buried snd lo -get ten, help the com
parative ethnologist in hi researches to

Of the huacas found studding the de
clivities of the gigantic Lit ma ot Bolivia,

quered by the I oca Ynpanqui, in the four
teenth century. There seem little doubt

buaneca belonged to this rsce. The ruins

following reasons: 1. Because it was dis
covered ia a place where the loess had do
large centres of population but where the
Aninaraes evidently had. 2. Because the
relics formed a crown of sold on a dead
body which the Lucas never permitted to any
oi men awojecu. in princes even could
only wear a species of woollen turban.

Thus far President LTye has re
deemed his pledges. Thus fur he has
done the Union and the country's
peace the greatest goid ever achieTcd
in so brief a period bj any President.
He has abolished the "South" as an
idea and passion and as the origin of
distinctiveness in partisanship. lie
kas lifted us op from tbe midst of the
battle field where we were destroyed
by two crazed bodies of maddened
contestants and placed us above the
reach of trie deadly missiles of the two
great armies that cane down upon us
quadriennially to enrage aid desolate
and destroy. Their coming was more
terrible and ruinous tbaii that of Goths
and Vandals. Not only did tbey sus
pend the industry of forty millions of
people, but struck down popular
morals and even threatened the peace
of society and stability of government.
They came as serried hosts one, mov
ing downward from the great lakes;
the other, northward from the gulf,
ami fatal, incurable, final collision was
recently prevented by the intervention
of a erad commission -a body of
plenipotentiaries whoestablishud peace
for a troubled nation. Their purposes
may bave been depraved but tbey
gave us a president whose ability to
serve tbe country, because of big rela
tion to parties, was infinitely greater
than could have arisen from any other
selection, and he has spared no ezer
tion of official and partisan power to
demonstrate his patriotism.
He has redeemed Lis first and most
important promise. In withdrawing
armies from tbe South, be has not only
declared peace, but given the States
freedom. In freeing Louisiana and
South Carolina he has at last freed the
South. For tbe first time, we repeat
it, in seventeen weary years, the tem
ple of Janus is closed and the people
snould celebrate the event with bon
fires and illuminations. It is not the
freedom of two ravaged States, but of
a continent, that was achieved when
enslaved Louisiana and South Carolina
were liberated. It was not a conces
sion to popular liberty alone, but to
the exactions of outraged constitu
tional law. Tbe country had borne
these wrongs till the law itself was
perverted in the very hearts of the peo
ple and President Hayes remedies an
evil greater in its moral than immediate
practical results, and yet how strangely
silent the press and the people
of the South Our very indifference
is demonstrative of the grandeur of
the end accomplished by the Presi
dent. We bad forgotten the value of
tbe freedom of Stitea. We have be'
come so diseased morally and dement
ed because of the maddening passions
of party aud of sectional hates that
we are absolutely incapable of appreci
ating the magnitude of dangers we
have escaped. or of the boon conferred
by the country'd chief magistrate.
True, Mhe has only done hisdaty,"
but no hero of battle fields, neither Lee,
nor Grant, nor Jackson, norMcPherson
ever discharged a duty that involved
so much human liberty or required
greater heroism of purpose and char
acter. To close the war, to strike
down the bayonet and uplift the law
and free tbe States was the proudest
deed and most beneficent of the cen
tury. It is base wrong to deny it, aud
the basest cowardice that prevents as
sertions of the fact. Miserable, dis
eased, beaten, purposeless Radicalism
and Democracy, ragged and beggared,
have such terrors for the people and
have so shackled the press that
even in Texas, where freedom of
thought and action should be as bound
less as these sunlit plains and uncon-
fined as the breezes that come up from
the sea bestrewing their pathway with
roses even here the press and people
are silent, and the cuurage and patri
otism of the President hardly recog
nized. "He has only done his duty,"
we are told, and yet when God comes
to reward man's virtues He can say no
morj than ''ue has only done his duty.
Let us not forget to quote the Presi
dent's enemy, and repeat it again and
again, and write it incflaceably upon
our hearts an i memories, "He has on
ly done his duty."
The army as a factor in State gov
ernments nd politics ia no more. The
South is free, tbe war ended, peace re
ktored and that new departure, for
which patriotism prays and bv whiah
tbe fcjjuth is lifted up from between
colliding combatants and made finally
secure in local right and in perfect
freedom, Is at last inaugurated.

One of the most sensible, practical
letters ever written from Texas fills
three columns of the Troy, New Yoik,
Daily Times of the twentieth instant.
It was written by F. D.Curtis, recently
in this city. The writer is pleased to
say, and truthfully, that there is a
greater degree of cosmopolitanism and
liberality of opinion and lest prejulics
growing out of sectional antagonisms
in Texas than elsewhere in the South,
condiiion properly ascribed to the
fact that there is not a village or
neighborhood which has not represen
tative citizens of every portion of the
United States. The people of Texas
have learned at last, despite the deeds
of carpet-baggers and their base uses
of the helpless negro, that honesty and
personal virtue and patriotism are not
'facts of latitude or longitude, and that
the relative number of knaves, to the
whole population, average popular in
telligence being equal, is the same
everywhere the world over. Texans
bare also learned that partisan preju
dices and convictions and theories of
government, to a great extent, are mat
ters of early training, and that un
happy events Drought about by unwise
and violent party leaders have driven
men, of the North and of the South
alike, to adopt prejudice and bate
rather than reason and logic in shaping
partisan creeds and action. Among
the more intelligent people of Texas
Iiepablicanism is widely separated
from Radicalism, and the liberal De
mocrmcy of Douglv, Houston and
Throckmorton from that consti
pated, fevered, red -headed, bandy
shanked and run mad impersonation
of -red-eyed and red-handed Drmoc
racy which would frighten fools by
denunciation and strut into ollice by
invectives hurled at those who prefer
country to party. The moderation and
conservatism of the State, as distin
guished from radical Republicanism and
from radical red repubheanand chain
lightning Democracy will shape, in
coming years, the political fortunes of
the commonwealth of Texas. Tbe
knowledee of tbu fact, disused over
the North and Earope and over the
Middle States by writers like this ad
Durable correspondent of the Time,
will do more to indrce immigration
than all maps and specifications of
physical attractions ever printed. How
ever exuberant the coil of a country or
attractive its homes and farAS and
ranches, or however fat its flocks or
rich aad delicate its products of wool
and cotton, no intelligent population
will seek abiding places there unless
public opinion be as well taught and
thoroughly liberalized as its fields am
productive and winds full of delicious
perfumes. Therefore it is that vio-
lent denunciatory and extreme radical
Sruthern Democratic organs should
be suppressed. They do infinite mis
chief by misrepresenting the intelli
gent people. They make Hiycs re
sponsible for the acts of the commis
sion, which, fortunately for the South
and for tbe whole country, made him
President. They can see no good in
his converting Louisiana and South Car
olina into States; they discover nothing
to admire when he incurs the
hatred and active hostility of
Blaine and Wade and Wendell Phillips.
Ii fact, because Hayes is not labeled
"Democrat," he is without virtue or
patriotism, and the newspaper that
claims for him these qualities is not
Democratic. To tell the truth is not
Democratic To perpetuate partisan
hates and malignity and misconception
I Democratic. Such are the theories
of poor Martin and of rotund, fat and
greasy Dan McGary. These two pro
nounce the Statesman the latest new
convert to "Republicanism" when the
Statesman has only declared that tbe
South, with one voice, should approve
the President's conduct and uphold
him in his declared purposes and pol
icy. Those who would make farms and
homes in tke South valuable, who
would attract riches and numbers and
railways and schools and churches, will
be foremost in promoting this novel
and admirable species of "Republican
ism" illustrated in tbe President's pol
icy, and in causing its approbation by
the whole people of the South. Now
that the freedom of States is restored,
the writer for the New York paper,
whose letter we discuss, is especially
wise in asserting the growing freedom
of Texans from accursed narrow local
prejudices, and from that base trucu
lence and moral enslavement to party
which heretofore consolidated the
North against the South and impover-l-hed
the country we inhabit.
TEJIPEST AND !lliMll3IE.
Now that the South is liberated and
Bustccds and Warmoths and Eelloggs
and Packards and Chamberlains are no
more, and now that the country,
through the intervention of a grand
commission, has escaped civil war and
utter desolation, and now that the
Constitution and laws will be fairly
and justly administered, tbe control of
public affairs having passed from the
hands of a sectional into those of a
federal administration, tbe country
must lapse into a condition of politi
cal repose and business activity almost
unexampled. After all these years of
turmoil and poverty and demonism
brought down upon us by party lead
era, the unwise and the ambitious and
reckless and selfish and sectional, the re
bound must be universalty recognized,
and in all rotations of business life.
The elasticity of American energy will
be discovered everywhere, and if the
seemingly inevitable war in Europe
supervene, while American securities
will be sent home, and there will be
great foreign demand for gold, thore
must be concurrent requisitions for
beef, bread, clothing and munitions of
war. The demand for Texas bides
will grow with the consumption of
shoes by predatory murderous armies
in the Eist. Despite tempests and bail
storms, which may have destroyed lo
custs in their path, the great mass of
the people have escaped providential
calamities, and the influx of riches and
steady augmentation of property values
is seemingly inevitable. But we must
remember that this advance in prices
is measured now by a different stand
ard from that to which we resorted
not long ago. ibe volume of paper
money has been steadily lessened until
its value is that of silver j aud until
Europe interposed to disturb tbe ref
lations of paper and gold, the two de
scriptions of currency were almost an
unit in value. If tbe hail storm, when
destroying crops, bave destroyed the
locusts, we discover in an evil a com
pensatmg good, and there are seven
months of summer still before us in
this low latitude in which to i Btrieve,
here and along tbe seashore, misfor
tunes of the past thirty-six hours. If
we find that political revulsions and
war in Europe have disturbed even
here the relations of paper and g"ld
it will create an unprecedented demand
for everything we produce. For each
providential evil there is somewhere
a compensating good, and wken we re
member that after all the cost in idle
ness, whisky and bad morals of a terri
ble partisan conflict, and after a dis
puted succession to tbe presidency, we
found a way of escape from civil war,
and lapsed into the bands of a wise
and just and conservative chief magis
trate, there are few, whatever their
personal griefs and misfortunes, who
will not congratulate the country that
a splendid future awaits us, and tbe
people that their destiny is at last in
their own bands. The storm of to-day
is followed by the sunshine of the
morrow. The calamity of yesterday
is obliterated from tbe facts and mem'
ones oi tbe past by an energy that
quails not in tbe presence of plagues or
bail or tempests. The crop that was
destroyed yesterday will grow green
beneath tbe strokes of energy that is
tireless, and in the presence of courage
that defies misfortune. Heaven smiles
at last on those who rise with greatest
resoluteness when providential blows
are heaviest, and respects those who
respect and honor themselves.
Look where we will, whether to the
condition of our own country, under a
patriotic Instead of partisan President,
or to that of Europe, which goes to
war as we devote ourslves to arts and
toils of peace, there is everywhere
arcbing the heavens on every hand the
splendid bow of promise, telling of the
dawning of a brighter day than has
greeted America for twenty years.
Thk Athens Courier, one of tbe sore
headed sort of papers, says that Gov ernor
Nicholls, of Louisiana, is greatly
in the way of the people who will fight
and have a military government rather
than submit to Packard. Even so wsa
Hampton ia the way ia South Carolina.
Aad this little spitfire, red-Republican-Wendell
Phillips organ at Athens is a
small model ia a small way of all
J Southern follies ia politics.
The New York World talks bravely,
and honestly no doubt, of the reform
its party in New York City has under
gone since Tweed was "the boss." It ia
a'.l very well, but there are forty or
fifty thousand voters in New York
Ci'y who, however honest themselves,
are peculiarly subject to victimization
at the bands of knaves, and the knaves
can't resist the tempition, and decency,
and honesty will not deign to cajole or
buy such a mob, and there ia no other
means of controlling it. Should such
people be suffered to desolate such a
city, would such people be wronged if
made incapable of wronging them
selves and the great metropolis? Ought
not suffrage, in municipal elections,
to be restricted North as well as South?
Tweed and his insatiate ring of rob
bers were the products of the "sav
age suffrage" of the Eist even as
we have our Tweeds, of tie same
paternity, everywhere doa South.
The politicians and party organs arc,
of course, afraid to discuss this ques
tion. The leaden do uot approve,
and the press, in leading strings and
daring not to call its soul its "wn, even
here in tbe South, evades the question.
But in New York, as in Texas, the ne
cessity for the exemption of towns
from tbe ravages of these 40,000, and
of other tens of thousands like them in
all cities, impels the New York legisla
ture to declare that "no money shall be
voted out of a city or town treasury
except by a vote of the taxpayers."
In New York City a commission
to be elected by taxpay
ers alone, without whose assent
the taxpayers' money cannot be with
drawn from the treasury. This is a
cowardly devloe by which the 40,000
son cullottet are indirectly "disfran
chised," the party leaders being too
cowardly to do it directly. Tbe same
sort of miserable svasions were fast
ened on the face of the organic law of
Texas. But here the expenditure is to
be made at last, if approved by tax
payers, by the chosen representatives
of tramps and roughs and of the igno
rant and incapable, and it follows that
taxpayers, willing to invest in a rail
way or bridge or work house, are not
willing to suffer men chosen by the
rabble to make or control the invest
ment, and therefore the paralysis of
Texas towns and cities and counties.
The people dare not, however indispen
sable to progress and prosperity, im
pose a public burden or undertake, in
a corporate capacity, any public work.
And Texas is "ham struDg."

There is a common ground for Mr.

Mr. Atkinson contributes excellent
papers to the New York Herald affect
ing cotton culture in the South. It is
truthfully stated that here in Southern
Texas cotton is profitably raised by
white farmers, and that negro labor is
not absolutely necessary in a cotton
field. Tbe Southern States want emi
grants, and the accounts published by
i show that they are prepared to wel
come all who come. The settlement
ot the Southern troubles, which seems
near at hand, will, we have no doubt,
lead to such a state of good feeling in
the South as will draw many Northern
men and Europeans to the cotton
States. There is no doubt at all that
workingmen who desire to buy cheap
land and become farmers have a better
opportunity in such States as Texas at
this time than in any Northern State;
and it would not surprise us to see the
tide of European emigration hereafter
setting toward the cotton States. But
the people of these States should re
member that the best, tbe most indus
trious and useful class of immigrants
from Europe seek out those States
where they find free schools, churches,
free speech and a hearty welcome, and
with an unfailing instinct go there and
nowhere else.
It is stated that all prices which are
directly fixed by the wholesale opera
tions of trade, tmh as wheat, day la
bor, pork, prints, iron or books, have
yielded easily to the inevitable pres
sure, but prices which depend on indi
vidual bargaining still hold their own.
Thus the servant girl still receives the
same wages, while the railroad hand is
paid little more than half of the old
price; flour is down, but the price of
bread is unchanged tbe cloth is
cheaper, but the coat no cheaper, and
canned goods in tbe grocery undersell
the market-men. Tbe explanation of
this apparent contradiction is found in
tbe unwillingness of the American to
haggle over a trade, to beat down the
price of a dealer, or to make an issue
of a trifling overcharge. There is no
nation in the world which thinks more
of tbe dollar than we do, yet there is
no nation which despises the cent, or
even the dime, more heartily. But the
shoe is beginning to pinch the aver
age American is beginning tq grumble
over the petty expenses, and if he con
tinues long in his present frame of
mind, there are a great many prices
which are bound to be lowered before
any prices will go higher.
The rush to the Black Hills in search
of gold nuggets continues. There is a
strange fascination about this passion
for mining. Constant examples of suc
cess occur, but experience has shown,
as in case of lotteries, that the blanks
are more numerous than the prizes. If
our young men would engage in tbe
common pursuits of industry with the
same zeal and earnestness and submit
to the same sacrifices which are the
necessary accompaniments of mining
enterprise, they would, as a rule, be far
better off in the end. Tbe risks, phy
sical and moral, incident to this sort
of enterprise, are always large and toe
seldom taken into account. Money is
not the principal thing; ff it were,
there ia ample room for enterprise in
less doubtful and hazardous fields than
the gold regions of the West. But
everybody is going to the Black Hills,
and this summer more Texans than
ever before will see the wonders of
Colorado and the West and explore tbe
gold mining districts in the Black
Hills.
Col. R. W. Locohert, who is
thorough master of the art of journal
ism, says that of all the pursuits in
which men engage, that of the faith
ful journalist is the most self-sacrific
ing, unselfish and ennobling. Proper
ly understood and appreciated, his mis
sion is oue of unbounded generosity.
The demands of country, society, and
all that promotes individual happiness
and the general welfare, are inexorable.
There are no compromises, bo stopping
to count the cost; the path of duty lies
open before him. He must pursue it.
lesd where it may. Il ia seldom that
fortune smiles upon him; rarely, even
after a life of unremitting toil, does be
find himself blessed in old age with
competency. But his reward is none
the less because it comes not ia the
manner in which men usually look for
it, Life is full of its compensations.
and there is none greater thaa the
grateful sense of hsving earnestly striv
en to accomplish its mission of good,
ia which journalism offers a field to ex
panuveaad grabtyiag.
Now that States are getting even
and there ia a general handshaking all
around the board, wouldn't it be anv
versally proper for somebody to refund
the eight mijlions, more or less, stolen
from Georgia by Bollock and the other
agents of Thad Stevens aad of tbe party
of grand moral ideas, who ravaged aad
desolated tbe soata.
It has beea wisely written that men
judge a policy by its results, the tree
by it fruits. If these are wholesome
and of good report, it will be acqui
esced in, and it will none the less be
approved because it may give the po
litical management of States to oue
party or take it from another. A na
tional administration, conducted in a
national spirit, must first consider
what will be tbe best for the people,
what is best for tbe preservation of
peace, what is better for the general
contentment. Let it be conceded that
the withdrawal of troops from South
Carolina and Louisiana means the sur
render of those States to the Demo
cratic party. The people there have
the same right to be Democrats that
the people of Vermont or Massachu
setts bave to be Republicans. All that
is insisted upon is, that they' shall
respect the rights of those among
them who differ from the majority in
political opinions. That is what Pres
ident Hayes asks, and that is what
Hampton and Nicholls and tbe people
have proposed to do.
Thk quiet but incisive manner in
which Gen. Devens disposes of Wen
dell Phillips is decidedly artiBtic.
"When Mr. Phillips calls' you a liar, a
villain and a scoundrel," says Gen.
Devens, "he merely means that be does
not agree with you. He is a maker of
phrases, and must have some one to
apr-ly them to. One thing is very evi
dent, that the emancipation of the ne
gro, while it was a very good thing for
the negro, was a very bad thing for
Phillips; it took away bis stock in
trade. There is no use in calling
names. Here is this important and
difficult Southern problem to be set
tled. We are desirous of having the
negro protected and his interests ad
vanced, but his interests cannot be ad
vanced by keeping him perpetually in
a state ot war with the men who are
superior to him in intellect and wealth
and courage." There is abundance ot
good-natured, gentlemanly contempt
about that summing up of the case
that is worth whole reams of recrim
inative rhetoric.
Narrow gauge railroads have made
rapid progress, S000 miles having been
already built in tbe United States.
The latest is in Tennessee, connecting
Columbia and Lawrenceburg. Its
length is twenty-one miles, and the
cost of construction was $8500 per
mile. At Corpus Christi the cost is
less than $G000 per mile, iron and ma
terials at that point escaping the heavy
cost of land carriage. Here we must
needs pay tbe cost of transferring tbe
materials of construction from the coast
to the capital. The fate of Austin de
pends so palpably upon the retention
of the trade of noithwestern counties
that there can be little delay in tbe
execution of a task demanded by every
necessity of the city.
The steam street cars in Pans are re
ported to be unqualifiedly successful,
although tbey run along the grandest
boulevard in the city, and through
some of tbe busiest streets, turning
sharp angles and climbing and de
scending perceptible grades. The en
gines draw crowded cars from the Arc
de Triomphe to the Bastile, a distance
of seven miles, at a speed of eight
miles an hour, and no one has yet been
hurt, nor are horses frightened by tbe
sight of the engines. Tbe latter arc
like the dummies on Market street.
Philadelphia, noiseless and smokeless,
and steam power is said to have proved
much more economical than horse
power.
Judge X. B. Saunders, of the Bel
ton district, has sent in bis resignation
to take effect on tbe thirtieth of this
month. The district embraces McLen
nan, Falls and Bell counties, and tbe
bar is almost unanimously in favor of
the vacancy being filled by Alexander,
of Belton. This gentleman has been
and is still a very strong adherent of
Coke. He is endorsed by Coke and
Clark, bythe entire bar of bis city and
by most of the lawyers of McLennan
and Falls. He has been a poor en
dorser of Governor Hubbard, but per
soaal motives or feeling will by no
means control the acts of the liberal
and wise executive.
Good horses can be bought at Cor
pus Christi for $15 and ponies for $5,
and yet such auimals are worth in tbe
East from $50 to $150. Some enter
prising gentleman should charter i
dozen sleeping coaches and the like
and send 1000 Texas steeds to the auc
tion marts of Philadelphia and New
York. We are curious to see a specta
cled, bony Ichabod Crane astride
bucking mustang ia Central Park.
How tbe dry bones would rattle, and
now keenly Bonner or lieecber or
Train would enjoy the "ups and
clowns" of Southwestern life.
Taa Fort Worth paper says that hell
is no worse than tbe dance nouses oi
that sweet place. Whisky, most vil
lainous, and women to tempt and de
stroy are the agents employed by vice
and chartered by horrible city govern
ments in Texas. No wonder storms
desolate and lightnings blast and
locusts desolate the land. God's
heaviest curse should rest upoa popula
tions tbat create and maintain such in
fernal systems of pampered legalized
iniquity.
Sherjcak baaquetted Hon. S. 8.
Cox, and be was welcomed with ad'
dresses and civic honors, ne has
never faltered in adhesion to right and
justice, and though a steadfast Union
ist, never went mad and never failed
to concede honesty of purpose to tbe
people of the South. If tbe people
of the Sooth would make the Speaker,
Mr. Cox would be tbe next presiding
officer of the House; what tbe politi-
uciaas will do nobody can tell.
Hates, thus far, is a patriotic and
not a fanatical Republican. Ia his of
ficial acta he has beea all tbat the
Soatb hoped aad more thaa it expected
of a Democrat. Hostility to Hayes,
therefore, is only significant of our de
votion to partisanship rather thaa to
troth "aad country. But we advise
those who are afraid of the party lash,
and .whose souls are not their own, to
avoid the discuuioa oi this subject.
Dan McGart and four others of
the same breed as "Martin organized a
party at Brenham in 1S69, and trotted
cut a blooded ticket to walk over the
State course. This ticket secured
about a thousand votes, aud was an
important adjunct in bringing about
the election of Davis as Governor.
The terrible history of bis administra
tion is fresh in the memory of Texans,
and it ia just this kind of government
that Morton and Wendell Phillips and
McGary and Martin would perpetuate
in the Union and in the States.
Where judges are worthless and
drunken, and juries corrupt or timid,
mob law must be invoked now and
then to make property of any alue
and give life a share of security. Kill
ing people on any pretext must bave
an end, and the people should assert
their purpose, in public meetings in
some counties, where life is unpro
tected, to hang every murderer. Pis
tols and knives should be punishable
as death, since ho who carries them
contemplates murder.
Secretary Mibkmak declares that,
if Congress will leave bim alone, he
will bring about specie payments by
the first of January, 1870. He may rec
ommend such action as will facilitate
funding operations. He has no infor
mation that tbe pending European war
has affected tbe demand for four and a
half per ceU. bonds. Sales of ."four
and a ball's" now amount to one mil
lion daily, and Secretary Sherman con
templates suspending their issue and
placing four per cents, on the market.
The letter under the head of "Tex.
as Veterans" will be read with much
interest. It U written by one of the
few survivors of a band who gave Tex
as its freedom, and opened to its pres
ent population one of the grandest
empires on the globle. The State has
norcd these men with but a poor re
cognition of their services, and to-day
their pension warrants go unhonored,
in consequence of faulty and insuffi
cient legislation.
The Tennessee Ledyer, one of the
devoutest and straightest of its Demo
era tic sect, whose editor bears upon
his person tbe scars of terrible battle
fields falling once dead, it was
thought, on the field on which Me-
Pberaon fell to rise no more such a
newspaper and such an editor says of
the Democratic party: "If it cannot
maintain its organization for useful
purposes without this stock (sectionsl
ism and the negro) in trade, it must
KO."
It is very needless for Dan McGary
and Martin to attempt the organization
of a party in Texas to compel a return
to old practices and to the old policy
of partisan and sectional hate. But if
the fag ends of parties, North and
South, are resolved to sting themselves
to death, we see no special reason for
discouraging them. It is their pretty
funeral. They and the people have
parted company.
Tub Corpus Christi narrow gauge,
costing $0000 per mile, or $1.12 per
inear foot, defines for Austin the only
mode of escape from tbe clutches of
bard times. V hen will our vigorous
Chamber of Commerce lead the way?
Tbe people. are ready to act. Define a
practicable plan and it can be executed
Since Commodore Morgan stands
from under and does not shoulder the
debts of the Texas Central, it is said
he will at once proceed to extend the
Iodianola road from Cuero to Gonzales.
Wou't the Chamber of Commerce find
out on what terms be will bring it to
Austin?
When tbe Gainesville Gazette would
make Throckmortou Governor, an ex
change seems to think tbat Mr. T.'s ser
vices cannot be spared at Washington
and that Governor Hubbard may wnt
and should have a full term.
A new Pacific coast republic is pro
posed to be organized, consisting of
Sinaloa, Sonora, Durango, Chihuahua
and Lower California. Americans and
Aztecs have conspired for the purpose.
Gen. Forrest, some years ago, was in
terested ia this movement.
Our readers should basten to secure
maps of Turkey the one proposed to
be carved up by tbe Great Bear of tbe
north of Europe. We are inclined to
believe tbat the stuffing will be knocked
out very soon and tbe Porte epilled.
Martin says "grasshoppers roost
high in a storm." He is up there you
see, and knows now it is Limsen,
Martin's elevated views of things are
due mainly to tbam infernal legs.
nosTiLiTT to John Chinaman ex
tends from California even to Missouri.
We see that tbe Legislature of the lat
ter State has offered a bounty or. 23
cents for every rat killed.
Ultra Democracy and Radicalism
have no existence except in a divided
country and in warrinK sections, and
both should be utterly obliterated.
Reconstruction progresses. Gover
nor Hampton has been invited to at
tend tbe centennial celebration of tbe
battle of Benninirtnn, Vt.
TiiEAttieus Courier that neither
Mills nor Judge Ireland can be Gover
nor, aad tbe Courier' decrees ate final.
we suppose.
He should be hated, and even
shunned as a leper, who would destroy
tbe unity and Integrity or lexas.
Tbe California Farmer tbu describes
a new kind of mash lor horses now
coming into use It is composed of two
quarts of oats, one of bran, and half a
pint of flaxseed. Tbe oats are first
placed in tbe stable bucket, over which
is placed the linseed; add boiling
water, then the bran, covering tbe
mixture with aa old rug and allowing
it thus to rest for five hours; then stir
tbe mass up well. Tbe bran absorbs
while retaining the vapor, and the lie
seed binds tbe oaU and braa together
a greater quantity of flaxseed would
make tbe preparation too oily and less
relished. One feed per dsy of this
mash is sufficient; it is easily digested,
aad is especially adapted to young aal
mala, adding to their volume aad giv
lag substance to the frame.
Home and Farm.
A farmer says: "I bave kept hogs
for the last twenty-five years, both in
Ohio and Illinois, and seldom bave bad
a sick one or fail to get two litters a
year from my brood sows. I let my
pigs run in pasture in summer as much
as I do my cattle, for which an orchard
or wood lot is beat. Never breed
akin, and feed very little dry corn to
pigs or sows in pig. Especially in
winter be careful to give all hogs aa
warm and dry a bed as you would your -horses.
Feed roots, pumpkins and bran
swill plentifully, and my word for it
you will hear lea of cholera, abortion,
scurvy and trichina."
It is not uncommon for small par
ticles of butter to be formed during tbo
milking ot cows that give more than
usually rich milk. The butter globiles
become broken either in the udder or
in the pail, the thin pelliole which
covers them being ruptured, and the
minute particles of butter cohere and
form small masses visible to the eye.
This is not a defect on the contrary,
it indicates that the cow is au excellent
butter-maker. It sometimes occurs
with ordinary cows, when the milk
from neglect has net been drawn at the
usual time. The pressure in the udder
may then cause the globules to become
broken and the fresh butter to gather.
There is one thing which nature docs
not supply, and which civilization, the
Germantown Telegraph thinks, renders
quite necessary to fowls. It is char
coal. But charcoal made of wood does
not answer the purpose; it has no taste
of food, is not attractive to the fowl,
and is seldom eaten. But if any one
will put an ear of ripe corn into the
fire until the grains are well charred,
and then shell off the corn and throw
it to bis fowls, he will see sn eagurness
developed and a healthy conditum
brought about, which will make a de
cided improvement. All pale combs
will become a bright red, that busy
song which piecedes laying will be
heard, and the average yield of eggs
will be greatly increased.
Some time ago an intelligent farmer
asked which were the better fertilize S,
phosphates or potash salts. The reply
was, "potash salts where potash is
needed, phosphatos where phosphoric
acid is needed, and nitrogenous
manures where nitrogen is needed.
But if you do not know what your soil
lacks, and want to make sure of a crop,
and enrich your land at tbe same time,
use your nitrogenous superphosphates
and German potash salts together. The
former will furnish nitrogen, phos
phoric acid, sulphuric acid aad lime;
tbe latter, potash and magnesia. Thus
you will have a fertilizer with all that
tbe plant needs a complete manure.
At the same time it is well to remem
ber that you may lecd your cops, not
only directly by giving them these in
gredients in guanos, phosphates, potash
salts, and so on, but indirectly by
rendering stores of plant food, present
n the sou and atmosphere, available
through tbe tillage and use of cheaper
fertilizers. A little lime of plaster may
sometimes be thus more valuable than
an amount of phosphates or potash
salts tbat would cost several times as
much."
A letter writer tells that some fif-
een years ago I had a small apple tree
lURk icoucu wusiuciauiri a uiuvu m
stake by it, tied a string to a limb and
fastened it to a stake. The next year
the limb blossomed fully, and not an
other blossom appeared on the tree.
Therefore, after thinking the matter
over, I came to tbe conclusion tbat tbe
string was so tight tbat it prevented
the sap returning to the roots, conse
quently fruit buds were formed. Hav
ing a couple of pear trees that were
lartre enoup-u to bear, but never blos
somed, I took a piece of coarse twine
and wound it several times sround the
tree above the lower limbs, and tied
it as tightly as I could. The next
spring all the top above the cord blos
somed as white as a sheet, and there
was not one blossom below where tbo
cord was tied. A neighbor, seeing my
trees loaded with pears, used this meth
od with the same result. I bave kill teef
tried tbe experiment on several trees
with equal success. I think it is a
much better plan than root pruning.
In early summer, say June and July,
wind a piece of .strong cord several
times around the tree or single limb
and tie it, the tighter the better, and
you will bo pleased with tbe result.
Next winter or spring the cord may be
taken ou."
A correspondent says- "During a
long life I have had much experience
in the management of hogs, and I pro
pose to give your readers my preven
tive for that general fatal disease, chol
era. Prevention is better than cure.
Take pine tops or leaves aad boil in a
half barrel of water sufficiently temske
a strong solution; add thereto tbree
pounds each of copperas and sulphur
and one quart of salt. If pine tops or
leaves cannot be bad rosin will answer
say five pounds. This mixture is to
be poured over, say eight or ten bun
dles of corn fodder (though Hungarian
grass or oats will answer), saturating
well. This must be fed to your hog',
and they will eat it very well, but it is
advisable that tbey be kept up over
nigbt without feed. It will cut the
worms out of the hogs, which, I claim,
is tbe main cause of this disease. This
remedy should be given once a wetk
or mereaoouis. as to tne porportiou
per hog, any farmer can readily deter
mine that. I have adopted this treat
ment for the last forty years, and have
never had a hog sick with the chalera.
Hogs running on clover or other grasses
should be salted regularly, and never
should be allowed to remain on such
pasture more tban two hours at a time.
In addition to tha specific treatment
here given, I bave uniformly made it a
rule to keep tar, wood ashes and salt
in my hog troughs. Speaking of
troughs, 1 will give my plan for mak
ing them. For a trough sixteen feet
long take two heavy planks, one twelvo
inches, tbe other eleven inches in width,
fit them together in something like a
V shape, and at each end nail pieces
two feet long and one foot wide; make
it immovable by securing with sharp
ened stakes driven in the ground, so
as to draw to the trough. I find that
bogs will not stand in a trough thus
made, and can't root it over. In tbe
year 1843, ia Jefferson coanty, TeD net
see, my neighbor, CoL Wm. Moore,
was feeding 2400 head of large bogs,
and lost of this number fully ten per
cent. With my lot of COO, with the
treatment described, I lost none."
The Pacific Mail Steamship Company
has sold tbe steamships Henry Cbaan
cey, Montana and Arizona. Tbe first
named is in New York, and broogbt
$10,000. The other two are in San
Francisco, and were sold for $13,000
each. Tbey were built in lb63 and
cost a large sum cf money, and even as
late as September 10, 1873, the tbree
were valued at $440,000.
Tbe report of tbe Memphis Chamber
of Commerce shows that tbe blessed
town, which isn't very fat or much
puffed up just now, guzzles $10,000
worth of lager each summer month er
one thousand men take seven drinks
each dsy at fifteen cents each drink.
It's a dead lose which, invested ia a
manufactory, woold feed and clothe
and hleaa a multitude of woman anr!
Em mi cai.

States to Australia is ssiumiog propor
tions 1000 within two months.

TiNone need fear that th? Constitution now

Davis, the other by Hamilton. These men

"rebellion" and Hamilton opposing this
revolutionary, ruinous and despotic policy.
The quarrel grew in fierceness till the close

body. It adjourned with its tak half fin
ished. Davis left in high dudgeon. A
president of the body he forgot or refused

one part is fitted to another, and the whole
is not an unit. This framework of organic
law will be gladly overturned, and no mere
prejudice of the people will defeat the ail op

process of creation by as earnest thinkers
and honest patriots as have ever served this
or any American commonwealth.

WHAT THE CONVENTION I1AS DONE.
The first of the standing com-nittees of the
Convention reported yesterday. Another
will soon give us schemes defined for the per
fection of the Judiciary system of Texas.
Very certainly this committee has toiled as
siduously and surely, within the brief period
of its existence, it could hardly have agreed
upon a policy to which even the committee
itself assents with any degree of unanimity.
Upon one single point tbe whole Conven
tion is an unit, that one-third of the coun
ties of Texas are not in a fit condition,
while universal suffrage is the absurd pol
icy of the State, to create for themselves
local courts. .In Northern and Central and
Western Texas where the people, and not
the rabble, are supreme, the masses have
declared that they preferred a judiciary elec
tive by "the people; but since the govern
ment of Texas ia now and must forever re
main a white man' government and there
is no danger of wrong if the judiciary
be creative by its chief magistrate and
legislature, it is commonly asserted that
if no other recourse be presented, the jud
ges slia'd be eligible by the Legislature, the
Governor nominating. But another scheme
has been defined which may accomplish the
purposes of. conservatism, and at the same
time be universally approved. The judges
will be elected by the State at large or by
districts so defined that there will be no
danger that the ermine will be worn by one
unworthy of Us stainless honors. The dis
cussion of this necessity, and the pitiable'
condition to which counties and towns in
southern districts may be reduced, if the
Constitution do not guard them against in
tolerable local taxation imposed by those
who pay no taxes, will lead the Convention
to make the payment of a poll tax a condi
tion precedent to the exercise of the right
of suffrage. It will further be provided
that no indebtedness shall be incurred by a
county or town unless the proposition to
create the debt be first approved, at an elec
tion held for the purpose of determining
the. question by .the .taxpayers and free
holders, who must pay the debt. The De
mocracy of the Belton district and other
like conventions have approved this pur
pose entertained, we believe, by. the great
majority of. the Convention. Practically,
by this provision of organic law, conjoined
with tbe imposition of a poll tax of two or
e7en five dollars, the very aim proposed to
be attained by restricting suffrage in corpo
ations to freeholders is attained. Rings
ruling and ruining towns and counties of
Texas at this hour, constituted of counfy
and town officers co-operating to retain
power in each other's hands and making a
joint stock arrangement by which the reve
nues of both the county and town are made
to serve as a corruption fund and as a basis
of all sorts of speculations all these beau
tiful schemes, now operative in many por
tions of Northern and Southern Texas, will
be suddenly cut off, and that without rem
edy. Thejieople of Texas will not be dis
appointed in these or kindred results. Two
thirds of the members of the Convention
have already shown that they are not dema
gogues. They came to make the best and
most enduring government possible
for Texas, and would make the
people of towns and counties abso
lutely free to do as tbey please; but, as
society is now organized, and as the Fed
eral Constitution subsists, it becomes abso
lutely necessary,' in more than a third of
the counties of Texas and in half of the
towns,' to prevent robbery and wrong and
the desolation of the fairest districts of the
empire by restricting suffrage, to the ex
tent that it affects land and money alone,
to those who have land and money. "TJicre
is and can be," said a hard-fisted black
smith yesterday, "no justice in one man's
voting my money out of my pocket into bis
own and be has. no right to vote awsy my
money unless, at the same time and by the
same act, be votes away his own." No
one can object to J.a scheme of legisla
tion thus wisely approved. With its
perfection and the publication of the
fact everywhere, there will be fewer
person to object to tbe proposition,
already agreed upon in committee, to abol
ish the Immigration Bureau." The best
population of the Southern and .Western
States, when they find that they will be
forever protected, in towns and counties,
against the ravages of the rabble, will seek
homes in Texas. Tbe curse of society and
government at this hour ia wide district of
Tenneaser'-4of Mississippi, Alabama,
Georgia and S iuin Carolia came of the fact
tbat constitutional conventions, wben they
sat in tboe States, were moral coward, and
constituted of demagogue instead of
statesmen. They dared not," a will be done
here in Texas, declare for absolute local
freedom, and to make this desirable, for tbe
restriction of suffrage in corporate elections.

LERDO AND DIAZ-HAYES AND
TILDE.1.
The restoration of the government of
Lerdo in Mexico is desirable simply be
cause it is the only legally constituted
government of the country. Its legit
imacy is unquestioned and legitimacy
is of infinite value to peace and order
and perpetuity of wisely conceived
political institutions. No event in
American history was of such value to
the United States as the peaceful solu
tion of the Tilden-IIiyes contest for
tbe presidency. The forms and facts
of law and of pcaoe and order were
observed and Hayes became President.
When we were almost Mexicani.eJ
and statesmanship and patriotism in
terposed and gave us legitimacy in
stead of ceaseless revolution. To the
forty millions of to-day and the count
less millions of the future it really sig
nifies little what may be the name of
tbe President, but it does signify
everything whether he hold office in
accordance with th: forms and rtq'iire
menU of the law. If he be the lawful
president and a wise and honest chief
magistrate, the many reck little whether
his name be Hayes or Tilden and pos
terity will care infiuitely less whether
he were called Republican or Democrat.
What the man sajH and does affect the
living -f to-day that he was Vie legit
imately and peacefully choten Presi
dent affects posterity forever. Ten years
hence it will hardly occur to the peo
ple to inqdire what was his appellation
and it will signify absolutely nothing.
Mr. Hayes is conscious of this fact, and
therefore, while making history, rarely
inquires whether partisanship approves
or condemns his deeds. But the great
est good fortune that ever befel the
country consists in the fact that Mr.
Hayes became President legally and
without a Mexican struggle for
office. The world thinks the better of
America for its wisdom and conserva
tism property has a surer value
peace and order gild the horizon of
tbe country's future with most brill
iant hues. If we admit the genius and
wisdom and patriotism of Diaz and the
unworthiness of Lerdo, yet Lerdo was
the legally chosen President; his Su
preme Court was legal, and so with
his Congress created and existing in
conformity with the country's laws,
and Lsrdu's was the lawful, constitu
tional government of Mexico. Priests
and political adventurers, ambitious
Iglesias and Diaz, and patriots here
and there, overthrew Lerdo and he was
exiled. A lawless adventurer of great
ability, but still lawless, Laving no le
gal or .legitimate claim to power, has
thrust right and law and legitimacy, in
the person of Lerdo, from office, and
Diaz, in order to retain his place, like
all usurpers, is generous to the people
and makes the best possible president.
But Diaz at last, however wise and
just in his own conduct and fortunes
and hiatnrjr, ii vll tu
Mexico, and the greater his success as
an usurper tbe greater the inducement
for others like him to revolt, and peace
and order under such a ruler are im
possible. Stability is more wanting in
Mexican systems of law and order than
even-handed justice. Diaz is the im
personation of lawless instability. Re
spect for law and conservatism are es
pecial needs of Mexican populations.
Diaz is the embodiment of lawlessness
an J force enthroned and triumphant
in spite ot tbe laws and the constitu
tion. Ilcsius, the Chief Justice, eon
spired with the conspirators, not that
Diaz might ba enthroned, but that he
himself might be president. Bat he
gave Diaz such strength that he made
himself helpless, and Iglesias, like
Lerdo and Mejia and Eacobedo, is an
exile. There can be no peace or good
government in Mexico until the people
of that distracted country have ac
cepted the invaluable lesson taught by
Conkling, Thurinan, Gordon and La
mar and by President Hayes. The
law is wiser than the bayonet; wis
dom, than force; Throckmorton and
Hancock than Mills. So true is this
tbat the violation of legitimacy pun
ishes fearfully and with absolute cer
tainty the wronjj-doers. Peace or re
pose or industry or prosperity cannot
bless Mexico till the law and the con
stitution reassert omnipotence and the
government of Lerdo must be rein-
tated.

TON 1IIIL1S IN THE KOUl'If.
It is stated that Lamar and Gordon
have agreed to make such combina
tions, when Congress meets, that the
duty on cotton mill machinery may be
removed. Every member from the
South, unless there be srrviviug car
pet-baggers who came hither to give
additional power in Congress to New
England, will support the measure.
Western Congressmen who hope to
accomplish local purposes, and would
therefore win favor in the eyes of the
compact South, will co-operate with
Lamar and Gordon, and the result may
be the extinction of duties on' cotton
mill machinery and the transfer of
British and French cotton manufactur
ing industries to these States. Here
tofore New England has shaped at
will, and with selfishness harder tban
tbe granite of New Hampshire, the
commercial policy of the government
of the United States. Navigation acta,
tariffs, fishing bounties, river and har
bor and light bouse improvements have
all beta monopolized or shaped in ab
solute conformity with tbe interests of
New England. In fact, the Democratic
party baa been the Southern party, and
in adbeaion to lofty principle it bu
evar refused to sharn benefactions At
the hands of Federal power. Falling
back, like Hark Twain's male, on iu
baanches, it refused to draw titer it
into tbe South a railway or a subei
dixed staamship line, or that in pretti
est benefaction X the poor, a cotton
buIL Democracy haa learned wisdom
la tbe school of terrible adversity. It
"roost lower" than it naed to. It
un't "stuck up any more. It
bow it has lost wealth, and bow New
England and tbe East are aggrandized.
8terile hillsides, in a climate in
tolerable, blossom a the rose.
while in States' on which God
exh soiled beneficence man bat ex-
bausted crime and folly. We are poor;
they rich. We have been cursed in
the sublime foliy and then in the mock
heroic stupidity of our leaders. But
we are getting bravely over it. Lamar
was foremost of Bourbon Democrats
and of violent secessionists and now
he has turned his back upon the past
and is foremost of rogretxiAa. Gor
don, a younger and more vigorous man,
keeps pace with the Demosthenian de
claimer of the Federal Senate, and
Southern poverty and dire Southern
necessities, imposed by the follies and
ambition and local selfishness and
want of original common sense of
these men, are to be retrieved at last,
and simply because the people demand
it. Lamar, the very impersonation of
what was original Democracy, is the
father of the Texas Pacific Railway
bill in tbe Senate, and Clint Atkins,
tbe first cousin of Isham G. Harris and
his full brother because born like him
of the womb of straight secession
Democracy, is the father of this in
ternal improvement anti-Democratic
measure in the House. Lamar
and Gordon and Atkins and
Harris dare not vote against the
subvention to steamship lines to con
nect St. Louis, Memphis a id New Or
leans with Havana and Rio and Vera
Cruz. The President will commend
the iichemc, and Democrats are no long
er Democrats when they approve. The
South has spouted enough about chiv
alry; the people have left the politi
cians. We demanded bread the party
leaders gave us stones. When we
asked for ti.h they gave us a serpent.
We were dragged down to rags and
wretchedness because there were no
statesmen and only ranting, fiery, fu
rious prejudice and passion-breeding
declaimers. The people followed for a
time; but they have gone far enough,
and Lamar and Gordon and Ben Hill,
the most vigorous and boldest reform
ers and thinkers of the day, accept the
injunctions pronounced by our necessi
ties, and in. future years the South.like
New England, will te the beneficiary
of the Federal system. No scheme
more promotive of Southern wealth
and prosperity, none that will avail
more in giving employment to the re
cently multiplied poor and helpless of
the South, none that could result in a
greater revolution in the condition of
poverty-stricken Southern towns and
cities could be devised than this which
may induce European manufacturers to
transfer their costly cotton mill ma
chinery to the cotton fields of the
South.

The corporators in the new Austin
and Pacific Railway Company are Geo.
K. Davis, Boston W. H. Hart, Henry
Marquand, Henry K. Sheldon, W. F.
Buckley, John Barker, Lewis T. Gun
ther, James T. Raw I his and S. W.
Smith, New York, 400 shares each S.
W. Morton. New York. r00 J
F. Crosbv. F. A. Itinc. anI UV n. D
ker, of Houston, 490 shares each and
C. A. Burton, Geo. Goldthwaite, Hous
ton, 100 shares each. Tbe officers are
Crosby, president Baker, -vice presi
dent; Rice, secretary and treasurer.
The capital maybe twenty million dol
lars. The officers make oath that the
stock has been subscribed to the
amount of a thousand dollars per mile
and that five per cent, has been paid
in. Has it also been checked out Is
it proposed by these excellent gentle
men to build a railway or to force
Tom Scott, if he must add an Austin
branch to his trans-continental line, to
pay for this franchise? Very certainly,
since Texas contributes ten times more
than any State to the so-called "Texas
and Pacific" road, Texas should have,
when losing the trunk line, at least a
central branch to Austin, Houston and
Galveston. In view of this palpable
necessity these speculators in railway
stocks rush to Austin and seize this
franchise. Will it obstruct Scott's
plans If he must build a branch from
the one hundred and third parallel in
the Panhandle to the State capital
So obvious is Scott's need for this char
ter aud of this land grant, now se
cured by the gentlemen above named,
that very many suppose that these
corperators are only representatives
of Scott's interests. If they be not and
do not intend themselves to build the
road and only to urey upon Scott in
such a case we can assure them of lit
tle popular sympathy. If they do not
begin work in earnest we may know
that this is the scheme, and then,
since their device can only retard the
work which Scott would achieve, and
since it is in such a case only a scheme
by which blackmail may be levied up
on Scott in such a case tbe movement
will hardly be tolerated by popular
necessities or popular patience. But
the scheme is said to be legitimate,
and the hii;h character of those em
barked in it confirms the assertion.
They propose to build for themselves
a railway from Anstin to a point on
Tom Scott's trunk line. It will con
stitute part of the original Huntington
route, direct from San Francisco to
Austin, Houston and Galveston. It it
part of that route to which the States
man, through a series of months.
directed attention as the shortest
from the Pacific to the Gulf.
Perhaps, after all, Uontingtoa, whose
alliance with Scott was not of choice
or of ardent affection or admiration, is at

tend, through these corporators, still
to control the shortest line between

the point of junction of Scott's and
Huntington's lines. Huntington re
fused to buy the charter owned by
