and others, and said, when in this city,
tbat be proposed to take out this very

poration. Is this the execution of that
asserted purpose! Huntington gave
Scott tbe least possible assistance when
Congress sat. He Was eminently cold
aad listless and simply did not oppose
tbe schemes of tbe great Pennsy Iranian.
Will tbe conflict between these two
men be renewed t Is this tbe first gun
in a new war between tbe two grand
Turks 1 Is Huntington resolved to bold

LICK OF TEXAS.
Tue pood people of San Antonio
have accepted the proposition of the
Statesman, so often urged through
the past three years, that the extension
of a railway into Mexico, from the
United States is an absolute social and
political, as well aa international aad
commt-icial necessity. Nothing else
can maintain peace between the two
republics and a six months' war would
cost tenfold more than tbe proposed
railway; and while the war and its re
sults would be an unmixed, measure
less calamity, the railroad would pay
for itself again and again in the amel
ioration wrought in moral, social, gov
ernmental facts in Mexico and on tbe
frontier of Texas. The United Sutes
must choose between the two agencies
of conquest, and there can be but one
decision. It is evolved by the bare
statement of the facta and inspection
of the map of the two countries.
Assuming that the road must be
built to prosecute possible war cheap
ly and with immediate results, and to
maintain and perpetuate peace, if we
prefer peace, it is also assumed that
the materials of war and of commerce
to pass over tbe road must come like
our armies from the East. These army
supplies and munitions and soldiers
can be shipped from Boston to Austin
without change of cars. There are
three great competing routes from New
York to Austin one through Memphis
another through St. Louis and a third
through Chicago and Kansas City and
Denison. All these converge at Aus
tin on the shortest line from New York
to the City of Mexico and Mazatlan or
Acapulco. The detour via Houston
and the Sunset route adds three hun
drcd miles to the distance to be trav
ersed, and if tbe Federal government
must build a railway to the Rio Grande
it would necessarily extend the
great system of American railways
from Austin. It is sheer nonsense to
talk as do San Antonians of a narrow
gauge from San Antonio to Eagle Pass
or Laredo. The transfer of govern
ment freights, horses and munitions of
war from ships to shore or from one to
another car is not to be dreamed of.
An air line route, and a continuous
route, over bridged or under tunneled
rivers is an indispensable prerequisite
to the approval of a route between the
capitals of tbe two republics. San An
tonio is keenly interested in this mat
tcr. It seems tbat great soldiers, like
Johnston and Ord, accustomed to make
San Antonio an abiding place, have
been discussing questions to which the
Statesman has again and again ad
verted. The press and people have be
come profoundly interested in the
scheme, and Mr. Schleicher, the excel
lent member of Congress from the city
of entombed civilizations, will proba
bly have the question presented when
Congress sits in October. Meanwhile,
th nvnprs of th Tntprnntltt a r
the Missouri, Kansas and Texas road,
which seeks access to Austin through
Fort "Worth and Meridian, will appear
in Washington, if a war cloud arise on
the confines of Mexico, to urge the im
mediate extension of the International
as a military road from Austin to Lar
edo. If there be no change in our re
lations with Mexico the road. must be
built, and its iron bars will constitute
a stronger bond of peace aad assurance
of prosperity than .any protocol or
treaty ever executed between two co
terminous States.
T11K EASTERN WA11,
It is singular that England manifests
keener anxiety than Prussia and Aus
tria when Russia threatens the obliter
ation of Turkey from the map of Eu
rope, and England is surely in no great
er danger in Europe, and as a Euro
pean fact, than Austria or Prussia, and
England's power in India is no more
endangered than that of Prussia and
Austria in Europe. Still, it seems that
Prussia only watches France intently
while a great black cloud which may
burst fatally at any moment upon Aus
tria and the German Empire overshad
ows the East. Austria must surrender
the Danube to Russia, and as willingly
as we would the mouth of the Missis
sippi to Mexico, and yet people never
seem to think of these facts, and only
remember that England owns a major
ity of the stock in the Suez canal, and
that the security of England's East In
dian possessions is threatened by tbe
advance of Russian armies towards
Persia and Hindoostan. Tbe opinion
seems to obtain in England tbat war
for India would cost more tban
India is worth. British troops
can only be transferred te
the remote seat of war at enormous
cost, and John Bull is accustomed to
contemplate war purely as a commer
cial transaction. It is also trne that
England is no longer warlike in
strength, -habits or .tastes. British
armies are weak and inefficient in ma
terials and organization. Officers buy
places and weakness is dominant from
corporal to the general-m-chief,
Rationally enough there are members
of Lord Beacon&fieM's cabinet who
oppose participation, on any terms, in
the war in the East, and it seems tbat
a large majority in parliament concur
with these party leaders. Very
certainly, as we contemplate the
inimenss resources of Russia and
its comparatively easy routes of access
to tbe scene of hostilities, it would be
sheer folly and madness for England
to enter the lists alone. If France and
Germany stand aloof, England will
aiso remain at peace, f rom no
quarter of Europe do we discover the
slightest symptom of any purposes en
tertained by aay leading power to co
operate with England. The growling
of the Great Bear near tbe north star
has silenced tbe stars tbat sang to
gether, and tbe cross of St. George
pales its ineffectual fires. Just now the
prospect for world-wide war is dimmed
by tbe wise policy of England, which
may lose India. This loss Is less tban
tbe cost of war, to which might still
be added, as a clear loss, tbe Britisb
poasessioBsia tbe East.
Tats steamer City of Brussels is still
missing fifteen days after she should
bare appeared in port.
A shbewd observer has said that the
impressions a foreigner forms of this
country depeid to some extent upon
what kinJ of a government Le has
been used to, but in a far greater de
gree upon the position he held socially
and politically in that government.
Those coming here from the lower or
peasant classes of almost any part of
the world will find many advantages
that are elsewhere deuied them. la a
very short time they are permitted to
enjoy all the rights and privileges of
the oldest citizens, and of course they
cannot look with other than admiring
eyes upon a country tbat is so condu
cive to their welfare and happiness.
On the other hand, those coming from
the higher or educated classes find
themselves at once reduced to the
level of plebeians, and are natnrally dis
gusted to see that education and re
finement count for comparatively little
in a country where sharpness in bar
gaining and money-making constitute
a chief, if not the chief element or euc-
cess, and the longer they remain here,
while they may find much to admiic
the more things they will see to con
demn, and very justly, too. But we
are not equaled by any country in the
world for bribery and political corrup
tion, and the Japaneee was not far
wrong when he adjudged us to be
among the most backward of nations
in governmental progress and reform.
It is really shameful the way we allow
ourselves to be led about by the noses
by dishonest and disreputable politi
cians. Men that would be a disgrace
to any community are elected and ap
pointed to offices of the highest re
sponsibility, while honest men, who
refuse to sell themselves to political
mobs,are nearly always defeated if ever
they attempt to stand for an office.
Any man with a little talent, plenty of
trickery and a pocketful of money may
foist himself into almost any position
he chooses, and if any ones dares to
stand up and propose a scheme of im
provement he is immediately hissed
down by one class, who are so ignor
ant that they don't know what good
manners are, and by another class who
have prostituted whatever talents they
may have had to greedy and unscrupu
lous ends. We all know these thincs
are so; and we deserve to have a set of
knaves administer our laws for us, be
cause we cling with such a death grip
to these old forms and parties with
which savages would consicier them
selves disgraced by having any con
nection. Franc M. Paul, who owned and ed
ited with Henry Wattetsm and Albert
Roberts tbo llchd, Mlo flagrante, in
Chattanooga, till Sherman smashed
this and oth r things to the sea, has
begun tbe publicati n of another Itehd
at Chattanooga. Paul is an apostle of
honest, just, fearless conservatism. He
says:
m j ci oi tue I'resiaens naving
for its purpose tbe restoration of good
government to all parts of the country,
the reform of the civil service, the
bringing about of good fellowship, and
cementing the bonds of union and
brotherhood between all the people of
all the States, shall have our cordial
approbation. The South needs all the
good tbe President can bestow, and a
continuance of the liberal policy thus
far pursued by Mr. Uavei certainly
challenges the respect, and will win
the confidence and support of her peo
ple. We shall ever hold the perform
ance of duty and the award of exact
justice as far above mere party fealty.
We snail be as quick to condemn and
denounce anything obnoxious to just
and pure administration as to approve
that which is just and good."
The Statesman and every honest,
intelligent patriot of the South, loving
his country more than his party, has
given utterance to the same purpose.
Such sentiments can overspread the
whole South and never become thin,
and we are led to reflect that less of
partisanship and more patriotism are
the greatest need of the times. It is
a new gospel but the true one for adop
tion by the intelligence of the South.
The disposition of the people to think
for themselves, to forget the old and
accept the new, to forget the past and
live for the present and future, grows
stronger with the diffusion of intelli
gence and incursion of diverse popula
tions. The more we see and read and
know, the more men from more coun
tries we meet, the less our isolation of
fact and thought, the less strong our
narrow, selfish prejudices of country
and of party and the more perfect our
cosmopolitanism and the worthier are
we of the age and country. A Repub
lican, we may be forced to learn by
the conduct of the President, may be
honest and wise and patriotic, a fact
which few Southern people would
have admitted six months ao and the
last of these would have been Franc
M. Paul.

tainly each aspirant must speak on

ocratic members from Tennessee and

struction of the Pacific Railway. Then,

must not, like Randall, vote for North
ern and never for Southern measures.

like those of the Texas and Pacific
Railroad, are resolved to press this is
sue. Hon. Randall Gibson, of Louiai
ana, will offer a resolution in the Dem
ocratic caucus declaring in favor of
the rebuilding of tbe levees, and of
the completion of tbe Texas and Pa
cific Railroad by the aid of the
national government. If the present
candidates, inside tbe party, will not
approve these measures, there msy be
a new deal, and tbe party itself msy

Wong Chino Foo, the Buddhist in
New York City, disclaims being a
Buddhist missionary, denying that he
has any mission at all but to tell the
truth about his countrymen, who have

tian lands. For instance, be never

mals were eaten by the people of China.
The most absurd stories of every kind
were told-about the Chinese and be
lieved by intelligent Americans. The
Christian missionaries told them, and
the Christian peoples credited them to
barbarous heathenism. But there were
facts that ought to explode such mis
representations. For example, he
thonght it mi jilt be admitted that it
takes a high order of intelligence and
enlightenment to govern four hundred
aad fifty millions of people and keep
them in order. The United States held
only forty million?, and tbe robberies
auu murders ana otuer crimes here far
outnumbered such occurrences in China
with ten times as many people. He
thought this a noble and practical
showing for China. If it did not rec
ommend the religion of the people, it
spoke well for their morality. Besides,
image worship does not enter any more
largely into Chinese devotion than it
does into the worship of the Christian
church. The Chinese idols, as they
are culled, were simply emblems of vir
tue, just as tbe figures of the cross and
tbe saints arc, and 'Wong Ching Foo
couldn't sec the difference in the spirit
of such rites.

riyal.

Editor IkHhvriitic statesman Iu a
number of your paper a few weeks
since, I saw an nnalysis of what pnrpoas
ud t be m ot the votes cASl by The
members of our late Constitutional
Convention entitled "Taxation and.
Revenue." This vote is not fully and
correctly stated, as I atn prepared to
show. But it seems to me that if on
cote of the twenty or thirty cast bv
that body on said article and its differ- 1
ent portions, is to be taken as an inder
tt) t tie sentiments and intention. of ita
vuuio, turn, mo uuai vole tne ono
by which tho articlo was adopted
should be the one selected, and wbicb
you will find on page 720 of the con
vention journals, l will now analyze
it. Of the members of the convention
whose votjs are here recorded, forty-
one are for it and thirty against, (four
paired oil.) The Democratic lawyers
on this vote were nearly equally di
vided, eleven voting tor and thirteen
against the article. Of thn farmer, a
large majority voted for it. All the
doctors, preachers, teacher, all the

i tors but one or two, voted for it.
While on the othor hand tjvery Repub
lican that was present when tho vote

of this aiticlc, and they had four or five

land holder, land agent and ultra
railroad man in the body.
Aow, with a little demagoiniery.
this vote can be used with telling ef
fect against our anti-constitution agi
tators. Everybody knows that preach
urs, teachers, doctors, farmers and -merchants
aud editors are seldom 1
speculators in wild lands or railroads.
while many of those classes who voted
against this article are noted for there
propensities. All those voting for it
were Democrats, while every Radical
voted against it.
Manv of the ablest and mirest men
in our body voted against the adoption
of the article entitled "Taxation and
Revenue." aud- principally in conse
quence of its rigid provisions with re-
gard to taxiug railroads and out-county
landB. 1 also opposed some of these
provisions, but voted, as many others
did, for its adoption, because we saw,
and plainly so, too, that it was the best
we could do, and tiiatil faithfully car
ried out it would redeem thi State and
counties from their indebtedness and
save the people from being overbur

It is a well known fact that mnny of
our Republican friends in the Consti
tutional Convention opposed tho best
provisions in tho Constitution In the
Convention, and tried to have as many
obnoxious measures adopted as possi
ble, so as to defeat our organic law
before the people aud they had several
able men, too, in our body. T bey also
declurcd solemnly against its adoption
in their party conventions, and rnarrlipd
tv n.v. AsnaJiu ouim iiioi'.lili VBiT mCT
iu vicrcat, it .uutwiiuBianuing too ul

Now the truth is that no ono vote
is a fair test of the views of any of the

article (Taxation and Revenue) as a
whole, but the only fair way to reach,
this is by consulting all the votes cast

Now, Mr. Editor, we dislike very
much to no'ico these little matters, but
the "squib" (saddling the produce
article entirely ou the Grangers)
gone all over the State uncontradicted.
We very well know that the object of

pretend to know what "smart Aleck"

State, but wo will now see if the dif
ferent papers will be ss ready to pub
lish my analysis." as tbe other not
my letter, but simply my analysis ot
the final vote on the article on "Taxa
tion and Revenue." I guess not but
let us see.
Your's respectfully,

Ex-Governor J. Davis has been in pos
seasion of the matter embodied in the so-!
called memorial of Merrick Durant for
uiacy months, and has been .holding it to
ue at some opportune moment against the
present feute administration. It was got
ten n; for him by a special agent at Wash
ington, and in D-eembcr last, just a
few days before, the meeting of the
Galveston convention, before which Coke
was to be a candidate for re-eleetioo,
these very papers, now presented to the
Legislature, were brought to the States
man office by Col. DcGrf.ssand propositions
made for their publication in the States
han. These propositions were declined,
though we must admit, having heard so
many whimperings in regard to exorbitant
commissions allowed the Messrs. Gid
dinga, we had some apprehension that
truth, partially at least, attached to the re
ports in circulation. However, the prop
osition to publish was rejected, and now
this matter is sprung, and that too when
truth has proved it as unworthy of considera
tion. The question arises, has Merrick
Dttrant, presumed to be proper business
men, and in face even of a complete settle
ment, of their own choice, made between
them and the Governor, been the instigators
in the presentation of Jiis memorial, or is it
the work of Republicahs desiring to cre
ate d'mcord iu the Democratic party,
Tiik Ereuham Banner thinks that the
greatest need of Texas would be supplied
by wot ton mills, and therefore we have
suggested tbeir exemption from taxation
that capital and skilled labor may be in
ducted into the State. We Would have an
immense establishment, and for the reason
that the costliest employes of cotton mills
esn supervise a large as well as a small
manufactory. A great cotton mill creates
a home demand for all surplus products of
plantations in the vicinity of the cotton
mills. Were these large manufactories es-
tablished in the midst of a co.ton district,
the expense of ginning, baling, waste,
transportation, etc., would be saved.
There is do good reason why the manufac
turing of cotton should not be done on the
soil that produces it, and that sootier or
later it will be in the manner indicated
above, we have no doubt; but until this
can be accomplished the Banner is content
with small factories, and any is preferable
to none.
The International must come to the Ave
nue and to the river. Every public and
private interest confesses the necessity. By
whatever route or at what3ver point the
railway may approach the center of trade,
it must encounter violent opposition.
Property-holders too remote to derive ad
vantage from the location of the depot, and
those so near that locomotives may disturb
the repose of those who "love mine ease
in mine inn" all these will hurry up and
down the streets and bjzz-fuz city fathers
and rush frantically into the embraces of
loving lawyers and file bills and pray for
eternal injunctions, and there will be a nine
days' wonder and excitement, to be forgot
ten when the volume of trade and popula
tion rolls over and crushes it out. We will
soon forget, in the progress of grand events,
the woes of the helpless and hapless who
would forget the interests of the many in
conserving those of the few. The many
must triumph.

The committee bkd the beneiit of lull dis
cussion orally, and by written brief of
learned counsel, both for contestant nl
Hitting member, and, after mature reflec
tion and examination, believe the silting
member entitled to the seat.
Granting that the word "eligible,"' used
in section 19, f article 3, of I he Constitu
tion, refers tj the time of the election a is
stated by Cushmg (Law Leg. Ase-euibliee,
sec. 78,) and to signify '"capatde of beiug
chosen." as it is dchned in our lexicons as
well as in the case of Searcy v. Grow, 15
California Reports, 121. Still tne commit
tee believe that the section referred to can
not affect the election held oi the fifteenth
of February, inasmuch as the present
Constitution did not take tffoct, or to use
the language of theordTuauce eubinitring it
to the vote ot the ptoplr, ''b .-couie tlin o
ganic and fundamental law of the State"
until the eighteenth day of April, 1870.
It is true the ordioauce providing fur tbe
election of this Senate took effect in
November, 1875, but the .ordinance no
where prescribes, by reference to the Con
stitution or otherwise, the qualifications of
tbe Senarora to be elected uuder it,
It will not be contended for a moment
that the Constitution just superseded can
govern in cases arising under the present,
for the old t'onstitutiou nowhere contem
plates such an election, and could not pro
vide for its own destruction or anything in
cidental thereto. The committee, bailieving
that the sections of the Constitution pre
scribing the qualification)! of Senators can
have only a prospective operation, recom
mend the passage of the following resolu
tion, to-wu i
Benolted, That A. P. McCormick possesses
All the qualifications necessary for a S na-
tor under the Consti'iiuon, ami was proper
ly and constitutionally elected to the office
be now holds, nod is entitled to hold bis
seat in this Senate.
Other matters of law and fact not being
necessary to a disposition of the case, aud
in tbe view taken of it by the committee,
have not been thoroughly considered and
are not reported on.
McLkart, Chairman'.

The recent grand illaplay In celebra
tion of MardI Gras at Gulveston wna
not onlv very creditable to tin taste
and liberality of the good people of
that city, but showa them to oeiuny
alive to the Importance of making
their city an attractive resort In win
ter. The fact of there being no cele
bration In New Orleans Increased the
attendance and attractiveness of the
Galveston affair. That beautiful city
caught the mantle of her radiant and
yet sorrowing sister us it fell, mid hus
worthily worn It. The ur'istie taste
and culture displayed In the produc
tion of the pageant and tableaux, the
refinement and warmth of the hos
pitality so lavishly bestowed on her
guests, will do more towards, enhanc
ing the reputation of onr chief city as
tho abode of the beat southern soelnl
qualities than anything else. The
people of Texas have reason to be
proud of the handsome way in which
this "admirable fooling" was carried
out. GalveBtou does nothing in the
way of entertainment by hal"es.
There is a royal profusion alioiit her
that becomes a queen city of bruve
men and fair women. Her people are
not so engrossed In money making
that they can not provide relaxation
once a year for themselves and thou,
gawds of guests besides.

The bill to provide for the publica
tion of all legal notices by tho press, as
well as by the form of posting at the
court house, will shortly b introduced
Into the legislature, and we Iruat that
It will receive, at once, the favorable
consideration of that body. It is a no
torious fact that the present mode of
publication by means of a mere posted
written notice, is a fruitful source of in
justice to all parlies concerned. But
few see It, and olten notices involving
Important interest never reach the
parties they are Intended for. The peo
ple look to the press to keep them in
formed of all public matters, and legal
uotlues are, in one sense, matters of pub
lic interest. The press, as the recognis
ed vehicle of Information, is entitled to
whatever support the publication of
notices may give it, Just as the bar is
for its share in the active machlnervof
the law. It asks charity of no man,
but requires that It shall be paid for its
Jabor. The necessity and Justice of the
proposed law are too plain to all who
have examined into the evils of the
preunt system. With such a law in
force as the one proposed, there will
then be absolutely no excuse for inat
tention or. forgetfulnesa. The notices
will be published, and if men will not
read enough to keep themselves inform
ed about what is going on around tbeni,
then they deserve to sutler and litigate
in the dark. 1

ah a.af the legislature. If ihe presi

The noticeable feature In the above

S7.j caused tne iauureoi an uuuiuu

further, that the bouses which suc

ceding years.
From the past aud Its experlenceswe
naturally turn to the future with its

those who see nothing but brightness
therein, we certainly do discern some
ground for encouragement, and think
ft will not be difficult to place It in au

its succeeding months of depres

if tbe necessity for retrenchment has

That excellent German newspaper, the
Texaa SiaitIiulletin, tells us. tuat, before
tho war between the State, the jury system
here waa bartl endurable, and now it has
become utterly corrupt, and must be
thoroughly reformed. As it exists, thi
supposed lTJ?warkof republican right and
liberty ia absolutely odious. The evils of
which we complain are not due exclusively
to the acceatiion of the npgro to the jury
box; but when negroes ronstitnte juries
decent white men, if poHHiblc, evade
this eervice, even aa the surging black
mob about a vot'iDj' place disguota decency
and repels intelligence, giving absolute su
premacy, in many places, to ignorance and
barbarism. But the exclusion of the negro
from the jury box ia not enough. We have
suffered republicanism in America to go
mad, and even to destroy itself, and while
it ia quite impossible, since we have laws
for the collection of debt, to destroy the
jury eyst?m utterly, Its purification is quite
as indispensable as that of the ballot-box.
Our German contemporary says that
From a Democratic point of view we tnav
desire to regulate, as a sovereign State, our
own legal procedures, uud we are much in
cliiii'd to claim it as a right. Still, with
all our dfsire for relorm, we can not place
ourselves in such a position. The Consti
tution of the United States unmistakably
declares, that iu all criminal cartes the ac
cused is entitled to a jury; also, in all civil
cases, where the subject of dispute exceeds
the sum ot twenty dollars, litis stipula
tion makes it useless to investigate the
value of any proposition, which tenda to
curtail the powers of juries. Keforma in
side of prescribed umiut cannot extinguish
entirely the jury system, but ouly correct
its fault. e may prescribe qualifications
for jurymen, without regard to color or
descent, and il our conception of the mat
ter be corrt-ct, may lay down rules guiding
them in their fuuetions, aud which may
correct the greateat errois that from ail
our experieuce the tiystetu at present is bur
dened with.
The present Constitution, Radical in
origin,- demands of jurjrueu no other quali
fication, save citizen.ilup of the county, and
right to vote. The fact of their being paid
for services rendered, also the auihontv
granted- to sheriffs and their deputies to
pick them up according to their momentary
whim, if convenient, even from the gutter,
are causes tending to create a desire for
change. In opposition to the doctrines of
the Republican party we contend that the
juryman be subjected to greater scrutiny
regarding character, than ia now required.
To strengthen our argument, we refer to
a matter of fact, that wherever the jury
system exists either in America (with the
exception of the Sou i hern States), or be
yond the ocean, qualifications for jurymen
are required.
Iu England, where our present jury sys
tem originated, according to an act of Par
liament it is necessary that the juryman
should be a landowner with a yearly income
of ten pounds sterling. In France he must
be thirty years of age and bring evidence
that he is capable of reading and writing.
In Germany be is only qualified who pays
at leant twenty thalers land tax or twenty
four thalera business license. And, not
withstanding all these restrictions, expe
rience has taught that formal qualifications,
mentioned as examples here, are not suffi
cient guarantees against abuses, fortbeyare J
not sufficient to exclude from the jury box
men of deficient education and doubtful
character. Iu Europe, the making out a
list of jurors, means the selection of
citizens who meet all the requirement ao
law. It is intrusted to responsible offi
cials having the additional power to exact
such evidences of moral and intellectual
worth as they deem necessary. According
to our opinion, founded upou facts which
experience has furnished, he alone should
be accepted as a juror who can read and
write, resides in the county, and is acknowl
edged by the county court to be a man mor
ally and intellectually qualified. The so
called packed juries brought the system
into disrepute; professional jury loaf
ers, who besiege the court house, and
who, fit for nothing else, are placed
upon the jury bench by careless sheriffs.
These should be restraiued in their
nefarious practices and compelled to make
up jury lists constituted of men of un
doubted capacity and integrity, Industri
ous aud honorable men would care nothing
for the compensation of a few dollars;
whereas, the professional jury loafer craves
it, and claims it as a just reward for idle
ness, and for a stupid verdict, conflicting
with common sense. To be called to the duty
of a juror, in itself, ought to be evidence
of a man's worth, and thia public asser
tion of bis excellence should be compensa
tion for pecuniary sacrifices. To redeem
the reputation of the now little thought-of
juror, he must understand that it ia an
honor to be called to such duty, which the
State has a right to demand of turn for,
if we grant to the State the privilege of
calling on us (as it may do according to an
act of the Fourteenth Legislature) to follow
in the tracks of a murderer at the peril of
onr life and without compensation, we sure
ly may ask the juror, who risks nothing,
to serve without pay.
Another abuse in the jury system is the
almost evtr-ready disposition of our judgea
to excuse jurors. Should sheriffs ever auc-
ceed in selecting a body of respectable citi
zens there will be produced before "his
honor the most trifling causes for release.
a&d if none other ia available the last re
sort remains, "the gentleman, who shuns
thia duty, has already formed and expressed
aa opinion ;n and thus it happens that when
the puulic interest is deeply concerned the
box ia filled either with stupidity, incapable
of an unbiased and just decision, or with
elements too indifferent or too lazy to ex
ert themselves in behalf of justice and right
The St aatt-Bulletin concludes that there
should be prescribed qualifications for ju
ror ot property and intelligence; aecond,
that juror ahould aerra without moneyed
recompense and deem this employment aa
office of honor; third, that xemptiona
from jury service be rigidly construed, and
that the cxpraaaioa ot aa opinion is evi
dence of fines for jury service in criminal
cases; and. fourth, that a verdict may be
rendered by two-thirds of the jury ia crim
inal and other casea.
Opinion aa conservative a these were
hardly looked lor ia a German newspaper,
But this journal, the J?u"wi, is conducted
with singular ability, and its assertions hare
deservedly great force a n on g onr German
fellow-countrymen. But may we not ask
oar excellent contemporary ir it be not
more important to purify the ballot-box than
the jury box. The one affects the whole
people; te ether litigant. The one de
stroy communities: the other wrong In
dividual alone." 'Both reform are indi
pensable to food government, and when
H'T TEXIK GERII MAX
TUB EW ,08JlTi:TIOfl.
the necessity for the excUioaof the less
evil is conceded on all hands, we may surely
hope that the timidity of demagogue will
not prevent the purification of the ballot
box when they will not hesitate to purge
the jury box.
THE CROti-ntfsae CMIMK O K1DI
CAt.la.1t. Every government, whatever its form,
must be responsible for the condition of its
people. Save when some distinct, well-defined,
providential calamity befalls the
State, it condition most be ascribed to
those who make it law and th i shape the
morals and industry and modes of thinking
and acting of the people. The condition
of the people of Texas ia influenced largely
by the action of the State, and of the whole
Union, by the conduct of the party in power.
Through fifteen terrible year Republican
statesmen or Republican party leaders bare
defined the fearful fortune of the United
States, and at last their condition becomes
absolutely intolerable. The Democratic
part;, thus far, is responsible for nothing
of evil, and claims no honor for aught of
good supposed to hsve been achieved.
Whatever of prosperity, of hopefulness and
of perfect blessedness prevails should be
ascribed to Republicanism exclusively, and
whatever of suffering, of poverty, of want
of employment, of scarcity of money, of
thieving in office, of extravagance in taxa
tion practiced even here whatever of these
evils may be operative must be charged to
the account of Republicanism. The deficit
in its account with the American people is
enormous. It is needless to add to the
"wrong side" of this balance sheet the vast
sums stolen by the petty thief of Louisville
who killed himself last week and only rob
bed taxpayers f $75,000. The sums stolen
by the Credit Mobilier, by the Pacific Mail
Steamship Company, by Boss Shepherd, by
the whisky ring,' the cotton stealing ring,
by the proposed San Domingo swindle, by
the Freed men's Bureau bank, and the
twenty millions stolen annually from tax
payers by the national banks it is needless
to name these little incidents of knavery of
daily recurrence while custom houses exist
as an organized system of robbery that the
deluded people may be perpetually robbed
and never dream of the extent of losses to
which they are subjected. For all these
incidents of horrible government the Repub
lican party is wholly responsible. Worse
than all these calamities, and worse than
the iniquitous governments set np in the
South worse than Governor Bullock's inim
itable knavery in Georgia, when he stole
millions in the very face of the whole coun
try worse than the depravity of that foul
mob which preyed upon South Carolina;
worse than Warmoth's and Kellogg s enor
mities done in Louisiana, or Spencer's name
less crimes in Alabama, where negro legis
latures were bought and sold in market
overt; worse than the fortunes of Missis
sippi, where black savages are rapidly de
stroying, in wide districts, every vestige of
civilization, and where a citizen and soldier
of Maine employs a yellow substitute to
discharge the office of chief magistrate
of the once proud commonwealth worse,
thousand-fold worse, for the whole people
of all these States is the scheme of unpar
alleled wrong by which these Southern and
Western communities are to become the
meanest vassals forever of the holders of
two thousand three hundred millions of
gold-bearing bonds. African slavery ele
vated the race that was imported by our
beneficent New England forefathers, and
sold to us, when no longer profitable to
themselves, under the operation of beauti
ful prospective local emancipation acts;
but these bonds make ns slaves for whom
there are no prospective emancipation acts.

Dispatches of lat night announce the
sudden deati., ia Eautern Tennessee, of
lion. Jtudre ,oboon. ery certainly, a
few mcatl'S ago, there was not a living
mau seemingly sturdier, stronger or brawn
ier thsn'tbe ex-President.' Fie was the very
impersonation of fixed indurated physical
and mental strength and endurannce. flis
head was simply enormous, his chest thick, j
is shoulders broad and slightly rounded
by a tailor's training,' his skin brnorad,
and from his great gray eyes, be
neath shaggy brow a, there gleamed,
when the massive machinery of bis
intellect was set in motion, a lambent
light that fascinated those whom he ad
dressed. His body, in fact, was of the
same mould with bis intellect. It was i
atrong, rugged, muscular, but devoid of
comeliness. Mr. Johnson never uttered a
sentence that was remembered for its beau
ty, and yet few. living men have exerted
greater power as orators over the multitude.
Of rhetoric he knew absolutely nothing,
and nothing of the arts of declamation. His
voice was unmusical and his face by no means
attractive but there was magical power iu
the strong good sense that distinguished
all his utterances and made him a master of
the multitude. Wbcu we add to this source
of power the fact that he was deemed
singularly honest ajj truthful by those who
new him best, and that profound earnest
ness characterized bis speeches, we may
comprehend the source of that unvarying
good fortune which elevated the unlettered
tailor of Greenville to the Presidency of the
United States. He was born in North Car
olina. His parents were wretchedly poor.
and though the story comes down to us
that bis mother was a rude woman
extraordinary genius, his father ,was
worthless. He came a lad to Eastern Ten
nessee; was wedded, when hardly grown,
a woman who taught him to read and
write. Ve have seen men, now distin
guished in letters and in professional life,
who were educated at Greenville, and who,
while Johnson was making their clothes,
were constantly subjected by him to ques
tionings that little concerned the -tailor as
such. He became, after a time, a member
f a college debating club, and soon made
his mark in that body. But his intellectual
training was his own unaided work. It
began when be became an alderman,
and ended when he breathed no more. He
was a tireless student, and was perhaps as
thoroughly versed in English and American
history as any statesman of modern times.
His illustrations of modern American facts
were all drawn from these sources. He had
profound admiration for Burke and the
great men who shone with him in undimmed
lustre one hundred years ago, and from
their lives and deeds Andrew Johnson drew
the loftiest inspirations of his genius.
ne was a member of the Legislature of
Tennessee, for many years a member of the
lower house of Congress, and there
distinguished as the father of the
homestead bill, ne was twice Governor
of Tennessee, twice chosen United States
Senator, then made Vice-President, and be
came President by the death of Abraham
Lincoln. His career from that day forth is
too freshly remembered to require recital at
our hands. He was sixty-six years of age.
It may not be improper to add that Gen. W.
B. Bate will worthily succeed Mr. Johnson
in the Senate of the United States.

There is one special prohibition that might
well be asserted by statute law, but it is of
such vast importance to public decency and
to public and private morals that even the
State's organic law might recognize and
adopt it. No licensed minister of the Gos
pel should be suffered to hold a political
office. When preachers abandon the pulpit
they become desperately reckless, and have
done infinite harm. Beecher's degradation
began in his Kansas rifle and other political
speeches and culminated in the infamies of
the recent trial. Kalloch and Simpson, the
latter Grant's confessor, are names that be
come hideous in the country's history, and
the more because, not long ago, they were
brilliant lights illuminating the pathway of
the blest towards God's throne.' Grant's
conscience-keeper has been polluted, and
the cause he represents degraded by his
fraudulent investiture with an office, which
was no office, that the government might
pay him $10,000 and his wife $3000 while
he circumnavigated the globe discharging
the pretended duty of an inspector of consu
iates. The pretence for public robbery is so
thin that men contemplating it are insulted
that the priest and president thought to
cloak a fraud beneath the cowl and cassock.
That this graceless public plunderer should
come back to Washington, and, before the
public turn up the white of hi awful
eye, and ask God to bless the country he
has roobed, crowns depravity with such un
hallowed impudence and publicity that the
country s murals recognize the shock, and
men ask, is there truth or virtue or Christian
honesty under the sun? Let the Constitu
tion of Texas render Simpson's and
Beecher's names and vices immortal by de
claring that no professed evangelist of God
shall be a government placeholder. 1 Let
this temptation to evil be removed, and,
since church and State, in accordance with
the faith and practices and wisdom of our
fathers, are wholly distinct, let ns assure
the perpetuation of thia relation of church
and Stat by excluding the special repre
sentatives of the churches the priesthood
of all denominations from the country's
offices.

It is matter of surprise unongst tbini
i ing fcople throughout tho country, both ia
and out of the State, that tho Legislature
of Texaa hcitata xnomcct la the
course proper to bo pursued" La' the tttIo
mest of the matter of controrersj be
tween this compaor and the State. As a
State enterpriiu it is the ob!t" single road La
which the people generally in erery portion
of Texas are directly Interested. By in
otu line whea completed originally con
tctaplated, and by the Tarious roads which
it taps and crosdee, it will become the oiij
trunk railway bearing upor the" whole face
of the State and which will be practically
used by the people of erery section. Taking
Texarkana or Jefferson as its eastern initial
point, it diroctly concerns all Northeastern
and Eastern Texas in farnishDg the rnqst
direct route for freight and pas sago to Gal
veston, and as a means of intercommnnica
tion between at leaat one-thirtf of the State
on the East with Anetin, the capital, the
seat of tbe General Land OUice, and ali the
departments of governments, as well
as with San Antonio, and the West. While
this is undeniable it ia generally true that
the whole of the Southwest, embracing
nearly another third of the State, is practi
cally and peculiarly interested in the road
as a State highway, affording as it will the
only direct means of communication be
tween the Southwest and tho capital and
the whole of Eastern and Northeastern
Texas.
Kunning diagonally accros3 the State,
two-thirds of the people are direct
ly interested la its construction. So
far as they are concerned, therefore, it ia
not a private enterprise, but a great public
highway. But it is equally plain that the
whole people of the State are as much in
terested in the completion of tho road as
those of the East and of tho West. Con
tinue the line to San Antonio, and let us
see how the people of tboXortb and North
west aro immediately and personally af
fected. The line of tho road, in its exten
sion from tho northeast to San Antonio, it
will be observed, crosue9 nearly every im
portant' railroad and every riTer in the
State. At Jefferson it touches the naviga
ble waters of Red Hirer; at Longview it
crosses the Texas and Pacific road, .and a
branch intersects the same road at Mincola,
in Wood county. At ITearne it crosses the
Texas Central. West of the Brazos it will
croES the'Galvcston and Santa Fo road; at
Austin it touches the western branch of tho
Central, and will cross the contemplated
road from Indianola or Galveston through
Austin to the northwestern portion of
the State. The people of the whole
of the northern and northwestern por
tion of Texas are, therefore, interested
in the road as if the, highway
passed directly by their doors, nave they
business at tho capital, or are they inter
ested in baring direct communication with
the whole West and Southwest? They have
it in the North by direct communication
from Longview or Minoolawith Austin and
San Antonio. All that broad slopo of
country between Paris in tho North and
Gainesville, Fort Worth and far beyond,
including Sherman, Dallas,' Waxahachie
and flourishing towns and country border
ing on the Central, are interested in this
road as a highway for themselves to the
capital and to the Southwest. To reach tbe
capital and the Southwest from tho whole of
this great region of country, by tbe gn c
est saving of time, distance and money, is
by way of -the Central road, and the roads
conneoting with it, to Hcarne, and thence by
a short and direct line to Austin and San
Antonio, the International must be built.
Tho saving in costs of transportation will
equal tbe taxes to bo paid, not to speak of
tho economy of time and augmented val
cc3 of property everywhere.
The objection, therefore, that the people
in the extreme portions of the State are
not interested In the road, is unfuudcd.
To illustrate: suppose you have a
large county, and it is daairable to tax the
people to keep up one or two mam
thoroughfares to tho court house, and to
erect necessary bridges. WIll it be said
that the people in the remote sections of
the county are not interested in such
bridges, and ought not to pay any portion
of the taxes?
But. as a "national hiebwaj," its im
portance to the whole people of Texas can
not bo over-cetimated. 'i bis Interactional
route is the true Southern trans-continental
road. Extend it only to the Rio Grande,
and see how it will allect all Taa: It will
do more- to make Galveston the great city of
the South than any other preation of states
manship. At tho Rio Grande it will be met
-by the Mexican and Tierce systems of
roads, and the great body of the rich
Mexican trade will fall into the lap of our
Gulf ports. The extension of the road to
Saa "Antonio will accomplish its early
completion to the Rio Grande, and
to tho City of Mexico and the PaciEc coast.
Connected, as the road will be, with the Texas
and TaciSc, the Central, the Galveston and
Santa Fe, and the Galveston and Saa Anto
nio road, every village of Texas is deeply
interested in the enterprise as though it
passed by the door of each inhabitant. It
will do more to consolidate and urufy the
State than all othr enterprises combined
more to bring all the varied interests and
sections of the State together, socially
and commercially, more to develop the
State into one Tiat, indissoluble empire,
and to foster aa ennobling State pride ia
the breasts of all fcer people than evea hal
lowed mrraories of early Texan history.
And will not our 8tatesmcn,-havirg ia view
tto largest and best interest cf our whole
people, comprehend tho magnitude of this
rrcat question. a it ailccts tho future, and
so shapo legislvJea with jastice, modera
tion and liberality, that graal results cer
tain to follow tie completion of tiis tigh-

of Texas that the woes of government are
i Kal in origin, and that heavy tax t

words, the security oT the people against

The blossoming beauties of the Bureau and

freedom, to adventurers from abroad,' and
their fatal intervention in affairs of govern
ment are confessed, not only by every citi
zen of the South, but by Morton and other

terly incapable of shaping tbe fortunes of a

sage in which he is forced, by glaring and

of all future years, involved in this perfec

end proposed to be achieved can only bo
effected by restricting suffrage to those who
are both capable and interested in exercis
ing the right of suffrage wisely and well.

For ten days or longer nothing has ueen
done in Congress. The supremacy of the
Radical party terminates on the fourth of
March, when a large Democratic majority
begins to shape the country's legislation.
Meanwhile, there are favorite schemes of
Radical leaders which they would hasten to
perfect; one of tbese, the civil rights bilL
In order to- reach it, it was necessary to sus
pend the rules of the House requiring a
two-thirds vote, and Ben Butler could not
control this voting force. Saturday, Mr.
Speaker Blaine, the. facile representative
from Maine, suggested to Mr. Butler the
propriety of amending the rules. In this a
simple majority only is required. These
rules, sanctioned by time and approved, by
long experience, have now been transformed,
and the majority is invostod with absolute
despotic mastery of the country's legisla
tion. There would be less to condemn in
this action of the majority if recent elec
tions, everywhere, had not pronounced
against this Tery scheme of Radicalism pro
posed to be enacted, and there is no appli
ance of legislative art or parliamentary
expedient to which the Democratic minor
ity were not justified in resorting. Ben
Butler and his 'adherents owo a share of re-
tpct to the people of the country if none
to the rules of Congress, and since, in re
cent popular elections, the civil rights bill
was evciywhere condemned, the odium in
cident to the measure will only be height
ened by its fraal enactment under the opera
tion of a gag law.
There may be a deal of good evoked from
this terrible struggle in Congress, and from
the flagitious enactment of the civil rights
bill. It will drive down great wedges be
tween the races, and beget the enactment
of laws, wherever it is proposed to have a
white man's government, restricting'suffrage
in corporations to freeholders. Social sys
tems are beyond tho reach of legislation,
and Congressmen only illustrate hatred ol
Southern whites in this effort to enibi.ce so
cial and race equality.. The perfect rem
edy is in our own hands. Gov. Coke's
philosophy, involving the concession of the
rights and powers of counties and towns to
the Legislature-", may now be inverted and
the greatest possible freedom given munici
palities. Surely this result may be attained
when none vote save those interested in the
operation of local laws. There will be no
danger that counties will be bankrupted by
the people of counties when only those
vote who must pay. Southern power in
Congress is not lessened by restricting suf
frage in local elections, and while Boa But
ler and his coadjators, pronouncing us
murderers and savages, would seat Sambo
at our tables, we may as well unseat him
in counties and towns. The gaia in tht
latter instance to ourselves is of infinite
valnn: tha loss to Sambo is nothinjT, to
Radicalism everything.
Whether the IWyujia- belongs to War
mnth or not. it signifies nothinr. The
ricayun is the best of Southern papers.
But it does not belong to Warmoth. We
were only going to fsy, however, tiat the
Piccwne. and thJ Democratic Legislature,
should now declare that no debt contracted
or parer issued cr tax levied by the Kello
government shall ever be paid. Negroes
and tieir white masters have plunged every

and now let the State government live

when tie people cf Louisiana aro restored
to power all tax titles new made bhall be
annulled. Br tiis means, as proposed by
Join Quincy Adams tie robbers may b

tic Crescent City, and tio next Presidential

and perfect relief.

$ot many daya ago M. Chevalier, a dis
tinguished French sawn end political econ
omist made a speech at Greenwich, Eng
land, in which he said substantially that
before the end of 1877 all tbe Governments
of Continental Europe would be obliged to
decide respecting tbe renewal of their com
mercial treaties, and that this fact brought
up the question of free trade. He added
that this question is not confined to Europe;
the Universal Exhibition that is organizing
at Philadelphia ia a si-p that the day draws
near for the practical introduction of this
policy into the Republic of the New World.
Experience and facts are the best evidence
nod tbe best arguments to persuade the
people and their legislators of a necessity
of a change from prohibition and protec
tion to free trade, from heavy customs du
ties to a very liberal tariff. The most con
vincing proof to le employed is the exam
ple of England, whose progress since she
threw off the restraints of prohibitive pro
tection is among the miracles of National
advancement. M. Chevalier said that the
protectionists understood that if they were
beaten now, they were beaten forever.
They would consider it equivalent to a vic
tory if they could only arrest the course of
the liberal current which has been running
in Europe since 1800, mainly under the
guidance ot the late government of France,
and of Napoleon personally. To the new
trade policy M. Chevalier said France owed
her ability to bear the heavy burden im
posed by the war of 1870. She is able to
supply an annual budget of $500,000,000,
besides some $200,000,000 of local ex
penses. Her bank, notes likewise, to the
aventge amount f $500,000,000, circalate
in all part of the nation witbout any dis
count, a financial phenomenon witbout a
parallel.
It was also stated by the same speaker
that the narrow escajie Continental Europe
had lust April from a bloody contest has
aroused a feeling very favorable to free
trade, for the reason that it is endowed
with a great power to lorce the war spirit to
recede. Not that free trade may always de
feat warlike enterprise, but that mankind
more and more clearly comprehends the
waste of war and opposes it with contin
ually increasing effect. This does but son
firm the philosophic theory that the more
intimate the trade relations of different na
tions are, the less disposed are they to enter
upon the work of mutual destruction.
"Free trade is the intimate friend and ally
of peace." The Iiight Hon. William E.
Forster made an excel lent speech, following
that of M. .Chevalier, and on the part of
the American gentlemen present, Mr. Na
than Appleton, of New York, made an ad
dress which was cordially received by the
company. Mr. Appleton declared that he
came from a community were protection
was. the practice but he gave his reasons
for believing that -the manufacturers them
selves had got as much out of it as a system
as it would yield. His view was that high
duties inv:ted smuggling and fraud, and
that the demoralizing effect upon the people
and the 1 atioaal character is more than
can be compensated for by all that enters
the treasury in duties. He likewise quoted
with extreme pertinency the saying of the
son-in-law of Mahomet: 'Ti the course of
my long life I have often observed that
men are more like the times they live in
than they are like their father," a saying
which Professor Draper pronounces to be
profoundly true. Mr. Appleton thought
that the surroundings of the American
people are compelling them to accept the
truths of the free trade theory, and that it
would form "one of the stoutest planks of
the winning platform in the Presidential
year of 1870."

We would not have Texas sulrdivided.
While centralization and boundless power
over men and property is steadily usurped
by coup re ss and State lines are rapidly ob
literated, the iutegrity of Texas, its local
memories, history and purposes of agraud
izeinent must be cherished and perpetuated.
The Union, iu maintaining peace aud com
merce between the States aud with foreign
powers, is an admirable adjunct of political
existence; but the invasion of every
local right, the infraction of each in
cident uf local freedom Iy steady ag
gressions of congrebs and of the chief
executive render the physical potency
of a Slate eminently desirable. Grant and
Kellogg would never have defied aud plun
dered and tyramzed over New York as over
Louisiana, and there is nothing like capac
ity to defend one's self when a bully struts
about a village gieeu or an ignorant reckless
soldier wields a despot's armies and de.
praved judicatories, the one to rob, the
other to enslave and degrade a people. We
never expect to see another military machine
made president that the country's laws may
be set at naught and local freedom crushed
but flie woes of Louisiana and terrors of
savage governments in South Carolina and
Mississippi illustrate the necessity for power
at home to command respect abroad. Dis
tricts of Texas of which it has been proposed
to make separate Scates might become negro
er Mexican governed adjuncts of the
Union aud profitless neighbors of Texas.
With its present confines the northern
and western counties occupied by farm
ers from beyoud '"the" Ohio "and from
Western and Central Southern States and
counties bordering on the Gulf of Mexict
peopled mainly from the Gulf States, while
the West is enriched by Germau industry
and intelligence from all these diversities
of population of diverse modes of think
iug, there must be evolved that perfect
unity shaped by common interests and a
common destiny. To make a government
which protects so many different popula
tions successful and even endurable, it
must move, as the Federal government was
designed to do, within a very narrow
sphere. Counties and towns must!be assurred
of tke broadest freedom, and suffrage there
fore, must be restricted to those interested
in making these governments cheap and the
people prosperous. The greater and
broader the State, the greater the legisla
tive, freedom and power to be entrusted to
counties and towns. The interests of Jef
ferson and of Laredo, in future years, may
be as diverse as those of Maine and of
Texas, and no one central government can
legislate for both localities in reference to
local facts with equal justice. Each dis
trict, like each State, should have the great
est freedom compatible .with, tbe mainte
nance and perpetuity of the common gov
ernment. Therefore we have said that the
constitutional convention had a very sim
ple task before it. It need only create a
frame work of government. The legisla
ture and county and town governments
must do the rest, and that these m&y serve
the people well, and make Texas the most
attractive countiy ia the world, aad that the
best classes of people of all the States
may be induced to seek homes in Texas, the
right of suffrage must be restricted to those
interested in its proper exercise. Without
this, local freedom is not desirable, and with
out it, the protracted unity of this great
State is neither possible nor desirable.

SCHOOLS.
If there were only ten farmer in each
county, living ten miles apart, wj would
see at once the impossibility of maintaining
any system of public schools in Texas. To
succeed, under such circumstances, we must
needs have a narrow gauge road passing
each cabin, and all converging at the schi'xd
hou-a s. But we have no such road., and
cannot build them, aud Judge lieagaa Is
right when he pronounces the maintenance
of a public school system, in sparsely pop
ulated districts, wholly impossible. More
over there is a deal of justice in the asser
tion that no man should be forced to toil io
educate anotter's children. He ia not only
wrongfully robbed, but the strongest incen
tive to honest industry is removed from the
path of him whose children become Hhe
beneficiaries of this public charity. :The
products of such institutions are wanting in
personal independence and are fit subjects of
adispotism and not of a republic. Bismarck
understands this scheme of beneficence
thorousihly, and expounds it justly,' when
be tells that he would make the empire or
the government first and the country's reli
gion second, and the household,' if it may
continue to exist, last in the affection of a
citizen of the empire."-We would place iEe
family first, the country' second, and, above
all,' and first in the hearts and thoughts of
the people,1 the supreme majesty of the
Ruler of the Universe. Professional peda
gogues naturally go mad about this mat
ter of forced education at the cost of
somebody else. .They, gather statistics
with wonderful facility, showing that
in order to make people proper citizens the
youths of the country must be converted
in shysters and pill pedlers.
There are certainly two sides to the ques
tion and our govefnment was purest and
best, there was less knavery and more
plain, simple honesty when the public
school system had not given us a system in
the conduct of which there is said to be
more money stolen by placemen than ever
reaches the Federal treasury and that it
costs two to collect and pay into the treas
ury a single dollar. In any event there are
two sides to this question as applied to
Texas in its present condition, and we need
only move slowly and if patriots would
have the future of Texas perfectly blest
they need only guard, for future use,
the m enormous wealth in lands of the
public schools and university of Texas.
Let this be honestly preserved and leased,
but never sold, and then each citizen will
be educated, not by taxation, but by a re
source which a wise statesmanship pro
vided, and which criminal impatience and
folly would squander. Wrhen we have suf
ficient density of population to make the
existence of schools possible the school
lands will furnish revenues enough for their
maintenance, and there will be no necessity
for the taxation of one citizen to pay for
the education of another's children.

Great aud terrible conflagrations in many
cities, especially of this country, serve td
show that we have progressed very slowly
in the art of controlling fires. The destruc
tion of Chicago was unavoidable even when
the firemen of many cities, with most ap
proved steam machinery, exhausted every
appliance of art and mechanical and engi
neering skiil. Even so of the great con
flagration in Boston and when water pipes
are frozen in New York, if a fire swept over
a single street, the magnificent city would
be laid in ashes. The danger of complete
annihilation is even greater in tbe small
city than in tbe great metropolis. Houses
are compactly built, the sun shines as fierce
ly, roofs are as dry and materials used as
combustible, and winds blow as strongly
here on the confine of boundless plains as on
the shores of Lake Michigan. What secu
rity have we against utter annihilation
There are a few bncketsful of water in each
public cistern, and the .city authorities are
excavating a few more; but these,, if a
great conflagration occur, would be speed
ily exhausted. Of recent years much at
tention has been given to this subject, and
it is now universally agreed that steam and
other fire engines and patented devices for
the ejection of carbonic acid 'gas, while
often very serviceable, are not trustworthy.
One building at least is .destroyed before
a, steam fire engine, can be put
in motion and moved a wile, and
cisterns leak or contain too little water.
The decree is final that each building must
have within it: the means vf saving itself.
When constructed iron pipes must be pro
vided, by which each, story may be flooded
at once, and there is no security for prop
erty or health or public decency till the city
sought to be supplied with water furnishes
baths for each household, and a stream of
living water penetrates each story of every
building.' This is of more importance than
the free distribution of atmosphere, siuce
he who does not obey laws of cleanliness
should, of course, be deprived of breath.
In this, the capital f agreat1State, grow
ing in greatness as no other can progress,
there is n adequate security for property
or public health, or provision for popular
cleanliness. With' a river at the base of
the bills, as clear and beautiful as the San
Marcos,- with heights from which -it de
scends, on whose summits good reservoirs
might be cbes ply constructed overlooking
the capital: witn every facility that nature
could provide for the construction of water
works,- such aa are required in ungating
farms and gardens, and, iq cleaning .gutter
and sewers, and in rendering conflagration
impossible, we are content to pay enormous
rates ot insurance, and live, each year,
through nine months of Bummer, with no
more water tliAu should be supplied for i
equal number of canary bird.. The scheme
ot constructing temporary water works, as
approved by our city government, is emi
nently laudable, and will serve many ad
niiiable purposes, and do, now and then.
infinite Koi but if it operates to prevent
the utilization of the Advantage given us
ry tne nver, ana Dy tne natural reservoir At
Mount Bonnell, it must become, in the eyes
oi property-Doiaers, a great calamity.

Intklligkmt people judge of the weight
of ideas and argument by reference to their
own intrinsic worth. It never occurs to
thoughtful person to measure the value of
a production by the name and fame of the
writer, and it signifies nothing, to an intel
ligent busy population, who may be the ed
itors of a paper. They want a quid pro quo
for their money in news, in fresh suggest
ive thoughts, and in 'facts and fancies."
Therefore it is that the Herald, or World, or
Tribune speaks, and never Cbamberlin, or
Manton Marble, or Whitelaw Read. A
trained journalist may designate the pro
ductions of either of these writers, recog
nizing tbe modes of thinking and of ex
pression of each as readily as he would bia
face but tbe great majority of reader only
exact originality, force, clearness and com
pactness, and never inquire, and there is no
reason why they should, as to the author
ship of that which pleases them. Thus the
newspaper becomes impersonal, and to p ro
mp te this result editors even avoid the use
of the impersonal oracular "we" and em
ploy, instead, the name of the newspaper.
We have written this to explain the recur
rence of the word Statesman in these
pages. It is properly substituted for the
wearisom "we" which most delights tyros
in the art of ionrnaliam. v t

VOL. III.
COnPLMEXT TO TUB PUESIDE7TT
A resolution was recently introduced ia
the Senate, complimenting President Grant
for Lis course in tlie late Davis imbroglio,
in which there was a gross and flagrant
attempt opon the part of the ex-Governor
and his satellites to set aside the cml
government, and to reinangurate that con
dition of anarchy, mifgovernmeut and mil
itary despotism, which prevailed here three
years ago, aud which was continued Ly the
List ad ministration. It was amended by
the House and came up in the senate on
Monday last.
lion. John Ireland arose in his place in
the Senate, and moved its postponement
until Friday, announcing that ho intended
to oppose its adoption, and that on that
day he would present analytically his ob
jections to it. For this, without knowing
the grounds of opposition, for they wer
not stated, his course has been the subject
of censure by the extreme Il&dical press.
"What will be the character of Judge Ire
land's remarks we do not know; but we
feci assured, while entertaining the high
est respect for the distinguished gentleman,
who Introduced the original resolution, that
it ought rot to pas?.
It ii au extraordinary spectacle, and in a
purer and better day than this, would have
shocked the moral sense of the country, that
the chief Executive officer of the general
government should be complimented for
simply doing his duty, and that iu terms of
sycophantic laudation. It ia a severe com
mentary upon the evil days through which
we have passed, in which corruption has
been the rule nnd public virtue tho excep
tion. If litis resolution was to pass, which
we trust it will not, no greater satire could
bo uttered upon the Grant administration,
which wo cannot forget,, and tho country
will never sease to rememljer, has, since its
inception violated, every principle of public
liberty.
"We have no unworthy prejudices against
the President; we have no desire to offer
any factious opposition to his administra
tion; we would be among the first to give
credit to all that is worthy in it. And,
while we admire many things he has done
recently, and particularly the course he pur
sued in relation to Texas affairs, we think
it unworthy of the representatives of a great
commonwealth to compliment him for it
They owe something to their constituents,
not less than to themselves, and to that
sense of delicate self-respect which "belongs
to the character and the dignity of a free
people.
Senators and Representatives should re
member that we have passed the Rubicon
when such adulations will be relished or
gratefully received by the people. When
we say this it is with no spirit of censure
or animadversion. Our people have passed
through so much, and have patiently borne
so many wrongs, that when an. officer of the
government, actuated by a patriotic im
pulse, and for once does right, they natu
rally think his conduct should bo the sub
ject of all tho deliberate nnd solemn com
mendation with which it could be invested
by a deliberative body. r

FORE TUB LEGISLATUHE.
Tho appointment of Gen. Jerome B.
Robertson as general agent of immigration,
is one that will meet with genera approba
tion. His ability and untiring industry pe
culiarly fit him for this position. Puring
the late civil war he ro3C rapidly in the line
of promotion, nnd bo it said to his praise,
was beloved and admired in the army for
his amiability of manners, his gallantry,
and that nobility of purpose which always
inspires respect and confidence. The hum
blest soldier found in him a true friend, and,
in saying this,' wc feci that wo could not be
stow upon him a higher encomium.
But we did not set out so much to pay a
compliment to Gen. Robertson, nnd to ex
press our gratification at his appointment,
which wo feel assured he will fill with
credit to himself and advantage to the
State, as to alludo to the important depart
ment over which ho has been fortunately
called to preside.
Tha subject of immigration is of para
mount importance to our, people, and one
that, we regret to say, has been seriously
neglected. The strongest card in tho late
State canvass by the Republican p-rty was
tho failure of tho Thirteenth legislature
to make suitable provision for this depart
ment. It is of very little moment to con
sider whether their, animadversions were
just or unwarranted but now, when we
have a pure and unobstructed government,
and when proper men can be selected, there
13 no longer any excuse tor neglect.
Wc trust that no "penny wise and pound
foolish" policy will ba adopted. Every
good citizen that settles In Texas, beyond
his social and political influence, adds an
nually to the wealth of tho State rorn two
hundred to twenty thousand dollars. With
aa exhaustless public and private domain,
and immense mineral resources, we are
rapidly 'advancing ia population, agricul
tural resources, and commercial wealth.
With these, End with two, and perhaps
three, railroads to the Pacif c, with their
arteries perforating every portion of our
territory, all of which, under a wiso and
bcncScent administration of the govern
ment, will be constructed ia a few years,
Texas, in its colossal size, and in the unifi
cation and .solidarity of itc people, will
present the grandest State on the American
continent.
It is the narch of eir.pire. Shall it be
slow, or under a liberal policy, accelerated,
so that the present generation shall realize
its benefits!

L.lMDLOItls A!IO TENANT BILL..
On Wednesday the above bill was under
discussion in the House. It provides the
most ample security both to the landlord
and the tenant, but in our judgment this
very ample security is apt to work a gen
eral hardship upon the public. It is neces
sary thut some such bill do pass, but the one
as now presented will, we fear, lock up the
entire resources from agriculture for too
long a time for the general good. The bill
appears to 1k just and proper, but its opera
tion under its stringency will, as we con
ctivc iro too far for entire safety. Accor
ding to its provisions there is left no secu
rity against ad vers' ty. It may be intended
to n-itch a class of worthless laborers who
can never be depended upon, but at the
s tine time it reaches all, and, in the end, if
put in force as a permanency, will degrade
the higher classes of labor. In fact, in mis
fortune it will come home to all of us,
White labor under it will Iks brought down
and subjugated. We may be wrong, but
four that the provisions of this law will be
attended with evils that arc not intended
by the framers of the bill. The bill was
ably supported by Messrs. O'Neal, Delany
and others, and opposed in a strong speech
by 5Ir. Triplett. The majority in the House
are strongly in favor of passing a bill of
this nature, and while we feci it is neces
sary, we also admit that the question should
not be hastily dealt with.
iiEVOLirrioNS never
GO BACK-
WABDS.
Tho popular error that "revolutions never
go backwards," finds its contradiction in an
cicnt and modern history. The solution of the
problem of individual and national progress,
prosperity, and happiness, is to be found in
their conduct in the virtues and vices of pri
vate life, and in public affairs,: by that demor
alization, engendered by personal corruption.
How much have we seen of that in the last
few years, and with what gloom has it fallen
upon good men. With what grief have they
beheld the sacrifice of integrity and manhood.
No nation has ever been permanently lost
unless it sacrificed its autonomy by irremedia
ble sins. It forfeited faith in itself, nnd its peo
ple became corrupt.
Greece fell, not by the sword of Alexander,
but by tho gold, of Philip Rome by public
and private corruption Poland by the want
of unanimity of its people, and the ambition
of its leaders Hungary, from a faith hi those
upon .whom it should have relied. Demosthenes
with all his eloquence, could not save Greece,
nor Kossuth Hungary.
What a lesson what a rchuko to all men
who lose sight, even momentarily.of honorable
and patriotic impulses
Revolutions only go backwards with a peo
ple who are governed by noble instincts.
There is a Divinity that shapes our ends,'
and it manifests itself most signally in tho day
of calamity. Wo find this in the history of
England, of France and Germany. A return
ing sense of public and private virtue brought
with it, in every period of their history, a res
toration of good government, i
It is operating in our own country to-day.
Within tho last twelve months vico and cor
ruption in every section of the republic have
been rebuked and punished, and with this,
borne on the resistless tide of popular favor,
comes the first welcome notes of a restoration
of those1 old land marks, whose enduring
lines can never be obliterated.
The ccntrifical and centrifugal agencies that
commenced with the origin of the government
centralization and tho rights of the States,
based upon our federative system of govern
ment are looming up to-day. Upon its dis
cussion wo propose to enter, believing that
centralization carries with it anarchy and a
loss of public liberty.
STATE FINANCES.
To allow the holders of Treasury warrants
heretofore issued, cither to fund them in State
bonds, or to draw interest until it i3 the pleas
ure of tho Slate to pay them, la no indemnity
for tlie suspension of the payment of such
warrants at tho State Treasury, because the
owners of these warrants are compelled to
have the money to pay current expenses. They
arc not able to invest; their warrant are
motdly held as collateral security for money
borrowed In tlie expectation of realizing par
value as soon as sufficient revenue comes into
the treasury, and this expectation was greatly
raised upon their aith in the incoming admin
istration; so much so, that warrants rose from
seventy to eighty cents, and would have con
tinued upward had it not been for a serious
apprehension of tho proposed suspension,
which, if adopted, would so reduce the value
of warrants that they could no longer be held
as security; and a sale of them, under such
circumstances, would bo at rates ruinous to
all parties concerned. Thus a very grave and
serious injury would result from such a
measure.
It is indeed such an easy matter to relieve
the treasury from the present cmbarassmcnt
by just and lawful means, that we cannot see
the necessity of resort in? to harsh and
doubtful measures, such as would injure those
of our own citizens, who confidingly trusted
their own government and who have invested
their faith in the -wisdom and justice of a
Democratic administration.
It b a fact worthy of mention that at least
four-fifths of all outstanding warrants belong
to the ofiicers of the State government to
whom they were issued, while warrants that
were bought on speculation have been
cashed at the treasury. It is to be regretted,
however, that any one who had been chosen
for his capacity to make laws should attempt
to lo-:..vale ci'Jier fcr or against lawful rights
accruing ia legitimate speculations, or to vote
against any measure cf sound public policy,
because tiKculating creditors are to be chiefly
benefitted. Such a rrciudlce. to sav the
:ast of It, is unworthy of a statesman.
Would it not serve tho purposes and credit
ef the State much better to allow a premium
on Treasury warrants in the settlement cf ac

occpeutly so damaging to the credit cf the

PUBLIC AVEIGnEKS.
Since we have already given some promi
nence in our columns to the subject of public
weighers a3 affected by the provisions of Mr.
Simpson's bill, we cannot refrain noticing
what the Galveston Aetf has to say upon the
subject. The 2fac knows that the office of
weigher has existed by law since 1858; that
the charge or fee now paid for weighing at

and that the bill proposes to reduce the num
ber of officers and appointees in the ratio of
one. to seven, besides putting the appointees
under greater and 'graver responsibilities in
discharge of their duties. Tho iliac also
knows that it is no uncommon thing, though
it may by custom be uncommon in Galveston,
for both parties interested in a trade to
be present, by agent or otherwise, at tlie
weighing or measuring of produce bought

for tho cotton crop of Texas and must
the producers of the State, because a
privileged class know it is highly to their ad
vantage, and because the iVVjr takes sides

It wants him to leave this little matter
to take care of itself in the hands of those in

and that the dear people must not put them

It even goes a great deal further, in its un

veston, and wants it, we presume, to continue
its oppressions upon the producing public
This institution, not satisfied in restricting the

arc curious to know whoso interest the Xejif
