THE tEGISLATrBE-DlSCCSSIOXS.
There were two interesting debates Lithe
L. .-Mature Friday. In the Senate, relative
Jo t..e adoption or rejection of the resolution
thanking President Grant for his course in
Texas affairs, connected with the ex-Governor
Davis embroglio.
We were not present, but we understand the
speeches were interesting and able. Hon.
J ohn Ireland, who was opposed to the resolu
tion, and as we thought properly, opened the
debate. He was followed by Messrs. Wood
of Leon, Dillard of Cherokee, and Col. Hub
bard of Smith, the Lieutenant Governor. The
resolution as amended in the Senate was
adopted. Tlie view of the Senate, from what
we can learu, was, that without discussing the
propriety or impropriety of this vote of thanks
and commendation to the President, that, if
rejected, it might be misunderstood, and be
the subject of unmerited animadversion and
provoke unprofitable discussions. The gor-
uion knot was cut by passing it. Perhaps it
was best.
In tlie House the question of a constitutional
convention was the order of tlie da v. In
favor of the proposed convention were Rainey
of Houston county, who opened the debate;
Saver of Gonzales, Henderson of Kendall,
and Ivemble of Ellis. Opposed to the con
vention, and in favor of a commission to
frame a Constitution, were Barziza of Harris,
and Delany of Colorado. Mr. Barziza followed
Mr. Rainey in opposition. The speeches
were ingenious and of marked ability. The
subject was to have been discussed last night,
and doubtless will be resumed to-day. The
people of the State are clamorous for a so
lution of this question, and if newspaper ar
tides, communications and letters, tre any in
dications of popular sentiment, it is almost
unanimous for a convention of the people.
A CORRECTION.
Hon. John II. Cochran, of Dallas, has
been done injustice in our rcport of those
who debated the constitutional convention
question in the House on the night of the
thirteenth. In this report his name was
mentioned among those who spoke in favor
of the proposed commission to frame a
Constitution. Instead oi this he made a
speech in opposition to this plan, and un
qualifiedly in favor of a constitutional con
vention. He boldly claims that .the people
should have an affirmative as well as a neg
ative voice in framing their organic law,
and therefore judges it as harsh that any
attempt should be made to deny them the
power of exercising this plain and cs
tablishcd right, especially when they
have so plainly expressed their dcsiie that
the Legislature should accord them this
right. Mr. Cochran is one of the clearest
headed members of the House, and wc are
sorry that the error committed by one of
the editors of this paper found its way into
our columns, nis people at home admire
him for his honesty and generous impulses,
and it would bo far from our intention to
do him injustice. We also hope that if our
error has caused any other paper r person
to repeat what has been said that they will
promptly extend him a similar correction.
AVitat gives us great hopes of this Lcgishi
laturc is its untiring industry, conscientious
ness, ability, and the calmness with which
every important question is considered. It is
a matter of profound solicitude to every citi
zen of tho State that this assembly of their
best men should, present n record that will
stand out in history as a model of patriotism
and enlightened legislation. We believe the
members appreciate the responsibilities tt
surround them that they recognize the fact that
the Argus eyes of the Stato are upon them,
and that each member must stand or fall be
fore his constituency, who, in this period of
our history, will be either prepared to praise
or to censure. Wc believe that tho enlight
ened sentiment of the State has on abiding
faith in tlie ultimate fortunate result of their
deliberations, and is already prepared to an
ticipate favorable action on all questions in
which the people arc interested. But, if our
counsels could be heard, we would adviso har
mony and good feeling, looking only to the
enlightened and dignified discussion of every
question involving the public interest and that
attrition of mind which leads to the just so
lution of political problems.
It may bo the fortune of the Statesman to
differ from them, and very widely, upon many
important measures, but it will take part in
their discussion in no hypercritical or censori
ous spirit. Its only object will be the eluci
dation of that w hich is just and right, and to
advance the interests, honor, and prosperity of
a commonwealth, which only requires in the
future, fidelity, firmness, and faithfulness from
its public men.
The objection urged by Radicals to this
Legislature, and their great hope of its destruc
tion is, that its members arc nearly all of one
political faith, and that there will be conse
quent errors and ultimate discord. But the
Republicans 6trangely forget a fact, which all
good men remember, that our people have
passed through an ordeal, which has impressed
its thoughtful influence upon the country.
Since the meeting of the first Congress under
tho Republic of Texas, and the convention of
the people that followed annexation, no Buch
important convocation has been called to
gether. The people so regard it, and the new
government is fully alive to the duties lw-
fore it.

the State will never lc abridged to the thou

cept the plan thus laid down, authorize the

ker of high reputation in New York to sell
the bonds of the State. Such a plan, put on
foot in a regular business way, wiU bring about
the early solution of our financial troubles.

fore bonds could be sold ia New York at
eighty-five cents. They could be pat
the market slowly, and each lime as v. s
made it wouhi be done at increased rate.

confidence ia the present administration, would

withheld, pul lie confidence would be cs:a

Such a course would defeat the popular in
tettion for such a set of men, not King
uelegated by the people for such a purpose,
would fail to embody in the fundameatal
law those provisions that would come
through direct instruction for that particu
lar purpose. Then, again, in conseo.aencc of
a great desire to have an entirely fresh or
ganic law, the conception of such a com-
missiob, though not meeting cxpcctaiicn,
might be accepted. Below wc givo the two
reports upon the subject. Tho minority re
port is an able document, and covers the
question

The undersigned memljcrs of the Commit-
tco upon Constitutional Amendments feel
constrained to di.cc!cnt from tho majority re
port of said coinmittco recommending that
a commission be formed for the purpose of
framing a constitution. We do not conceive
that it was contemplated by our constitu
encies that we should arrogate to ourselves
the power of framing the organic law cf tho
land, nor were we, in ouropinion, elected for
a purpose so foreign to their intontion. The
usurpation of power would be so flagrant,
the precedent so startling, as to crcatcjin the
minds of all thoughtful men the gravest
auxicty. Since the establishment of tho
Federal Union, tho uniform mode of pro
cedure 'concurred' in with an almost un
varying unanimity by all the States in
framing their organic law has lccn to as
semble delegates for that especial purpose
direct from tho people. 80 long has this
rule of proccelure obtained, and 60 satis
factory has been its results, that it is now
firmly established as tho common law of
this Republic. We believe that should th's
commission bo constituted, that tho over
shadowing influence of powerful, unscru-
Kulous and wealthy railway companies will
c brought ti lcar with peculiar force upon
the small number of men composing this
commission, witll a view to mould ttic or
ganic law of the land so as to subserve their
purposes. Your committee aro satisfied that
no undue influence whatsoever will lc
brought to bear upon this commission by
the Executive of this State, nor by tho hon
orable Judges of the Supremo Court,
nor do your committee anticipate that
any of the departmental officers hold
ing under the .'present Constitution,
a tenure of tv.Ticc for" four years, would un
tlcrtake for tinoment to influence the ac
tion of this commission, yet, in the opin
ion of tho committee," the? opportunity af
forded uy the formation of this royal
commission for the exercise of improper
i-a a. a- 1
muucnccsis rupugnaut to eviry principle
of Democracy, and every dictate of right.
Will it not be said by the great mass of
the people of Texas, should tho present in
cumbents bo continued in power, that un
due influences we.c brought to bear upon
this plempotcntial commission winch gra
tuitously proposes to represent the sov
ereignty of a great and intelligent people!
let is it advisable that a Bwcepmg change
bo made in tho officers of State who so
universally command the esteem of all true
men? Can a commission assume that most
responsible task of determining whether or
not tho present Stato officers shall con
tinue in power or a new election be
declared under the provisions of tho Con
stitution they may frame and submit? The
present officers of the government would
naturally feel a great hesitancy and deli
cacy in being confirmed ia positions by any
other authority than that invoked by the
people in the exercise of their inherent sov
ereignty.
Tho very fact of the establishment of a
commission is, in our opinion, anti-Democratic
it evidences centralization; a fear
of the people; an unwillingness to trust
them with the destines of their State.
It is a reflection upon the intelligence of
tho people, and an egotistic assumption of
the superior attainments, knowlcdgo and
qualifications of the Fourteenth Legislature.
We believe that a Constitution, to meet
the varied and diverse interests of an em
pire Stata must necessarily lc carved out of
concession and compromise, and that dele
gates specially authorized to exercise their
judgment in concessions and compromiaci
only should undertake this grave duty.
Should the precedent once be established
that a body elected as a Legislature to frame
laws under an existing Constitution, are em
powered to frame constitutions by commis
sions, with what hesitancy would the peo
ple elect their next legislative asicrcblyi If
one Legislature can assume this power, all
subsequent Legislatures can do likewise,
and an ever-varying, ever-changing and un
settled organic law would be the result.
We believe that it Incomes Democrats, who
are proud of their history and jealocs of
their honor, not to abandon our principle
by delegating our most sacred rights to any
commission, however honored or pure, and
wc sincerely trust that a measure of sueh
tloubtful expediency and fraught with so
much danger, will commend not itself to
our associates in the House.
Should a commission be established, not
more hopeless was the cause of (he luckiest
Peri when 44 the chrjs:ai bar of Eden moved
not," than will be the condition cf tho en
fortunate Fourteenth Legislature, when
they arc forced to confront the Democrat!
of Texa. who have demanded ia no uncer
tain voice a convention from the jxjople.
Your minority of aaid committee, there
fore, with all due deference to the report of
the majority, respectfully recommend the
adoption of the accompanying resolution.

The bill repirted to the senate to day
from the committee on Indian attaint
authorizes the eastern hand of Chero
kee Indiana to bring suit in the court
of claims against the Cherokee uatlou.
It provides for a judicial controversy

prorata share of the moneys urhdug

land aud other property, which the
eastern band maiutaiued belonged to
the people of the Cherokees eastern
baud. The bill authorize the employ

penses, The bill also provides that
the Uhlted States shall be made a par
ty to this suit and be represented by
the attorney general.
In the senate, after the morning
hour, the regular order was laid aside,
nd Senator Lamar proceeded to ad-
ress tbe senate on the Texas aud Pa
nic road. He spoke of the great im
portance of the work, nud said the
ompauy had already completed lour
hundred aud forty-five miles of road,
which is doing valuable aervlce In de'
veloping the country through which It
nasaed. The length of road to be cod
structed Is fourteen hundred miles, the
amount of bouds to he issued could not
exceed $32,000,000, while the amount
of Interest to be guaranteed by the
government could not exceed in round
numbers two million dollars.
The frieuds of the bill advocated Iw
passage to complete a part of the great
railway system connecting tne Allan
tin and Pacitlo ocean. lueconsiruo
tion of this road will open new and
valuable markets for surplus produce,
and the advantage to the government
and neoule to be derived from the
road would be ample compensation for
the aid now asked.
He argues that tbe government
would be secured for the aid extended.
hv a mortgage on the entire road, by
all earnings of the road and by all dues
from the government for the transpor

whether Urn government would ex
pend two million dollar, annually for

the value of which could hoi be meas

The amount asked was hot half the

ets and harbor.; waa not a much as
the government expended lie for o the
war for mall transportation alone to
the Paclfla coast, ami that was at a
time when wo had hut thirty-two

of the oountry were not what
they are now. It was not a fair states
mentofthis case to say that the pas

fifty years were they regarded now as
a debt? They brought uo remunera
tion but lit the case of the road the
government becomes a creditor of the
road!
inferring to the Patilllo status and

tion of our l'aclrlo empire was a re

He then quoted from the speech ot

ImiUo hy a series of railroads, which
uo private enterprise could accom

He then spoke of a bill Introduced
in the house of representatives In 1862,

defeated by those who at that time

the government to oouslruct the
road. Had It not been for
those constitutional objections a
railroad would be now running over

Were no constitutional grounds agalust
government aid lu the construction of
the road, aud read a letter of It, M. T.
Hunter, of Virginia, who formerly op
posed the bill on constitutional grounds,

general views, but In many respects
they had been modi tied by the expe
rience aud results of the war.
He next referred to the grants to
other Pacific railroads and said to the
Northern Pacific tbe government had
giveu a grant of forty-seveu million
acres, to the Union Pacitlo aud Ceutral
acltlo It had given fifty million acres
of laud and its bonds to a prodigious
amouut, but Its aid to the Texas Pacific
was but eighteeu million acres of laud.
The people of the south had already
Invested a million dollars lu the road,
built four hundred aud forty-live miles,
hut they were unable to build more.
The legislatures of every state lu the
south have passed resolutions favoring
government aid In the construction of
this road. He spoke of the advantage
of the soil aud the climate of the south, 1
aud argued that the cost of living
there was cheap, and taxation every
year was growing less burdensome.
Throughout the territory were great
rivers, which never froze up in winter,
nor dried up lu summer, and could
turn her spindles all tbe year round.
The soutberu states bad but a small
portion of their cotton lands under cul
tivation. They could raise enough
to clothe tbe whole world. The mar
kets of Chiua, Japan aud other eastern
oountries preseuted greater advantages
to the United States, aud with the con-
strdctlon of the Texas and Pacific road,
we would be put Into more interme
diate relations with these couutries.
He was satislled that the construc
tion of the Texas and Pad 11 0 road
would be a prompt and most tfllclent
means for the development ot south
ern commerce :111a agriculture, tie
spoke of labor troubles In the country,
and said what we needed was tbe
inauguration of new enterprises to
employ labor. In conclusion, he api
pealed to tbe senators from tbe fnew
England states especially, to support
tbe measure as a work of national Im
portance, in the Interest of the whole
American people.

LAND. The Unit cntury of Aniericao freedom
clows with the annoaocetnent, not only of
the extinction of slavery, perhaps every
where among civili.cd peoples," but' of
sieged wonderful discoveries destined to
revolutionize wholly the- condition and
habits of onr race. If we may attach crer
dence to recitals of facts given with seeni
isijr absolute confidence by newspaper
writers, our relations and mode, of life
are to be thoroughly chansed. Ptenm
is no more. It was tininijMHent three
tourths of a centnry. It reigned su
preme ia Europe and America and was a
mightier conqueror, shaping more effec
tively the condition and woes and delights
of mankind than priests or kings or presi
dents. It was an every day adjunct of our
lives, and served us in all things, and with
a' fidelity that never faltered, a force that
was never weary, and a matchless skill pe
culiar to the genius of steam. It built 'onr
houses, made onr clothes, perfected our
food,' almost enabled ns to fly,, and served
us in all things, save in the mere act of
breathing. It seems that steam must die
with the dawning of the American centen
nial year, and he who pronounces the fun
ereal eulogium upon the dying century
must lavish most eloquent encomiums upon
tbs giant who may be buried in its grave.
For months past we have been reading
incredible accounts of a discovery made by
toiling mechanician and chemist in Phila
delphia, The story goes that he has learned
by accident to use component elements of
water and air in such a manner that boilers
and fuel are practically dispensed with, and
water and air became the resistless motor
of machinery. The power generated is
simply limitless, and we do not see, if rep
resentations made by capitalists who have
already invested, it is asserted, two millions
of dollars in the discovery, be well
founded, why Eads may not abandon
his task at the Balize; why Galves
ton need indulge further anxiety
because a sandbar walls in the city;
and why Cowdon should dream of croco
diles digging a slack water ship canal even
from New Orleans to Fort Livingston. The
weight of machinery and of fnel, and space
occupied on sea-going vessels, must be infi
nitely reduced, and thus the draft of ves
sels relatively lessened and carrying capac
ity maintained. But if the facts slated be
well founded, there will come of this dis
covery even more startling results. The
power of a bird's wing, as related to' the
weight of the bird, is tiie secret of its ca
pacity for aerial flight, and Buch is the infi
nite power said to be generated by this use
of aqueous gases, and comparative lightness
of machinery, that winged locomotives will
be borne about by balloons, and the cost of
attending Democratic conventions at Lam
pasas and other inaccessible places will be
infinitely lessened. The politicians will
then chant in ecstacy the time-worn ditty:
On wing, of (team I'll flr
From gioceree togrocerryi.
Simultaneously the art,: known of old,
even among Phoenicians, of making mallea
ble glass, is rediscovered, and glass as soft
Ind flexible and irrefragable and transpar
ent as DeMorse's cranium is on exhibition
St Cooper's Institute. Everybody except
ropers-in of parties, distributors of spoils
and habitual placeseekers, will build their
dwelling-places of this indestructible mate
rial, stronger and more durable and beauti
ful than Pentelican marble. Will not Aus
tin become a city of dreamland when these
heights gleam in unutterable splendor be
neath the rays of a tropical sun, and the
scene by night will be of transcend ant
beauty when each lamp that glows along the
streets flashes forth reproduced from a thou
sand reflected images of itself Cleanliness
will invade each kitchen, for each cooking
implement will be of stainless glass. With
this comes necessarily, since filth begets
vice, an immeasurable improvement in pop
ular morals:
The night Khali be filled with mnetc,
Airt the cart, that infert the day
Will fold their tent, lice the Arabs,
And aa etlently steal aw.y.
But we cannot find space to recite the
thorough revolution in all popular habits
aad modes of thinking and living to arise
necessarily from these two' discoveries,
the one as wonderful and beneficent as the
other; the one ministering to every practi
cal want, even giving us wings and driving
the sewing machine, and the other giving
brilliancy and beanty to every incident of
every-day life.1 But let ns be patieut.' Letter-writers
are more Inventive than artisans
or mechanics, and even wise men have been
thoroughly misled by discoverers who,
themselves, are insane. For all that there
is much, reason to believe that malleable
glass is reproduced at last and aerostation
becomes possible, by means of a substitute
for steam.

SIT.ENT WILDE
was a "news carrier" in those days. lie
delivered letters and papers for hire, and
charged each citi7en S2.50 per annum." Il
want-iev-rylxdy to fork up for six months

I-:iiali Thoinax, the editor, publishes a
card, in which he tells of his escape from
the British iu obtou, and tbnt having re
moved his printing materials to Worcester
he proposes to remain herC iTesay: "I
escaped myself from Boston on tte ineuior-'
ahle 19th of April, 177.1, which will ever be

publish a good weekly and "Maintain
those Rights and Privileges for which we

this weekly was six and eight pence peraB- 1

Among the many agents for the paper ap
pears the name of the present editor of the i
Spy, the author of a curious hook, by the
way, eulitled "Ancient America." We
have, in the facts here given, satisfactory
evidunce of his intimate knowledge of many

Fl'NEHAL.
The Spy tells of the death of one Jere
miah Lee, Rscriliing to him every virtue,
and especially does it weep when telling
that Jeremiah bequeathed two thousand
pounds to the province. Then, in the next
paragraph, the Spy tells that the six regi-i
ments required of Massachusetts were all

"And may God preserve and make them1
victorious," and just below the. women of
America are requested to send rags to Cam
bridge. Twelve years ago such appeals to
the women of the South filled our Sjy.
M V6TEULY INACTIVITY.
New Ycik City asked Congress what
should be done when a fresh force of Brit

This was before Bunker Hill was fought, j
and Charles Thompson, Secretary of .Con

THE PBLOtilSTS.
Next we have the spread eagle nd of
Levi R'cepherd Co.'s medicinal store.
The term "drug store" was unknown one
hundred years ago, but there were patent

pectoral balm find female strengthening

JOHN n.VNCOCK's LC( K.
Then we have a full account of a great
fire in Boston, its destructiveness ascribed
to the fact that the British wouldn't suffer
the fire engines to be used. On a long list
of suilerers appears the name of Hon. John
Hancock, whose store was burned. They
did not have big stocks in those days.
Eighteen business establishments, with Mr.
Hancock's and ethers," are mentioned as
having been destroyed, and yet the whole
loss was only 25,000 pounds.
brevity.
The editor devotes only four lines to con
gratulations of the country on the fall of
Tieonderogaand Crown Point. The Herald
of modern times would have given half a
column of lightning head lines prefixed to
such a story. Two lines tell as that David
Bancroft, great-grandfather of the lawyer
at Texarkana, and grandfather of the his
torian, had been chosen to represent this
town orcesterj in the Provincial Con
gress.
We have seen the like when the Confed
eracy was hardly born, and those good old
fellows of V orcester ordered all the Tories
(submissionists) to appear before the com
mittee of safety and surrender their arms,
and keep quiet, and have no assem-.
blics. Then an editorial paragraph tells
that Peyton Randolph is President of the
Grand American Congress, and Charles
Thomson a "seasou"able cuss, spelling
his name, in any event, without a "p"-r
was Secretary. We are told of the spirit
ing away in Charleston, South Carolina, of
alt the powder and shot Held there oy the

ists in the council of the new Governor

CTauhs tell of the death of a devout

piety.

the Congress, recommending a convention

JOSEPH WAHREN. t
A closelv printed half column contains

"Per order Joseph Warren, President ;r
News from V lrginia is starying. ana

NOW AND TIIF BLINDERS
CON FKOKK ATIS.n.
OF
The Shreveport Time adverts to a sub
ject of which the Statesmak has to say
that they who sought to establish and prac
tice theories of State rights, or of local
independence, adopted the very policy to
rende- the achievement Of their purpose im
possible. The capital' blunder of the Con
federacy was the creation of its so-called
permanent stovernnaesvs- ead pcrpctwel Con
stitution. This schein s was deemed iatai
to local freedom, and opponents of the
movement asserted the necessary speedy
overthrow .of this Southern laion. une
or two newspapers at intervals, even when
the unthiuking multitude shouted "trea
son asserted persistently that the blun
der committed was fatal, and that two
Unions could not coexist on this continent.
To establish andtiphold them for a time
by means of great standing armies of blacks
was possible; but since such conditions
were not desirable among a people who had
once, known in wnat ireeoom cuuswcu,
these duplicated burdens and duplicated
practical armed despotisms would be redis
solved into free States, and these would re
produce s proper Union, in which absolute
local freedom would be coincident with an
indestructible Union. These results, which
should hsve len achieved by secession,
will be attained by reason 'sad evolved
from the Tery physical facts that define
boundaries for States and fix climatic laws
and those of industry and production. In
fact, the most devout adherent of Wendell
Phillips can never devise a scheme con
templating the nnity of the States which
does not confess and. maintain separate
State integrity. The obliteration of. State
boundary lines substitutes the wore dao-
gerous consolidation T. of "sections for
the sn noosed dangerous force of indi-
viduil Sute sovereignty. The destruction
of slavery was a mere incident of ihia wr
between systems oi philosophy in jroTem
ment. Four millions of negroes, as prop-
AUSTIN, TEXAS, THURSDAY. JULY 1, I875i
erty, were not worth the life-blood of any
single Northern or rVmt hern hero who fell
on any Southern battlefield. If the war
were waged to save slavery the owners of
i slaves would not hsve been exempted front
soldierly service, and it i true that no in
telligent ex-slavehoh'er, however terrible
his poverty, has been beard to say that he
would have .the institution restored,
ne may. be forced by unaccustomed want
to say he would gladly have the- whilom
moneyed value of his slave?, but he wonld
have no more of negro slavery. There was
a higher, nobler sentiment and purpose, ani
mating the masst s of those who fought in
Southern armies. They believed, as shown
in the discjssions affecting Kansas, that
their local rights and personal independence
were violated. The war, in its spirit and
purpose, was one thing: the war, as waged,
was quite a different thing, and was ac
tually fought for a Very different purpose.
As we were governed we absolutely fought
to set up a consolidated empire at Rich
mond and, to. oov throw, this-; Southern
union, another assailed' it, that the
first and older might not jxr Jforre
cease to have any desirable existence.
As the existence of two Unions was infinite
ly worse than that of one, and would have
been, from what we see of this one that
survives, doubly intolerable, there were
few tears shed when the weaker was no
more and its death was a collapse, and with
the frightful lesson of frightful horrors in
cident to consolidation, the only legacy
left us, it is not strange that, North and
South, ti.e pendulum of popular opinion
moves steadily backward from Richmond
and from Washington, as enibod iments of the
idea of central despotism, towards Texas
and local independence. It is surely need
less to suggest that while States are integers
and of themselves distinct republics con
fessing the full force of every compact of
union, that counties and cities, are most
wisely governed when freest. But this as
sertion is only applicable to communities
capable of self-government. It has been
demonstrated, times without number, that
legislative and executive powers of corpor
ations cannot be wisely or decently admin
istered when suffrage in, mixed white and
African districts is universal. Therefore,
to perfect the idea of local freedom, suf
frage in local elections must be restricted to
those interested in the maintenance of hon
est government and capable of sclectiug
men to shape it.
RELIUIOUS TOLKHAACE.
As this is the American centennial year,
it is legitimate to note that among all the
changes for good wrought among men1 dur
ing the last hundred years none has been
greater than that which has so largely
tended to the superinduction of. a spirit of
charity and toleratiou for every religious
belief bearing upon it the stamp of sincer
ity, and no change so surely as this shows
the advance that mankind has made to
wards true religion. The breaking down
of the barriers between sects, or, rather, the
recognition by each of the common pur
pose of all fresh evidence of which we
see every day in acts of mutual concession
-ris the best proof how wide-spreading and
far-reaching the tolerant spirit has become.
Poets, it ii said, are but mirrors in which
are reuected the sout-uie ot tne people
among whom they sing, and Tennyson
truly reflects the spirit of the present age
Ti not the creed that makes the man,
It is the man that justices the creed;
For each mutt sare hi. own sou I aa he can,
Since each ia burdeLed with a ilitti reol net d.
The contrast between the feeling on re
ligious subjects now and that which existed
a century ago is great indeed. Then, in
each State of Europe, one single belief was
regnant as the national religion, and to all
who dared stray beyond its iron bounds was
meted out the harsh penalty of fine, im
prisonment, exile, or death. In Asia still
darker creeds held Bway, and upon their
doubters were visited like penalties, while
in no part of the world could there be
found a nation, State or people in whom
the principle of freedom in things religious
bad made an abiding place. To contem
plate these benighted ages behind us causes
soul-sickness to every true man, and the
fact that this sad record, of intolerance
reaches back almost to the foundation of
the world brings up in deeper, blacker out
line its weary, despairing gloom.
Now," in each civilized land, spiritual lib
erty is not only conceded as a privilege,
but accorded as a right; while even in most
countries that we term heathen "-because
their ways are not our ways there
is practically the same unrestricted freedom
of worshio. The dars; night through which
the world has passed was not without its
uses. As progressive ages have rolled on,
cleansing man .with the fires of tribulation.
a purer; life has been generated, and the
fierce hates of sects and churches have
been forced gradually to fade away and
give place to a letter spirit of fellowship
and love. The world is beginning clearly
to see that the true religious spirit is that
of sympathy, of helpfulness, of active love.
It enters into the lives of other people, re
joices in their jy, sorrows with their sor
row, and forgets itself in them. It cannot
be shut up within the bosom of aoy sect

We may talk as. eloquently and write as
charmingly of the, physical attractions of
Texas as if .inspired.; It is easy enough to
tell the world bow redundant are the riches
and boundless the resources and matchless
the grain crops of Texas, each bushel 'of
wheat weighing and worth twenty percent.
more than an equal quantity of the same
product of Eastern' "States."1 Strangers tra
versing boundless plains of living verdure
cannot be mistaken as to the productive
value of 'Texan 1 farms.' There is'nd
country, in all its incidents of soil and cli
mate, under the sun... Ihere u no
country whose very diversity of products
constitutes such perfect unity and begets
all the incidents of s mighty commonwealth.
The breezes that blow perennially from the
sea bear upon their wings ever refreshing
draughts, drunk by vegetation and exhaled
as the perfumes of matchless flowers in the
sweetest breath of spring time. There are
boundless forests of pine in eastern coun
ties, like those of the East, to supply hodses
and fences for the empire, and people of
western districts wilt be blest with every
accessary of comfort and civilization begot
ten of railways. One harbor after another
will be opened snd its channels deepened
and railwavs built, until live oak as well as
pine forests will enrich the people of Texas
as no people have been enriched from such
sources of incalculable riches. The native
home of the bison, succeeded by cattle ef
civilization as the .narrow gaage railway
supplants the mustang of the plains, be
comes the boundless pasture for countless
herds that feed the. nations. In fact, he
who. prophesies most wildly end extrava
gantly of the future magnificence of this
mighty commonwealth is the most rational
of thinkers and capable and accurate of
statesmen.

lanians, had learned,' even before the war,

and that no description of molasses, except
that" drawn directly from sugar houses, is
cither safe or palatable. Golden syrups,
augar.drips, etc., are delusions and snares.
professor of chemistry has examined a
dozen varieties of syrups sold at the gro
ceries, and says that all of them are ".doc
tored,'' made by the 44 sulphuric acid pro
cess," as follows "A warm (131 degrees
Fah.) mixture of starch and water, of
about the consistency of cream, slowly
poured into a boiling solution of one per
cent sulphuric acid (oil of vitriol), the
whole lxul4 for some time then the acid
is neutralized by chalk, and the mixture set
aside. -When the sediment has settled in
the bottom the liquid is dipped off and
boiled down to a syrup. Thissyrup may be

of starch, however, "old rags can be and are

The partial details of terrible incidents
of the earthquake come from Mar.caibo.
The city of San Jose de Cucata was utter
ly destroyed. The sun rose on the eigh
teenth ult. brightly and the day was beau
tiful. At 11 o'clock the terrific earthquake
shock struck the city. The whole popula
tion rushed into the streets, and the move
ment of the earth was like that of
the waves of the sea and continued
for fifteen seconds. Every house, almost,
Was leveled with the ground and thousands
of people were suffocated by an impalpable
dust that escaped from crevices in the
earth. All was over in two minutes, when
the wind boreaway the dust. The surviv
ors fled to the country and the fearful
day was followed by the Btill more
terrible night. 1 hey had moved to an
open plain where encampments were
established. Rain commenced to fall in
torrents, and amid the impenetrable dark
ness the groans of the wounded filled the
air, varied only by the sharp detona
tions proceeding from explosive materials
amid the ruins. The coming of the next
day showed that not more than 2000 re
mained alive. Beneath the fallen houses
lay from 8000 to 10,000 dead. The letter
also reports the destruction of other places,
as mentioned in the account of the earth
quake by way of Panama some .two weeks
ago.
Perhaps the grandest and most imposing
military and civic-pageant -ever seen in
America ?was witnessed by the countless
thousands of spectators in Boston, who
crowded every balcony, window, tree snd
standing place along a route of eight miles,
The Veteran Division, among the military,
Keras the object of universal attraction.
headed by the Ancient and Honorable Artil
lery, in whose ranks the first gentlemen in
Boston paraded as full privates; judges.
doctors, lawyers, mechanics and manufac
turers "the solid men' of Boston" Gen.
N. P. Banks in command." Old Virginia
and the Palmetto State were honored with
assignments in this trtme de la ereme divis
ion. The Artillery Blues, from Norfolk.
and the Washington Light Infantry, from
Charleston, were specially honored with
continuous rounds of cheers and compli
ments throughout the grand march. The
Eutaw flag, as soon as recognized, brought
tremendous outbursts of patriotic feeling.
Every man in the line wore palmetto cock
ades and bannerets of palmetto leaves in the
muzzles of their rifles. Col. Andrews and
Col. Simmons were with Governor Gaston,
on the grand stand, while the Southern
column passed in review. Their welcome
was hearty, snd was honored by the pres
ence of a large number of the most prom
inent and influential Boston merchants.

ot style tlw message is almost ludicrous.
Grant's legs must have been weary when
he twisted about among the crooked, io
Tolved and incomprehensible sentences that
eciftlly distinguish the. first paragraphs
of this unique and cgly state papeK; There
are'inTol?ed modes of expression, which

"all the world and the rest of mankind.?
The President first adrisea the speed
iest possible return to. specie payment;

Then the President discusses our foreign

No rccommsndation is made with reference

Reform -of naturalization- laws and of

thus presented, there will arise a fierce con

facts. lie would have Congress define

The attloloa of the Statesman upon con

good old democratic doctrines, which,
cmVodicd in the early fundamental laws
of the country, havo sunk so deeply
into the American -heart' as to make
it impregnable to tho -'wily Workings
of Radicalism. The disposition," thus
evinced, among ether thing points to
wards the curtailing of executive power,
which for the sake of good and pure "gov
ernment must bcabridged. Every session
ot Congress and every session of Stato leg
islatortSf for a decada and, a half have

Eut the turning point baa keen, gained,
and for the future it can be-. expected the

points concerning1'.' the 3ac'arJ R

the State at large. Such a course is the
part of wisdom. Delegates chosen in
this way would act as a check upon any
local interests which might present them
selves. They would feel that their selec
tion was in the interest of every part of the
State, and would prove a most wholesome
check upon all narrow .questions.
Then, again, it is certain that a
State convention, nominating candidates
at large, would exorcise peculiar
judgment in selecting them. From such a
source we would expect the nomination of
men of wide views, ability, and earnest de
votion to the interests of good government.
A sound fundamental law procures sound
legislation, and sound legislation brings ma
terial prosperity. All know that local pop
ularity, sometimes accompanied, with no
marked ability, often procures preference,
while the action of a State convention
wduld override all such effects. Again, too,
in the selection of delegates at large, those
influences which often maneuver nomina
tions would not succeed, and this clement
of a constitutional body would bo untram-
meled by such influences for control.
Let the convention be composed, then, of
ninety members let two members be se
lected from each district by a district nomi
nation, and let the same body which nomi
nates these chooso delegates to a State con
vention to nominate candidates fof election
at large. Each district,' then, would select
two representatives, making sixty, and the
State thirty, creating a body of ninety.
Such a plan, in our opinion, has much safety
in lt,: and we believe 'it is the one which
comes nearest meeting the public desire,
Ix supporting its idea that the district
judges of the State should be appointed by
the Governor, the Austin Statesman wrote
down the following as an argument:
"It is well known that in some of the
districts tho Radicals havo a majority of
voters,: and as the main support of that
party comes from the negroes, there can be
no doubt that they would, run a man who
would be utterly incompetent, or ona not
fit to preside on the bench-" 1
Granting that the argument is specious
and addresses itself directly to the policy of
'.party domination," we stop to ask if such
a policy is honest and Democratic? In the
text-book from which we gleaned our ideas
of Democracy, It Was .written by the great
Thomas Jefferson that one of the funda
mental nrinciDles of DemoeracT is abso
lute acqniesence ia tho decisions of the ma
jority the rital principle of republics
from which there is no appeal but to force
the-vital principle and immediate parent
of despotism and again, that Democracy
means "a jealous care of the right of elec
tion by the people." These are the ideas,
which should control the settlement of this
discussion. If we profess Democracy, let us
also practice it, remembering that to crush
tho voice of any majority is tantamount to
establishing the favorite "bayonet rule"
of Radicalism. Houtton Age. t
The j&g seems to have, overlooked the
reason which we gave for having the -district
judges appointed. It was not so much
to defeat Radical candidates as to secure
comment and tu'UaUa judges. Our cotem
porary must remember that Jefferson's De
mocracy did not anticipate negro suffrage,
but looked to the rule of the white man alone.
Judge Taney, in the celebrated Dred Scott
case, decided that the negro was" not a citi
zen of the United States, lie was the
purest and ablest judge who ever wore the
judicial ermine.
Shall we now throw away good govern
ment by placing the ballot into tfie'. hands
of the negroes, who are notoriously corrupt,
without limitation or restriction I Shall
we, in order to be considered sound Demo
crats, giro up the substance and grasp the
shadow?
"While wo havo never approved bfT any
principles except thoso of Democracy, and
still hold to them as tho sheet anchor, of
liberty, we cannot shut our eyes to the out
rages which havo been Inflicted upon the
South by negro suffrage. Therefore- in
a spirit of conservatism wo advocate the ap
pointment of the district judges. Both
whites and blacks are equally interested in
haying pure and able men upon the bench,
and both races will be represented in the
Legislature where tho appointments will bo
either confirmed or rejected.

Not' long ago the Galveston Xev$ in
veighed against the conduet of Gen. Robert

that they were going about ever the South
ern States and inducing travel over railway

ton. If all immigrants sent by these gen

through Galveston, Governor Hubbard and
his associates would have deserved all com
mendation, but sinco Texan railways were
enriched, ,.and tho .wharf company1 cot
profited, the JYirwj was sorely grieved and
tho peripatetic lecturers on the glories of
Toxas were ungratefully snubbed. Every
thing must pay tribute to Galveston as Gal
veston does to the Xart. 1.' f
Col. Hobby lectures in Galveston on the
charms of Tom Scott and his ncwly-faegled
Pacific Railway scheme, and the Galveston
Chamber of Commerce Is sought to be in-j
veigled into an approval of Scott s plans
devised, it Bcems, solely for tho aggrandize
ment of, the Pennsylvania RoaaV The
Xexrt thinks the Texas and Pacific
Road worthy of moderate approval
since it crosses the northern counties of
Tixas, but sinco it docs not go down into
the boarels of the devil fish, encompassing
Galveston, its trade, harbor, present, past
and future, the XettM coolly snuh3.CoL
Hobby and Tom Scott's hobbjj and goes
jogging awsy'on its own hobby, the ever
lasting wharf question. We wpuld mod
estly suggest to our vivacious co temporary
that Galveston is not Texas; that tho value
of a road to Texas is not to be determined
by its relations to the devil fish, or to the
city it encompasses, and that when distin
guished citizens of Texas go abroad to
make known the attractiveness and re
sources cf our mighty State, that the worth'
and Ti!ue cf their miasioa are not to be
measured by results that aHect a vigorous

an 'empiraT Galvetoa is not Texas, and

property, and th 0 ragged, wretched editor
will nevermore hear the reason assigned for
Li3 robbery that 4 i.!;e public is interested in
this article," or "the public good will be
promoted, and therefore I can't pay for this."
Newspapers, like tanneries or saw mills,
have existence to make a living for their
owners. These owners are no more or Jess
concerned about the public weal than other
good citizens, and are under no more or less
obligation to serve the public. If, therefore,
any wish to promote a good cause by using
the machinery of a newspaper, they must
pay the cost. In most cities, religious no
tices are published at half the rates charged
for other advertisements, and people of an
Eastern community no more think of using
a newspaper for any purpose, however be
neficent; "without" relieving the newspaper
6wner of the ost of laboror without pay
ing for the use of materialsthan they would
expect touso the horse and buggy of a
liveryman jn a funeral procession without
paying for tho pleasure. Tho airing of
one's name in print is infinitely more profit
able,, and even more delightful, than of the
person in the most faultless landau, and in
no event tteitherthe good nor the bad of a
community have tho right to make a pack
horse, as is commonly done, of a poor devil
of an editor.

A'people, hko acity or State, are known
abroad cmlythrough the press that repre
sents them. JIf coarse, vulgar, bullying,
swaggering newspapers .went abroad, from
Austin, strangers would shun this city as
travelers, do oriental pest houses. The
spirit of the press is naturally supposed to
animate the community in which the news
paper has existeuce. A tastefully printed
newspaper, wisely conducted and filled
with reading matter beyond the reach of
criticism, makes tho homo of that paper at
tractive to educated and: refined people.
There is lawlessness in fact and brutality in
action wherever there is lawlessness of ex
pressiba and brutality in thinking illus
trated in a local newspaper press. No de
cent community will, sustain an indecent
newspaper, and no ignorant town or vil
lage will support an admirably well printed
and costly publication, The Statesman
will never give publicity .to a story that
will make a woman blush, and never thrust
the private grievances of its conductors in
tho face of the public, and simply because,
if there were no better reason, no decent,
intelligent community will sustain an inde
cent newspaper, and no ignorant town 01
village will sustain a costly and well printed
publication. '.As .the 'Statesxas grows in
circulation, and thus, in its resources, it is
steadily improved in every., incident and ap
pliance of .mechanical, art, and, sinco it
has sought sedulously never to offend the
taste ot the most fastidious, even when in
veighing mofet bitterly against the depraved,
may we 'not hope that each reader of the
Statesman, interested in promoting the
fortunes. of Austin, and of Texas, will be
come our active agent, and each secure fur
us at least one additional reader?
THE SIIJInTIS AND GLORY OF GRANT.
Of all disorderly governments, those of
Central American States nor excepted, that
of Mississippi is unexcelled. It is black in
all its incidents and adjuncts, and when
ever unafricanized, is redolent of purest
carpct-bagism. .Alcorn and. Lamar in Con
gress, and Christian and Orr upon the cir
cuvt.bencb,' are, we believe, tho only four
genuine wlijte men holding office under the
laws of tho blessed negro-gowned com
monwealth.; If white people were in power
Northern Radicalism would ascribe the
Yicksburg emtute- to the overbearing inso
lence of ex-slaveholders or to .wretched
systems defined by Democracy; but here
are all elements of discord actively ruinous
and there is a faultless civil rights act, as
originally written by Charles Sumner, in
remorseless operation in Mississippi. In
fact tho absolute perfection of the whole
scheme of reconstruction is illustrated in
the policy, laws, and condition of Missis
sippi. Property is almost valueless; vil
lages and towns are dismantled, deserted and
go to decay farms, onco worth thousands,
aro sold for hundreds; -poverty supplants
opulence the cost of local government is
tenfold greater than when tho people were
tenfold richer than to-day; and now, to
crown the work of Radical optimists, a war
of races is inauguratdd. There is singular
appositencss discovered in the fact that as
Grant won his brightest military laarels at
Yicksburg, so his civil citreer finds here its
crowning disgrace.
A BniGimcn Fcturh. One hundred and
fifty or more negroes are said to have been
killed in the recent fights in and about
Yickabilrg. We have enjoyed ten years of
uninterrupted Radical rule, and still havo
na peace, and still have tho intolerable sys
tem of taxation incident to a' stato of war.
Is it strange that Radicalism is everywhere
condemned by the people, or that the Pres
ident virtually abandons the party by fail
ing to approve its choicest poliey of Wrong
to the white people of the South I "Honest"
Ben Wade, so called hj the organs of his
party, ia 1 the face of facts here defined,
adopts a new coune and, condemning the
civil rights bill, declares for moderation and
justlcev The course of political events
will be very materially modified,' even, by
the. present Congress, and the South gains
everything by each incident now dis
tinguishing' tho conduct of parties and
course of public events. Not only do the
signs of the times portend the election of a
Democratic President, but a degree of pros
perity for the whole country never before
known; Radicalism,with all its blundering
and crimes at Yicksburg and elsewrhere,"
cannot destroy the growing greatness, re
press the energies or defeat tlx aims of
American patriotism. The shadow of the
negro, as cast across the pathway of bouth
ern progress, is not so black as whea the
war closed, when Thad. Stevens raved and
Bon Wade stormed and roared ia Washing
ton. A brighter day dawns, and especially
epea Texas, where the crjro, as aa element
of political force, is almost unknown and

The U-adrr in your dailv issue of tho
eleventh instant in refmncc t the comjH).
aitionof the constitution! convention soou
to be called in thia State, closes w follows:
'Let the couvi ulioa be compod, then, of
ninety members; let two members, be se
lected from each district by a district nomi
nation, and t the aome body which: nomi
nates those choode. dclfgates ta a State con
vuntion to nominate candidates for election
at large. Each district, then, would select
two representatives, making sixty, and tho
State thirty, creating a body of ninety.
Such a plan, in our opinion, has much safety
in it, and wo believe it. is the one .which
comes nearest nif-lini the public desiic.".
From tho views expressed permit "me in
the most friendly spirit to dissent wholly.
In the first place, in a constitutional con
vention in a Suto like this, having so many
counties, which arc distinct political organi
zations, and which represent interests, pur
suits and products so widely different, a full
representation of each interest," pursuit or
staple production is requisite Ninety mem
bers will not give it, and the district system,
if it be according to population, will drown
the voico not only of counties but of whole
sections. 4:1
No such injustice is intended by you, but
such a mo Jo of districting representation,
and such a limit in number as proposed, will
necessarly produco tho injustice stated, j
Nor is there tny sanction for the supposi
tion, that the smaller number will hare
greater wisdom than the larger, and expe
rience in legislative bodies does not prove
it. If such be tho case, your argument
proves too much, and shows, that the fewer
tho number of parsons who are in
trusted with the powers of government the
better for tho people Carry this but
logically, and you prove an aristocracy bet
ter than a republic, and 'so tho. one-manpower
better than an aristocracy t or li
jrarchy.
The number must not,' therefore, bo so
limited that each section of tho State and
each interest will not have representation.
Limit the convention to ninety.' and
make representation by voting population,
or by the number of inhabitants, and the
voico of the whole frontier, from Red River
to the Gulf, is practically silenced, or else j
the districts w. I be fin large that no one man
could ,19 rega. ,j as a fair representative
of the whole. J
Fortunately for this question there Js in
Texas a precedent, more than one pre
cedent, viz. The consultation that framed
tho Constitution of 1800 and later, the
convention which framed the Constitution
of 1S43, and still later, the secession conven
tion of 13C1.1
Tho public acts of tho people of Texas
at different times, under various: circuta
stances, have sanctioned the practice
adopted, and it is now part of tho unwrit
ten law, as deeply engraven upon the minds
of tho people as if it had been, promulgated
as law by some superior power. In' the last
two cases, 184.-J and 18Glf.S.re was a basis
on population, but there was a provision
also, that each organized county should -be
entitled to at least one representative..
In tho populous counties the .basis niiglic
be high on account of the expense attached
to the convention, but each political organ
ization should be represented. This subject,
by way of illustration,- might be enlarged
on to a great extent, but I only proposQ to
stato points. y.f
I am utterly opposed .to any system
which will give tho selection of candidates
to a party caucus or convention, in: a dis
trict composed of. several counties, pr for
the btate at large.
Do not understand mo as beln? opposed
to the party caucus or conventioa Bystem in
proper cases, but, happily, thesd cases are
comparatively few, and generally relate, to
the expression 01 a particular policy to be
pursued by the State At best, caucuses
and conventions are among what may "bp
denominated necc$nrv evils. In 'this "case-
there is no necessity for a party caucus,
either district or State.
The Democratic party is so largely ia the
ascendant that it can afford to be liberal
and can allow nn untrammelcd expression
of opinion. It can even allow a number of
tho opposition to bo electod, if their con
stituency chooso to send them. An entire
absence of the opposition in political bodLcs
is not a healthy state; all experience shows
this:
But there is no danger that Ue f inven
tion, however constituted, will not bcDcmo
cratic to the core; hence, wc need not erect
a machinery to dictate or chooso candidates.
Neither need we fear that the people. If let
alone, will not take an interest in tho .elec
tion, or that they will not in tho main elect
the very best.mon who will serve i
This is to bo a very different body from
that of ordinary Legislatures. It is to be
one which is to f ramo the Organic law
hence, you will scarcely bo ablo to find the
farmer, mechanic, merchant, physician, or
lawyer, however his business relntioms may
press tjpon him, who will not answer to the
call of bis fellow-citizens. It is an honor
to servo in such a body, and scarcely any
one will refuse But tho great majority of
men worthy of having a place in such a
body will hesitate before going into a pcram
blo for the candidacy before a district or
State convention. So it is plain that the
convention or caucus, district and State,
system will keep out of the .conven
tion many of the men who should be
there, and at the same time place iu the
convention men who ought not to bo there.
I am opposed to such a system ia all cases
whenever it can be dispensed with, for it is
plain that the wishes of tho people of the
oountics to bo represented cannot have fair
play.
The question of expense- is of secondary
importance. What is wanted is a full rep
resentation of every interest in the State,
and that the delegates sent shall bo the free
choice of the people who send them.
In this way only will we give satisfaction
to. the peopio at large, and bo able to pro
duce a Constitution that will last for gen
erations. LicEnrv."

Tha pretest Constitution of ur State
was, in many respecU, objectionable to
the people. It was sot created by them,
nor was s the product of u emphatic pop
alar demand. It was the creature of the
military power, bora of rerolatioo, and the
result of aril war. Wbea enforced by a
narrow, obstinate and reckleae execntire,
smrUined by partisan and corrupt Legis
lature, it rested with exasperating and op
prtsaslre weight npon the energies of the
Bute. It waa, therefore, fitting and natu
ral that when the right of republican self
government was restored and the people
coold once more regulate their own affairs
in their own war, the delegated power of
the people abonld assemble In convention
to da away with the obnoxious organic la w,
and replace it by a constitution more in ac
cordance with the popular will, and with
the changed aspect of the times.
Accordingly the convention aa-embled,
but composed as is was of jarring and dis
cordant materials, representing most inade
quately toe intelligence and brain-power of
the Bute, paralysed from the very incep
tion by narrow jealousies and petty, local
prejudices, domineered over by dema
gogues, and potently misguided by secret
combinationa of cliques, the work of the
convention, after a lengthened and confused
session, is pronounced by the press and the
intelligence of Texas to be a failure a con
fessed and manifest failure. v:
-The most earnest of its defenders do not
speak of it as being positively good. They
advocate it apologetically, and when its
multitudinous defects, blunders and absurd
ities are exposed they refer to the "ease and
rapidity with which it can be amended" as
the only answer to adverse criticism and
hostile assaults..- The fact is patent that
the time had not yet come in Texas for the
formation of av satisfactory constitution.
The rude and evil times of war and military
occupation were tod recent. The pendulum
so long subjected to violent oscillations had
not yet regained its steadiness and poise.
Because extra vaga ice in administration and
corruption in legislation had become preva
lent, the convention, to prevent their recur
rence, burdened the Constitution with spe
cial enactment and inculcated the insuffer
able political heresy that the people are not
to be trusted with the management of their
own affairs, and that they who, in a re
public, are the fountain head of all power,
are not capable of self-government. Feel
ing the need of economy, the convention
swung to the farthest extremity of unwise
parsimony in doling out to its public servants
compensation inadequate and even piti
ful. TJnier the same pretext a bureau was
abolished which, in all communities need
ing the influx of labor and population, has
been found scarcely second to the railroad
and the public school in forwarding the ma
terial interests of the State.
Under the demagogue's plea that the
poor man's right to vote is inalienable and
inherept, and unmindful of the sound max
im taught by the accumulated experience
of a thousand years that universal suffrage
and universal education must no together,
they made a transparent show of providing
a school system, while limiting the amount
that future legislatures shall expend for
schools, and refusing even to permit the
taxpayers to vote their own money in their
own school districts for educational pur
poses, but tbey fastened unrestricted suf
frage on our Btate to the constant peril of
good order, to the danger of peaceable soci
ety, thereby arraying In every municipal
election labor agaiust capital, poverty
against wealth, and a monocracy against a
civilized, intelligent well regulated govern
ment of the industrious, hard-working tax
pavers.
The' convention interfered with every
thing else, meddled with everything else.
filled and cumbered their constitution with
restrictions of all kinds on the legislature
and on the people, but the right to vote
was not regulated the divine right of the
loafer,' the tramp, the bummer,, and the
drone to have an equal voice in the regula
tion of local and Suta affairs with those
who are taxed to maintain the whole ma
chinery of our government, and whose
Bvurdy shoulders uphold the pillars of the
constitutional fabric, waa passed without
limit or question. The direct tendency of
such action is to abolish and annihilate the
doctrine of Scale rule, and to bring the
whole 8outh under a general centralized
government of an imperial nature and char
acter.
A constitution against public schools,
against immigration, against local govern
ment by the taxpayers, against. railroads.
against the' just and proper regulation of
suffrage, against fair and remunerative sal
aries, against the development of the re
source of this mighty commonwealth,
against the progress and life of this age
and century, against all those means and
agencies that this great imperial State so
constantly needs, and in favor of anything
that checks and blocks and hinders the on
ward growth and steady prosperity of this
State, of mob suffrage and domination, of
fee and perquisites, of anything to get low
taxes such a wUUaty of melancholy blun
ders, timid compromises and confessed inca
pacity is not likely to win its way to the accep
tance of tb intelligent public. The organic
law nnder wbich we at present live is bad
enough, but its defect have been neutral
ised and well-nigh obviated by careful and
competent Democratic administration, and
the people bar nothing to fear in the fu
ture from It 'inaniul law" or other op
pressive feature; they are null and void
and dead, and can be so declared by the
Voice of the people. But this new supreme
law is, in U practical results, far worse.
To restriction it throws aronnd the legis
lature render that body powerless for cood,
and no amendments can work such sure and
prompt relief si its n j.cti.tn at the poll.
Our State at this period of ifs development
needs an organic law wtaitb shall be tl e
highest and mot complete expression at-
tataabic of the ngnts, duties ana rratchises
u the people 1 in a word, it needs every
thing which this Constitution is not,' and
wb it needs It mast and wilt have.
Tu Sultan of Turkey bas twelve hun
dred hoarisla hi harem. Brigham Young,
who la douis that sort of business for this
continent, thoald see to it tbstsre are not
eclipsed by any et the effete and busted
monarchic. This is the centennial year,
Brig., yots know, and H behoove a man of
jour development to arise aad shin.
Exwaxd said La that power had de
parted from th AtUalic States, to rest
thereafter In the Ulialsairpi Yaiky. Sew
ard was only a liu' too soon with that
lirijess.ii!e cotzxt.
OCH tELF-LOTB FLATTEBBB.
Twelve months aco the Btatssxai began
the advocacy of an enactment, statutory or j
constitutional, restricting suffrage in county
and municipal elections to taxpayers or
freeholders. The proposition has grown j
steadily in popularity until it acceptance
by a majority of the great convention in
Galveston and by the ablest newspaper in
America is confessed, and. sooner or later
the reform proposed must be effected. The
potency of a newspaper that steadily per
sists in adhesion to a fixed purpose is de
monstrated, and it is only the unthinking
or the uninformed or the timid demagogues
who withhold assent to the theoties the
Statkskah has persistently and fearlessly
promulgated. This eiuus approval at the
hands of the sblett body r representative
men ever gathered in Texas ia certainly
most gratifying to our vanity, ''even as it
gives assurance that the day is not distant
when Texas will be governed by the true
people of Tet jid when the confiscation
of towns and cities and degradation of
counties will be impossible. Then, too,
those provisions of the new Constitution
estricting the powers of local govern
ments sad thus violating the funda
mental principles of Democracy may
be annulled and, each county and town
making its own local laws, the disintegra
tion of Texas becomes no more possible
than it is desirable. The unity of the State
;s only compatible with absolute local inde
pendence and home rule,, and this fact is
no more peitinent to States in the Union
than to counties and districts of the empire
of Texas. Very' certainly, borne rule and
local freedom cannot be attained in Texas
while a nomadic mob is transferred from
one city to another to stuff its ballot-boxes,
and very certainly we can have no desirable
county governments in districts ravaged by
Africans. Plainly, therefore,' results we
have contemplated can only be attained by
the proposed restriction of suffrage,' and
while our vanity was flattered by the action
of the convention, infinitely more were we
well pleased thst the redemption of the
counties and towns from the clutches of
constitutional despotismbecomes possible,
pari passu, with the chances for the adop
tion of restricted suffrage.
IBB SIDB SCENB AT GALVESTON.
Ludicrous as well as grave events occurred
last week in Galveston in the midst of a
magnificent assemblage of seven or eight
hundred of the leaders of the people of Tex
as. Of these there were probably one hun
dred gay young gentlemen who could
hardly govern themselves when at endless
tables burdened with luxuries they sat
in Armory HalL With each of a thousand
champagne bottles-there were as many
throats, uncorked and each delegate ran
over until be was gaseous; and pungent and
exhilarating were the speeches made. Late
in the evening there were quite a score all
speaking at the same time, and the scene of
confusion was ludicrous to the last degree.
The excellent orator, John Henry Brown,
who bad no. opportunity to ventilate his
notions while the convention, sat, occupied,
at this noisy hour, one end of a long table.
He spoke with infinite vehemence standing
in a chair, and then he rose upon the table,
still seeking the attention of the multitude.
Of course he failed, and grew angry and
waxed exceeding wroth, condemning the
noisy throng very violently. .Meanwhile,
Col. Finley occupied the other. end of the
table. He, too, seemed irrepressible. But
be never grew angry, and while he gesticu
lated vehemently and his face evinced much
excitemeut, and eyes flashed forth all
the brilliant rainbow, hues that
are evolved from unsealed' champagne bot
tles, his lips were sealed. He opened not
his mouth. Men seeing him "much moved.
and conscious of his power and wit, gathered
closely about him. Those nearest soon dis
covered that he was a pantomimist and that
in action only was he 'copying our friend
John Henry Brown. 1 A the multitude com
prehended the facts and the practical joke
was appreciated,' Fin ley 'a silent eloquence
gained fresh admirer, and the uproar at the
close of each supposed terrific sentence was
simply fearful, and the greater the deafen
ing applause provoked by Finley, the great
er the impotent rage of the excitable Mr.
urown, and the process of inflation on
Brown's side and contraction on Finley's was
progressive till Brown burst and subsided
and Finley collapsed amid roars of laughter,
and the multitude roared.
We were told that the scene was fatal to
the capacity for locomotion of several mem
bers of the grave convention who passed the
night on the spot, and of a truth a jollier
night was never spent at a festal board thsn
at that spread by the people of Galveston
whose generosity was as boundless as the
capacity for er joyment of their delighted
guests. n
IBOtlr TEtAS,
The editors of tit North ate taking an
interest in our young State, and the force of
circumstances, coupled with the facts which
are daily rolling in npon them, urge them
to thi, a important and valuable news to
be given to the nation at large, and to peo
ple on the other side of the Atlantic If
the growth of our State was wonderful
during the past year w are well satisfied
with what ia now passing ia our midst that
oar increase in population and wealth dar
ing the coming year will be double that of
the past. Another thing, the das of peo
ple immigrating hitherward are not medi
ocre either in brains or wealth. Tbey all
bring more or lees ot money, wagons, im
plements and cattle for work. Any man
coming to oar Stat with one hundred dol
lars and having a fair amount of prudence,
industry and economy can make himself in
dependent in five years. Tb liberal journal,
the New Totk give. ome; good and
truthful words about oor -State. Read
them t Very few people I the' North
nave any idea of the progress which Texas
is aatklog in population and wealth thronfth
immigration from xher Stat and from
Europe. During the. year ending Sep
tember' 1, 1875, the tide of. immigra
tion was eaormoas. The local agent at
Deniaon reports to the Stat Superintendent
of Immigration that at the points under his
immediate jurisdiction, S3, 353 bad arrived,
bringing with them horses, mules, wagons.
cash and other property to the value of four
million .dollar. Besides, there were arri
vals by the way of the ferries of the Sabine
river, by the foreign steamers at Galveston,
and by other route. The superintendent
ia bis annual report claims that not less than
three hundred thousand immigrants have set
tled ia Texas iniisg the fiscal year just
ended.- Tie people of tt Sute also E?ic-
ipate a large increase of foreign immigration
ia consequence of the success which has at
tended the importation of live stock into
the English market. A live stock transpor
tation company bas been organized in Eng
land, which, by way of experiment, shipped
last spring over 1000 horses end cattle by
steamer from Rock port to Liverpool, and
the venture resulted so profitably that the
company reorganized, the capital increased,
and it is now proposed to put eight first-
class steamers in the trade. As the company
will rely mainly on thvir transportation of
stock for their profits, tbey will be able to
bring out immigrants at very low rates.
"The natural advantages of Texas are very
great, and now that it baa freed itself from
the domination of the Grant officials who
nearly bankrupted it with their jobbery
and extravagance, there is every reason to
believe thst it will increase in prosperity,"
AJt BXTUAOKIIINSBt INtlOEST,.
The most extraordinary revulsion in pop
ular feeling and ofiuion was manifested in
the Democratic State Convention when res
olutions were presented, drawn with re
markable clearness and force, proposing the
restriction of suffrage in county and town
elections to taxpayers. Some one proposed
that the resolutions be laid on the table:
The chairman said that the motion was out
of order, and that the resolutions must go
to the committee under the rule consigning
all resolutions, without debate, to the com
mittee on platforms and resolutions." Then
it was moved that the resolution be reject
ed, and this was regarded a test vote, de
fining the opinion of the boiy on the ques
tion and was, in fact, a vote on the merits
of the proposition. The motion to reject
was rejected amid loud acclamations. As
thoroughly as we are convinced that it is
the true policy of Texas to restrict suffrage
in local elections that affect nothing but
property rights, and not civil rights, to
householders or freeholders, we cannot see
the necessity of making this an issue in the
pending elections in Texas. We may vote
against the new Constitution because it
turns over towns and counties everywhere
to the tender mercies of the venal, drifting
mob that is bought and sold like cattle in
market overt, as has happened within a
twelvemonth here in Austin but why the
Democratic Convention should discuss this
question, even now, save as an incident of
a grievously defective code of organic law,
cannot well be seen. We supposed, there
fore, that the resolution would be summa
rily rejected, but the Democratic State Con
vention, certainly as learned and able
body of men as were ever gathered for like
purposes in the State, refused to reject and
the resolutions went to the committee. Of
course nothing more will be heard of them.
In. making a party platiorm special ques
tions and special narrow issues will be
evaded, and yet the question has been pre
sented and it must be discussed, and sooner
or later the right will triumph and Texan
towns and counties will no more be ravaged
by adventurers.
Thb most extraordinary act of magnanim
ity ever done was that of 3Ijor Buck Wal
ton. When a large majority in the Gal
veston Convention, s nd really every thought
ful, intelligent citizen of Texas, could name
no one more thoroughly qualified for the
Attorney Generalship, and when he bad re
ceived a large majority of the votes of the
Galveston Convention the rule requiring a
two-thirds majority then it was that Buck
Walton claimed the attention of the vast
concourse, and in choicest words of simple
eloquence withdrew from the contest. He
said that he did not know till his competi
tor appeared before the assembly that he
had lost an arm in battle, and that wben he
fell that with the unharmed hftnd .he
pointed the way and urged iiis comrades on
to victory. Then, too, this left hand was
shattered. Major Walton said that he con
fessed the fitness and supreme worth of his
competitor, and that he himself was no
longer a candidate. The audience did not
as sent very gracefully to Major Wal
ton's proposition, but his assertion of
purpose was "inexorable and the maimed
soldier i nominated. The nobility
of Walton's nature is confessed, but
the wisdom of the convention mty be
questioned. A public office is not Green
wich hospital for maimed and lamed sol
diers or sailors. If these must bu cared for
it were cheaper and wiser to provide fixed
stipends snd charities and Buffer offices to
be administered by the capable and learned
than to gratify a sense of public gratitude
or popular admiration by conceding offices
to the incapable. But in this cam it seems
that the heroic soldier is also'a learned and
able lawyer, and those who knew him per
sonally declared his perfect fitness for the
position to which the convention proposes
to assign him. v.
BlkllOP HAVE.
The above clergyman is another of Pres
ident Grant's favorites. He is plain and
outspoken, and is apparently not afraid to

Mr. Conway, in his lecture last week in

show us the fierce light of science beating
upon .man. Looking hack twenty -five
years,, the great scientific question that then
agitated the world was the antiquity of
man. Sir Charles Lyon was then in the

dering about the face of tbe earth trying
to pick up a few fa. t. With what horror
was Sir Charles Lyon looked upon, how
wild he was considered to be, wben be in
timated that men might have inhabited this
planet one hundred thousand years ago.
And yet before he did in what a d.fferebt
light waa Sir Charles looked upon. And
Mr. Conway humorously told ol an inter
view he bad with Sir Charles Lyon a few
months before be was laid in Westminster
Abbey, when be said I have become quite
conservative, and the ministers have told
me they are quite willing to make a bishop
of me." Twenty five years airo the finding
of skulls and flint arrows filled the world
with dismay, the more so as they seemed to
prove unquestionably that the world had
been inhabited by a race of men at an ear
lier date than certain religious people bad
arbitrarily chosen to place them. There
was found a skull deep beneath a mound
which nobody could tell was of artificial or
natural formation.' A great commotion

was found that tbe teeth of theskull had
been worn away apparently by the holding
of a clay pipe, rejoicings were bad in Chris
tian churches, because it established the
fact that that skull must have been anima
ted with life at a later period thap the dis
covet y of tobacco. But the proofs of man's
antiquity were not wanting. Mr. Conway
here told of the Kent cavern, with its con
clusive proof. There is in that cav
ern, he said, a strata of atalagmitic
rock formed by the filtration of water
through the roof a strata five feet deep,
and beneath this rock have been found flint
arrows. Two hundred and twenty-five years
ago, after the usual fashion of the Anglo
Saxon, a man cut his name upon the top of
the rock in letters one-eighth of. an inch
deep, and although tbe work of nature in
adding to the stone has incessantly gone on
siace that date, this one-eighth of an inch
has not yet been filled up or obliterated.
Thus, to have formed the five feet, of rock
beneath which tbe arrows were found a pe
riod of 130,000 yoaru was necessary. There
is nothing in the Bible, continued Mr. Con
way, to give mankind the impression that
tbe world has only exit-b-d 6000 years. An
old archbishop speculted and placed the
beginning of the world on !a November
morning, 4002 years B. C. It w a ignorance
that opposed Sir Charles Lyon as it has
been and is ignorance that opposes all the

arrows, too, was thought to militate against
the., liible by snowing that man was not

whereas, there is not a word in that book to
prove that man was not created a savage. It

glish. But even if it had been a step away
from tbe statements of the liible, Mr. Con
way said, science must have triumphed
where men were not ignorant or blinded by

antiquity, however little importance there

the truth.
The great field was struck by Darwin.

iiut the present Darwin probably conceived

it is that she heap .up knowledge.
uspect and criticises until tbe result is
